I think you are on to something there and also may have a good indicator for the future. My mother slowly acquired a massive health library. Sadly she's crashing her health anyway. I remember the D'Adamo "Eat Right..." series was the first time I heard about lectins and it explained so much about many of my issues. Gundry takes it to the next level. Did you hear his interview over at self hacked? You're likely ahead of that level of info but I always like to hear the voice of people if I can (maybe just my quirk). Potatoes and lentils used to be a killer for me. Today I mostly avoid them.

I stumbled on the idea of using your kryptonite foods as a redox test accidentally by eating something at a party. When I've been doing Docs routine right I have zero to low reactions to my leptin foods. About once a month I'll down something that would usually leave me hurting just to see if my "environment" is still right for me. I've found that to be very helpful as a mini BUN/creat like measure.

Enjoy your time with family. Sorry to hear about your stepdad. My own mother is seriously falling apart in front of me and part of me doesn't want to admit that at this rate she won't be around much longer, but it's true. I can tell that I'm not going to be very good at losing people close to me. It feels lie a kind of claustrophobia.

# Thread: Dosing DHEA using labs
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dosing-dhea-using-labs.3227/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-08-24T18:09:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dosing-dhea-using-labs.3227/post-46736>

How do you settle on a DHEA dose using serum labs? My serum DHEAs was 72 (range of 40-325mcg) and my ASI DHEA (DHEA + DHEAs) was 6 (range 3-10). I had been doing 4mg sublingually, but had to stop temporarily. Now I want to restart. I'm also wondering about doing the 4mg sublingual like before, and then adding in my 7-keto DHEA. But I'm wondering if the 4mg has to come way up. I figure there is better absorption sublingual avoiding the liver, but I'm not sure to what extent. Also noticing the vast difference in units of the supplement (mg) versus the lab values (mcg = factor of 1000 less).

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-25T01:35:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dosing-dhea-using-labs.3227/post-46861>

I'd say Dr K can probably judge that by experience a bit but really everybody is unique. I'd say the best way to go about this is to guestimate your dose, and then retest and adjust as you go along.

### shilohman — 2012-08-25T02:08:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dosing-dhea-using-labs.3227/post-46870>

Soul is absolutely correct. You need to supplement, test, adjust and retest. 4 mg is very low. Personally, I take 200 mg of DHEA and 200 mg of 7-Keto DHEA. With DHEA, you are better off starting high and working downward. Dr. Kruse could tell you after seeing your labs in a consult or you could just do your own N-1.

### vkiernan — 2012-08-25T02:49:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dosing-dhea-using-labs.3227/post-46881>

> **shilohman;47287 wrote:** Soul is absolutely correct. You need to supplement, test, adjust and retest. 4 mg is very low. Personally, I take 200 mg of DHEA and 200 mg of 7-Keto DHEA. With DHEA, you are better off starting high and working downward. Dr. Kruse could tell you after seeing your labs in a consult or you could just do your own N-1.

I wonder what the conversion rate is though when taking sublingal? It may sound low but may not be. Any idea?

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-25T03:05:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dosing-dhea-using-labs.3227/post-46891>

I'm also wondering about better conversion sublingually. I think you lose 90% (if I remember correctly) of oral hormones thanks to the liver. That's why progesterone creams have a much lower dose. I would \*assume\* the conversion rate would be similar for sublingual DHEA. So 4mg sublingual would be like 40mg in capsule....?

Shilohman - how did you know if your dose was too high??

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-25T03:11:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dosing-dhea-using-labs.3227/post-46894>

SL topical and PO are all different......for example I found in me topical DHEA works great. Oral too. SL not so good. Everyone is different.

### ealachan — 2012-08-26T14:58:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/facial-a-good-thing-orcrazy-thing.3246/post-47198>

All I do for my face now is wash vigorously with my hands (no soap, no washcloth) in the coldest water I can get from my tap, and then slather on some coconut oil every other day or so. Once in a while, I'll take some plain ol' baking soda and make a paste of it with water and use that to exfoliate, but I find I need to do this a lot less often now than when I first started. My skin looks and feels awesome.

edited to add: I hardly ever wear makeup, though...and when I do, it's only mineral eye makeup. I can't stand foundation.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-26T15:18:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/facial-a-good-thing-orcrazy-thing.3246/post-47202>

my facial is sticking my face in the wet sand at the beach while surfing.......that is a the epi paleo facial.

### ealachan — 2012-08-26T15:32:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/facial-a-good-thing-orcrazy-thing.3246/post-47207>

> **Jack Kruse;47640 wrote:** my facial is sticking my face in the wet sand at the beach while surfing.......that is a the epi paleo facial.

Funnily enough, we used to do that as little kids at the beach. My cousins and I loved to dig a hole in the wet sand, scoop it onto our skin and rub it all over, then go jump into the waves to wash it off. I remember it being very invigorating.

Makes you wonder - if we were to just let kids be and not try to make them stop doing things we as adults see as "silly", how they might end up in the long run...

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-26T16:02:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/facial-a-good-thing-orcrazy-thing.3246/post-47212>

instincts are primal knowledge telling you something buried in your DNA. Your socialization and culture cause you to ignore that knowledge at your peril.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-26T16:43:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/facial-a-good-thing-orcrazy-thing.3246/post-47221>

I've stopped washing my face with any kind of products, too. Cold, cold water and a loofah every once in a while does the job.

What about \*mud\* facial masks though.... aren't those totally epi-paleo? People take mud-baths, too  And mineral water baths, in Germany.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-26T17:06:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/facial-a-good-thing-orcrazy-thing.3246/post-47234>

face towel in cold water each day, scrub skin, moisturize with CO or almond oil.

(I use a new towel each day to avoid bacteria)

close friends always wanting to know what product I am using to get good skin

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-26T19:44:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/facial-a-good-thing-orcrazy-thing.3246/post-47282>

> **Souldanzer;47659 wrote:** I've stopped washing my face with any kind of products, too. Cold, cold water and a loofah every once in a while does the job.
>
>
>
> What about \*mud\* facial masks though.... aren't those totally epi-paleo? People take mud-baths, too  And mineral water baths, in Germany.

Which is what made me think of this! My BHRT Doc's appt next week is in a town with hot mineral springs. . .and I was thinking of going to take a mineral bath. If I can take my son with me - they have big giant mineral swimming pools. If I dont' take him with me , I was thinking of hanging at a spa, which got me to thinking about facials.

I've never actually had one - as I already look like a tomato, don't want any irritation. Logic tells me it's all cosmetic marketing BS, but then. . . . perhaps? I mean, is it some kind of hold over from grooming instincts? Was there a health reason for grooming each other - or was that simply a social bonding thing to make the tribe stronger.

### NWgirl — 2012-08-26T20:07:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/facial-a-good-thing-orcrazy-thing.3246/post-47290>

>
>
> What about \*mud\* facial masks though.... aren't those totally epi-paleo? People take mud-baths, too  And mineral water baths, in Germany.

I think there's some truth to this. When I was in Israel I visited the dead sea and went in the water and covered myself with mud. Felt amazing for days afterward. Actually, israel was the place on earth that I felt the best until I went epi-paleo. Their food is amazing. They are not afraid of food or fat and they eat large lunches and breakfasts and a lot of local products. Even though I ate some grains I felt good there. Not as good as now though, so imagine an epi-paleo diet there!

Are you guys day tripping down to grand junction w/the natural springs??? OMG effing Jealous. We always made a stop there on our way back from NM or UT on travel trips for swimming on our way back to the Springs.

### ealachan — 2012-08-27T21:39:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/facial-a-good-thing-orcrazy-thing.3246/post-47534>

> **Anna K.;47981 wrote:** Interesting, I'll give it a try. Anybody else tried this?

I used to do oil cleansing, yes. I used castor oil and olive oil, and I found that I had to use way less castor oil in my mix than the website recommended, or I'd get really dried out. I did it for about a year, and while it did help my skin, I found it to be a total pain even with having my oils pre-mixed. I HATE futzing around with stuff in the bathroom, though. I don't own a hair drier, I hardly ever wear makeup, my eyebrows are...well, let's just not go there.  Suffice it to say, I'm ultra-low-maintenance when it comes to beauty routines, so YMMV.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-27T23:06:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/facial-a-good-thing-orcrazy-thing.3246/post-47549>

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-27T23:39:54+0300

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-28T21:33:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/facial-a-good-thing-orcrazy-thing.3246/post-47875>

Ok, I confess, I thought I would go back/finish off my alpha hydroxy and beta hydroxy expensive lady crap before going out and down the castor oil route.

It's been almost a year of just doing oils, and I think my skin is find. . I've not noticed dramatic improvements, but nothing negative either so "just oil" seems to work just fine.

I do notice when using oil as an eye makeup remover, if I'm not careful it stings a little. That's probably an operator error situation tho.

I think I won't get a "facial" however, I don't really have blackheads. I am able to squeeze the pores and get something out of them - but is it the kind of thing that our pores SHOULD have something in them? Like it's always moving stuff around and that's just the way it is?

AND, why is it just the face pores that are full of "stuff"?

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-28T21:39:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/facial-a-good-thing-orcrazy-thing.3246/post-47878>

> **Cavemam;48326 wrote:** Ok, I confess, I thought I would go back/finish off my alpha hydroxy and beta hydroxy expensive lady crap before going out and down the castor oil route.
>
>
>
> It's been almost a year of just doing oils, and I think my skin is find. . I've not noticed dramatic improvements, but nothing negative either so "just oil" seems to work just fine.
>
>
>
> I do notice when using oil as an eye makeup remover, if I'm not careful it stings a little. That's probably an operator error situation tho.
>
>
>
> I think I won't get a "facial" however, I don't really have blackheads. I am able to squeeze the pores and get something out of them - but is it the kind of thing that our pores SHOULD have something in them? Like it's always moving stuff around and that's just the way it is?
>
>
>
> AND, why is it just the face pores that are full of "stuff"?

I hear you... I'm just using the CO, mac nut oil, cacao butter, and shea butter mix - I'm not going to do the castor oil stuff... in 3 days of "oil cleansing" w/the home made moisturizer my skin looks/feels better than it did when I was using perricone regularly... so I'm done... not going back.... I've also noticed that the black heads have lessened - too... maybe just using oil - balances your skin's natural barrier - which helps clear it out??? IDK

### diane — 2012-08-29T16:22:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/facial-a-good-thing-orcrazy-thing.3246/post-48075>

Last year my skin was so dry and blocked, nothing helped. When I went for a facial, the esthetician told me I needed to "exfoliate" - but I knew it wasn't an external problem - it was an internal problem.

I have noticed a huge difference upping my seafood - my skin almost glows! Plus, when my moisturizer ran out, I started using olive oil which is amazing. I want to try the coconut oil as well. Actually tried coconut butter - but the little pieces of coconut left a film on my face LOL. Oh well - live and learn! I use a bit of Burts Bees cleanser, witch hazel toner (rarely). But I do love makeup - and I won't give that up LOL. I've definitely cut down use on weekends, but somehow just can't get past not having my mascara.

### chewingthefat — 2012-08-30T17:33:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/facial-a-good-thing-orcrazy-thing.3246/post-48370>

I've been combining powdered colostrum with castor oil for a mask that I use during my (warm) magnesium bath, once a week. Just tried it because the colostum caused a little sensitivity when I ate it and, as someone with AI, maybe the dairy aspect isn't a good idea. Haha...wanted to get some use out of it. I wonder if it could cause any problems transdermally, since it made my gut hurt? Anyway, I \*like\* what it did to my skin. It seemed to do fine for both exfoliation (the colostrum powder was slightly rough) and it didn't dry me out at all. I had dry skin before I changed my life style, but now it is normal. I use a home-concocted toner afterwards: 2/3 Thayer's Origional Witch Hazel with aloe vera, 1/3 rose water and a few drops of vit. e oil. Then I apply a few drops of jojoba oil mixed with a few drops of argan oil. Only thing I'd change about my skin is the melasma and I'm trying to approach that internally...and hormonally.

# Thread: EMail Consults
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/email-consults.3254/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions

══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Pamela — 2012-08-25T18:32:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/email-consults.3254/post-47035>

I sent in all my info on Thursday morning but haven't heard anything yet. I really do understand Dr. K probably got inundated with these after the $99 offer but it would be good to know everything was received and that I am in the que. My suggestion is maybe set up an autoresponder that will at least let us know the email was received to the right email address.

Next suggestion, I also bought an email consult for my husband with the same PP address as mine. It probably would be good to have the people send in what PP address they paid with since we use different email addresses than what the Paypal address is.

I don't know how you're keeping track of knowing who is paid or not since someone posted the email address to send in the disclaimer right on the forum. In my opinion that should be a confidential email address for those who have purchased consults.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-25T18:47:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/email-consults.3254/post-47038>

I have only done about half of them......Lots of fires to put out.....they will get done. They always do. When I get done with consults today I a may bang some out. and when you do that with two emails.....make sure you let me know....a duplicate is often refunded on the spot unless you make it clear.

# Thread: Antibiotics

### Sem — 2012-08-26T06:39:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antibiotics.3262/post-47190>

My five year old had a staph infection on his leg. Not sure how he got it. And, thankfully, it was not the terrible kind but it does require ten days of antibiotics. Besides probiotics, what else can I do to help combat the negative effects?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-26T15:59:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antibiotics.3262/post-47209>

dirty veggies and lots of shellfish.......and a kiss on the forehead everyday.

His appendix will help him but you could also give him some vitamin D liquid for about three weeks to help him too

Thanks! We don't have a garden so the dirty veggies part is hard but I can do extra D and shellfish for sure. And kisses .... Absolutely!

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-27T01:41:08+0300

# Thread: Bacterial Resistance
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bacterial-resistance.3263/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-08-26T10:36:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bacterial-resistance.3263/post-47195>

This is amazing. I've been reading about bacterial resistance to antibiotics. The number of different ways in which bacteria can swap DNA (i.e. information, in this case about resistance strategies) and the speed at which they're doing it!

I heard about this book on one of the more unusual and eclectic paleo podcasts.

[http://www.amazon.com/The-Lost-Language-Plants-Ecological/dp/1890132888/](http://http://www.amazon.com/The-Lost-Language-Plants-Ecological/dp/1890132888/)

It's Chapter 6 that starts to get into all this:

> Nor do bacteria compete with each other for resources, as standard evolutionary theory predicted, but rather, they promiscuously cooperate in the sharing of survival information.

We do live in interesting times. It's not just that penicillin will no longer do, but that the current intellectual models for understanding ourselves and the world around us will longer do.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-26T15:17:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bacterial-resistance.3263/post-47201>

Now stop a minute and think why bad gut flora is a tough thing to change........now you get it. You need to consider a transplant when other things have failed.

### Michael — 2012-08-26T16:49:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bacterial-resistance.3263/post-47223>

Because they can adapt as fast you throw something at them?

And, _pace the neo-Darwinian's, they don't have to sit around waiting round for a helpful mutation to occur at random (which would probably take some while).

Part of the problem, as I'm currently understanding it, is that you don't get unused space in nature (or don't tend to at any rate). Where do we usually see bare earth? In someone's garden between their flowers. You don't get that in the wild. And if something benign is not occupying space, there's room for something else to get in there. If you take an antibiotic, it could take almost everything out: you risk clearing the space of benign growth and actually making a "seed bed" for something nasty.

Even more spooky, something benign could turn nasty. So e-coli seems to be mostly benign and originally what we would now tend to term a "beneficial bacteria". But there are virulent strains of it. I read in that book that genetic markers indicate that it learned its resistance strategy from shigella.

The author is keen on saying that we're mistaken on attributing consciousness only to animals and intelligence only to ourselves. I don't know: I've long wondered about this one. There are phenomena that make me doubt the current viewpoint. Richard Jefferies has an interesting passage where he says that if you observe a man and a dog looking for a way across a stream you can't from an external viewpoint distinguish between how they behave:

> The dog races to the bank—he has been hitherto hunting in a hedge and suddenly misses his master—and, like his lord, stops short on the brink. He has had but little experience in jumping as yet; water is not his natural element, and he pauses doubtfully. He looks across earnestly, sniffs the air as if to smell the distance, then whines in distress of mind. Presently he makes a movement to spring, checks it, and turns round as if looking for advice or encouragement. Next he runs back a short way as if about to give it up; returns, and cranes over the brink; after which he follows the bank up and down, barking in excitement, but always coming back to the original spot. The lines of his face, the straining eye, the voice that seems struggling to articulate in the throat, the attitude of the body, all convey the idea of intense desire which fear prevents him from translating into action. There is indecision—uncertainty—in the nervous grasp of the paws on the grass, in the quick short coursings to and fro. Would infallible instinct hesitate? He has no knowledge of yards, feet and inches—yet he is clearly trying to judge the distance. Finally, just as his master disappears through a gateway, the agony of his “mind” rises to the highest pitch. He advances to the very brink—he half springs, stays himself, his hinder paws slip down the steep bank, he partly loses his balance, and then makes a great leap, lights with a splash in mid-stream, and swims the remainder with ease. There is, at least, a singular coincidence in the outward actions of the two.

[http://www.gutenberg.org/files/36948/36948-h/36948-h.htm](http://http://www.gutenberg.org/files/36948/36948-h/36948-h.htm)

Jeffries says you will never find a countryman - and these are the people who actually live with animals - who sees an animal as a superior kind of machine.

Then there are those birds that flutter along apparently pretending they have a broken wing to draw a fox away from their nest. How could a complex behaviour like that - and not just a complex one but one apparently full of intention - arise at random? Burn me at the stake, Richard Dawkins, but I'm not convinced.

I don't know whether consciousness exists in the plants and bacteria - and even the rocks - as Stephen Buhner (and maybe the Buddhists) would say, but it might just as well do.

There's certainly an "as if".

It's _as if_ the e-coil had thought, "F--- you with your antibiotics and antibacterial soaps and whatnot. I need space to live, so I'm gonna tool up."

So, yeah, fecal transplants. As I understand it, you use polyethylene glycol (PEG) to sterilize the patient's gut, so that really does finish off everything and allow you to start with a clean slate. You then reinoculate with a healthy person's gut-contents ... having learnt that something_ has to be there.

### Michael — 2012-08-26T20:20:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bacterial-resistance.3263/post-47294>

Here's what really made my jaw drop.

The author said that if we _had_ succeeded in coming up with antibiotics that bacteria couldn't develop resistance to, then, given that we have this co-dependency with them, we'd really have been in trouble. We'd have cooked our goose.

I thought: now you say it ...

> The ecosystem, both on land and in the water, depends heavily upon the activity of bacteria. The cycling of nutrients such as carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur is completed by their ceaseless labor. ...

[http://www.ucmp.berkeley.edu/bacteria/bacterialh.html](http://http://www.ucmp.berkeley.edu/bacteria/bacterialh.html)

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-08-27T16:30:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bacterial-resistance.3263/post-47479>

Fascinating! I've decided that if I am not satisfied with my overall health or goals within a few months, then I would go the route of fecal transplant.

But question for anyone: I wonder when the best time would be to do such transplant? I know that same-sex would be good, although I do know of succesful transplants were it was between sexes...

Thanks for the info guys!

# Thread: Would taking T3 lower bilirubin?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-taking-t3-lower-bilirubin.3274/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-08-26T21:08:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-taking-t3-lower-bilirubin.3274/post-47305>

My Dr annoyed & disappointed me when she wouldn't prescribe what Dr K had recommended, but she did agree to Cytomel (T3)--though less than the amount I wanted.

She wants me to get an ultrasound due to my elevated bilirubin (2.2, with a preferred range of 0-1.2).

My question is--Does anyone know if taking T3 can have an effect on bilirubin? Raise or lower? If it's addressed in a blog, I missed it.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-27T01:43:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-taking-t3-lower-bilirubin.3274/post-47382>

Bilirubin is present in blood at all times due to the breakdown of hemoglobin which occurs all the time. Normally, bilirubin is removed from the blood by the liver. Increased serum bilirubin levels indicate obstructive disease of the liver, hemolysis or actual liver cell damage. T3 replacement should not really affect it.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-27T20:59:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-taking-t3-lower-bilirubin.3274/post-47523>

> **Jack Kruse;47826 wrote:** Bilirubin is present in blood at all times due to the breakdown of hemoglobin which occurs all the time. Normally, bilirubin is removed from the blood by the liver. Increased serum bilirubin levels indicate obstructive disease of the liver, hemolysis or actual liver cell damage. T3 replacement should not really affect it.

Crap, but thanks much for your response.

You test for

progesterone,serum to adjust prometrium dose.

Draw blood on day 2-4 of cycle and (latter) on day 19-21

Better way is to do progesterone optimization indirectly,

using

pregnenolone as proxy

only Micronized Lipid Matrix Pregnenolone from Nutricology/Allergy Research

works as expected.

Good pregnenolone levels are cascading down as progesterone (latter cortisol),

additionally good pregnenolone (and DHEAs) levels will adjust positively your cholesterol, specially the LDL type.

so no need for a secod trip,

and for the USA address use nearest lamp post.

Only saliva cortisol panel is acceptable.

When you are sleepy and tired day in and day out, if you need afternoon naps, if you get dizzy raising up qickly,

saliva test becomes hardly important,

you already know that results will be bad.

So whats the point in testing it.

Work on your Circadian Rhytm and diet, it may get better that way.

### JanSz — 2012-08-28T21:37:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/western-canada-has-anyone-been-able-to-order-rt3-from-anywhere.3276/post-47876>

> **Endless;48260 wrote:** Thanks, JanSz!
>
> I will be planning a trip there soon, that's less than 4 hours from me.

Do not rush.

Lets figure out first your known and suspected problems,

figure out what tests may help you,

consider financial side (including cost of trip and lost wages).

.....

For example, personally I like to give thyroid hormone panel only cursory look.

Prefer to investigate and check

Complete Blood Count, Metabolic Panel, inflammation markers and few others

Steroid Hormone Panel (from top to bottom)

Fatty Acids

Micronutrients

then

second pass on thyroid

I do that for my self (I am not a doctor, but have doctor who humors me)

\-----------------------

One of important tests is

Spectracell Comprehensive Nutritional Panel

you can buy it here (no doc)

[http://www.healthremedies.com/intracellular\_vitamin\_analysis\_spectracell.html](http://http://www.healthremedies.com/intracellular_vitamin_analysis_spectracell.html)

and draw blood in your neighborhood.

[http://www.spectracell.com/find-a-draw-site-results/?branch\_country=CA&search\_by\_other\_go=Search](http://http://www.spectracell.com/find-a-draw-site-results/?branch_country=CA&search_by_other_go=Search)

LIFE LABS (Kimberly)

Address:

260 4th Avenue

Kimberley

BC CA V1A 2R6

There are other places.

\------------------------------

Fatty acids you can check at Genova Diagnostics (but I would have to) (no charge, I am not a doctor) tweak the results,

Genova must have sold out to Fish/Krill oil industry (and allows infinite amounts of those oils to be consumed, without affecting test results.)

Obvious absurd.

Essential & Metabolic Fatty Acids Analysis (EMFA) by Genova Diagnostics

[http://www.healthremedies.com/essential\_metabolic\_fatty\_acids\_analysis\_emfa\_genova\_diagnostics.html](http://http://www.healthremedies.com/essential_metabolic_fatty_acids_analysis_emfa_genova_diagnostics.html)

The place that would do blood draw and shipping for Spectracell likely would do same for Fatty Acids (all within Canadian mainland).

\------------------------------

Now would be good time to figure out list of blood tests at LabCorp.

I would need to know more.

\-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Generally, everybody

do not eat fish or krill oils (period).

eat

2/day-->Super Selenium Complex, 200 mcg 100 capsules (lef.org)

1 dropperfull/day

Lugol's Iodine Family Pack -- 6 (2 fl. oz.) bottles

[http://www.amazon.com/Lugols-Iodine-Family-Pack-bottles/dp/B001RRP3FW](http://http://www.amazon.com/Lugols-Iodine-Family-Pack-bottles/dp/B001RRP3FW)

must be this product or rework dose.

.............

.....

If after eating large amounts of shellfish & fish

the fatty acid analysis shows defficiencies,

that will be time to look for supplementation.

DHA alone comes from algae.

Note (good fatty acid analysis), most are worthless.

specially AA (Arachidonic Acid).

nutritionists in this bussiness advocate Evening Primrose Oil (it eventually raises AA).

Destiny, did Dr K say why berberine wouldn't work as well. I haven't been able to read these full studies, but according to the synopsis I found, the research showed that berberine was as effective as Metformin in dealing with blood sugar (which is what they were measuring, as opposed to gut health).

Jun Yina, Huili Xinga, and Jianping Yeb. “Efficacy of Berberine in Patients with Type 2 Diabetes” _Metabolism. 2008 May; 57(5): 712-717.

Ma, X., T. Egawa, H. Kimura, K. Karaike, S. Masuda, N. Iwanaka, and T. Hayashi. “Berberine-induced activation of 5′-adenosine monophosphate-activated protein kinase and glucose transport in rat skeletal muscles.” Metabolism_. 2010 November;59(11):1619-27.

### Destiny — 2012-08-29T13:13:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin-online-destiny.3278/post-48040>

> **KiwiLauren;48464 wrote: For those of you taking Metformin, when are you dosing? First thing in the morning? More than once a day?**

**

Hi Lauren! I ordered my metformin from inhousepharmacy as per Shijin. This is what they say on that website about taking it.

How should Apotex Metformin be taken?

You should take your Apotex Metformin tablets 500mg with a glass of water and with food. The dose you take and how often should be as recommended by your doctor. The usual starting dose is one tablet once, twice or 3 times daily during or immediately after a meal, to reduce stomach effects. You will have your blood sugar levels monitored and your dose may be adjusted after a few weeks, depending on how well your blood sugar levels are controlled. You should continue to take your Metformin tablets 500mg for as long as recommended by your doctor, which may be for long periods.**

The usual starting dose is one tablet once, twice or 3 times daily during or immediately after a meal, to reduce stomach effects. You will have your blood sugar levels monitored and your dose may be adjusted after a few weeks, depending on how well your blood sugar levels are controlled. You should continue to take your Metformin tablets 500mg for as long as recommended by your doctor, which may be for long periods.**

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-29T23:56:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin-online-destiny.3278/post-48197>

Regarding Insurance coverage for compounded drugs:

Because compounded medications are exempt by law from requiring National Drug Code ID numbers (NDC) many insurance companies will not reimburse without that number. However almost every insurance plan allows for the patient to be reimbursed by sending in claims forms. While you may be paying a pharmacy directly for a compounded prescription, most insurance plans should cover the final cost. I would suggest most of you should check with your insurance companies directly.

### Destiny — 2012-08-30T00:29:14+0300

However almost every insurance plan allows for the patient to be reimbursed by sending in claims forms. I would suggest most of you should check with your insurance companies directly.

Using menstrual cycle chart (below)

and the two commonly used days of menstrual cycle, (2-4) and (19-21)

the average, desirable levels are:

day (2-4)

Progesterone in ng per ml= 1.5

Estradiol in pg per ml = 35

P/E=37

day (19-21)

Progesterone in ng per ml= 14

Estradiol in pg per ml = 130

P/E=93

For men

Progesterone in ng per ml= 1.4

Estradiol in pg per ml = 20

P/E=60.6

\=============================================================

Those PE numbers are interesting.

They do not look like dr K's (400-500)

or

what I have previously seen at dr Lam's (drlam.com) web page (200-300)

It would be nice to reconcile this.

### JanSz — 2012-08-28T15:31:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pg-e-ratio.3280/post-47759>

> **Jack Kruse;48043 wrote: JanSz yours are blood numbers the ones I ref were saliva.....that is the difference.**

**

Rhythm Plus is saliva test

Sample report is here:

[http://www.gdx.net/core/sample-reports/Rhythm-Plus-Sample-Report.pdf](http://http://www.gdx.net/core/sample-reports/Rhythm-Plus-Sample-Report.pdf)

On a chart they wrote (using molar units):

P/E2 Ratio

Follicular(23-159) (first half of cycle)

Luteal(25-141) (second half of cycle)

Menapausal(33-116)

When I read P & E2 numbers from chart, for days (2-4) & (19-21), in the mid of (1 SD) range

the ones I marked with magenta short lines, I get:

Day (2-4)

Estradiol= 4.5 pmol/L

Progesterone= 325 pmol/L

P/E2= 325/4.5= 72

Day (19-21)

Estradiol=6.25 pmol/L

Progesterone= 650 pmol/L

P/E2= 104

\------------------------

For men, good (serum) values are:

Progesterone=1.4 ng/mL=1400pg/mL

Estradiol=20 pg/mL

P/E2=61 (molar ratio), weight ratio would be 1400/20=70 (note, almost the same as women's at the start of the cycle)

\------------------------

[http://www.drlam.com/articles/estrogen\_dominance.asp?page=1](http://http://www.drlam.com/articles/estrogen_dominance.asp?page=1)

For optimum health, the progesterone to estrogen ratio should be

between 200 and 300** to 1.

note:

this statement could use discussion/clarification.

\----------------------

At the moment, I am not able to find good reference, but from memory,

when we are speaking of P/E ratio,

it is really about molar ratio,

not

weight ratio.

So any input in weight units (say pg/mL) must be converted to molar units (pmol/L)

Calculator is handy when using results from serum, which are commonly in weight units.

[http://new.instacalc.com/421](http://http://new.instacalc.com/421)

\-------------------

.

What test have you done?

Post results on separate thread.

Scan all tests results then post them.

Do not re-write, to many opportunities for trouble.

P/E calculator is used (only) when test results are in

Progesterone in ng per ml

Estradiol in pg per ml

If your results are in other units, you must first convert them.

As far as I can tell, the P/E ratio is meant to be ratio of molar units.

Calculator converts weight based units into molar and then takes ratio.

.........

Do not panic, look at my posts above.

Depending on time in the monthly cycle you should be (72-102) to be in vicinity of ideal numbers, but there is (significant) room for differences.

Next, important consideration would be the absolute numbers for progesterone, E2 and Total E.

////////////////////////////

Complete Hormones™

[http://www.gdx.net/product/10049](http://http://www.gdx.net/product/10049)

Sample report

[http://www.gdx.net/core/sample-reports/Complete-Hormones-FMV-Sample-Report.pdf](http://http://www.gdx.net/core/sample-reports/Complete-Hormones-FMV-Sample-Report.pdf)

In results I do not see P/E2 on this 24hr urine test.

Value of progesterone can only be estimated using Pregnanediol.

It would be a stretch to use them to figure P/E ratio.

Note;

this is normal, urine tests do no figure direct progesterone levels.

/

.

Depending how deep doodoo you are in,

you may want to consider

24 Hour Urine Hormone Testing

[http://meridianvalleylab.com/urine-hormone-testing](http://http://meridianvalleylab.com/urine-hormone-testing)

probably the best test on the market available to public.

..........

### JanSz — 2012-08-28T21:56:41+0300

> **Lyndra;48311 wrote:** I've done a couple of Genova 24-hour tests. A big downside is that I can't figure out my Pg/E2 ratio. They return E results in mcg/g and Prenanediol (progesterone is not measured) in micromol/24 hour.

24hr urine test is a very good and overall liked by doctors in this business.

Likely it is excellent test to evaluate "virgin" patient. Someone who does not use many supplements or medicines.

Genova have a nice graphics in report.

Meridian Valley is likely the best.

I have a problem (created by me).

Most doctors give cursory hand waving to pregnenolone, those who want to use it (have on a list of stuff used in treatment), use pregnenolone in topical creams.

Since I give a preference to well-being of my own body, and am able to do as I figure logical (and as I please),

I have found that the best pregnenolone creams based on best carriers, costing top $$, are useless.

And I have found that only one pregnenolone tablets, work for me (Nutricology MLM).

Problem is,

use of those tablets (completely) messes up urine test

(likely because there is a boat load of unused pregnenolone and its metabolites on a way out).

So, I do not use urine tests.

I use blood testing and once a blue moon, saliva for cortisol.

//////////////////

I am not a doctor.

//

# Thread: Amber's optimal journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ambers-optimal-journal.3284/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-08-27T12:39:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ambers-optimal-journal.3284/post-47453>

Today is the start of my paleo/ct regime. I discovered this by 'accident' about 3 weeks ago and have been reading as much as i can. I started paleo diet today (50 grams of protein is SUCH A LOT!!) Just did my first face dunking , was quite pleasant - but cant imagine my whole body in water that cold just yet!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-27T14:07:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ambers-optimal-journal.3284/post-47455>

welcome ...............

### Lyndra — 2012-08-27T20:07:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ambers-optimal-journal.3284/post-47507>

Good to have you here! No need to rush into the full cold bath . Work your way into it over time. Before love, you'll be craving cold water on your face.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-30T09:48:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ambers-optimal-journal.3284/post-48319>

I'm at the end of day 4 now. I have lost 2 and a half kilos (5.5 pounds!) I noticed I had a slight headache all day yesterday and then woke up this morning with quite a bad headache (detoxing I suppose), so I took some painkillers this morning. But now I feel wonderful, the fog has cleared, have great energy, and I'm feeling thinner, and more enthusiastic than ever!

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-03T02:28:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ambers-optimal-journal.3284/post-49080>

One week under my belt - weight loss 3.8kg (8.4 pounds)! I'm really happy about that! I forgot to do the face dunking 2 nights, I've got my outside bath all set up now, so its ready to go. Bit nervous about full body ct, but this week I'll take the plunge! Or should I wait another week? My energy is quite good, but I know it could be a lot better, by elbows are sore - thats from working hard physically all week (yesterday I was building a dry stone wall which requires lifting heavy stones). I'm sure the ct would help with the soreness I'm feeling. Anyways, am going to check in later and share a couple of experiences under 'meet and greet' to do with ct.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-04T09:06:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ambers-optimal-journal.3284/post-49320>

When Dr Jack said that after a week of eating Paleo and having BAB, you may not feel like eating lunch - well I thought to myself "I doubt it in my case". Guess what. Today (day 8 of eating Paleo) I just didn't feel like lunch! Not at all! So I thought I'll see how long I last, its now 6 pm and we are about to have dinner and I feel fine. I haven't eaten since 7am! Wow I'm amazed!!!!

### Endless — 2012-09-05T07:24:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ambers-optimal-journal.3284/post-49554>

Wow, you are doing really great! How did the full body CT go?

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-05T07:53:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ambers-optimal-journal.3284/post-49557>

Thanks Endless, had my first bath last night... Our tap water is 53 degrees so brought it up to 55, could only sit in it up to just past my belly button, and could only take it for about 5 minutes. I was cold for about 2 hours afterwards but it was a pleasant skin coldness. Am going to attempt full body tonight, but i think i will ease myself into it a bit better by making the temp a little higher than that!

### Endless — 2012-09-05T08:19:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ambers-optimal-journal.3284/post-49558>

Good idea....I did the same thing for my first one! My water was 58 so I left it at that, but I could only take it up to the navel. I really am not looking forward to doing the upper body...

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-06T01:44:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ambers-optimal-journal.3284/post-49629>

Amber, the colder temps (strangely enough) don't tend to make it harder. In fact, many of us found it easier. I think you must go numb quicker! In any case, it's the warm up that can be tougher, but it sounds like you're handling that like a champ. Did you do it at sunset with your outback bath? I still want to plan a trip to the boulders at Oamaru and CT there. What do you think?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-06T01:44:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ambers-optimal-journal.3284/post-49630>

I mean, Moeraki. But you knew that.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-27T04:15:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ambers-optimal-journal.3284/post-54667>

> **KiwiLauren;50165 wrote:** Amber, the colder temps (strangely enough) don't tend to make it harder. In fact, many of us found it easier. I think you must go numb quicker! In any case, it's the warm up that can be tougher, but it sounds like you're handling that like a champ. Did you do it at sunset with your outback bath? I still want to plan a trip to the boulders at Oamaru and CT there. What do you think?

Hi Lauren. I just saw this comment now! It didn't come through to my email and I haven't been on the forum in ages as I have been going to bed a lot earlier and not wanting to use the computer in the evening. A trip to Moeraki sounds like a plan! Your welcome to come and stay with us - I promise not to turn the lights on! We live about an hour from Moeraki boulders. Are you still able to catch up when I come up to Chch during the school holidays? I can talk to you about it then. Looking forward to meeting you!

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-27T04:33:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ambers-optimal-journal.3284/post-54672>

Thought I better update here - its exactly a month since I started paleo. I started off very gungho and lost 3.8 kilos in my first week. Then I suddenly thought that I should have got blood tests done first. I made an appointment for the following week and straight away reverted to eating carbs (including gluten when I have been gluten free for 2 years). I wanted to get a more accurate 'before' picture by having grains and that bad stuff on board. Needless to say, during that week I felt terrible, low energy and of course I put on 2 kilos. Had the blood tests done, and went straight back to paleo, felt better instantly. And I'm delighted that my blood tests came back and I have (slightly) high cholestorol and high CRP, so that gave me the not so good before picture. Since then I have been very consistent, eating lots of protein and fat and I've lost a total of 5.1 kilos (11 pounds).

I haven't done much ct in the last couple of weeks. Except for one day when it was really hot I tried my outdoor bath for the first time. Will be starting again this week with the face dunking again. Daylight savings starts here this weekend. I'm really looking forward to the longer evenings. Oh must mention... doing paleo and going to bed to read by candlelight is making me wake up much earlier in the morning. I've always been terrible in the mornings and sleep in for as long as I can. I have been waking up sometimes at 5.30 to the amazement of my husband who can hardly believe it! Hehe, I also shocked him the other night by refusing a chocolate (favourites), he said in the 20 years since he has known me he has never seen me refuse a chocolate! I honestly didn't feel like it anyway! Its amazing.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-27T04:40:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ambers-optimal-journal.3284/post-54675>

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-23T06:49:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ambers-optimal-journal.3284/post-59788>

Hello again. Just updating... Its nearly 4 weeks since my last update. I decided to give HCG a go, am doing the homeopathic version. I am on day 15 of the vlcd and and now at 10.2 kilos (22 lbs) lost. Its given me a great head start to my weight loss. I've been very overweight for too long. I'm going to go straight onto the LR and CT as soon as i come off HCG but havn't decided if i should do 2 short rounds or one long one. I really don't want to see those pounds creeping on again! I am really looking forward to those BAB's again!

### Lyndra — 2012-08-27T21:39:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/growing-your-own.3292/post-47533>

Here's a thought-provoking article. A whole fleet of 747s exists to transport New Zealand kiwi fruit to the US.

[http://innerself.com/content/social-a-political/trends/8322-going-local-cutting-out-the-transportation.html](http://http://innerself.com/content/social-a-political/trends/8322-going-local-cutting-out-the-transportation.html)

Are you growing or raising more of your own food? This year was not a great growing season for us - virtually no rain and record heat. I put in the cole crops too late and the bugs ate more kale than we did. That tomato plant is going gret guns but I'm avoid nightshades. At least the squash are doing well and something we both eat.

I'm starting to plan ahead for next year, though, and hope someday (after I convince the hubby) to get some chickens. Oh and maybe a goat or two but that will require a lot of convincing...

# Thread: Dutch Researchers: Water Resources in the Paleolithic
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dutch-researchers-water-resources-in-the-paleolithic.3294/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-08-27T21:55:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dutch-researchers-water-resources-in-the-paleolithic.3294/post-47537>

Here's a paper from some Dutch researchers entitled "A multidisciplinary reconstruction of Palaeolithic nutrition that holds promise for the prevention and treatment of diseases of civilisation".

Among other things it says in the abstract:

> There is ample evidence that our ancestors lived in a landâ€“water ecosystem and extracted a substantial part of their diets from both terrestrial and aquatic resources. Rather than rejecting this possibility by lack of evidence, the default assumption should be that hominins, living in coastal ecosystems with catchable aquatic resources, consumed these resources.

[http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=8627773&fulltextType=RV&fileId=S0954422412000017](http://http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=8627773&fulltextType=RV&fileId=S0954422412000017)

### nonchalant — 2012-08-27T22:25:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dutch-researchers-water-resources-in-the-paleolithic.3294/post-47541>

Remko S. Kuipers FTW!

### Michael — 2012-08-27T22:39:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dutch-researchers-water-resources-in-the-paleolithic.3294/post-47542>

Yeah, I realized after posting it.

I happened to see that Dr. Eades had just tweeted this paper and took a look and thought, "Ah, that looks interesting, and along the lines of what Dr. Kruse is saying."

Then after I hit "post" I looked back at the article again, saw the names, and realized I'd seen "Kuipers" here in a post recently.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-28T01:38:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dutch-researchers-water-resources-in-the-paleolithic.3294/post-47589>

The paleo crowd is going to \*\*\*\* when they realize how much data is out there on this. Kuipers et al are super stars that few in paleo know about. I am glad Mike Eades is doing it now. The truth will set you free but first it usually pisses you off........and this will shock many paleoites.

### Michael — 2012-08-28T08:52:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dutch-researchers-water-resources-in-the-paleolithic.3294/post-47735>

I love paleo, but it can all get a little weird. Someone thinks something, and then suddenly half the paleosphere "knows" it.

Take this thing about low carb diets screwing with your thyroid. Apparently, everyone knows this. Yet I heard Steve Phinney talking to Jimmy Moore only the other day, and he's got data from people who've been locked in metabolic wards, and it just doesn't support it.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-28T14:26:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dutch-researchers-water-resources-in-the-paleolithic.3294/post-47750>

Michael that is what most of them want you to believe.......that is the problem. On issues like this it has become more of a religion and dogma and that will hurt them going forward. As a patient you just have to be aware of the bias and take the good out from the science that supports them. Their is a lot of good wind behind them.......but it always seems the lead with the crap science for some reason.

### Michael — 2012-08-28T15:28:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dutch-researchers-water-resources-in-the-paleolithic.3294/post-47758>

> **Jack Kruse;48201 wrote:** Michael that is what most of them want you to believe.......that is the problem.

Carbs seem to be in fashion. I can see that some in season would be natural, but the idea that you have to have them all year round and there'll be dire consequences if you don't! This week's Robb Wolf podcast he's talking about some guy, who's apparently big in the gym world, who every day stuffs himself silly with refined carbohydrate at dinner time. And Robb's saying that it's helpful that they're _refined, and he's looked at it all, and it makes sense, and the man has qualifications in physics (unspoken: so don't think he's just a meathead) and he's having him on the podcast.

And yet if you hear Cordain, Robb's own teacher, speak he says one of the few_ things everyone is agreed on, that even the mainstream gets, it's that high glycemic load carbohydrates are bad news.

I have to wonder why paleo (or at least Robb's version of it) might now be in danger of being led by the nose by what's going on in the "gym culture". I'm sure some of that stuff works for at least limited purposes some of the time, but what's it's got to do with the ancestral diet, evolutionary biology, and all the rest of it? And yet that was supposed to be the ultimate justification for what he was offering. Wouldn't it be nice if he tried get Kuipers up on the phone instead? I'd have thought what researchers were finding out about the paleolithic would be what would get paleo guys excited.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-28T15:46:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dutch-researchers-water-resources-in-the-paleolithic.3294/post-47760>

Look most of my critics want to say I am like 100% like Dr. Rosedale and totally low carb.......I am not, and have never been. I rotate my carbs on light and temp cycles and base it off labs........There is a huge difference.......but instead they want to paint me with a brush they feel comfortable with. I am ok with the critic approach really. Why? Our worst enemies are your greatest teachers because they allow you to examine the emotions of anger and revenge and then to transcend them.

# Thread: candida
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida.3301/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jonty — 2012-08-28T00:11:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida.3301/post-47563>

What would Dr Kruse's philosophy regarding the treatment of candida be?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-28T00:20:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida.3301/post-47566>

Get rid of it by all means possible! I believe he used words like that on more than one occasion! But you may want to make sure you have candida (maybe you already do know). Metametrix GI fx test is a good one to use.

### Jonty — 2012-08-28T01:10:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida.3301/post-47571>

Is that even possible? I dont know the answer, perhaps reduce influence, but for individuals who were given lots of antibiotics during childhood, getting rid of it seems unlikely.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-28T01:20:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida.3301/post-47575>

so why did you ask then?

### Jonty — 2012-08-28T02:45:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida.3301/post-47642>

Just trying to get my head around this candida thing.

It seems forums everywhere are full of folk trying to be rid of candida. Then there's every elimination therapy under the sun each promising to be 'the answer'.

Personally, I have tried many alternatives and have explored many philosophies regarding candida but I have not found definitive success.

Dr. Kruse, I was hoping to hear your candida philosophy, as it is always good to get different perspectives on issues.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-28T03:38:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida.3301/post-47661>

small bowel fumigation with diflucan and then re establish the flora.....6-8 months of work It is doable but takes a boat load of time for doc and patients

### kathylu — 2012-08-28T03:49:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida.3301/post-47666>

I found that 3 months of 200 mg diflucan daily seems to have fixed the problem. Strict low carb eating, coconut oil, oil of oregano and lots of garlic help too.

### IceBarbie — 2012-08-28T08:23:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida.3301/post-47732>

Colloidal Silver works for me. I've been using the Silver Biotics brand from iherb.com lately. 16 oz costs about $26. I use about 3 oz in the morning and again at night. I've tried lots of Diflucan in the past and didn't get as good as results as I did using CS. I also take 50 Billion Probiotics from Now brand. I've backed off that too lately since my symptoms are currently gone (1 capsule every other day or so). And I've had it bad too in the past--raw sores on my body and chronic athletes foot. I haven't had those symptoms in a few years now. Every 6 months or so I use CS again if I start to notice any discomfort from the Candida--usually after a sugar-binge (yeah, I know!). The CS works well. Allow a few weeks (or 1 16 oz bottle of CS) for a significant improvement. Trust me, I've spent THOUSANDS on my Candida in the last 35 years. CS is pretty cheap compared to all the other things that didn't work for me.

### stephaniesherie — 2012-08-28T11:58:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida.3301/post-47738>

IceBarbie - what symptoms do you get after a sugar-binge?

### vkiernan — 2012-08-28T14:39:21+0300

The CS works well. Allow a few weeks (or 1 16 oz bottle of CS) for a significant improvement. Trust me, I've spent THOUSANDS on my Candida in the last 35 years. You are saying that 1 bottle gets it back in check? I still am not sure if I have it or not. My blood work back in March said I did and again a couple months later. I did a GI Effects test in July and it said none found but I had worked hard at getting rid of it. What would you make of it?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-28T18:48:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida.3301/post-47800>

# Thread: Ketosis musings
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-musings.3303/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TribalSpice — 2012-08-28T00:45:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-musings.3303/post-47568>

Please be gentle, I am only new here.

I have been completely absorbed in reading the blog (and blowing my mind at the same time). For the past 7 years I have been supporting women in birth and about 2 years into it I thought I was starting to become psychic, alas I had no super power but realised that I could smell on a woman's breath when she hit that 8-9cm mark. Not only that, a lack of change in smell always marked either an obstructed labour or some other sort of pathology (ie sick baby). What I have noticed over the years is that no matter how long a labour is, be it 3 hours or 3 days, mum kicks into ketosis mode before pushing starts. This doesn't happen though if she has an induction or pain relief.

I am wondering if this is some sort of protective mechanism for bub when there is lower oxygen levels to the brain during 2nd stage. The more I am reading here, the more this change in energy systems seems significant.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-28T01:31:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-musings.3303/post-47584>

I buy this.......oxytocin is a phermone and it has amazing absorption on the nasal mucosa. I think I am quite sensitive to oxytocin as well for a variety of reasons.

### TribalSpice — 2012-08-28T02:38:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-musings.3303/post-47639>

Oh yes, oxytocin is an incredibly powerful pheromone. If you want to know what love feels like, sit quietly in a room as a woman births a baby unhindered. It is intoxicating. I believe a lot of the worlds problems could be solved with a big dose of oxytocin. More altruism, more empathy, deeper bonding and connection, increased levels of trust just to name a few.

### nonchalant — 2012-08-28T03:51:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-musings.3303/post-47668>

Hi TribalSpice, glad to have you here. So you can smell acetone-like breath when the mother is ready to push? Perhaps the ketosis kicks off some big hormone changes.

### TribalSpice — 2012-08-28T04:20:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-musings.3303/post-47681>

Thanks for the welcome. Yes, that is the smell.

Ah and here is the question. Does ketosis kick off the hormonal changes or do hormonal changes kick in ketosis? There are a lot of hormonal changes that occur at that transition time of labour, adrenalin increases, pulling mum slightly out of here beta-endorphin induced off her face away in labour land state and helping facilitate fetal ejection reflex. Prolactin increases allowing bub to cope better with hypoxia as well as putting mum into mother bear mode. Up goes the oxytocin...

What I didnt understand was why ketosis kicked in. It is pathologised in birth because it could also be a symptom of pre eclampsia, but reading about myelinisation it is sort of starting to make sense that this is the healthy response for the major hormonal and. Neurological changes just about to occur.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-28T15:51:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-musings.3303/post-47762>

ketosis is needed for the babies myelination......children are ketotic for quite sometime when they are on the tit......again something else that the paleo world needs to learn. Ketosis is critical for human myelination.

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-28T15:55:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-musings.3303/post-47763>

This is amazing.... that you've figure this out... going to share w/my MW/Birth Doulas etc.....

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-28T20:45:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-musings.3303/post-47848>

> **TribalSpice;48090 wrote:** Oh yes, oxytocin is an incredibly powerful pheromone. If you want to know what love feels like, sit quietly in a room as a woman births a baby unhindered. It is intoxicating. I believe a lot of the worlds problems could be solved with a big dose of oxytocin. More altruism, more empathy, deeper bonding and connection, increased levels of trust just to name a few.

Think babies have more of that oxytocin thing lingering right after birth? You know, that baby smell that is so endearing?

### nonchalant — 2012-08-28T21:12:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-musings.3303/post-47862>

> **TribalSpice;48132 wrote:** There are a lot of hormonal changes that occur at that transition time of labour, adrenalin increases, pulling mum slightly out of here beta-endorphin induced off her face away in labour land state and helping facilitate fetal ejection reflex. Prolactin increases allowing bub to cope better with hypoxia as well as putting mum into mother bear mode. Up goes the oxytocin...

Interesting. And here I just recall that it REALLY HURT at about this time...

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-28T23:53:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-musings.3303/post-47925>

Nothing to add here, except to say I enjoyed reading your observations. I have 4 children, and the last 3 were all born at home without any interventions. What a rush of all the senses, but it sure didn't get any easier lol!

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-29T06:49:12+0300

# Thread: Modern Living
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/modern-living.3307/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-08-28T03:02:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/modern-living.3307/post-47650>

Hey, guys; Back from my moose hunt--have a freezer full of moose meat, organs and bones to get me through the winter. Just want to share this Facebook post that was on my wife's wall. Does this exemplifiy everything that's wrong with modern humans or what? From a 40 year old woman:

"feeling a bit battered this morning. I hate to admit it but this age thing kicks my butt sometimes, a couple of flexiril's later and a few more hours of sleep, feeling almost like new... ok maybe not new but better. Hubby did wake up and went to this new bread place (Great Harvest Bread Co.) OMG amazing bread!!!"

first comment: "I'm going to have to try their bread. A friend once told me that getting old isn't for sissies and boy was she right. I hate it!!"

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-28T03:22:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/modern-living.3307/post-47655>

and I bet they went at night and sat in fake light..........she better get more flexeril.......and reservations for a knee and hip replacement.

### Lyndra — 2012-08-28T18:31:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/modern-living.3307/post-47794>

I used to love GHB, especially the 5-grain wheat. That was for lunch; for breakfast I'd have Kashi Go Lean. Gotta get in those 6-11 servings of whole grain every day, don't ya know.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-28T18:49:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/modern-living.3307/post-47801>

Recovering Kashi Go Lean addict here, too  And balance bars omg. I still crave those once in a while. Wonder what they put in there.

### fitness@home — 2012-08-28T19:30:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/modern-living.3307/post-47804>

I think it's sad that a 40-year old woman feels "old". This may only be the halfway point in her life! What will she feel like if she makes it to 80?

Kasha Go Lean Crunch was one of my favorite snacking foods in the past. Haven't had any in about three years now...

### Endless — 2012-08-28T19:49:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/modern-living.3307/post-47811>

I have to admit to missing quinoa a bit, but not really missing any other grain. I make a great quinoa salad......

### Lyndra — 2012-08-28T21:05:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/modern-living.3307/post-47854>

> **fitness@home;48255 wrote:** I think it's sad that a 40-year old woman feels "old". This may only be the halfway point in her life! What will she feel like if she makes it to 80?

Back in my running days (I quit at 45), I felt old when I got up in the mornings. Some days my feet were so creaky I could barely walk. Much better now that I quit that!

### AKMan — 2012-08-29T00:21:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/modern-living.3307/post-47934>

> **fitness@home;48255 wrote:** I think it's sad that a 40-year old woman feels "old". This may only be the halfway point in her life! What will she feel like if she makes it to 80?

What's even sadder is that this particular young lady had gastric bypass surgery 3 years ago, lost 200lbs and has since regained it all back.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-08-29T19:13:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/modern-living.3307/post-48110>

...yikes! Sounds like she's headed towards cancer then...:-(

I can't believe that a friend of mine from high school just died on Monday (and I only found out today)...from cancer! Only 27...:S.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-08-29T19:16:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/modern-living.3307/post-48113>

Of course my mother had to sneeringly say: "See...proves that you never know and YOU could get cancer no matter what ...you think eating healthy will 'save' you...What a \*\*\*\*ty mentality...

But in general to counteract that argument though...it would be: OK, so if I DID get cancer (but don't think I will at this rate) and I did everything 'right'...well then I wouldn't be able to blamed then right? At least I would not be to blame...

I know I'm so prone to any and all of these health problems due to my hormonal status...SO...I don't cry over it. I fix it.

...Got a craving for seaweed and sardines right about now...

# Thread: DIY fecal transplant
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 120
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-08-28T03:34:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-47659>

Am I the only one who's thinking about this?! It doesn't seem like it would be that difficult a procedure to do (haven't exactly researched it yet though). I think the biggest thing would be choosing a donor. Well, I could use one of my children. It certainly wouldn't be perfect, but it end up better than where I'm at right now... Besides, it's seems less disgusting to deal with the poop of one of my kids, given all the years I've spent cleaning their rear ends already lol! Seriously though, I'm going to have to look into it a little more. I'm wondering if antibiotics are used beforehand though, to "clear some space." And then, I'm not sure if I'll just be inheriting the problems and challenges coming their way, as they mature and their hormones change. None of the 4 are perfect donors... 2 have autoimmune issues, one had digestive issues at a young age (constipation). My son (with celiac and hashimoto's) has pretty good body comp though, has eaten pretty limited grains instinctively for several years (before I knew about all this stuff) and really decent quantities of fish (sardines and salmon) for several years. Not to mention his dentition is excellent, and the orthodontist says so far it looks like no braces will be needed (he's 9)... Otherwise, there's my daughter (6), with no autoimmune issues (so far), but not nearly the fish lover that her brother is....

### kathylu — 2012-08-28T03:58:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-47670>

Here's a link to a thread discussing a paper from Journal of Gastroenterology on DIY fecal transplants. The procedure is described in the paper. Seems very simple. You need an enema kit, a blender (I know...yuck!) and some coffee filters. And good poop, of course. Let us know if you try it!

[http://forums.phoenixrising.me/index.php?threads/fecal-transplants-fecal-bacteriotherapy-diy-home-version-very-straightforward.13744/](http://http://forums.phoenixrising.me/index.php?threads/fecal-transplants-fecal-bacteriotherapy-diy-home-version-very-straightforward.13744/)

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-28T05:22:27+0300

I was thinking the same thing. Why wouldn't that work? Good question for Jack. I wonder if there are no where near the quantity and quality in prob. as the real thing.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-28T05:51:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-47708>

Personally I like the idea of a fecal transplant because it fixes so much so fast. I watched the video posted on the forum a short while ago and found it fascinating. Of course I suppose after the transplant you would have to make sure your lifestyle was dramatically different so you didn't return to the same ole gut issues!

### Kiera2 — 2012-08-28T06:44:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-47723>

We need _healthy_ poo! Another income stream for Jack perhaps?

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-28T06:54:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-47726>

lol..... not really with ya' all with the fascination about stranger's poo. I did recently have an applicant for my own though, hehe. I think it was the calorie intake to weight gain ratio that was attractive, not necessarily my pitiful state of hormones  Gotta have your priorities right. Ok, it wasn't really serious....

The weird thing is that I always get people asking me if I can "give them a little bit of anorexia". I used to get real angry at that. But I guess it's possible after all?

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-28T14:50:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-47756>

For some reason, it doesn't creep me out too much to think about doing it (probably because my youngest has only been a year or so out of diapers)... Those areas are designed to deal with all kinds of bacteria pretty well. In fact, I remember watching "Survivor Man," a few years back, and when he didn't have access to any clean water, injecting the unclean water up there was a way to get some fluid absorption without worrying about the bacteria too much. Can't vouch for how true this is. Also, my guess is that ideally you want to use someone from your own household, rather than starting with a foreign bacterial ecosystem. But then there's the trade-off between using an "imperfect" household member, or a "more optimal" foreigner (worse ick factor here too!)... Back to that native bacterial species thing... Hmm, so maybe we should be using our parents feces for transplant..?! Mine still live in the house we moved to at age 14, 1 mile from the house of my early childhood.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-28T15:05:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-47757>

Soon this will be the gold standard for gut flora issues I predict.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-28T16:25:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-47767>

> **Jack Kruse;48208 wrote:** Soon this will be the gold standard for gut flora issues I predict.

well, and since \*everything\* pretty much starts in the gut.....

### ealachan — 2012-08-28T16:55:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-47773>

Wouldn't your body, if suddenly inundated with a whole bunch of new bacteria, have some kind of reaction? Is there a risk of the "transplant" being rejected? Also, I'm totally curious as to how quick the results would be. Would it literally be a case of "insert poo, start losing weight", or would there be a period of time where the new bacteria would need to build up in your system, etc? And even then, would the new, better bacteria just make it easier to lose weight with proper diet and exercise, or would it really be a matter of your body starting to shed weight even without exercise?

Part of me thinks "yeah sure, why the hell not, it's not like you have to EAT the poop"...but another part of me thinks it all sounds way too easy and there must be some kind of catch.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-28T17:08:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-47778>

I find it creepy. I mean, based on what we're learning about gut floras' impact on health, well-being, and also the brain..... I wouldn't want somebody else's to take over. What is it really going to affect all.... my thinking???

I understand for people who are in dire need of it.... somehow I don't see myself in that place. A few years ago, maybe I would have. Actually, I'm pretty sure I would have.

### nonchalant — 2012-08-28T17:25:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-47781>

To determine the quality of a stranger's 'donation', you would look at their diet. But what tests could be run before the fact? HS-CRP?

### Stavity — 2012-08-28T17:42:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-47782>

Is it going to be a fad? Hopefully, I do not need anything that drastic done and the diet seems to be doing the trick for me. No tummy problems as long as diet is optimal.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-28T17:57:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-47784>

>

>

you might not realize this but your insides of your gut ate a continuation of the outside of your body.......that is why the immune system is in the intestinal wall. It is akin to getting bacteria on you skin.......if your immune system is good you wont have any reaction......if its not then maybe

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-28T18:03:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-47785>

> **Stavity;48233 wrote:** I would never consider a strangers poo being transplanted into my body, gold standard or not. Where did they come up with this? Anytime something becomes popular I want no part of it. Is it going to be a fad? Hopefully, I do not need anything that drastic done and the diet seems to be doing the trick for me. No tummy problems as long as diet is optimal.

eating a SAD is pretty drastic from an evolutionary context.......and we do it everyday to destroy a gut flora.......? This is not that drastic......it is just new. And with newness comes fear.

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-28T18:10:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-47788>

> **Jack Kruse;48235 wrote:** you might not realize this but your insides of your gut ate a continuation of the outside of your body.......that is why the immune system is in the intestinal wall. It is akin to getting bacteria on you skin.......if your immune system is good you wont have any reaction......if its not then maybe

This is also why I think it's "safer" to go with someone in your household, optimal or not. You are already exposed to their fecal bacteria all the time (especially with kids in the house), so it wouldn't be that foreign, I would think. Once I deal with my new high iron status, improve my am cortisol (hopefully cycloset here), and improve my T3 status (not tolerated well currently), and improve my vitamin D status, as well as DHEA, then, if things are still not dramatically improved, I may have to have one of my kids do a "poop catch" for me... I got really deep into this mess doing a drastic elimination diet to help my (nursing) toddler, now it's my kids turn to help get me out of this!

### ealachan — 2012-08-28T18:19:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-47790>

It is akin to getting bacteria on you skin.......if your immune system is good you wont have any reaction......if its not then maybe

Ok, I get that, but if one is sub-optimal to the point that they're considering a fecal transplant in order to "fix" things that are wrong with them, then wouldn't that person's immune system by default be in the toilet (no pun intended)? Also, if one DID have a reaction...does that negate any of the good effects the transplant might have?

I don't think I'm entirely in the "this is creepy" camp...I'm just curious and still a little skeptical...
