### Inger — 2012-08-28T18:32:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-47795>

Considering dogs, that we keep as pets letting them lick our face etc, eat other animals poo if there is a possibility.. Then I think it is nothing to do a fecal transplant from our own kids lets say, that we know. I would do this anytime if needed. Ok we are not dogs but we are mammals too. Thinking about how stupidly we have destroyed our self and our guts one should be very thankful that there is such a effective and easy solution after all. I see it as a real blessing.

It also cost nothing (just a enema kit for a few bucks) and everyone could do it easily at home.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-28T18:40:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-47797>

> **Stavity;48234 wrote:** Laughing my butt off at your saying "is it going to take over my thinking?" Yes, we will be possessed by poo. Our thinking just maybe a bit crappy now but what till it takes over.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-28T18:45:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-47799>

> **Jack Kruse;48236 wrote:** eating a SAD is pretty drastic from an evolutionary context.......and we do it everyday to destroy a gut flora.......? This is not that drastic......it is just new. And with newness comes fear.

I don't know if it's fear for me. It's more like is it necessary? Or will I get bigger problems than I am having already? Or just different ones? Also, I've worked a hell of a lot on my health and gotten pretty far (in my perspective) that I kinda want to finish the job I guess.

Yes, if a little poo transplant would fix all of my problems tomorrow, I'd probably consider it. But I guess we're not quite there yet. Agree on the SAD though.

### vkiernan — 2012-08-28T20:09:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-47818>

I would consider this as long as the person was clean and had a good gi effects test result. My granddaughters might be a good donor and wouldn't bother me a bit. The thought is a little off but if it fixed my gut, why not. Poo comes out, who cares if it is put back in. If you look at it with remembering that poo starts with food; it just ends up a lot smellier along with a different texture but our body is what made it that way.

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-28T20:10:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-47819>

> **Inger;48246 wrote:** Considering dogs, that we keep as pets letting them lick our face etc, eat other animals poo if there is a possibility.. Then I think it is nothing to do a fecal transplant from our own kids lets say, that we know. I would do this anytime if needed. Ok we are not dogs but we are mammals too. Thinking about how stupidly we have destroyed our self and our guts one should be very thankful that there is such a effective and easy solution after all. I see it as a real blessing.
>
> It also cost nothing (just a enema kit for a few bucks) and everyone could do it easily at home.

I think the hardest part about doing this on a practical basis, is figuring out what poo to look for, and finding a donor. . seems like if you are thinking of this to optimize, vs get rid of a specific infection, that you would want to test the donor to see if they had some awesome bacteria to provide you that you were missing. You could easily give yourself lots of bad thing (law of un intended consequences) like candida or something else your body might not be prepared for, right?

With biggies like CDiff, it makes perfect sense. . .but when fine tuning "optimal" it's a lot more complex as far as what poo would be of benefit.

### vkiernan — 2012-08-28T20:11:38+0300

That sounds easier to me than the whole other end, so to speak. But I think it might not survive the whole trip. You may need to do both to get bacterial to all the right places. One for upper smaller intestines is digested, and larger intestines get the transplant.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-08-29T00:34:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-47937>

I am a huge fan of fecal transplant. It seems like a good idea.

Here's why:

Well, a lot of ppl trying to reverse the diseases they have...and sometimes the method is AWESOME, but sometimes it's like 'pissing in a lake that's rising'. Know what I mean? This would be like a super booster charger.

In regards to the donor....I don't know if their diet is THAT important...b/c if they can eat \*\*\*\* and STILL not have inflammation (which is 'any' disease) and look amazing for it too...wouldn't that clearly point to them having a SUPERIOR gut flora? Makes you wonder.

Then once transplant is finished you can resume with epi-paleo....since these foods simply taste better anyway. lol (And for future immunity building).

So back up to what I mean about 'pissing in a lake' (to which Dr. K has said to me before...:S). So you do epi-paleo...or you take BHRT or whatever many interventions described on here....but ONE little TINY 'error'...like if you have to frigging have a damn cupcake for social reasons like ONE time...and that ONE little error can totally set you back when you're still in the healing process...how is that even fair? Like for crocheting...you can pick the one thread and unravel it within seconds...but it took maybe hours to make... Makes you wonder....

\*But on the other hand...I think with 'crocheting' you can use that to your advantage too...go back to the 'error' which is easy to unravel, and start over again.

Now, saving one's poop right before they know they have to take abx for whatever test seems like a super idea.

Also, if I ever encountered something like c. diff or some infection from over seas...yes I would absolutely use fecal transplant NO MATTER WHAT and skip the anti-biotic stage completely...or if in even worse scenario and had no choice with the anti-biotics...then I'd do the transplant as soon after that as well.

For the issue of possible 'changes in behaviour', I think that would be true, but is it so 'outrageous' of a concept as some portray? For example...if someone with candida cannot stop eating sugar and processed stuff...b/c the 'bacteria' is telling them to, then having healthy gut flora would then change their behaviour so that they don't NEED to eat that crap anyway....and their tastes would change. That is a change in behaviour and there is nothing mystical or 'scary' about that. I don't think it would change at some freakish rate like with the Phinneas Gage dude who got implaed in his frontal cortex and changed personality for the worse.

Long rant over. Hope I don't come across as annoying. Sorry if I do. Thanks for reading.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-19T22:37:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-52759>

How do you guys know when you can't "tolerate" certain things? Are there obvious symptoms? I keep hearing that I might be intolerant of X or X, but my poops are find and I don't have any negative processing problems so I'm curious how did you know you could only eat one veggie etc.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-19T22:51:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-52760>

> **Cavemam;53387 wrote:** How do you guys know when you can't "tolerate" certain things? Are there obvious symptoms? I keep hearing that I might be intolerant of X or X, but my poops are find and I don't have any negative processing problems so I'm curious how did you know you could only eat one veggie etc.

Usually my only symptom is dramatic weight gain (over the course of 24-48 hours from ingestion) anywhere from 1.5 to 3.5 pounds. Usually the max is at 24 hours after eating. It holds until 48, and then starts to fall over the next 2 days or so. A 4 day event. If I eat another offending food, it just adds to the total (another 1-3ish pounds). I gained 15 pounds over 3 weeks once before I panicked and started low carb eating. I gained 8 pounds over 6 days over last Christmas - New years. I know Jack says the scale is not part of the leptin reset, but for me, it's critical. To figure this all out, I had to drop down to turkey only for 2 days, and then add in slowly from there (had I known about JK back then, I would have picked fish!). HTH... I literally have no other symptoms. I've had all kinds of allergy testing done (skin prick, serum, intra-dermal). Nothing tells me anything reliable compared to full elimination...

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-25T02:30:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-53944>

Illuminating discussion of gut microbiome by U.K. Dr. Myhill.

[http://www.drmyhill.co.uk/wiki/Fermentation\_in\_the\_gut\_and\_CFS](http://http://www.drmyhill.co.uk/wiki/Fermentation_in_the_gut_and_CFS)

The relevance to this thread is that bacteriodes are supposed to be the predominate colon bugs. They are anaerobes and largely responsible for fermenting cellulose and resistant starches into SCFA that are critical for colon health but also used for fuel body-wide. Being anaerobes they only survive a few minutes outside the body, and cannot be supplemented like acidophilus, bifidus, etc. If your personal colony of bacteroides gets wiped out by antibiotics, or is starved because prevotella (bacteroides in places they're not supposed to be) in the small intestine is fermenting cellulose into SCFA before it reaches the colon, the ONLY way to replenish is a fecal implant using stool from a healthy donor.

Comments on various blogs dealing with FI seem to indicate that stool from small children is not as effective as that from an adult. This somewhat off-putting procedure is likely to become the preferred treatment for ulcerative colitus, crohns, etc. A google search will return lots of links.

### sjoshua — 2013-08-26T19:31:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-fecal-transplant.3309/post-93826>

This was an entertaining discussion. I love the thinking outside the box attitude that this forum spurs. Having a window into so many minds that think in a similar fashion as myself is a very interesting opportunity that larger communities typically do not support. The gloves off, no holds barred, openness and honesty in these discussions is inspiring, and in actuality I believe it is also these same traits that spur innovation. I've never been "broken" as most would define it here, and have been moving exponentially closer to optimal over the past 2-3 years. It is incredible to me that no matter how much I do and how awesome it makes me feel, a week later I will have surpassed that and achieved even better. I know you are all on the same path no matter how far along your individual journey you have come thus far. If not sooner, I hope to re-group with all of you at the next ice age.

I witness what broken is first hand with my girlfriend.. I don't think she could fathom actually doing this, but if anyone is in the Bay area I'm always down for experimenting if someone wants to borrow some poo (...errr, you can keep it... also, no return policy please and thanks)!

### serotonin99 — 2012-08-28T23:46:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/from-newbie-serotonin99-history-and-questions.3322/post-47923>

Hello everybody,

I found this site via serendipity. Here's my story:

Many years ago I was diagnosed with CFS and Fibromyalgia and because I could not sleep I was given paxil and klonopin. The paxil started acting up over three years ago so I stopped it cold turkey as I had no idea how addictive it is. It has left me with gut pain (nausea/cramping), fear, terror, weight loss (I have a hard time keeping my weight up), PTSD and OCD.

I am sensitive to sunlight, and I have osteoperosis and I have some tooth decay. It's hard to eat as my gut hurts most of the time, but I do eat--I am not anorexic but I look that way.

I have done some research and I think I have too much serotonin in my brain and gut. Clues: sunlight exacerbates my symptoms; gardening does too. Eating chocolate is problematic.

My post paxil world feels like things are moving way too quickly and I'm pretty anxious all the time. I never was this depressed, but I am now.

I also think that I have been poisoned by fluoride as that is in paxil.

Help....this is no way to live. I want to gain weight, not lose, feel much calmer than I am now, and live a semblance of a life. Thanks for reading.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-29T00:49:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/from-newbie-serotonin99-history-and-questions.3322/post-47942>

welcome.............

### nonchalant — 2012-08-29T01:23:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/from-newbie-serotonin99-history-and-questions.3322/post-47954>

Hi serotonin99. Do you take tyrosine to raise dopamine levels? You might consider the urine testing for neurotransmitter levels.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-29T01:28:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/from-newbie-serotonin99-history-and-questions.3322/post-47959>

Hi There

If you were taking an SSRI I doubt you have too much serotonin. It is much more likely that you got depleted. That is why you feel like crap when you get off them. Read a bit more about how antidepressant medicines work and what they do to your brain.

[http://www.drkaslow.com/html/neurotransmitter\_depletion.html](http://http://www.drkaslow.com/html/neurotransmitter_depletion.html)

You can restore your neurotransmitter levels by eating epi-paleo rx and some of the other recommendations in the blog. Or you might need more help - look into Dr Kalish, he does organic cation transporter testing (neurotransmitter level testing) and will be able to help you get your NTs back in balance, most likely. I'm working with him

[http://www.kalishresearch.com/](http://http://www.kalishresearch.com/)

Sorry about the anorexic look. People can be a\*\*holes. I looked like that forever and I hated people staring at me. But I actually was anorexic

# Thread: Hernando Dâ€™Escalante Fontanedaâ€™s Memoir
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hernando-d%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2escalante-fontaneda%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2s-memoir.3325/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-08-29T09:31:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hernando-d%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2escalante-fontaneda%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2s-memoir.3325/post-48033>

Interesting as providing a snapshot of people living in historical time in a coastal zone and exploiting marine and land-based resources.

This would be what's now Florida. They sound pretty healthy:

> Indians are on these islands, who are of a large size: the women are well proportioned, and have good countenances. ... They go naked, except only some breech-cloths ... archers and men of strength.
>
>
> Thwir food includes:
>
>
>
> The common food is fish, turtle, and snails (all of which are alike fish), and tunny and whale ...

More interesting stuff:

[http://www.keyshistory.org/Fontenada.html](http://http://www.keyshistory.org/Fontenada.html)

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-08-29T19:08:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hernando-d%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2escalante-fontaneda%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2s-memoir.3325/post-48109>

I'm coming to the conclusion that when looking at traditional peoples, it's more beneficial to look at the women...and if they are in good shape. See only looking at men can be dangerous, b/c the men sometimes might do special rights and training therefore they look strong...then you see the women fat as ever. No thanks.

So the Bajau people I noticed that the women are quite a nice shape with good composition, yet not looking TOO lean or 'ripped'. They are not fat either though...but seem to have JUST the right amount of subcutaneous fat for healthy fertility, yet their body composition is impeccable and they are also strong.

...and the skin quality is another thing to look at.

Too bad these Florida Indians got wiped out...:-(

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-29T20:28:40+0300

### JoeBranca — 2012-08-29T21:51:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hernando-d%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2escalante-fontaneda%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2s-memoir.3325/post-48163>

This reminds me to go back and re-read some historical accounts again. Sven Hedin (Swedish explorer) has memoirs from his explorations around the turn of the 20th century, into Persia and up through the Himalayas into Mongolia. His accounts of locals he encoutered were somewhat detailed, iirc.

Also, Thomas Hariot (Elizabethan astronomer/mathematician) wrote an account of his New World travels including a wintering over with the Powhatans at Roanoke (prior to Jamestown disaster), who lived a stone age life without nary a stone in the region, and prided themselves on minimal clothing even in winter. Not sure what he wrote about diet, but he did end up bringing the potato back to Britain : /

### Michael — 2012-08-29T22:05:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hernando-d%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2escalante-fontaneda%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2s-memoir.3325/post-48169>

If he's the man I think he is, he had views on just about everything and certainly wrote about diet, although I don't think any of it would stand modern scrutiny. In fact, I think one of his health prescriptions was smoking. I had an idea that the man in question has been said to be perhaps the first European victim of tobacco. I just consulted wikipedia - what's the verb for that? I just wikkied him, perhaps? - and it said Hariot died of an ulcerous cancer on his lip. So maybe.

If you get anything good that's online would love a link.

# Thread: World's Oldest People
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/worlds-oldest-people.3333/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-08-29T19:15:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/worlds-oldest-people.3333/post-48112>

Besse Cooper of Monroe, GA, the world's oldest woman, just turned 116 this week. [http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2012/08/27/besse-cooper-worlds-oldest-living-woman\_n\_1833864.html](http://http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2012/08/27/besse-cooper-worlds-oldest-living-woman_n_1833864.html) Her secret to longevity: "I mind my own business and don't eat junk food."

Jiroemon Kimura of Japan, the worlds's oldest man, just turned 115 this week. [http://www.ibtimes.com/articles/331132/20120420/jiroemon-kimura-worlds-oldest-man-diet-life.htm](http://http://www.ibtimes.com/articles/331132/20120420/jiroemon-kimura-worlds-oldest-man-diet-life.htm) In honor of his birthday, Kimura divulged some of his secrets to a long life, which he says is attributed to eating small portions with meals three times a day of red bean cake and rice. For his last birthday, he dined on grilled fish, steamed rice and red beans, a Japanese tradition on special occasions.

"I don't know exactly...maybe it's all thanks to the sun above me," a modest Kimura told The Telegraph. "I am always looking up towards the sky, that is how I am."

Thoughts?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-29T20:33:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/worlds-oldest-people.3333/post-48142>

Instincts they follow......

### fitness@home — 2012-08-29T20:34:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/worlds-oldest-people.3333/post-48144>

Interesting articles. Thank for posting the links. The older I get, the more interested I am in extending my "shelf life".

Real food (not processed) is definitely a factor. I love how the old man acknowledges the power of the sun.

My great-grandmother lived to age 101. She had breast cancer twice while in her 70s and 80s. Things she had in common with them were eating real foods, smaller portion sizes with no snacking between meals, and spending time outside in the sun. She also read her bible every afternoon, canned/preserved her home grown fruits & veggies and drank catnip tea (dried the leaves herself).

Simpler way of life? She was always in bed at dusk and up at dawn.

### ealachan — 2012-08-29T20:36:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/worlds-oldest-people.3333/post-48145>

> "I mind my own business and don't eat junk food."

I love this! She sounds like someone I'd like to hang out with.

### kathylu — 2012-09-02T19:29:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/worlds-oldest-people.3333/post-48992>

OMG, I followed some of the links above and looked at the world's oldest people. They look terrible! Do you want to live to be 116 if you look like Besse Cooper? I only want extreme old age if I am functional and feeling good. Too much to hope for, or not?

### AKMan — 2012-09-02T20:32:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/worlds-oldest-people.3333/post-49001>

> **kathylu;49492 wrote:** OMG, I followed some of the links above and looked at the world's oldest people. They look terrible! Do you want to live to be 116 if you look like Besse Cooper? I only want extreme old age if I am functional and feeling good. Too much to hope for, or not?

I think this is the point of "Living an Optimized Life". Aging without the neolithic aging diseases. If it's possible to live to be 116 by accident, what will it be like for us who are trying hard to optimize our guts, diets, hormones, etc...

As to smoking...nicotine is a powerful anti-inflammatory. That's why quitting results in weight gain. It seems to me that all the harmful chemicals added to smoking products are what gets you, but the nicotine actually extends the misery with it's anti-inflammatory action.

I've often thought there was probably a dose of smoking that could be beneficial. Native Americans used it for health and ceremony--maybe they were on to something.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-02T20:34:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/worlds-oldest-people.3333/post-49003>

nice observation AKman.......

### AKMan — 2012-09-03T02:13:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/worlds-oldest-people.3333/post-49076>

Please don't take up smoking because of this!

[http://suite101.com/article/nicotine-antiinflammatory-h1n1-cure-a114895](http://http://suite101.com/article/nicotine-antiinflammatory-h1n1-cure-a114895)

Nicotine stimulates the cholinergic anti-inflammatory pathway. At the end of this pathway are immune cells that produce anti-inflammatory cytokines that block inflammation. Thus, nicotine, although one of the most addictive chemicals, can have beneficial effects on inflammatory diseases, such as arthritis, asthma, cancer, inflammatory bowel diseases and perhaps, H1N1.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC404586/](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC404586/)

Tobacco smoking suppresses the immune system, and smokers have a higher incidence of diseases, such as lung cancer, cardiovascular disease, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and respiratory infections (38). Interestingly, smokers have a lower incidence of some diseases, including ulcerative colitis, sarcoidosis, endometriosis, uterine fibroids, farmers' lung, pigeon breeders' disease, Parkinson's disease, and SjÃ¶gren's syndrome (38). Many of these diseases are inflammatory in nature or have an inflammatory component. In animal studies, NT, a major constituent of cigarette smoke, suppresses the immune system when provided by subcutaneously implanted miniosmotic pumps (38). NT may help in human cutaneous inflammation (19) and ulcerative colitis

# Thread: How much protein?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-protein.3338/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 30
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-08-29T21:52:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-protein.3338/post-48164>

In reading Dr. Rosedale's site I see he recommends eating 1 gram/day per kg of body weight for protein. I eat MUCH more than that. In fact, my breakfast alone is pretty darn close! I know Dr. Kruse has never given specific guidelines for macronutrients other than for BAB and the general low carb guidelines, so this now has my curiosity piqued.

What do you all think of the 1 gram protein suggestion?

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-29T22:14:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-protein.3338/post-48173>

I asked him about that once and he said a great white shark doesn't count protein..... I'd not eat turkey breast all day but fatty fish etc.... I think that's pretty safe.

### Michael — 2012-08-29T22:32:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-protein.3338/post-48178>

I think Rosedale is currently saying 1 gram per day of _ideal bodyweight _minus ten percent. So a bit less than that on two counts. (I'd think the 10% is a rough way of getting a figure to correspond to lean_ bodymass.)

Some other low-carb advocates are around this figure. Kwasniewski says to take your height in centimeters, take off a hundred, and that's your allowance in grams of protein. That would give you something close to Rosedale's figure I guess.

Robert Atkins seems originally to have been fairly high-protein, and Kwasniewski corresponded with him on it:

[http://www.cybernaut.com.au/optimal\_nutrition/information/articles/optimal\_nutrition\_vs\_atkins.pdf](http://http://www.cybernaut.com.au/optimal_nutrition/information/articles/optimal_nutrition_vs_atkins.pdf)

I think Volek and Phinney, "modern Atkins", if you like, are saying a little more than Rosedale - 1.5 to 2 grams of protein per kg of lean_ bodymass.

Figures in the paleo world are higher. The recent paper that Cordain contributed to, that I inked below, estimates an intake of 25-29% protein. The estimated daily energy expenditure of a hunter-gatherer in the same paper is given as 12500KJ. That would be 2985 kcal. So up to 866 kcal as protein, which equals up to about 215 grams of protein a day.

If a man's lean bodymass were 150 lb - 68 kg - then I make that 3 g per kg of lean bodymass.

I guess Rosedale would probably say he wasn't trying to replicate how paleolithic humans ate but aiming to down-regulate the mTOR pathway or something.

And I guess Volek and Phinney would probably say that what they were interested in was what would get the subject into ketosis - or keep him out of it. Or maybe what would achieve nitrogen balance.

> What do you all think of the 1 gram protein suggestion?

I don't know. I know what some well-qualified people are saying and there's no agreement. What's anyone to think?

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-29T22:38:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-protein.3338/post-48179>

I shoot for at least what my lean body weight should be given my height ~ 125g/day I'm probably pretty close to that... considering I'm still averaging btwn 45-60g of protein for BAB depending on what I eat (salmon burger, 3eggs w/ guacamole & salsa ~ 60g), then there's lunch (which was ~20g of protein - smoked oysters, and upgraded Whey HWC shake and sauerkraut), dinner tonight will be pork chops, sauerkraut and apples (6oz of pork chops is about 16g -I'll probably eat 2 chops w/dinner)

so today that puts me at about 112g protein(assuming I eat what I think I'm gonna eat for dinner)...w/a small amt of carbs, the rest is FAT!

FWIW I do not want to weigh 125lbs I'd be too effing skinny... I think when I get to ideal body comp - I'll be some where btwn 130-145

### Michael — 2012-08-29T22:49:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-protein.3338/post-48180>

> **Souldanzer;48626 wrote:** I asked him about that once and he said a great white shark doesn't count protein.....

LOL. That's a good answer.

### BJK77 — 2012-08-29T23:32:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-protein.3338/post-48188>

> **Souldanzer;48626 wrote:** I asked him about that once and he said a great white shark doesn't count protein..... I'd not eat turkey breast all day but fatty fish etc.... I think that's pretty safe.

I agree with that. I don't want to have to count protein. I'm just concerned because I'm finding my protein intake to increase while my fat decreases now that I'm following the epi-paleo diet. Maybe I'm doing something wrong?!?!

I eat most of my fat with my BAB, which is always a combo of red meat and offal - usually chuck roast and liver. I add some CO and tallow to the mix and tons of sea veg.

I'm trying to avoid any sat fats with my seafood meals, which are lunch and dinner, since I know my brain needs lots of help  I do add bacon and avocados, but I used to eat tons more CO and animal fats.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-29T23:43:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-protein.3338/post-48192>

my protein has gone up too with epi-paleo rx

I'm trying to get a lot of whole seafood. I figure that's the complete package. And then I try to stop worrying about the rest.

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-30T00:29:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-protein.3338/post-48207>

I'm wondering about this too. Rosedale worries about too much protein in the context of elevated inflammation (if I understand correctly). I'm doing everything I can to manage this, but don't think the scales are tipping in my favor at the moment (low T3 levels, and high total iron levels evolving since eating this way). I'm wondering about moving temporarily to very high fat eating like he recommends, and moderate protein (fish), at least until I get things a little more stable. I just wish I could find a way of eating that makes me feel satiated, at least for a little while.

Ditto on this! I find myself starving lately . . . can't figure out why or what on earth to eat to fill my bottomless pit!

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-30T01:04:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-protein.3338/post-48217>

I'm always fighting the urge to eat more, even when I'm physically full. Since I've had to change my thyroid meds, I've started gaining weight again too. Hormones are my enemy at the moment.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-30T01:11:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-protein.3338/post-48220>

Here is the key most I guess dont get. The high protein in the Leptin Rx is used to stop the cravings. Once that is solved you can move to higher fat and moderate protein......but I still disagree with Ron that protein is bad. He still thinks CROn is best but today in Nature journal an article came out and said CRON does work in rhesus monkey's, a primate. On PH Ron argued with me that the primates would be the same as us.......well guess who was right. I told ROn that just because CRON works in other species it is not shown to be correct in primates or humans......and the BG series is showing just how different primates and humans are. You can generalize via species. It is the same argument I have with obesity researchers who claim rat data is the same as humans......no it is not. It is interesting to draw a hypothesis but their biochemistry is way different than ours. That is how we wound up with food reward theory.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-30T01:12:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-protein.3338/post-48221>

>
>
>
> I'm trying to get a lot of whole seafood. I figure that's the complete package. And then I try to stop worrying about the rest.

> I don't know. I know what some well-qualified people are saying and there's no agreement. What's anyone to think?

mTOR and IGF1 are problems in the face of inflammation.......not on their face value.......that is where he and I are major divergent. To his credit the research he sites never controls for inflammation. So the data makes on think high mTOR and high IGF1 is bad when in reality is is exactly opposite in my view.

### Michael — 2012-08-30T10:42:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-protein.3338/post-48320>

> **Jack Kruse;48679 wrote: It is the same argument I have with obesity researchers who claim rat data is the same as humans......no it is not. It is interesting to draw a hypothesis but their biochemistry is way different than ours. That is how we wound up with food reward theory.
>
>
> Doesn't seem like a good place to start.
>
>
>
> Kwasniewski's views, although so different, also seem to have rodent experiments as their point of departure. And I think he experimented with feeding pigs diets with different macronutrient ratios and then slaughtering and autopsying them. I guess Peter at Hyperlipid would know. (I don't read Polish!)
>
>
>
> Kwasniewski is intrigued by how unique human breast-milk is. However, it again seems to be all about macronutrient ratios. I think someone like Weston Price, if he'd paid much attention to that, would have wanted to ask additonally about all the micronutrients and would also have wanted to ask not about any general case but "what's in the breast-milk of a woman whose offspring seems to be growing ideally?". And Price would have wanted to embed that in an athroplogical context, relating it to food sourcing.
>
>
>
> It seems to me one of the things _you_ want to put front and centre is encephalization. That's a huge fact. As relates to diet it tends to enter our consciousness whenever a website publishes some kind of foolishness from a vegetarian telling us that "the caveman was a vegetarian" - because the stark fact of encephalisation is inconsistent with that. Otherwise, we maybe don't pay it that much attention?
>
>
>
> Jack Kruse;48679 wrote:** mTOR and IGF1 are problems in the face of inflammation.......not on their face value.......that is where he and I are major divergent. To his credit the research he sites never controls for inflammation. So the data makes on think high mTOR and high IGF1 is bad when in reality is is exactly opposite in my view.

That's interesting. I think he also says the relatively low level of dietary protein he recommends is because a high level will teach your body to use it for fuel, and that while you sleep it'll then do that to your lean tissue and bone-matrix. It sounds like a reasonable argument, but if Kuipers, Cordain and the others are right in the 2010 paper on East African Paleolithic diets our ancestors can't have been running into that problem.

That 2010 paper is interesting. It's incredibly sophisticated and has an impressive list of references. I can't help wondering if there's a methodolgical error in it somewhere. They end up with something like 30% protein, 30% fat, and 40% carbohydrate. (That sounds like the Zone!) And the other thing is that those reconstructed diets aren't seasonal.

Also regarding 'CRON', I think going out of the way to achieve 'CRON' might make sense...but I feel like your Rx naturally ends up as 'CRON' for a lot of people...if it helps control cravings and appetite and they can get by on less, but yet feel satiated AND are absorbing the nutrients better...so is that not exactly 'CRON' since it's not TRYING to be CRON?

p.s. I've recently gave up even on butter...I think it was affecting me (using BP coffee) (June and July). I think I feel safer with ghee for sure if I want any 'butter' at all. In my coffee, I still like to put CO in it though and I think I feel better on that than CO + butter. But is that a summer vs.. winter thing? Would I feel better with the butter added in winter? ### Butters — 2016-08-25T14:36:58+0300

For which amount of protein I should aim, when I bunker nutrition for a long duration?

### Jack Kruse — 2016-08-25T15:41:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-protein.3338/post-200182>

Depends upon your light mismatch and % heteroplasmy rate at present moment. The key is if you cannot fat burn you need most protein and fat intake to keep TCA cycling low. When mitochondria are damaged for any physiologic or environmental reason they cannot utilized beta oxidation to burn fat so they have to revert to the older evolutionary pathways in the TCA cycle. This older pathway is OK for simple organism like Bacteria and Archea but it is not good for chronic use in complex eukaryotic cells. If you rely on the TCA cycle for too long it will stimulate cell growth to try to offset the energy deficit. The reason for this is that simple life ability to make energy is limited by the geometry of their cell membranes. So they have to lean heavy on the TCA cycle to make up the energy deficit. When you have no ability to fat burn you become more bacteria like in energy generation because our mitochondria reverts to its ancient bacterial past. This is why the Warburg metabolism occurs. This amount of energy does not allow for FULL CELL function and this is why their disease phenotype manifest because the change in the mitochondria forces epigenetic changes in the nuclear genome to help offset the energy loss.

It is not that hard to see when you understand Dr. Doug Wallace's work. This is why patients with high % heteroplasmy crave carbohydrates and love blue light devices. You need to not allow these things to happen in your local environment because it deepens the "energy deficit" for these people to crawl out of. These two environmental ACTIONS act in unison to keep their gut microbiome simplified and allow for chronic rapid TCA cycling to make energy from carbohydrates. The problem is the longer they stay in this cycle the deeper the mitochondrial defects get the worse their mitochondria get in many tissues. This is why obesity leads to so many other diseases as more mitochondria are mowed down. Fixing neolithic disease is a mitochondrial Rx to regain fat burning, nothing else and nothing more. Blood glutamate and glucose are always raised in these people because glutamate and glucose are part of the TCA being overused because they cannot access beta oxidation of fats. The proof: Studies have shown that increased oxygen consumption and N-acetylglutamate and urea synthesis are coupled to agmatine-induced stimulation of mitochondrial fatty acid oxidation. IMPLICATIONS: So if you cannot use beta oxidation it means glutamate and glucose will rise in the blood plasma. This explains many disease because it is the common pathways seen in altered mitochondrial as laid out by Dr. Doug Wallace's 40 years of work in mitochondrial medicine. [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16452488](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16452488)

# Thread: Chocolate Prevents Strokes, Study finds
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate-prevents-strokes-study-finds.3340/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-08-30T00:10:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate-prevents-strokes-study-finds.3340/post-48199>

As if we need more reasons to like chocolate!

[http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-08-chocolate-sweet-method-men.html](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-08-chocolate-sweet-method-men.html)

In a larger analysis of five studies that included 4,260 stroke cases, the risk of stroke for individuals in the highest category of chocolate consumption was 19 percent lower compared to non-chocolate consumers. For every increase in chocolate consumption of 50 grams per week, or about a quarter cup of chocolate chips, the risk of stroke decreased by about 14 percent.

"The beneficial effect of chocolate consumption on stroke may be related to the flavonoids in chocolate. Flavonoids appear to be protective against cardiovascular disease through antioxidant, anti-clotting and anti-inflammatory properties. It's also possible that flavonoids in chocolate may decrease blood concentrations of bad cholesterol and reduce blood pressure," said Larsson.

"Interestingly, dark chocolate has previously been associated with heart health benefits, but about 90 percent of the chocolate intake in Sweden, including what was consumed during our study, is milk chocolate," Larsson added.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-30T00:12:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate-prevents-strokes-study-finds.3340/post-48200>

Cool! More chocolate please....

# Thread: Sous Vide and endocrine distrupting
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sous-vide-and-endocrine-distrupting.3343/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-08-30T01:53:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sous-vide-and-endocrine-distrupting.3343/post-48235>

So, I keep coming back to Sous Vide cooking as a way to "gently" cook things, as right now I grill just about anything moving in my path.

I've not tried Sous Vide because the thought of cooking something in the plastic bags seems like a bad idea. But is it worse than grilling? Of course, I'll probably grill finish my Sous Vide steaks - but hey, it could be a great way to cook fish.

I have a friend with one just sitting unused, and I'm thinking of borrowing. Any wisdom out there on the wisdom of eating food heated in the vacuum seal bags?

### Lyndra — 2012-08-30T02:15:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sous-vide-and-endocrine-distrupting.3343/post-48238>

No knowledge here but I'm distrustful when I hear, "don't worry, none of the bad chemicals will leach out." How about braising if you want to gently cook? Or obviously, a slow cooker. Or scrape off the brown bits after grilling. Later Paleo woman had fire but not plastic and BPA.

I'm transitioning away from plastic storage containers to glass and prefer aluminum foil over plastic wrap. But that's just me.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-30T02:33:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sous-vide-and-endocrine-distrupting.3343/post-48241>

I'm really curious about this too. DH offered to make me a Sous Vide about a year ago after we chatted with a waiter who said he made one. DH is really good with his hands! Anyway, I declined the wonderful offer because I didn't like the idea of plastic heated up and the xenoestrogens coming out. Don't need any more estrogen no matter where it's coming from.

### janagram — 2012-08-30T02:51:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sous-vide-and-endocrine-distrupting.3343/post-48253>

I do my grilled fish wrapped in parchment paper then wrapped in foil....comes out awesome!

### nonchalant — 2012-08-30T02:56:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sous-vide-and-endocrine-distrupting.3343/post-48256>

I would really like to get one of these, but I've been concerned, yes. The Sous Vide Supreme cooks food unlike anything else.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-30T03:01:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sous-vide-and-endocrine-distrupting.3343/post-48259>

I voiced concerned over the phythates in the plastic but they have say no?

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-30T03:55:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sous-vide-and-endocrine-distrupting.3343/post-48271>

IMO just say no and stick to traditional slow cooking methods or eat it raw

### kathylu — 2012-08-30T04:20:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sous-vide-and-endocrine-distrupting.3343/post-48281>

The safer option would be food grade silicone bags. They don't leach anything, but are rather expensive. Reusable though. Amazon sells them. I agree that sous vide is a lovely way to prepare food, but the plastic bags may be a concern.

### johnnyb — 2012-08-30T06:28:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.3347/post-48307>

My first question for eating is which meal will I be having seafood. For the seafood meal I try to avoid saturated fat. That usually means using bacon fat. So to breakfast. Weekday breakfast needs a certain amount of convenience. I prep stuff a head of time. I par cook 2 lbs of bacon at a time. That gives me a supply of bacon fat for seafood and vegetables. Finishing the slices leaves enough fat in the pan to cook eggs. I like to have sautÃ©ed onions and mushrooms, grounbeef made into taco meat, ready to go. Sausage often ready to go but not so much recently. So its 3-4 eggs made with the other stuff.

My meals keep evolving. I recently stopped eating night shades. I never knew how many tomatoes and bell peppers I ate until I stopped. So no more salsa or sautÃ©ed peppers. With the increase seafood consumption salmon patties have been breakfast too. Those little pre-cooked shrimp are pretty tasty with avocado in an omelette. I saw recipe for making shrimp sausage. They were served like a hot dog, but I plan to make some patty shaped ones to have in the freezer for any meal. Having having reasonable choices ready to go makes eating better way easier.

### BJK77 — 2012-08-30T15:21:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.3347/post-48342>

Since starting to eat more epi-paleo, I've gotten into a good routine with having my BAB my only non-seafood meal. I generally have some kind of red meat (chuck roast, burger, etc) with offal (heart or liver). I add a ton of CO and tallow since this is the only meal where I eat saturated fats. I always add a ton of sea veggies to this meal too and usually some mushrooms and greens like kale.

Both lunch and dinner are now always seafood for me. I do anything from sashimi to broiled salmon or scallops, shrimp, clams, oysters - whatever I'm in the mood for that day! I almost always have an avocado with lunch and dinner too along with bacon and a little more sea veg.

My only concern is that I just feel like I'm not getting enough fat. The last few weeks I've been starving all the time and just feel like I could eat and eat and eat! I'm hoping I just need to adjust to the new influx of nutrients and things will settle down soon.

### Stavity — 2012-08-30T15:25:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.3347/post-48343>

I eat seafood for breakfast and for dinner. I feel and sleep so much better now. I have decided to eat a rare steak once a week. I was advised by my doctor to eat this way over a year ago, but didn't actually start until Dr Kruse started hinting about epi paleo. I make a lot of ceviches, and salmon tartares ahead for the days I don't have time to cook. This morning it's raw oysters and cold cooked shrimp. I cannot believe the change in me in just two months. My hair stylist said she couldn't get over how shiny and healthy my hair is and I am getting compliments about how healthy I look. I still have a long long way to go, and much to improve, but can't see going back to the way I was eating.

### nonchalant — 2012-08-30T17:15:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.3347/post-48364>

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-30T18:02:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.3347/post-48381>

Here is my favorite salmon tartar recipe that I have used for years.

Salmon Tartar

3/4 # Salmon cut into 1/4" dice

1/4 cup capers, drained but not rinsed

1/4 cup green onions, finely chopped (both white and green parts)

2 Tbsp Dijon mustard (can use a little less)

2 tsp pureed Chipotles (come in a little can in Mex food section- I just chop mine)

2 Tbsp Olive Oil

3 Tbsp Cilantro chopped

Mix salmon, capers and onions.

Combine mustard and chipoltles. Drizzle mixture over salmon.

Drizzle olive oil and mix thoroughly.

Add cilantro and mix thoroughly

It should be refrigerated for a couple of hours if you have time.

### Stavity — 2012-08-30T18:05:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.3347/post-48382>

> **Endless;48833 wrote:** Stavity, can you share your salmon tartare recipe? Do you use fresh or frozen?

I use frozen, but I have used fresh from WF. The wild caught from Alaska frozen, tastes the best to me, but it is from an honest source.

Here are two of the recipes I love: [http://www.epicurious.com/recipes/food/views/Dilled-Salmon-Tartare-on-Whole-Grain-Bread-236498](http://http://www.epicurious.com/recipes/food/views/Dilled-Salmon-Tartare-on-Whole-Grain-Bread-236498), [http://www.epicurious.com/recipes/food/views/Salmon-Tartare-365150](http://http://www.epicurious.com/recipes/food/views/Salmon-Tartare-365150). I make my own mayo for the one and use macedamia for the other in place of the oil that is in the recipes. I also play around with different veggies, one of my favorite is to use fresh cucumbers right now since they are in season at my local market.

Tartare is mild tasting. It's when salmon is cooked to much that makes it strong. I prefer it raw for sure.

### Endless — 2012-08-30T18:44:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.3347/post-48394>

Thanks! I was thinking that using frozen might make it mushy, but I'm glad it works, I don't have as much access to fresh.

### ealachan — 2012-08-30T19:15:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.3347/post-48402>

> **Sem;48737 wrote:** What is everyone eating epi-paleo Leptin style? I'm confused about the fat issue so interested to see what others are doing. How are you getting 50 grams of protein and what are you doing about fat?

If I eat fish in the morning, it's usually salmon or sardines mixed into scrambled eggs. I probably have that about half the time, and the rest of the time have eggs and some kind of other meat for breakfast - sausage, steak, sometimes ham, ground beef, whatever I happen to have enough of in the fridge. I also have fatted-up coffee - I put CO, butter and sometimes heavy cream in mine.

### Sem — 2012-08-31T04:53:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.3347/post-48513>

I am a little thrown by the no fat with seafood thing. I am not at a place where I can do raw seafood and, certainly not in the morning. I can handle ceviche but I'm not going to get 50 grams of protein that way. I like the suggestion of having meat with fat in the morning and fish for lunch and/or dinner. I'll try that. For those of you who still cook your fish, are you using any oils or butter at all? Olive oil?

### vkiernan — 2012-08-31T05:01:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.3347/post-48515>

> **Sem;48990 wrote:** I am a little thrown by the no fat with seafood thing. I am not at a place where I can do raw seafood and, certainly not in the morning. I can handle ceviche but I'm not going to get 50 grams of protein that way. I like the suggestion of having meat with fat in the morning and fish for lunch and/or dinner. I'll try that. For those of you who still cook your fish, are you using any oils or butter at all? Olive oil?

It is not "no fat" with seafood, it is mufa fat preferred. Fish doesn't have sat. fat and it inhibits the absorbtion of dha. Yes, olive oil, bacon grease, mac nut oil, etc. are acceptable. I love shrimp or scallops cooked in bacon grease. Or, wrap shrimp in bacon and grill. I also love plain sardines with mustard and mac nut oil just poured over them. I drain them first though to remove the water.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-08-31T17:53:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.3347/post-48576>

I made mussels last night and used olive oil. I always have a nice organic extra virgin olive oil in the house for cooking and a second bottle of fabulously great tasting organic extra virgin that is just for drizzling or pouring on the fish for flavor. If you heat up the really good stuff you lose so much if not all of the flavor.

### Sem — 2012-09-02T09:06:29+0300

# Thread: Stroke and the Epi Paleo Rx.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stroke-and-the-epi-paleo-rx.3349/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-30T05:21:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stroke-and-the-epi-paleo-rx.3349/post-48292>

Impact of polyunsaturated fatty acid consumption prior to ischemic stroke

Acta Neurologica Scandinavica, 07/02/2012 Clinical Article

Mishina M et al. â€“ High eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA)/arachidonic acid (AA) ratio was associated with good outcome in ischemic stroke. The paper suggests that prestroke dietary habits affect the severity in patients with ischemic stroke.

Methods

The authors studied 75 patients with lacunar infarction (LI; n=25), atherothrombotic infarction (AT; n=32), and cardiogenic embolism (CE; n=18).

The patients underwent blood examinations in a fasting state next morning of hospitalization, including examination of low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL), high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL), triglyceride (TG), blood glucose, hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c), uric acid, and fatty acid fractions of EPA, DHA, dihomo-γ-linolenic acid (DGLA), and arachidonic acid (AA).

They used the modified Rankin Scale (mRS) to assess clinical severity at discharge.

Results

There was no significant difference in the EPA/AA and DHA/AA ratio among the three stroke subgroups, although the DGLA/AA ratio was significantly higher in patients with LI than in patients with CE.

Considering the confounding factors, the mRS was negatively correlated with EPA/AA and positively correlated with age, DHA/AA, and blood glucose

Read more: [http://www.mdlinx.com/neurology/news-article.cfm/4107572/stroke#ixzz24zZQru4Y](http://http://www.mdlinx.com/neurology/news-article.cfm/4107572/stroke#ixzz24zZQru4Y)

# Thread: Bone collectors get it wrong about birth?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-collectors-get-it-wrong-about-birth.3352/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TribalSpice — 2012-08-30T09:01:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-collectors-get-it-wrong-about-birth.3352/post-48314>

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/08/120827152037.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/08/120827152037.htm)

I am on my iWank, so I can't copy and paste, but the general thought is we are born prematurely due to metabolism, not because of defective pelvises. ( Pelvis open an enormous amount in birth and babies heads mould)

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-30T14:35:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-collectors-get-it-wrong-about-birth.3352/post-48334>

I wonder what Ina May would say about this???

Birth has become a small obsession for me as I had two distinctly different births - both were intended to be natural w/o medical interference but required it any way. I now sit back and wonder at how my own birth (emergency C-sec due to loss of fetal hb -my moms OB was attempting to deliver me breach) impacted my ability to birth trusting nature.

Dd was 7lbs 6oz total labor was 51 hrs (39 of which was natural) w/a vaginal delivery

Ds labor was great until about hr 7 when I had an urge to push but knew that it wasn't time -spent the next 6 hrs trying labor w/I pushing until my BP got too high and I had to ask for a transfer intuitively I knew something was wrong. Ds was trying come down like superman and his elbows got hung up on my pelvis. If that hadn't happened I would have successfully delivered a 9lb 5oz baby (how much of that weight was natural and how much was influenced by epidural, pit, & fluids) vaginally. Instead he was a C-sect baby.

Physically he could have passed through the birth canal as my maternal grand mother gave birth naturally to several 9-11 lb babies

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-30T15:01:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-collectors-get-it-wrong-about-birth.3352/post-48340>

Very interesting read, now I'm wondering about what each of children's birth tells me about my own metabolism at the time. I also wonder how the time of year (circadian cycles) affect gestation.

1st 3 weeks early 8 pounds 6 ounces (hospital, epidural) June, effortless weight loss

2nd 1 day late 8 pounds 8 ounces (home) June, effortless weight loss

3rd 10 days early 7 pounds 14 ounces (home) May, effortless weight loss

4th on time 9 pounds 6 ounces (home) December, no weight loss

The fourth was definitely harder to push out, she took 45 minutes, whereas the other 2 homebirths were 10-15 minutes of pushing. The hospital delivery was a nightmare. That's why I never repeated it.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-08-30T23:53:34+0300

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-08T06:56:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-collectors-get-it-wrong-about-birth.3352/post-50201>

I wonder, does this also imply something about the future health of the mother. In other words, if she has sufficient energy and DHA on board to get pregnant and carry a healthy child to term, but not actually enough to support both her and the fetus, then pregnancy will create the next generation but potentially compromise mother's health. I ask because I see myself in this scenario. My health really declined after my second child was born. Yet I got pregnant very easily both times and breastfed both children for 3 years. Since nature designs pregnancy to take everything from mum and put it into baby's growth, if mum isn't optimal, her own health will then suffer. Yes?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-collectors-get-it-wrong-about-birth.3352/post-50283>

This describes me pretty well too. No problems getting pregnant any of the 4 times. Easy pregnancies, and each child breastfed from 2-3 years, with pretty exclusive nursing until 18ish months (they didn't want to eat much until then). I also tandem nursed #1 and #2... My health started going downhill after baby #4. Everyone says it's because I was "older," (I was 33 when she was born), but I don't believe it for a minute. And then, when she was 18 months old (still nursing a ton), and I got really sick I lost a ton of weight very quickly. Then came the elimination diet to figure out her food sensitivities.... And then I really, really crashed and burned... That was 2 years ago and I'm still struggling to figure it out, let alone fix myself.

I was looking forward to good sleep, had worn my orange specs and not eaten after 5 pm so was not amused to be still awake after and hour in bed.

### somewhatchubby — 2012-08-30T19:59:06+0300

Destiny, yes, I have noticed somethings already. My arms have been itching too. I have caught some insect bites just recently but I think there is something else goin on too.

Gladina, thanks ,you may well be close. I have wondered about thyroid ,i tracked my basal temperature first thing in the morning and am regularly 36.1 /36.2 .My thermometer is a few years old so need to get a new one. So, although that temp. does seem low I dont seem to have a lot of the obvious symptoms ,my hair is really thick and glossy with hardly any grey, my eyebrows are good.I dont have cold hands and feet.

What I do have is anxiety ,its been a stressful year(,lost several family members ,business worries) ,intermittant palpitations which I attributed to meno and my breasts do have fibrous areas but are no longer tender like before menopause.I need to lose about 14lbs of fat but its proving very tenacious. So, I seem to be a mixed bag. I think I will take myself off to the docs but I dont know how far I'll get.The UK dosnt seem to do it the same way as the US. Still, I'll withold judgement until I see what happens. As I sit here now,I have this full and slightly beating sensation below the ribs and it restricts my appetite as I feel full.

Hey ho , what it is to get old!

# Thread: Odd statement in Cunnane
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-statement-in-cunnane.3375/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 26
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-08-31T11:47:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-statement-in-cunnane.3375/post-48546>

So he says:

> Like Phinney, Loren Cordain ... has ... cautioned that meat intake exceeding about 100 grams per day causes metabolic problems and cannot be sustained by humans

In the first place, I suppose Cunnane means "protein" not "meat".

Secondly, where does Cordain say this? AFAIK, he \*doesn't\*. In fact, as we know, he recommends anything up to 30% protein, has research that appears to back that, and offers sample meal plans that deliver that and more.

In fact, in his own way he started a kind of (polite) war with the low-carb movement by describing their regimen as a "fad" in _The Paleo Diet_ and saying a "high protein, low glycemic index" diet was superior.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-31T14:46:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-statement-in-cunnane.3375/post-48554>

He has mentioned it in several of his research papers. Most of the articles were pre 2004 however.

### Michael — 2012-08-31T18:40:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-statement-in-cunnane.3375/post-48585>

There are so many fascinating and deep things in Cunnane's book that posting about macronutrient ratios seems absurd.

Yet paleo seems to be waging something of an undeclared war on the low carb people. Take the "safe starches" nonsense out of the picture and it's STILL there. I say "undeclared" but Cordain effectively did declare it.

So he's said 100g protein per day approx max in some paper somewhere but MUCH more than that all over the place, including the paper from 2000 as well as the one from 2010. Weird disconnect.

And it's all (seemingly) based on his models. But what if the models contain a mistaken assumption?

He seems to offer no PHYSIOLOGICAL argument for saying up to 30% or 33% or 35% or whatever.

Damn expensive, too.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-01T03:00:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-statement-in-cunnane.3375/post-48679>

Michael now you are finding out why the assumptions in all the paleo leaders books.......might be a house of cards.

### Michael — 2012-09-01T10:35:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-statement-in-cunnane.3375/post-48695>

Sisson excepted on this point. I guess that's down to his inborn shrewdness.

I don't know I should mention people's personalities but it inevitably does come down to that.

If people could only be calm and level-headed and ask themselves the right questions.

Here's a good illustration. So recently Sisson was quietly laughing in his column about an attack launched by John McDougall on the paleo movement. McDougall was so wrong on so many levels that reading his stuff was like watching a train wreck happening in slow-motion.

But here's just one thing (and relevant to the above). McDougall is saying that ketosis is "a state of illness". Well, everything else aside, he obviously doesn't know that babies spend a fair amount of time in it. (I didn't fully understand _why till I read Cunnane, but at least I knew that_ they did.) And this man's an MD. Hilarious!

But why's he getting all exercised over ketosis, anyway? The answer seems to be that in his mind he's associated paleo with Atkins ... because that's too much meat, too, you know ...

He doesn't stop to ask himself: "How do you get into ketosis on 40,30,30 (the East African paper) or even on 22%-40% carbs and a similar level of protein (the earlier paper)?" Cordain never discusses ketosis in his books - and no wonder.

It never ceases to surprise me how people want to start shouting the odds before informing themselves even minimimally.

Even if you didn't really want to get at the truth but merely want a ruck you might as well do that otherwise you're just punching the air.

But, actually, let's stop, get calm, and actually notice a few interesting phenomena and ask ourselves some questions about them, like Cunnane did.

Why are babies fat? How is the fat distributed and what's it's composition?

Until the 1930s cretinism was disturbingly common in inland areas of Europe and the US (till government mandated the iodization of salt). But why should that be? Whence the importance of iodine?

We might all get somewhere then.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-01T16:37:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-statement-in-cunnane.3375/post-48719>

Now you are beginning to see what the science is behind what I am saying. Cordain is a great researcher but he and his followers do not realize his research blind spots. I do and I point them out and because I do so I get painted by a broad paleo brush. Why? Because their franchises might be at risk. My job is to lead people to the science and let them make their own decisions. In the paleo community they are being successful because they are comparing themselves to a SAD.......but they are not looking to see if there is something more optimal for our species. Now the meme in paleo is about nutrient density. Well guess what? Nutrient density has to be put in context of the organs needs of that specific mammal. The human brain has different nutrient density requirements than other animals. The key is understanding what the organ specifically requires. The human brain separates us. The best nutrient density for the human brain is found in seafood. Oysters specificially, not liver. BG 5 and 6 are critical in this area.

### AKMan — 2012-09-01T17:52:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-statement-in-cunnane.3375/post-48736>

If the paleo gurus start preaching to eat more seafood, avoid light after dark, embrace the chill and get quarterly labs, they would still argue what seafood, what light, how cold and what labs--but at least they'd be a step closer to the best solution for population-wide health, I believe.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-01T18:37:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-statement-in-cunnane.3375/post-48755>

AKMan good observation.

### Michael — 2012-09-01T20:49:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-statement-in-cunnane.3375/post-48775>

> **Jack Kruse;49212 wrote:** Now you are beginning to see what the science is behind what I am saying. Cordain is a great researcher but he and his followers do not realize his research blind spots. I do and I point them out and because I do so I get painted by a broad paleo brush. Why? Because their franchises might be at risk. My job is to lead people to the science and let them make their own decisions. In the paleo community they are being successful because they are comparing themselves to a SAD.......but they are not looking to see if there is something more optimal for our species. Now the meme in paleo is about nutrient density. Well guess what? Nutrient density has to be put in context of the organs needs of that specific mammal. The human brain has different nutrient density requirements than other animals. The key is understanding what the organ specifically requires. The human brain separates us. The best nutrient density for the human brain is found in seafood. Oysters specificially, not liver. BG 5 and 6 are critical in this area.

I wonder if Cordain is entirely wise to keep pushing low salt given the importance to the brain of iodine. I might go back to his books and have a look at what, if anything, he says about iodine. Given that the iodination of salt seems to be all that is standing between modern man and increased cretinism, and perhaps a lot of other less-severe problems, this needs caution. I think after that discussion of Cunnane's nobody should be saying "cut your salt" without adding the rider "increase your intake of seafood".

Of course, paleo can and does change over time. Case in point. Jimmy Moore has a line about how paleo has taught him about "food quality". If he's not just using that as a formula to try to please everyone then he's forgetting something. Food quality matters in the movement _now, but it seems to have crept in sideways via the WAPF. There's an old book review by Sally Fallon of one of the original books, possibly Boyd Eaton's, possibly the first edition of Cordain's, or maybe it was a survey and pulled in both. And she ridicules them on just this point. It's actually quite a funny piece, although it was taken as a case of lese majeste_ in the paleo movement. I think the criticism was probably silenty if grudgingly noted.

Also there are people who've moved across from WAPF and brought their organ meats and bone-broth and organic and biodynamic interests with them.

At one time if you'd have gone somewhere like Mark's Daily Apple and said "Why not try a little liver?" people would have said, "No, no, no, you can't do that; the Vitamin D Council says vitamin A is dangerous." Whether it had said quite that, and even if it had ...

Paleo was all meat - sorry lean meat - and vegetables and fruit. But we've moved a little since then.

My only complaint about Cunnane: he seems a little careless with numbers. There's a table in which he shows different foods in terms of their content of brain-specific nutrients. He seems to be matching them against the RDA. Now he says that shellfish, the best of them, requires 900 grams to deliver them all, other foods being far less nutritious. But how can that be? That's two pounds of shellfish! Almost none of us would be anywhere near even the RDA for these nutrients (inadaequate as the RDA might possibly be) and that seems unlikely.

But I'm quibbling. At the end of the day that doesn't matter. What's important is that he has identified the brain specific nutrients and pointed to the best sources of them.

It changes the way at which we have to look at things. It's a shame Jimmy hasn't interviewed him.

### Inger — 2012-09-02T19:21:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-statement-in-cunnane.3375/post-48987>

fish eyes blow any liver away......and we do not eat them. They are loaded with A D E K DHA and lots of micronutrients in smaller quantities.......nutrient density is a function of the mammals most energy expensive tissues......in humans it is the brain heart and thyroid.

### Michael — 2012-09-02T21:17:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-statement-in-cunnane.3375/post-49023>

> Another illustration of the wisdom of the native Indians of that far north country came to me through two prospectors whom we rescued and brought out with us just before the fall freeze-up. ...
>
>
>
> They accordingly abandoned everything, and rather than remain in the country with very uncertain facilities and prospects for obtaining food and shelter, made a forced march to the Liard River with the hope that some expedition might be in that territory. One of the men told me the following tragic story. **While they were crossing the high plateau he nearly went blind with so violent a pain in his eyes that he feared he would go insane. It was not snow blindness, for they were equipped with glasses. **It was xeropthalmia, due to lack of vitamin A.** One day he almost ran into a mother grizzly bear and her two cubs. Fortunately, they did not attack him but moved off. He sat down on a stone and wept in despair of ever seeing his family again. As he sat there holding his throbbing head, he heard a voice and looked up. It was an old Indian who had been tracking that grizzly bear. He recognized this prospector's plight and while neither could understand the language of the other, the Indian after making an examination of his eyes, took him by the hand and led him to a stream that was coursing its way down the mountain. Here as the prospector sat waiting the Indian built a trap of stones across the stream. He then went upstream and waded down splashing as he came and thus drove the trout into the trap. He threw the fish out on the bank and told the prospector to eat the flesh of the head and the tissues back of the eyes, including the eyes, with the result that in a few hours his pain had largely subsided. In one day his sight was rapidly returning, and in two days his eyes were nearly normal.** He told me with profound emotion and gratitude that that Indian had certainly saved his life.
>
>
> This is Weston Price, who continues:
>
>
>
> I have been impressed to find that primitive racial stocks in various parts of the world are familiar with the fact that eyes constitute an invaluable adjunct for nutrition. Even the one time cannibals of the Fiji Islands, and the hereditary king of the Fiji Islands, told me in detail of the practices with regard to the use of eyes as an adjunct to diet. The chief, his father, and grandfather had the privilege of reserving the eyes of captives for their personal use. When among the natives of the islands north of Australia, I learned to enjoy greatly fish head soup made from certain selected tissues. After the fish had been cleaned the heads were split and the eyes left in.

[http://www.journeytoforever.org/farm\_library/price/price15.html](http://http://www.journeytoforever.org/farm_library/price/price15.html)

### Michael — 2012-09-03T22:15:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-statement-in-cunnane.3375/post-49222>

> **Anna K.;49725 wrote:** actually he did, not every tribe he observed had access to the seafood. Some did indeed eat the liver and other organs of large game.

No, the Indian, Anna. The Indian didn't say, "Hang on while I catch you a ruminant, and get ahold of its liver." He started pulling fish out and telling him to eat the eyes and the flesh in back of the eyes.

I think that tells you two interesting things. First, you notice that there's a very good source of the vitamin A. Secondly, it reminds you of how much easier it can be to acquire food resources from a river - which is a point of great interest from the view of human evolution.

### Michael — 2012-09-03T22:33:45+0300

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-31T20:15:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epipaleo-rx-and-increases-senses.3381/post-48598>

any one noticing an increase in their overall senses since switching to Epi-Pale RX?

the reason I ask... is well...  lets just say I've started noticing the opposite sex - as in I can instinctively tell which men have high T vs not having High T. I work in a building w/major eye candy for both men/women, as well as a representation of WDW and Walmart populations that Jack talks about. There are some very fit men and women running around the work place.

its crazy - there are guys who are no where fall into "my type" and I wouldn't consider them attractive but damn I've been doing the head swivel thing alot... hello there good looking...  the only thing I can think of is my senses have increased, and I can ID those men who have high T. there's no rhyme or reason to the build, height, coloring of the individual, most are lean and fit...but not all lean/fit and good looking turn my head...if you know what I'm saying

I really noticed it the other day as I was walking into the building - suddenly I was all aware and looking around, and this big (heck anyone's big given my height!), well defined guy walked past me and I about swooned (if I wasn't married I would have chased after him and given him my #  ). OMG WTF did that come from...the only thing I could think of was Inger's post on hormones smelling. so it seem my Radar has picked up who's got game vs those that done...

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?2185-Do-hormones-smell](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?2185-Do-hormones-smell)

obviously those in the WDW/Walmart category aren't setting off the "spidey" sense. there's even some fit/good looking guys not setting it off either....

DH is still working on raising his T...so the zing is coming back regarding my radar for him specifically... I can tell when he gets home based on his smell/aura etc if I'm going to get lucky at night....

I have also noticed, I'm getting more looks from both sexes as I carry out my day(even thought I've had no real change in weight in several months)... I think my mojo is coming back up

### [email redacted] — 2012-08-31T20:52:27+0300
