# Thread: Confused about Webinars
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-about-webinars.3404/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-09-02T04:27:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-about-webinars.3404/post-48875>

Hi, Would someone out there please, please, please explain to me how the heck to access the webinars???? Are they archived anywhere? Thanks.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-02T04:45:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-about-webinars.3404/post-48878>

Jerry, it has to do with your membership status. If you read the "Members' tab on the site it will explain what different membership confers. FYI, membership prices are set to change mid-September. If you are not a paid member you can't get webinars for download. However, you can buy one-off 'tickets' to individual webinars (though I think you need to sign up for the free membership to get the emails announcing upcoming webinars). Good luck.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-02T05:03:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-about-webinars.3404/post-48880>

Ok, but which level of membership do I need to get access? Karnivore or Kruse Klub? Or would one of the "Education Packages" do it? How about the $38 "ticket" does get you access to past webinars as well? As far as I can tell, webinar access is not mentioned anywhere.

### Kiera2 — 2012-09-02T05:06:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-about-webinars.3404/post-48883>

Jerry, you will get webinar access if you become a Karnivore or Kruse member. Things will change mid September. At present, if you are a Karnivore or Kruser you can access all webinars. After the changes, you will only have access to the previous three months. So as time goes by, access to earlier webinars will drop off. If you want to see, and/or download all the webinars, become a Karnivore now!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-02T05:10:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-about-webinars.3404/post-48884>

Webinars are meant for members. If members choose not to access them we open the remaining slots up to the public for a one time ticket to view the live event one time. You wont be able to hear it multiple times. If youre a full time member you get unlimited access. If you have questions contact misty at [[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted]) or [[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted])

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-02T05:17:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-about-webinars.3404/post-48888>

Ok, thanks very much. I just became a Karnivore. But I sill can't access the Webinars. Maybe the change hasn't registered yet, so I'll try again tomorrow morning...

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-02T05:21:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-about-webinars.3404/post-48890>

just email misty.......

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-02T05:46:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-about-webinars.3404/post-48893>

> **Jerry;49387 wrote:** Ok, thanks very much. I just became a Karnivore. But I sill can't access the Webinars. Maybe the change hasn't registered yet, so I'll try again tomorrow morning...

You need to sign in on the site itself with your password, not just the forums. The forums are separate.

# Thread: Magnesium Malate vs. Magnesium Oxide
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-malate-vs-magnesium-oxide.3406/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet

### mambo — 2012-09-02T07:26:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-malate-vs-magnesium-oxide.3406/post-48902>

Started taking 400mg of Magnesium Malate after hearing that it might help with diabetic neuropathy. I have peripheral and possibly small fiber neuropathy arising from diabetes and/or autoimmune conditions.

I tried Magnesium Malate but I did not like it as much as NatureMade's Magnesium Oxide 400mg, which I had been taking before. 400mg of Magnesium Oxide is really helpful in making me fall and stay asleep. Anyone with experience with Magnesium Malate vs. Magnesium Aspartate?

I'm thinking about going back to Magensium Oxide. I also take 900mg of Alpha Lipoic Acid (with 10mg of Biotin) for neuropathy.

### AKMan — 2012-09-02T07:36:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-malate-vs-magnesium-oxide.3406/post-48903>

A smart man once said:

Remember from the mitochondrial series or the Gnoll’s post that Magnesium is a co factor in making every last bit of ATP in the mitochondria. So if you don’t have optimal magnesium you are not going to be energy positive or optimal. T2D have the lowest levels of Mg I have ever seen. When people begin to eat paleo they usually are depleted in magnesium. They complain of poor sleep and develop constipation and muscle aches pretty often when they first adopt a paleo diet within the first few weeks. I offset this with a pretty hefty supplemented dose of Magnesium malate usually two hours prior to bed. If they need more I add a morning dose. This should never be taken with AM Vitamin D3 because they compete with one another for absorption. I am also a big believer in the use of Epsom salts topically in those with big depletion risks. There is another big benefit that comes with topical epsom salt use and that is increasing the sulfur intake topically as well. Those neolithic diseases include T2D, sleep apnea sufferers, restless leg syndrome, and those with fibromyalgia. Sulfur intake is notoriously poor in vegetarian diets and helps explain why they suffer from so many skin and connective tissue disorders (psoriasis, eczema, dandruff, folliculitis (infected hair follicles), warts, and pityriasis versicolor, vitalize are some). Sulfur is an important component of our body’s proteins. It is present in our hair, skin, nails and tendons, and plays a critical role in maintaining the integrity and elasticity of connective tissues too. After adopting a paleo diet, if the patient still complains of persistent skin issues I usually suspect a sulphur deficiency and recommend Epsom salt baths. Sulfur is an element that is part of four amino acids called methionine, cysteine, cystine and taurine. Sulfur is found in many animal meats and eggs, so longer term it need not to be supplemented on a long term paleo diet. This is very common in veggie to paleo conversions because they are so deficient and tend not to become ravenous carnivores right off the bat. So this is a real nice way to get some sulfur into your body to get optimal. If you need more you can consider a sulfur supplement like dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and methylsulfonylmethane (MSM). I like Magnesium malate 800 mgs two hours prior to bed. If you need more add 400 mgs in the AM or eat dark chocolate daily. For those with inflammatory brain diseases, like obesity, MS, AD, PD, and most any AI disease I use a special version of mag called Magnesium Threonate because it penetrates the CSF of the central nervous system while no other formulation can.

From [http://jackkruse.com/what-are-the-top-ten-paleo-supplements/](http://http://jackkruse.com/what-are-the-top-ten-paleo-supplements/)

### Jonathin — 2012-09-19T17:38:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-malate-vs-magnesium-oxide.3406/post-52702>

Magnesium oxide needs a very acidic environment to to turn into an absorbable salt. Your stomach may not have that environment. I determined this by mixing it with citric acid crystals and had to mix it with only a tiny amount of water to get it to react. If you can find it, getting pure magnesium malate powder might be the answer to tabs with added ingredients. Asparatate can be a neuro-stimlatant - something like glutamate. Besides the mag threonate, I am liking the mag glycinate powder from Nutrabio.

### Michael — 2012-09-02T10:43:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-again.3408/post-48908>

So here's another thing. I now understand why Jack said that while Steve Phinney often cited the Innuit he "doesn't get it". The Innuit, or most of them, eat plentifully of seafood.

Phinney also refers to the Plains Indians. These are two groups most of the paleosphere would probably rather forget about.

Looked at from the point of brain-selective nutrients, I guess the Plains Indians with plenty of meat & that coming from bison grazing on rich, undisturbed prairie would be fine. And they'd have no goitrogens in their diet.

Here's another thought. It may be totally wrong. The low carb people say to take 3 to 5 grams of salt a day. That seems to be because VLC makes the kidneys dump sodium.

However, Cunnane says that SEVERAL steps in ketosis are iodine-dependent. Is it possible that many people starting VLC are marginally deficient in iodine and that that three-quarters of a teaspoon of salt is also enough to ease them into ketosis through providing that? But maybe I'm getting far-fetched here.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-02T16:39:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-again.3408/post-48931>

You mike are getting it...........Jimmy Moore, Volek, Rosedale, Phinney.......none of them talk about it. If you go back and look at my top ten supplement post.......guess what is up high? Iodine. I also put a thread on PH a yr ago saying the same thing. No one gets it because the meme of paleo is meat and liver and bacon........and it misses the key points of what a human brain needs. In Matt LaLonde's recent AHS talk he was lauded for his nutrient density idea. Let me let you know what he missed on big time. Nutrient density has to be placed in context of what the mammal is. When the mammal has an organ like a brain that is an energy hog, this implies that the nutrient density has to take the energy hog into account. There is a meme in paleo set in force by Wolf, Cordain and others that say eating meat is what humans became human. Nikoly just tweeted that out not too long ago and I responded to the tweet this answer: Not exactly. Rich and Robb are good friends. Rich knows that Robb makes his money from the paleo community. This is major source of bias. When people become aware of what you are you now will begin to realize the meme has been wrong from the get go. What happens then? The key is to get it right. When you are the guy telling the community its leader may not be correct on major points.......well you can bet you will have a problem. He is a nice guy and very likeable but he does not like someone who is stirring a hornets nest that could upset the franchise he and Cordain has built. To Cordain's credit his first book warned about saturated fats........but for the wrong reasons. They are not good with seafood. They matter little with offal or meat. Matt LaLonde has just re enforced that mistake with his latest speech at AHS. People now think sat is a OK all the time.........well it is not. And when you realize the work of Cunnane, Crawford, Tobias and Kuipers (and many more) you have to begin to look at things differently. I do.......because I take care of sick people. The leaders do not. They are just driving the movement. I am OK with it because it is a better lifestyle than a SAD any day of the week. But when you are sick and ill......you should not settle for better than a SAD.......you should get optimal. That is the source of my concern. Iodine is critical.......but so is foods with all the brain specific nutrients I laid out in Brain Gut 5. I just want my readers dialed into this.........information and then decide for themselves. The most ironic thing about this community is that all on their sites their are thousands of people who are not getting the "expected paleo results" that Sisson and Wolf trump every week. My question is why? The answer on PH is work out more eat less and eat more carbs.........Try it. See what happens. Then try my way. See what happens. When you test smartly and see what is under the hood things kind change your perspective. The leaders of paleo are not clinicians..........and that is a major problem for patients. Patients know their is a disconnect and they are trying to find people to help them. There is a vacuum void for patients with clinicians. Listen to people who own gyms, are nice folks, and write books are great.......but when your labs are trashed and your hormone panel is a mess and you fell like \*\*\*\*........what are those things going to get you?

### Michael — 2012-09-02T17:26:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-again.3408/post-48948>

_The Paleo Answer_ Cordain's latest book. In the index under "Iodine" we have 65-67 and 142.

I looked those up: those references are discussing it in the context of vegetarianism.

> The good news is that antinutritional compounds have virtually no effect on our thyroid glands when out bodies' stores of iodine are normal and full. Because meat, fish, eggs, and poultry are rich sources of iodine, you will never have to worry about this nutrient when you eat Paleo style.

Never is a long time.

The reader, passing over that passage is then going to forget the issue.

But wouldn't that rather depend on the level of intake of fish (and eggs)? Would it not be affected by what the animals and birds from which the meat and poultry (less good sources anyway) come have been fed on? And there's no mention of shellfish or seaweed as desirable foods to include in the diet.

One might say the issue of iodine is raised only for it to be skated over and seen solely as a problem for vegetarians.

No hint either that Cunnane has identified it as \*the\* most important brain-specific nutrient out of five very important ones. No discussion of the role it looks to have played in encephalization. And it's not just anybody hypothesizing all this. Cunanne is an expert in fatty acid metabolism. His foreword is by Professor Michael Crawford, PhD, CBiol, FIBio, FRCPath previously director of a institute of brain chemistry and human nutrition, now, by the looks, at Imperial College, London, one of the most prestigious science places in the world.

### Michael — 2012-09-06T10:35:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-again.3408/post-49700>

Nearly got to the end of the book.

There's some interesting if very speculative stuff coming from a U.S. scientist called Jerome Dobson summarized.

He's interesting on the Venus figurines. I'd always assumed that these were highly stylized representations. I have heard people say they showed that Paleolithic women must have been fat, but that never made any sense to me. Some of them have no faces, so would that indicate that some Paleolithic women had no faces? Absurd.

It seems Dobson claims to find four different morphological features in them, including the protuberant belly, that he says are indicative of cretinism. If it were just the body-fat, it would be one thing, but that's a more detailed and complex claim. I think one would have to look carefully at a lot of these figurines to see whether what he thinks he sees could be susbstantiated, but at least there is something sort of testable there.

If he is right, what would be interesting would be that it would show that the Paleolithic diet - the actual thing not "paleo" - was not always optimal for human beings.

### Michael — 2012-09-06T17:09:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-again.3408/post-49744>

That such a figure represents a deity seems intuitively plausible to me.

That it's the other thing ... Well, perhaps it takes a certain sort of lady anthropologist to come up with something like that. It's their notion of what would appeal to a man. Did they think to ask one?

Why would they need an image at all for that - unless their minds were very different from ours? And if they were really very different, then we're not going to be able to jump to immediate conclusions regarding the nature and purpose of their artifacts.

Assuming it's not sometimes just too difficult to carve the face, maybe it sometimes wasn't done because to do so would be to approach the tremendous mystery of things too closely.

The idols of some peoples, including the Celts and Old Norse, haven't recognizably human faces and are perhaps all the more numinous for that.

But that anthropologists of all people should think to look for possible ethnographic parallels rather than theorizing in vacuo!

That one seems implausible to me.

What's interesting to me about Dobson's hypothesis is that he claims to identify SEVERAL traits in the figures.

And that it possibly might reflect certain tendencies in some of the women of the age doesn't of course preclude it from being meant to represent a goddess.

### Glamazon — 2012-09-06T17:56:41+0300

>

> And that it possibly might reflect certain tendencies in some of the women of the age doesn't of course preclude it from being meant to represent a goddess.

A big concern about anthropology is that is is androcentric like much science. I think we have masculine viewpoint dominant opinion in all cultures. Asking a man what his masturbatory fantasy image is would be highly subjective. If man did not need an image for masturbation would there even be masturbation and the porn industry? Pocket art porn has been around for ages. What appeals to a man in this time period may not appeal to a man in a different time period. Even now that female body shape of the Venus statues is very attractive to men. Must we say that only one shape can be attractive to men?

Interbreeding did take place. Did Paleolithic man find Neanderthal body type attractive?

To say, "did they even think to ask one (man)" shows the inadequacies of science even now. I don't think male or female anthropologists or scientists in general ever thought in the past to think it is important to survey women for their opinions and consider how that might affect data and outcomes. That is slowly changing. Medical science has come to recognize relatively recently that disease process manifest differently in genders. Medication is processed differently. But, for a long time, modern medicine treated women like men in health care.

Per Dobson, I found this: "The famous Venus statues bear other physical features associated with cretinism: protruding bellies, large, sagging breasts, short trunks, curved spine and short stubby fingers. Critics of Dobson's work point out that some Neanderthal sites are near the sea, where people usually get enough iodine from fish and other sea creatures, and some physical traits of cretinism are not found in Neanderthals." [http://factsanddetails.com/world.php?itemid=1476&catid=56&subcatid=361](http://http://factsanddetails.com/world.php?itemid=1476&catid=56&subcatid=361)

Dobson is only looking at physical signs. No where does he indicate other symptoms such as neurological and cognitive impairment. I don't know how well a Neanderthal could hunt, create and use stone tools with cretinism. Hypothryroidism includes sluggishness, low energy, increased sensitivity to cold, muscle weakness, fatigue so I don't know how they could have survived during the Ice Age like they did. How did they carve those figurines?

Venus figurines started showing up 40k years ago. Neanderthals became extinct roughly 30k years ago but had been around since about 200k years. Why are there no older figurines from over 100k years ago. Altho the figurines do not show true steatopygia, steatopygia is considered a desirable feminine trait today and even in American hip hop culture.

Neanderthal body structure has been proposed to conserve body heat by minimizing body surface area with short limbs and a deep wide rib cage. This would protect the body organs within. The heavier, well muscled body would last longer in the cold than a longer limbed, athletic person. But this body would also need much more food. The body that the neanderthal had may have been what caused his demise. To maintain that kind of body structure required a too high of energy cost in an environment that was changing.

[http://www.bbc.co.uk/sn/tvradio/programmes/horizon/neanderthal\_prog\_summary.shtml](http://http://www.bbc.co.uk/sn/tvradio/programmes/horizon/neanderthal_prog_summary.shtml)

>
>
> There's some interesting if very speculative stuff coming from a U.S. scientist called Jerome Dobson summarized.
>
>
>
> He's interesting on the Venus figurines. I'd always assumed that these were highly stylized representations. I have heard people say they showed that Paleolithic women must have been fat, but that never made any sense to me. Some of them have no faces, so would that indicate that some Paleolithic women had no faces? Absurd.
>
>
>
> It seems Dobson claims to find four different morphological features in them, including the protuberant belly, that he says are indicative of cretinism. If it were just the body-fat, it would be one thing, but that's a more detailed and complex claim. I think one would have to look carefully at a lot of these figurines to see whether what he thinks he sees could be susbstantiated, but at least there is something sort of testable there.
>
>
>
> If he is right, what would be interesting would be that it would show that the Paleolithic diet - the actual thing not "paleo" - was not always optimal for human beings.

### Michael — 2012-09-06T23:19:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-again.3408/post-49849>

> **Glamazon;50385 wrote:** You zeroed in on "androcentric, judged my whole response, and didn't look at the rest of what I wrote?

How did you establish that?

Of course, I had begun by saying that what Dobson said seemed to me to be "very speculative stuff". But that got lost somewhere. Nevertheless, he has got something vaguely testable there. it would be equally satisfactory to me if someone were to show he's seeing what's not there as it would be if someone were to show that his four criteria are pretty much always present.

I have a great -- no, very great -- respect for Cunnane, but even he seems to be moving onto thin ice at this point in the book. His suggestion is that the figurines represent Neanderthals. I guess it's possible, though I don't see quite why Cro Magnons would want to carry around portable art featuring Neanderthals. But maybe they would.

I don't know. It's fascinating. I would tend to have thought of all Paleolithic diets as pretty close to optimal, and maybe they were. OTOH, I can now conceive that iodine-deficiency _may_ have been stalking us around for much of our historical, and even prehistoric past, like a ghost in the room.

Either way I prefer to think of people's having faces. I think anything else would freak me out.

Not exactly lifelike, but, again, maybe more otherworldly, more numinous, for just that reason:

[http://www.monaghan.ie/contentv3/media/monaghanie/content/images/museum/collections/celtic\_stone\_head\_1\_922\_lo\_res.jpg](http://http://www.monaghan.ie/contentv3/media/monaghanie/content/images/museum/collections/celtic_stone_head_1_922_lo_res.jpg)

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-08T00:32:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-again.3408/post-50125>

They also don't fall down.

But back to the figurines. Consider the fact that these people (regardless if the were Neanderthals or homosapiens) did not understand reproduction. The woman held life & death within her body when pregnant. The figurines as I see them represent the pregnant or postpartum woman big boobs big belly give life. But sometimes pregnancy also brings death. Consider the figurines a goddess for women to pray to for protection like Tauret (Egyptian goddess who's carvings look very similar to the Donii goddess figurines only difference is the tools used in carving). Many of the birth goddesses in history also have a role in death as well.

Just a different perspective looking at the same issue When these people started looking for explanations as to why things happen they were searching out side their normal paradigms.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-03T05:50:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d-and-autoimmunity.3425/post-49129>

> **Can you have a normal plasma D level and still have low vitamin D activity? Yes you can, and it is probably the biggest silent epidemic out there today. I most commonly see this in obese folks with hypothyroidism. 90% of the cases of hypothyroidism in the USA are cause by Hashimotoâ€™s disease. This disease is an autoimmune disease and these patients universally have defective VDR receptors. That means they need very high levels of blood Vitamin D levels and optimization of their thyroid function to get results.** Often many obese people get stuck not losing weight because their doctors are fooled into thinking their thyroid and Vitamin D levels are fine. Most of the time the levels are sub therapeutic and patients find amazing results when their plasma levels are pushed a bit by the clinician. This is an area where you need to speak to your doctor. I often see this in post op cancer patients too under extreme stress. Patients with high cortisol levels suffer the same fate.

For those of you with AI, where do you like for your D levels to be, optimally? How do I go about finding my own optimal level?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-03T06:51:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d-and-autoimmunity.3425/post-49139>

This is an excellent question, Soul. So what I take from this is your blood tests can show high levels of D but you still need more. I wonder how you do go about finding the optimal level. My D tested just above the reference range and my doc wanted me to stop supplementing. I will probably stop/slow down as we head into summer, but how to determine if this causes a problem? If it's linked with immunity, does this mean we need to keep levels high enough that we don't get sick? Or high enough that we're in the top quartile for T3 and T4? I'm guessing context is once again everything here. But I'd love to hear the insights of others.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-08T06:09:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d-and-autoimmunity.3425/post-50195>

[http://www.vivo.colostate.edu/hbooks/pathphys/endocrine/otherendo/vitamind.html](http://http://www.vivo.colostate.edu/hbooks/pathphys/endocrine/otherendo/vitamind.html)

> **Vitamin D, as either D3 or D2, does not have significant biological activity. Rather, it must be metabolized within the body to the hormonally-active form known as 1,25-dihydroxycholecalciferol. This transformation occurs in two steps, as depicted in the diagram to the right:
>
>
>
> Within the liver, cholecalciferal is hydroxylated to 25-hydroxycholecalciferol by the enzyme 25-hydroxylase.
>
> Within the kidney, 25-hydroxycholecalciferol serves as a substrate for 1-alpha-hydroxylase, yielding 1,25-dihydroxycholecalciferol, the biologically active form.
>
> Each of the forms of vitamin D is hydrophobic, and is transported in blood bound to carrier proteins. The major carrier is called, appropriately, vitamin D-binding protein. The halflife of 25-hydroxycholecalciferol is several weeks, while that of 1,25-dihydroxycholecalciferol is only a few hours.
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vitamin\_D](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vitamin_D)
>
>
> > In the liver vitamin D is converted to calcidiol, which is also known as Calcifediol (INN), 25-hydroxycholecalciferol, or 25-hydroxyvitamin Dâ€”abbreviated 25(OH)D; and which is the specific vitamin D metabolite that is measured in serum to determine a person's vitamin D status.\[2\]\[3\] Part of the calcidiol is converted by the kidneys to calcitriol, the biologically active form of vitamin D.
>
> **
>
> > \[
> >
> >
> > [http://mpkb.org/home/tests/125d](http://http://mpkb.org/home/tests/125d)
> >
> > Test: 1,25-dihydroxvitamin D (1,25-D)
>
> Calcitriol, biologically active form of vitamin D. There is still the problem that the VDR receptor is defective in AI.
>
>
>
> Most of the time the levels are sub therapeutic and patients find amazing results when their plasma levels are pushed a bit by the clinician.

Sounds like you still need a good doc, some faith, and watching for signs of toxicity vs improvements. Sounds like this is individual. Choco talks a lot about defective VDR receptors. High serum calcium is one sign of toxicity. I'm still not quite clear on what is/was going on with me. I had D = 124, high intracellular Ca, and bottom of range serum Ca at the same time. Regular osteocalcin around 15 iirc, which I've now found is worthless b/c it needs to be carboxylated osteocalcin test? K2 is a good idea while you are experimenting?

and you thought the sun "causes" melanoma.....really? How about having a lack of sun exposure causes melanomas......read and think. Stop believing what the media tells you to think.

CDC fact: 300,000 Americans die/year from a lack of sunshine

CDC fact: 8,000 Americans die/year from melanomas

CDC fact: White males get their lethal melanomas on their backs

CDC fact: White females get their lethal melanomas on their thighs

CDC fact: 50% of African Americans get their lethal melanomas on the soles of their feet

That is how I roll too. I tan every second I have the possibility to do so. All day long If I can. I did not burn once this summer and I am a blond scandinavian. I use no sunscreen. Not even on my face.

I do not care if I get wrinkles.

The brother of (ex) husband died of melanoma 1,5 years ago. It was scary. He was a biker, he won lots of races too in his youth, he was only around 56 yo when he died. He looked healthy before the cancer got him, slim, ate "healthy", no red meat lots of chicken and avoided animal fat.. He was little in the sun. He got his melanoma on his back, in the lower neck area.

He wanted to live. He asked me for advice.. and I told him all I knew back then. I told him to tan naked alot. Eat raw organs and fish and all.. everything wild and seasonal, and rest, sleep a lot and be outside. Avoid all pasta and crap. But he was too stuck in his habit.. too stuck in his business.. he could not quit... he did it only halfheartedly.. he told me he loved his pasta so much. I guess he did not tan eather. Every doctor would say a person with melanoma MUST avoid the sun. That is too scary to go against your doctors advice for most.. very sad.

It was too sad to see he wanted to live so badly. He got very depressed and angry at everything at the end. Had a lot of pain too.

### Inger — 2012-09-08T08:27:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d-and-autoimmunity.3425/post-50206>

There was a article in the Finnish newspaper weeks ago about skin cancer.

It said the sun is so dangerous. We must be very careful or we are at high risk for cancer. It told about a young girl that had gotten melanoma.

The crazy thing is.. a bit into the article the girl told, she had always tried to avoid the sun... because she was so very light-skinned..

I wonder if anyone else reading the article realized the big mismatch..

A girl get mealoma.. a girl that almost never tanned... and the artikel told it was because of the sun...????

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-08T09:03:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d-and-autoimmunity.3425/post-50210>

I agree, wholeheartedly, Inger with all you say. It IS very hard to go against your doctor's advice, especially when dealing with something life-threatening. You feel they _must_ know more than little-old-you. But they often know much less.

I also agree that people rarely see the mismatch. Isn't that what Dr K is always suggesting... that we need to become aware of that which we are not aware of but is driving our thinking? My father has had skin cancer removed 3 times. He is fair skinned like me. We both have always burned easily. He ALWAYS stays out of the sun and wears hats/long sleeved tops. These days, I'm in the sun all the time. It's finally above 60 degrees F during most days now and I've started sitting in my back yard naked while I read. The other day I did ocean CT and was at the beach for an hour, then sat outside for another 2 hours. I didn't get close to burning. Before paleo and Dr K I would get a sunburn in 15 minutes.

I love the sun now. (I just need to figure out if I need to keep supplementing vitamin D or not.... i will retest and see).

I find it terribly sad that we are being taught to fear the very things that gives our planet life.

JanSz, the issue, I believe, is that Gretchen is trying to block artificial blue light. When she doesn't have natural light in her office environment, all she's getting is artificial blue light. And the conclusion seems to be that blue light is bad for you any time of day, with the dangers made even worse when there is a mismatch (ie it's dark outside and the lights are on inside). She is not trying to block any natural UV light and from what I understand, the glasses she purchased block only the blue light.

I'd guess the best way to avoid depression and SAD (seasonal affective disorder) is to get outside into natural light as much as possible, year round, and to avoid artificial light as much as possible.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-08T22:52:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d-and-autoimmunity.3425/post-50308>

While I agree with sunlight being good and necessary for us, I'm having some trouble believing that not being exposed to full sunlight most of the day really is a big problem if all other things are in place. I also doubt that SAD is caused by sunlight deficiency.... maybe by vitamin D deficiency but as we are learning vitamin D status really isn't so much a matter of sunlight exposure after all. If your gut and hormones are broken, sunlight is only going to help so much. And artificial light certainly won't do the trick.

I'm not feeling so well these days. I have no desire to go outside into the sun. I also do not use artificial lights while I am inside. I was contemplating if I'm hurting myself even more and decided probably not. What do animals do when they don't feel well? They hide. In their cave, burrow, under brush, in a den.... it's dark in there. My own dog will go into her "cave" (her crate which is darkened with a blanket over the wire cage) when she does not feel well. First thing she does..... into the darkest corner in the back. Then she sleeps until she feels better only coming out for a drink if she needs it or to do her business outside. When she is feeling well she prefers to sleep in full sunlight during the day.

I think artificial lights are bad. I think sunlight is good. I think natural darkness or dimmed light in a cave-like environment probably won't hurt me if all other things are in place. I'm wondering if there is a healing effect to naturally dark environments.... oh wait, dark, cold, sleepy - we already went there

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CIE\_Standard\_Illuminant\_D65](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CIE_Standard_Illuminant_D65)

[http://www.cie.co.at/publ/abst/s005.html](http://http://www.cie.co.at/publ/abst/s005.html)

Illuminant D65**

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from CIE Standard Illuminant D65)

Spectral power distribution of D65.

CIE Standard Illuminant D65 (sometimes written D65\[1\]\[2\]) is a commonly-used standard illuminant defined by the International Commission on Illumination (CIE).\[3\] It is part of the D series of illuminants that try to portray standard illumination conditions at open-air in different parts of the world.

D65 corresponds roughly to a midday sun in Western Europe / Northern Europe**, hence it is also called a daylight illuminant. As any standard illuminant is represented as a table of averaged spectrophotometric data, any light source which statistically has the same relative spectral power distribution (SPD) can be considered a D65 light source. There are no actual D65 light sources, only simulators. The quality of a simulator can be assessed with the CIE Metamerism Index.\[4\]\[5\]

The CIE positions D65 as the standard daylight illuminant:

\[D65\] is intended to represent average daylight and has a correlated colour temperature of approximately 6500 K. CIE standard illuminant D65 should be used in all colorimetric calculations requiring representative daylight, unless there are specific reasons for using a different illuminant. Variations in the relative spectral power distribution of daylight are known to occur, particularly in the ultraviolet spectral region, as a function of season, time of day, and geographic location.

—ISO 10526:1999/CIE S005/E-1998, CIE Standard Illuminants for Colorimetry

\====================================================

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standard\_illuminant](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standard_illuminant)

Standard illuminant

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-21T20:18:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d-and-autoimmunity.3425/post-53240>

Found something interesting. What does the AI crowd think?

[http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1749-6632.2009.04637.x/abstract](http://http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1749-6632.2009.04637.x/abstract)

> **Vitamin D research is discussed in light of the hypothesis that the lower average levels of vitamin D frequently observed in autoimmune disease are not a sign of deficiency. Instead, it is proposed that the lower levels result from chronic infection with intracellular bacteria that dysregulate vitamin D metabolism by causing vitamin D receptor (VDR) dysfunction within phagocytes. The VDR dysfunction causes a decline in innate immune function that causes susceptibility to additional infections that contribute to disease progression. Evidence has been accumulating that indicates that a number of autoimmune diseases can be reversed by gradually restoring VDR function with the VDR agonist olmesartan and subinhibitory dosages of certain bacteriostatic antibiotics. Diseases showing favorable responses to treatment so far include systemic lupus erythematosis, rheumatoid arthritis, scleroderma, sarcoidosis, Sjogren's syndrome, autoimmune thyroid disease**, psoriasis, ankylosing spondylitis, Reiter's syndrome, type I and II diabetes mellitus, and uveitis. Disease reversal using this approach requires limitation of vitamin D in order to avoid contributing to dysfunction of nuclear receptors and subsequent negative consequences for immune and endocrine function. Immunopathological reactions accompanying bacterial cell death require a gradual elimination of pathogens over several years. Practical and theoretical implications are discussed, along with the compatibility of this model with current research.

This program addresses this issue:

[http://marshallprotocol.com/](http://http://marshallprotocol.com/)

[http://mpkb.org/home/mp](http://http://mpkb.org/home/mp)

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-21T20:38:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d-and-autoimmunity.3425/post-53246>

> Role of vitamin D in chronic disease
>
> Main article: Science behind vitamin D
>
> A number of studies have suggested that patients with chronic inflammatory diseases are deficient in 25-hydroxyvitamin D (25-D) and that consuming greater quantities of vitamin D, which further elevates 25-D levels, alleviates disease symptoms.
>
>
>
> Some years ago, **molecular biology identified 25-D as a secosteroid. Secosteroids would typically be expected to depress inflammation, which is in line with the reports of short-term symptomatic improvement. The simplistic first-order mass-action model used to guide the early vitamin studies is now giving way to a more complex description of action.
>
>
>
> When active, the Vitamin D nuclear receptor (VDR) affects transcription of at least 913 genes and impacts processes ranging from calcium metabolism to expression of key antimicrobial peptides. Additionally, recent research on the Human Microbiome shows that bacteria are far more pervasive than previously thought, dramatically increasing the possibility that the spectrum of chronic diseases is bacterial in origin.
>
>
>
> **Emerging molecular evidence suggests that symptomatic improvements among those administered vitamin D is the result of 25-D’s ability to temper bacterial-induced inflammation by slowing VDR activity. While this results in short-term palliation, persistent pathogens that influence disease progression proliferate over the long-term.**
>
>
> [http://mpkb.org/home/patients/pathogenesis\_overview](http://http://mpkb.org/home/patients/pathogenesis_overview)
>
>
>
>
> > However, bacteria create ligands, which like 25-D, inactivate the VDR and, in turn, the innate immune response. This allows the microbes to proliferate. In response, the body increases production of 1,25-D from 25-D, leading to one of the hallmarks of chronic inflammatory disease: a low 25-D and a high 1,25-D.
> >
> >
> > [http://mpkb.org/home/pathogenesis/vitamind](http://http://mpkb.org/home/pathogenesis/vitamind)
> >
> >
> >
> >
> >
> > Osteoporosis
> >
> > Main article: Osteoporosis and osteopenia
> >
> > Both osteoporosis and osteopenia are diseases marked by a decrease in bone mineral density. Osteopenia is a less severe form of and sometimes precursor to osteoporosis. The loss of bone mass leads to a porous bone structure, frequent fractures, and delayed healing.
> >
> >
> >
> > Among doctors, and even many researchers, it is conventional wisdom that vitamin D supplementation reverses osteopenia and osteoporosis. However, a growing body of interventional trials and molecular evidence shows this is not the case. Instead, current research has demonstrated that osteoporosis and osteopenia are often the direct result of infection with the Th1 pathogens, a metagenomic microbiota, which produce inflammatory cytokines and inactivate the Vitamin D Receptor. The only way to achieve long-term reversal of bone loss is to kill the Th1 pathogens driving the disease process.
>
> **
>
> [http://mpkb.org/home/pathogenesis/vitamind](http://http://mpkb.org/home/pathogenesis/vitamind)
>
>
>
>
>
> One of the great mysteries in human biology is the fact that most human breast milk is deficient in vitamin D. How could Nature overlook such an important nutrient in the “perfect food”?
>
>
>
> Vitamin D Council

Take home messages

• Prevailing theories of vitamin D are imprecise and suggest

contradictory understandings of vitamin D metabolism.

• 25-hydroxyvitamin D is immunosuppressive.

• Supplementation of the secosteroid vitamin D temporarily

alleviates signs and symptoms of chronic disease but leads to a

long-term increase in morbidity.

• Molecular biology suggests that low levels of 25-D are a result

rather than a cause of the autoimmune disease process.

• A microbiota of bacterial pathogens may survive in the human body

by secreting proteins that antagonize the VDR and disable the

innate immune response.

• Elevated levels of 1,25-D exist at the site of disease and are an

indication that the innate immune system is responding to an

infection.

[http://autoimmunityresearch.org/transcripts/AR-Albert-VitD.pdf](http://http://autoimmunityresearch.org/transcripts/AR-Albert-VitD.pdf)

>
>
> http:/http://autoimmunityresearch.org/transcripts/AR-Albert-VitD.pdf
>
>
>
> According to the alternative model, true recovery from autoimmune
>
> disease involves an activated immune response and a
>
> corresponding spike in symptoms due to bacterial die-off -
>
> a phenomenon known as immunopathology \[26\]. Symptom
>
> exacerbation in the face of an activated immune response occurs in
>
> other diseases including AIDS, in which patients exhibit Immune
>
> Reconstitution Inﬂammatory Syndrome after beginning antiretroviral
>
> therapy aimed at targeting opportunistic infections. Syphillis,
>
> sarcoidosis \[27\], and a number of additional diseases \[26\] also induce
>
> immunopathological-type reactions during periods where the
>
> immune system succeeds in targeting chronic pathogens.
>
> Consequently, if patients with autoimmune disease succeed in killing
>
> bacteria associated with their disease state, their symptoms should be
>
> expected to escalate, at least in the short-term, as cytokines and
>
> endotoxins are generated \[28\]. Conversely, in cases in which the
>
> immune response has been suppressed by supplementation with an
>
> immunosuppressant such as the secosteroid 25-D, one would expect
>
> to see fewer clinical manifestations of disease in the short-term, yet
>
> more advanced disease in the long-term. At a certain point,
>
> depending on the clinical symptom or physiological markers of
>
> disease, patients supplementing with vitamin D would be expected to
>
> approach a "crossover point" when additional reduction of the
>
> immune response is eclipsed by the advancing disease** (Figure 2).
>
> This outcome has been demonstrated in longitudinal studies, with
>
> studies on sicker or older patients taking less time to realize the
>
> effect.

> > > **Jack Kruse;29025 wrote: The finding, published in the May 23, 2012 online issue of Science Translational Medicine, more fully explains how the immune system functions in different situations and presents a new avenue for treating infections, perhaps as an alternative to current antibiotic therapies (see also Skin Cancer).
> > >
> > >
> > >
> > > The immunological benefits of vitamin D are controversial. In cultured cell studies, the fat-soluble vitamin provides strong immunological benefits, but in repeated studies with humans and animal models, results have been inconsistent: People with low levels of dietary vitamin D do not suffer more infections. For reasons unknown, their immune response generally remains strong, undermining the touted immunological strength of vitamin D.
> > >
> > >
> > >
> > > Working with a mouse model and cultured human cells, Gallo and colleagues discovered why: When levels of dietary vitamin D are low (it's naturally present in very few foods), production of parathyroid hormone (PTH), which normally helps modulate calcium levels in blood, is ramped up. More PTH or a related peptide called PHTrP spurs increased expression of AMPs, such as cathelicidin, which kill a broad spectrum of harmful bacteria, fungi and viruses.**
> > >
> > >
> > >
> > > "No one suspected a role for PTH or the PTH-related peptide in immunity," said Richard L. Gallo, MD, PhD, professor of medicine and chief of UCSD's Division of Dermatology and the Dermatology section of the Veterans Affairs San Diego Healthcare System. "This may help resolve some of the controversy surrounding vitamin D. It fills in the blanks."
> > >
> > >
> > >
> > > For example, the findings relate to the on-going debate over sun exposure. Sunlight triggers the production of vitamin D. Low levels of vitamin D have been claimed in some studies to increase the risk of cardiovascular disease and cancer, but other studies have failed to confirm this. On the other hand, high levels of solar exposure that could increase vitamin D have been shown to increase the risk of skin cancer.
> > >
> > >
> > >
> > > "Since sunlight is a carcinogen, it's a bad idea to get too much of it," said Gallo. "PTH goes up when levels of vitamin D from diet and sun exposure are low. PTH may be what permits us to have low D in the diet and not kill ourselves with too much UV radiation."
> > >
> > >
> > >
> > > Gallo said PTH's newly revealed immunological role provides a new connection between the body's endocrine system (a system of glands secreting different regulatory hormones into the bloodstream) and its ability to fight invasive, health-harming pathogens.
> >
> >
> >

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-30T20:33:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d-and-autoimmunity.3425/post-55442>

The Vitamin D Conundrum: To Supplement or Not to Supplement? Don't Expect Reliable Results Before 2017

In a summary of the current state of vitamin D research by Kai Kupferschmidt, vitamin D supplementation enthusiasts and skeptics have their day in the sun.

Moreover Kupferschmidt includes an overview of recently started large scale trials that could eventually tell us whether supplementation is advisable, necessary, superfluous or hazardous and idiotic. [http://www.sciencemag.org/content/337/6101/1476.full](http://http://www.sciencemag.org/content/337/6101/1476.full) Here are all the trials on going to ask the questions you all want to know.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-30T20:44:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d-and-autoimmunity.3425/post-55445>

The real issue for me is that fatty fish, eggs, organ meats, full fat organic raw dairy products. Most of those good foods which have been banned from our plate because of the US government's MyPlate suggestion of what is healthy. These foods are not only high in vitamin D, they are also high in **fat**. This is the real conundrum for Vitamin D. It must be eaten with fats to be absorbed. It is usually found in its evolutionary package with these fats when it comes in foods over the sun. The skin of fish is loaded with it. Remember that our eutherian mammalian ancestors did not buy their vitamin D at the local vitamin shop, it is only reasonable to assume that our digestive tract was designed to absorb the little additional vitamin D we are supposed to get from foodstuff in the presence of fat.

Moreover, a recent study, which was obviously not published in a major journal like the the Journal of the American Medical Association so most doctors could examine the data shows exactly that: without the concomitant presence of significant amounts of dietary fat, even 50.000IU of supplemental vitamin D3 have no effect on the serum levels of 25(OH)D, the active form of vitamin D ([Raimundo. 2011](http://http:/http://www.hindawi.com/journals/ije/2011/809069/))

### Souldanzer — 2012-10-01T03:19:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d-and-autoimmunity.3425/post-55513>

> Vitamin D anti-inflammatory pathway identified
>
>
>
> February 27, 2012. Writing in the March 1, 2012, issue of The Journal of Immunology, researchers at National Jewish Health in Denver report their discovery of a molecular pathway through which vitamin D inhibits inflammation.
>
>
>
> Assistant professor of pediatrics Elena Goleva and her associates cultured human white blood cells with varying amounts of vitamin D or no vitamin D before exposing them to lipopolysaccharide, a pro-inflammatory compound. They found that cells incubated without vitamin D or with a reduced concentration of 15 nanograms per milliliter produced high amounts of the cytokines interleukin-6 (IL-6) and tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-a), both of which are involved in the inflammatory response. However, white blood cells that received concentrations of 30 nanograms per milliliter or more of vitamin D, which is a level considered by some researchers to be sufficient when measured in the bloodstream, had a decreased inflammatory response, with 50 nanograms per milliliter vitamin D resulting in the greatest reduction. Among other findings, the team observed that vitamin D treatment upregulated the expression of mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) phosphatase-1, which interferes with inflammation. "This study goes beyond previous associations of vitamin D with various health outcomes," stated Dr Goleva. "It outlines a clear chain of cellular events, from the binding of DNA, through a specific signaling pathway, to the reduction of proteins known to trigger inflammation. Patients with chronic inflammatory diseases, such as asthma, arthritis and prostate cancer, who are vitamin D deficient, may benefit from vitamin D supplementation to get their serum vitamin D levels above 30 nanograms/milliliter."
>
>
>
> "The fact that we showed a dose-dependent and varying response to levels commonly found in humans also adds weight to the argument for vitamin D's role in immune and inflammatory conditions," she added.
>
>
> .........................
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> > Also, though vitamin D is a secosteroid and can show anti-inflammatory effects, they would be rather weak.
>
>
>
> Vitamin D's metabolites
>
> After vitamin D is formed in the skin or taken orally, it is metabolized into two different substances within the body: 25-hydroxyvitamin D, known as calcidiol, and 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D, known as calcitriol.
>
>
>
> Calcidiol (25-hydroxyvitamin D or 25(OH)D)
>
>
>
> Calcidiol is a prehormone that is made in the liver. Because it is the body's main storage form of vitamin D, the amount of calcidiol available to the body is what determines vitamin D status. When someone refers to "vitamin D levels" they are referring to blood calcidiol levels.
>
>
>
> Calcitriol (1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D or 1,25(OH)2D3)
>
>
>
> **Calcitriol is made from calcidiol, in the kidneys as well as in other organs, and is the most potent steroid hormone in the human body.** Referred to as â€œactivated vitamin D,â€￾ calcitriol is said by the experts to â€œunlockâ€￾ a cellâ€™s DNA library. Acting through the vitamin D receptors (VDR), calcitriol controls the expression of genes, activating about two-thirds of the ones it controls, suppressing the rest. Blood calcitriol levels are not good indicators of the amount of vitamin D should never be used to determine if one is vitamin D deficient.

[http://www.vitamindcouncil.org/about-vitamin-d/what-is-vitamin-d/](http://http://www.vitamindcouncil.org/about-vitamin-d/what-is-vitamin-d/)

Jack, is dietary sulphur from EPCOTx sufficient to create cholestrol sulfate? And since the sulfate is made from homocysteine could a defect in conversion to homocysteine contribute to high LDL readings?

According to Dr. Seneff:

_So, in a nutshell, high LDL appears to be a sign of cholesterol sulfate deficiency . . .

Her suspicion is that the simple oral non-sulfated form of vitamin D likely will not provide the same benefits as the vitamin D created in your skin from sun exposure, because it cannot be converted to vitamin D sulfate. This is yet another reason to really make a concerted effort to get ALL your vitamin D requirements from exposure to sunshine!

"~ulfate actually inactivates vitamin D," Dr. Seneff says. "The sulfated form of vitamin D does not work for calcium transport, which I find very intriguing. And in fact, I think it's the sulfated form for vitamin D that offers the protection from cancer. It strengthens your immune system. It protects you from cardiovascular disease. It's good for your brain. It helps depression. I think all of those effects of vitamin D are effects of vitamin D sulfate."~_

### Souldanzer — 2012-10-05T02:48:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d-and-autoimmunity.3425/post-56374>

> .....explained the theory that AI diseases are potentially caused by infection. Then (in oversimplified terms) the body doesn't fight the infection properly, the infection then exists in all the cells, and the body begins to attack itself...... (Lauren)
>
>
>
> Got me thinking..... maybe the body is fighting the infection. Maybe it's killing the infected cells?
>
>
>
> So I googled (b/c Google know everything )
>
>
>
> [http://thyroid.about.com/cs/hashimotos/a/antibiotics.htm](http://http://thyroid.about.com/cs/hashimotos/a/antibiotics.htm)
>
>
>
>
> > In this research, investigators studied the prevalence of antibodies to Yersinia enterocolitica in patients with diagnosed Hashimoto's thyroiditis. What the researchers found was the the prevalence of antibodies to yersinia --evidence of exposure -- was 14 times higher in people with Hashimoto's thyroiditis than in the control groups.
> >
> >
> >
> > The researchers concluded that there is strong evidence for "an immunopathologic causative relationship between Yersinia enterocolitica infection and Hashimoto's thyroiditis." They also said that the possible effects of antibiotic therapies for Hashimoto's should be investigated.
> >
> >
> >
> >
> >
> >
> > [http://www.discoverymedicine.com/Kazuki-Morohoshi/2011/12/20/viral-infection-and-innate-pattern-recognition-receptors-in-induction-of-hashimotos-thyroiditis/](http://http://www.discoverymedicine.com/Kazuki-Morohoshi/2011/12/20/viral-infection-and-innate-pattern-recognition-receptors-in-induction-of-hashimotos-thyroiditis/)
> >
> >
> >
> > Abstract: Hashimoto's thyroiditis, a common organ-specific autoimmune disease, is multifactorial in which both genetic susceptibility and environmental factors including infection play a critical role in its pathogenesis. Viral infection activates both the innate and adaptive immunity and is implicated as a trigger of Hashimoto's thyroiditis. Candidate viruses include hepatitis C virus and human parvovirus B19. Viral components, which are recognized by innate receptors including Toll-like receptors (TLRs), are detected in thyroid tissues and sera of patients with Hashimoto's thyroiditis. While conflicting results have been obtained regarding the role of TLRs in autoimmune diseases, our preliminary study suggested a contribution of TLR2 and dectin-1 in combination, TLR4, or TLR7 to the production of anti-thyroglobulin antibody in nonobese diabetic mice, a mouse model of Hashimoto's thyroiditis. Despite interesting circumstantial evidence, however, whether viral infection and innate receptors are involved in the development of Hashimoto's thyroiditis remains largely unclear. In this review, we summarize our knowledge regarding the role of viral infection and innate receptors in the etiology of Hashimoto's thyroiditis.
>
>
>
> Since some AI diseases have turned out to be viral/bacterial infections..... I don't know. If the body's immune system is attacking certain organs... how come everybody is so sure that the body is attacking \*healthy\* tissues inappropriately? I'm not quite understanding..... maybe the thyroid is infected in Hashi's and killing off those cells is the only defense the body has to keep it from spreading to other parts of the body?
>
>
>
> I'm having trouble believing that the body would kill healthy cells. That does not make sense in my mind. How do we know whatever organ gets attacked is not infected with something?
>
>
>
> Viral components are detected in the thyroid of patients with Hashimoto’s thyroiditis

### Souldanzer — 2012-10-05T03:03:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d-and-autoimmunity.3425/post-56380>

> B19 infection has been implicated as a cause or trigger of various autoimmune disorders (Kerr, 2000; Lehmann et al., 2003). The viral DNA and protein were detected in synovial tissues from patients with rheumatoid arthritis, suggesting B19 may be a trigger of the disease (Takahashi et al., 1998). We consistently demonstrated the presence of B19 DNA in a thyroid tissue from a Hashimoto’s thyroiditis patient with persistent B19 infection (Mori et al., 2007).

I'm saying the body isn't attacking healthy tissue possibly as stated in lots of explanations of AI disease.... it's attacking infected cells. Unfortunately, those happen to not be replaceable in some organs. But it's not like the body is randomly attacking itself in this case.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-10T23:27:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d-and-autoimmunity.3425/post-57430>

In autoimmune diseases we need to advocate for much higher levels of Vitamin D. Why? In order for circulating vitamin D to perform its functions, it must first activate the vitamin D receptor (VDR). The problem is that many people with autoimmune disease have a genetic polymorphism that affects the expression and activation of the VDR and thus reduces the biologic activity of vitamin D. Studies have shown that a significant number of patients with autoimmune diseases have several VDR polymorphisms. There are over 25 variants of VDR polymorphisms now known and the list grows monthly. If you have a VDR problem you require much higher circulating levels of vitamin D to bind to these defective receptors. As we have mentioned in multiple previous blogs, a leaky gut predisposes to the development of autoimmunity. Moreover, optimal Vitamin D levels are also linked to “tighter junctions” between the enterocytes of our intestinal lining making our guts “less leaky”. If the gut is less leaky our immune system is stronger, because it does not have to be activated constantly to protect the rest of the body. We have also seen above, that vitamin D levels play a huge role in our immune surveillance in our GI tracts. It appears to be critical to push your levels to much higher plasma levels in these cases. I strongly recommend talking this over with your doctor. The fears of toxicity are very overblown in my estimation and the risk of too low a level for disease propagation is far too common and risky for your health.

There is now very recent evidence out from Dr. Hector DeLuca about MS and autoimmune encephalitis. Dr. DeLuca believes that the degradation products of vitamin D3 and/or some byproducts of solar radiation confer health to us in some fashion. He says vitamin D3 degradation and sunlight is some how active against a range of autoimmune illnesses. In his latest work, that “something” he is studying, is active against an experimental model of multiple sclerosis. I have always felt that MS, ALS and Guillian Barre are tied in some fashion to vitamin D metabolism. It appears Dr. DeLuca believes this as well now.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-10T23:28:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d-and-autoimmunity.3425/post-57431>

Can you have a normal plasma D level and still have low vitamin D activity? Yes you can, and it is probably the biggest silent epidemic out there today. I most commonly see this in obese folks with hypothyroidism. 90% of the cases of hypothyroidism in the USA are cause by Hashimoto’s disease. This disease is an autoimmune disease and these patients universally have defective VDR receptors. That means they need very high levels of blood Vitamin D levels and optimization of their thyroid function to get results.

### TheKid — 2012-10-14T11:02:59+0300

Jack talks about VDR polymorphisms in post #89. Does anyone know how/why the higher level of circulating Vitamin D that you get from higher supplemental dosages overcomes the receptor defect? If the receptor is defective, how would simply having more D fix the problem. It seems to me that you would need to do something else in order to allow for the vitamin D and the receptor to "make their connection." Maybe add some co-factor?

When it comes to receptors, I always hear the "lock & key" analogy used. So if a burglar damaged the lock to my front door when he tried to break in my house, it wouldn't matter how many good copies of the key I have with me. I would still need to replace the lock.

> **vkiernan;59798 wrote: Are the liquids better?** I have dry ones now too and have been wondering. I always take them with my BAB though so hope that is helping. Don't know when I will be tested again either. Wish I could remember what kind I had last time as my lab results clearly showed they were working. Next time it will be liguid. Just sort of feel that is the better way.

I use Natures Bounty liquid Vitamin D3. I get it from vitacost and it seems to have a positive impact on my D3 levels. The gastric bypass forces me to choose most liquid supplements to get the maximal effect whenever possible.

### Souldanzer — 2012-10-16T20:04:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d-and-autoimmunity.3425/post-58565>

> **TheKid;59801 wrote:** @Souldanzer, talk more about this please:
>
>
>
> "25-D dost not activate the VDR. Some research says that it can potentially block it by displacing 1,25-D, the active form of D."
>
>
>
> This seems to be an important point, since pretty much all of the D3 I have ever seen on the market is Cholecalciferol. If what you say is true, we would want to get ahold of Calcitrol instead. I don't remember Jack making a distinction.
>
>
>
> I know that sometimes you don't want to supplement active forms. This is part of the arguement behind taking T4 instead of T3 for thryoid problems. That way you let the body's normal process of conversion remain intack and self-limiting. So would have thought Cholecalciferol would be a better choice.

1,25-D is only available by rx and there is a higher risk for toxicity and elevated calcium, so it is used sparingly and only in certain cases. The issue isn't necessarily conversion since people with AI tend to have an elevated 1,25-D serum level. So most \*are\* converting. Yet if you supplement 25-D in order to elevate your 25-D level to a certain high number... then you end up with a lot of circulating 25-D that can potentially block your VDR b/c it can bind to it yet not activate it.

1,25-D is not commonly tested when testing for vitamin D levels b/c the body breaks it down very quickly. So it is not something that is at a very stable level in your body..... it depends on the messages in your body that say more is needed or less is needed and probably has to do with immune system response, bone metabolism and such. But this is just what my (bad) brain remembers from what I read a while ago.

# Thread: What do you do at night?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-do-at-night.3426/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 28
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TribalSpice — 2012-09-03T05:51:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-do-at-night.3426/post-49130>

I really want to embrace the full circadian rhythm thing. But I have to ask, what do you do at night? At the moment, for me it is cleaning up around the house, then some computer time then off to bed around 8:30-9 to read, listen to a hypnosis or occasionally to watch a TV program on my iWank.

Does having enough candles to read by defeat the purpose?

How do people with kids make the change? (I have 3 aged 9, 11 and 13 and they generally have homework that involves computer or at least a light to see what they are doing. I am also not sure about them just playing by candle light)

Have you encountered resistance from partners?

Thanks

### kathylu — 2012-09-03T06:37:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-do-at-night.3426/post-49137>

Trying to get up really early, so I can see first light and so that I am ready to go to bed at a reasonable hour. We have darkened our house quite a bit, but are still using some incandescent lighting. I wear Uvex glasses from sunset until bedtime and if I wake up during the night. Trying to listen to more music, play games, talk and read rather than use electronics.

### TribalSpice — 2012-09-03T07:04:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-do-at-night.3426/post-49141>

Thanks Kathylu. I already wake naturally just before dawn. So can't see myself going to bed any earlier. I might take some of the globes out of the lounge room/dinning area and switch to candles in my room to start. I would really like to just go the whole hack, but the family will crack a tanty over it.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-03T07:09:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-do-at-night.3426/post-49143>

TS, we have just made it through an entire winter (and 6 months total) of candlelight only from dusk onwards. No screens of any kind at all. No blue-blocking glasses. Just candles. My children are 8 and 11. We have done all our homework by candlelight. They are completely used to it now. And they have created amazing, creative games to entertain themselves since TV and iPod and computers are no-go. In July/August this has meant candles by 5pm. It was only an issue during the adjustment phase, but by a few weeks into it, they were sleeping beautifully, winding down naturally, and totally comfortable with the candles-only thing. It is simple to do, requires nothing special, and seems essential to optimal health. Don't assume your children will have major issues with it. Once I explained to my kids why we were doing this, they got on board... and interested. They can tell you ALL about melatonin and light cycles and your brain! (Their only caveat is that they do NOT tell their friends because we are 'too weird.')

### TribalSpice — 2012-09-03T10:25:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-do-at-night.3426/post-49156>

What about Partners? My DH goes to work at 9pm and it will be difficult to get him to accept the change. Any tips on tackling the hesitant to change partner?

### Michael — 2012-09-03T13:50:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-do-at-night.3426/post-49162>

> **TribalSpice;49631 wrote:** ... then some computer time then off to bed around 8:30-9 to read, listen to a hypnosis or occasionally to watch a TV program on my iWank.

I'd be wary of the iPad too late. Look what happened to Steve Jobs.

And it's not just the light going into your eyes. AFAIK, you've got some pretty strong electromagnetic fields put out by that, and there seems to be some evidence that those can disturb circadian rhythms. It'll presumably be keeping a connection open to WiFi and presumably to the mobile-phone network via 3G, too.

I wouldn't let it touch your body directly. If you're sitting with it, maybe put it on a couple of thick cushions rather than directly on your lap.

Ever since I heard an extremely well-qualified scientist - she had string of qualifications as long as your arm - speak on this, I've wondered a bit about this aspect of technology. She said most studies that give mobile phones a clean bill of health are funded by the network companies. So no surprise there. Most independent studies apparently tell a different story. You say this kind of thing to the average person, and they look at you like you're crazy - because they saw the headlines in the paper they were supposed to see - but it's worth considering. Apparently, the Israeli Government has said they would not accept 4G in their country when it arrives. That's not for nothing.

Not saying we can't use modern technology - just you have to watch when and where and how.

### ealachan — 2012-09-03T15:02:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-do-at-night.3426/post-49163>

We finish dinner around 7, sun sets here around 7:30 so we take the dog out for his last walk of the evening and watch the sun go down, come back in and put our orange glasses on, watch a little TV or dub on the computer for maybe half an hour to 45 minutes, then go to bed and read by orange light / with orange glasses on til we get sleepy (only takes about 15 min for me, longer for husband). There's usually some sex in there too, sometimes instead of the reading, sometimes after the reading.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-09-03T19:26:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-do-at-night.3426/post-49198>

> **TribalSpice;49631 wrote:** I really want to embrace the full circadian rhythm thing. But I have to ask, what do you do at night? At the moment, for me it is cleaning up around the house, then some computer time then off to bed around 8:30-9 to read, listen to a hypnosis or occasionally to watch a TV program on my iWank.
>
>
>
> Does having enough candles to read by defeat the purpose?
>
> How do people with kids make the change? (I have 3 aged 9, 11 and 13 and they generally have homework that involves computer or at least a light to see what they are doing. I am also not sure about them just playing by candle light)
>
> Have you encountered resistance from partners?
>
>
>
> Thanks

Hi TribalSpice,

I have children and we have been shutting down all the breakers, except the fridge, in the house at sunset since about March 2012. My girls flipped out at first. My youngest child is scared of the dark. After awhile they just adapted to the new routine. They always know where their headlamps are; they are completely ready for a power failure. I have noticed a number of things over the last few months:

1\. Our power use has gone down from our use last year at this time. This was a shock to my husband. He predicted that turning off the breakers would not have an effect on total power consumption.

2\. I am sleeping much deeper and better.

3\. My girls are getting used to seeing in the dark, a skill modern urban people seen to lack. (My husband grew up in a city, I grew up in a small town. I can see far better in the dark than he can. My children aren't much better either but I hope that will improve.)

4\. I really like some of the conversations we have as a family after the lights go off. It's been great. I wonder what winter will bring?

Watch out for technology. My girls were each given an ipad type device by a misguided auntie. I think these devices are really bad for people at night. Maybe the rest of the time too. This is too big of a topic for right now. If you must, take away the devices from your children at night, and don't use them yourself.

My husband has recently been listening to dvd story tapes after dark. He has had a bit of a sleep disorder but listening to tapes at night has solved all that. He said he thought that "listening" to stories is somehow rewiring his brain. He is totally on board with the lights off program after a few months of success. Yes, my husband thought it was a nutty idea but I have a record of solving problems with non-standard thinking. He loves me too and supports my projects!

For a long time I have wanted to build an off-the-grid house. After this successful experiment shutting off the breakers at night in my house, my hunger for an off-the-grid house has only increased. I have been greatly saddened that I can't start this project where I am presently living. Last night as I lay in the dark thinking about nothing and the answer came to me. I can built a "mobile stealth house with garden". After my husband got over the shock of the idea the engineer in him awakened and his great knowledge of mechanism system and design started bubbling over. Friends, I am going to have my off-the-grid house and its going to be mobile too! And I can do it right where I am. I am not sure if I am going to post this project on eatkamloops.org or not. There is only one way to keep a house stealthy and that is by not talking about it. Here is a post about where my thinking has come from. Have you ever wondered if you could live in a small house? It would mean giving up the envious looks of your neighbors:

[http://eatkamloops.org/what-if/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/what-if/)

[http://eatkamloops.org/port-fool-you/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/port-fool-you/)

I know what you mean about the fascination of these devices, but it may be best to switch them off and put them down a couple of hours before bedtime.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-04T00:44:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-do-at-night.3426/post-49260>

I'd just do your thing. When he's home, he can live by candlelight. Over to him to decide what to do with work. There are always the blue-blocking glasses (Shijin has just ordered a pair that are work-appropriate re: look/style, you could PM her). But as Dr K said in the last podcast, we need to take care of ourselves. My husband travels a lot. I don't know what he does with light when he's away. But when he's home, he lives by the cycles because he lives in our house!

### Endless — 2012-09-04T01:29:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-do-at-night.3426/post-49286>

I will use my computer (with f.lux and orange glasses) until dark, and then shut it down and unplug my router. I unplug my wireless printer as well. Haven't started turning off breakers yet but maybe!

I might watch some tv, I have the backlight turned down to 0 and use the orange glasses for that as well. I've been using only candle light except to read by, I am afraid to fall asleep with the candles on.

### hope2conceive — 2012-09-04T02:57:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-do-at-night.3426/post-49299>

> **Shijin13;49782 wrote:** I've seen a complete turn around in dd - who before doing lights out, eating by candle light etc couldn't go to sleep w/o her night light... now she yells at me to turn off my red head lamp when I'm reading while sitting beside her or ds while they go to sleep. funny...
>
>
>
> I was cleaning up some stuff this weekend - and found a 3rd headlamp that has a red light... I gave it to dd so she could have one when we read stories at night....once ds gets old enough he'll be getting one too

Shijin13,

may I ask, how did you get your husband and kids to come on board re sleep??? My hubby (we don't have kids...yet) thinks I'm crazy for trying to be so careful to have dark. I think it's mostly bc he's a night owl and loves being up at night (ugh) because it's quiet....how did you start the change? I would love to hear.....

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-04T04:26:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-do-at-night.3426/post-49307>

> **hope2conceive;49810 wrote:** Shijin13,
>
>
>
> may I ask, how did you get your husband and kids to come on board re sleep??? My hubby (we don't have kids...yet) thinks I'm crazy for trying to be so careful to have dark. I think it's mostly bc he's a night owl and loves being up at night (ugh) because it's quiet....how did you start the change? I would love to hear.....

All I did was send him to see Jack as a 2nd opinion regarding his spine. Sent him to Jack w/a full optimal lab panel (I've figured out how to get the health ins to pay for close to 90% of all labs w/the help of my PCP & chiropractor)

Dh & Jack had a come to Jesus appointment. Dh got home and threw away all the grains & hasn't looked back. He asked me to get him orange glasses and a headlamp w/a red light on it. That was July 10th of this year. We haven't looked back. The kids they're 2 & 4 they really don't get a choice. There have been days where they won't eat their seafood dinner when that happens it becomes breakfast. If its not eaten then it becomes lunch they've figured out they eat what we eat and they like it. Can't complain when they're noshing on veggies at bday parties and fill up their goodie bags w/carrots, celery, tomatoes, broccoli & cauliflower while all the other kids are shoveling down candy. Every other parent asks how we do it. It's pretty simple. Set the example and eat what nourishes the mind & body. Not degrade it.
