### Michael — 2012-09-04T08:43:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-do-at-night.3426/post-49319>

> **KiwiLauren;49757 wrote:** ... and no phones in pockets either.

Yes, I'm scrupulous about turning mine off before putting it in my coat pocket. I don't actually need it on all the time, not having a job where I'm on-call or anything, so I tend to turn it on when I need it. I do have it on at work sometimes, but then I'll put it on a desk away from me. The number of men who carry theirs on and in their trouser pockets right next to the "family jewels"!

And my router goes off at night.

One thing that rather spooked me was finding out that cordless phones are rather worse than cellphones. My landline phone at home is actually a cheap wired one. But it's just rather unpleasant to think of people, perhaps people you know and care about, chatting on the phone ... I'm not generally chatty on the phone, but any number of people do phone round friends and family in the evening and spend anything up to 30 or 40 minutes on a call regularly with one of those things against their head.

### hope2conceive — 2012-09-05T02:29:17+0300

I don't cook grains anymore (but he does get potatoes on occasions, is super skinny) but the whole lighting thing...ugh. Still a struggle. But I am SO inspired by stories like yours....and to know I'm not alone is such a gift!!!!!

Thanks for sharing your lab work too. fascinating to read all that....are you still getting migraines? Forgive me if I'm missing an update!

### TribalSpice — 2012-09-10T04:10:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-do-at-night.3426/post-50626>

Ok, so we haven't hit it hard as yet, we have done about 4 nights in the last week with no screens or artificial lights at night. The kids love it, we have been playing backgammon and reading The Hunger Games together by candle light. I am waiting until after football finals have finished, because the man will not cope not watching it and then we will go as full time as possible. At the moment Friday nights are hard because my youngest does little athletics until 8:30pm and it is under bright lights (and do get me started on the constant waft of fast food smell coming from the canteen).

### Michael — 2012-09-10T10:50:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-do-at-night.3426/post-50671>

Here's an interesting one -

Was just listening to Dr. Colin Champ - the Caveman Doctor - on Abel James' podcast.

AFAIK, he's an oncologist. Anyway, he says that blind people have a 30% lower rate of cancer. There's simply no way for light-signals that are mismatched in terms of circadian rhythms to get back from their eyes.

I know the connection between light and cancer has been known about for some time, but looking at data on blind people - what an obvious but smart thing to do!

### Kiera2 — 2012-09-10T11:00:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-do-at-night.3426/post-50672>

That is interesting Michael. I've watched lectures by Russell Foster, professor of circadian biology. He talks about circadian rhythm disruption in blind people, but some are unaffected. This is because the light that gets through to the SCN is detected by ganglion cells, not rods and cones. So, it's possible to be blind, but still wake up at sunrise, without knowing that the sun is rising, because there is still a way for light signals to get through.

It would be even more interesting to look at data on cancer rates in people who have lost their ganglion cells.

### Michael — 2012-09-10T15:05:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-do-at-night.3426/post-50688>

> **Coriander;51227 wrote:** That is interesting Michael. I've watched lectures by Russell Foster, professor of circadian biology. He talks about circadian rhythm disruption in blind people, but some are unaffected. This is because the light that gets through to the SCN is detected by ganglion cells, not rods and cones. So, it's possible to be blind, but still wake up at sunrise, without knowing that the sun is rising, because there is still a way for light signals to get through.
>
>
>
> It would be even more interesting to look at data on cancer rates in people who have lost their ganglion cells.

Wow. Thanks. Even as I heard it, I was thinking mightn't light have some effect on your eye even if you couldn't see? So, definitely, yes it can.

### SeaHorse — 2012-09-04T01:29:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/selenium.3432/post-49285>

I thought this was interesting....

Seasonal Selenium Variations in Mussels and Oysters from a French Marine Farm

J.-C. Amiard, B. Berthet, S. Boutaghou

ISOMer, URA 1356 du CNRS, Service d&rsquo;écotoxicologie, Faculté de Pharmacie, Université de Nautes, 1 Rue Gaston Veil, 44035 Nautes Cédex, France

[http://dx.doi.org/10.1006/jfca.1993.1041](http://http://dx.doi.org/10.1006/jfca.1993.1041), How to Cite or Link Using DOI

Permissions & Reprints

View full text

Purchase $31.50

Abstract

Selenium (Se) is an essential element for humans. Seafood is generally considered as a good source of Se. This element is principally present in the form of selenate, often easily bioavailable. but its availability can also be quite low in some species. The seasonal variations of Se (concentration and burden) in mussels (Mytilus edulis) and oysters (Crassostrea gigas) were examined from November 1982 to May 1984. The molluscs were sampled in a shellfish farm located in the Bay of Bourgneuf (France). Se concentrations in oysters were lowest in samples collected from September to November and highest in samples collected from April to July. In mussels, Se concentrations in the various organs were highest from February to June and lowest from July to October. Se quantity per individual peaked from February to mid-June, while minimum values were found from July to January. From a nutritional point of view, a consumer will ingest approximately six times more Se with mussels from February to June (2 to 13 μg Se Â· g−1) and only twice as much with oysters except from the September–November period (3 to 6 μg Se Â· g−1). In the case of oysters, the consumer ingests a fixed number of individuals and on this basis, Se intake via oysters is highest from April to July (2 to 18 μg Se per oyster). According to the season, the intake of Se with oysters varies from 1.3 to 14 μg per day and per consumer. In the case of mussels, these variations range from 6.4 to 13.3 μg per day and per consumer.

### IceBarbie — 2012-09-07T01:14:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/okay-just-to-be-sure-no-carbs-during-the-winter.3475/post-49871>

x\_danny\_x....sorry my comment caused distraction from your question. I felt that it did tie in to the carb/seafood/evolution topics from Kruse and I was laughing when I posted it. I'm not a troll--I've been following much of Kruse's ideas for a long time now. I did the Leptin Reset (took 4 months) and still struggled with weight gain. Tried CT but was starving instead of shredding fat. I've made so stinkin' many changes in the last year I'm just getting a little jaded and starting to wonder why the heck can't I eat a stupid banana without feeling like I'm committing suicide. Thus my cynicism lately. Sorry to mess up your thread. I figured it would get closed anyway whether I commented or not. But now I realize it's not in the Ask Jack section. My bad.

And Tom, I do not believe that a "day" in scripture necessarily means a literal 24 hour period of time. A "day" can be thousands of years or refer to a specific time period. "In my day" would refer to a time period earlier in my life but not one single day for example. I do not believe in 6 literal 24 hour days for Creation to have taken place like some do. And carbon dating of bones thousands of years old isn't necessarily accurate if those bones have been inbedded in rocks that are billions of years old. Just wanted to clarify my opinion on that because I really understand how most here would consider me to be nutty even using the word Creation in the first place. Obviously, I'm in the minority here. And that's okay. I've been questioning things since I was young and this is just one of those topics that has been important to me. My questioning everything in Conventional Wisdom has also brought me to this site for tidbits of good health info. I just filter out the stuff that doesn't make sense and I have still benefitted. So it's all good. KumBaYah!!

Now back to our Regularly Scheduled Program--Winter Carbs!!!

### AKMan — 2012-09-07T02:01:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/okay-just-to-be-sure-no-carbs-during-the-winter.3475/post-49881>

> **x\_danny\_x;50408 wrote:** I took a look at AKMAN's post and the dude eats dark chocolate during the winter and a couple other carbs? Why is that?

I started this last winter and may change things this winter based on labs. I have a good baseline now to look at. As I get into full-fledged winter mode in December, the carbs will be sparse, like under 20g total/day for 3 months, I will be very strict about this and check labs at the end. If lipids, thyroid, testosterone, and hs-crp are all great, I will do the same the next winter. If they are off, I will reconsider some things. By dark chocolate, I mean 100%, baking type--not Hershey's Special Dark. I'm living further north than about 99% of the world's population, so doing what I do may not be the best for someone in SoCal.

# Thread: BG 11 question
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bg-11-question.3481/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 22
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ealachan — 2012-09-06T16:06:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bg-11-question.3481/post-49737>

_I thought about posting this in Ask Jack, but I'm interested in a more involved discussion that I know Dr. K can't do in the Ask Jack section..._

So, in reading BG 11 this morning, I had a decidedly hard time wrapping my head around the BIG PICTURE in the face of all the minutia. Dr. K states that a person can't lose weight until they're leptin-sensitive. It is also inferred that they can't become leptin-sensitive unless they sleep well and get the resultant proper hormonal cascade. But then again, if one is leptin-resistant then it's impossible to get the proper sleep and hormonal cascade necessary to heal the body enough to regain leptin sensitivity?!

I feel like I'm chasing my leptin-resistant tail, here. Anybody have any insight they'd like to offer? I know the simple answer is "do what Dr. K tells you", but that doesn't entirely work for me, as I need to know why things work the way they do, and even having read all the blogs multiple times, I'm still getting a bit lost in my socks.

### kathylu — 2012-09-06T16:44:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bg-11-question.3481/post-49739>

Insight? Yes and no. Empirically I can see the truth of the ideas that Dr. K is weaving together. As I age, everything seems to be falling apart. My hormones have tanked, my FBG is creeping up, I am IR/LR, can't lose weight, memory issues, brain fog, I could go on, but it's too depressing! It's important to understand the principles, but I think it can be a trap to get bogged down in the details. That may be why you feel like you're chasing your tail.

It's actually pretty simple. Light has the greatest influence on hormones and metabolism, more than diet. To become LS/IS again you must correct the function of your circadian cycle. Best way to do that (in order of importance) is to obey natural light cycles, become cold adapted and adopt a seasonally ketogenic, seafood based diet. If you are obese, you must remain cold adapted and ketogenic year round (until you are "fixed"). If you are under 38, lucky you, as you may not need BHRT hormone tweaking like older women and men do. If you are obese, or have problems such as OSA or T2D, cycloset and metformin are helpful. However, I think Dr. K is right, controlling light exposure, lots of CT and the correct diet will work wonders for most.

I can find all kinds of stuff about timing of meals helping to restore leptin sensitivity, but I guess what I'm really looking for is a definitive yes or no on whether controlling the light in our environment is effective in specifically improving our leptin sensitivity.

And, for what it's worth, he may have said just that somewhere and I was just on info overload and missed it...which is why I was asking the general reading public.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-07T02:11:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bg-11-question.3481/post-49883>

The low T3 thing is killing me too. I got my levels up pretty much where they needed to be this past summer. Unfortunately I couldn't handle to dose (high heart rate, low blood pressure) and it was incapacitating. Now I am back on mainly T4 meds and have terrible T3 levels. I'm hoping the light/cycloset combo will make me more tolerant to taking more T3. And right now, CT is really, really hard.

I would love to see more info on using light to our advantage, for healing purposes. I see comments about getting first morning light on the eyes, but for how long (each sitting) to produce a result? Two minutes, 10? As much as possible? I suppose ideally we would move outside. Also, what about getting more natural light during the day? I think Dr. Kruse has been pretty clear now about the problems created by artificial light, but I'd love to learn more of the details about using natural light to our advantage. These kinds of changes are way easier for me to slip in than all the dietary ones!

### SeaHorse — 2012-09-07T02:26:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bg-11-question.3481/post-49885>

A minute or so in the early a.m. is enough to get the message to your brain. ( look at the sun even if there are clouds or rain) Being outside during the day is optimal...getting as much sun as you can. Being inside isn't great and being under artificial light isn't great at all. So blueblockers on if you sit at a computer all day or are in an office. When the sun goes down, low natural lighting, blueblockers and no computers, etc. Were you able to listen to the light webinar or the QandA? Lots of detail in there. hope this helps.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-07T03:15:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bg-11-question.3481/post-49894>

> **BJK77;50417 wrote:** What about those who are really struggling to cold adapt due to low T3 and/or other hormonal issues? I'm following the light cycle as best I can and am eating what I feel is the best diet I can. Two out of three meals are always seafood - some raw, some cooked with no saturated fats. My problem is that after 7 months of CT I still struggle with it BIG time. I'm sure it's related to low T3/progesterone/wonky cortisol issues.
>
>
>
> Will following the light cycle and eating an epi-paleo diet help to resolve those issues enough that one can become cold adapted to have the perfect trifecta going on?

The answer should be intuitive......your labs are your mirror to your path. If you are doing the environmental things 100% correct and thing dont work you're metabolic deranged and you need exogenous help of meds and a doc. If you dont your a rock star using evolutionary medicine principles to guide you. The hormone panel is your North Star. Sounds like Brain Gut 5.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-07T04:07:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bg-11-question.3481/post-49910>

Light is the key that starts/stops the engine. (circadian cycles)

Food & hormones are the fuel

CT is the accelerator

The Brain is the navigator

Optimal requires all systems working in concert, if one is off the entire system doesn't work properly or efficiently

So for those of us who've been struggling & seeing minimal if any success w/out paying attention to light should begin to see changes when we pay attn to it. However we must remember we have to turn back our epigenetics switches that have been on for Xyears. We also have to contend with the reversal of disease and illness as a result of our own personal mismatches

This isn't going to happen over night. I've been working on my health in one way shape or form since 1999 only now am I getting some where Each success peels back another layer. Each layer brings me that much closer to cracking the code w/in me.

While we'd all love to have the BodyRock.tv bodies in 30 days we're broken for most of us that isn't going to happen over night. Once we get our stuff straightened out & flying right the possibilities are endless. While its not fun hearing it's going to take time it's the truth. That's because we're each working on our own personal masterpiece. And those things just don't happen over night. They take time effort an love and many of us have to balance working on the masterpiece with the rest of our lives b/c they are integral

Remember what your masterpiece looks like today is not the final product. Think about the following question and how it relates to your journey: How long did Michael Angelo work on David to get it to its final phase???

### BJK77 — 2012-09-07T16:47:32+0300

>

Gretchen,

Your post makes me both helpful and jealous  Hopeful because I remember lurking on MDA almost a year ago, reading your posts and now seeing how far you've come and jealous because I truly cannot wait for the day I understand all of this enough to clearly explain it to others. Maybe I shouldn't even say "understand" it, but more "comprehend" it since I just don't feel like I have the brain capacity these days to really dig into the science and absorb it all.

Thankfully I have seen HUGE improvements in many areas of my health and overall life so I will continue on this path with primarily blind faith that Jack is pushing us in the right direction. Generally I like to really be able to think things through and know without a doubt that what I'm doing is best. In this case I just can't do that so I'm banking on the improvements to just keep coming and coming . . . so far Jack hasn't let me down one bit so it's been pretty easy!

I get what you're saying. Thanks for spelling it out so clearly. Again, I find myself hopeful that my increased focus on the light cycle will begin to turn my hormones around. I feel like my diet is as tight as can be, so we'll see if I'm giving my brain and hormones enough support to turn themselves around. Hopefully that's the case; if not, I'll just have to continue to pursue BHRT.

# Thread: Karen from Arizona
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/karen-from-arizona.3482/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### mafixit — 2012-09-06T17:04:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/karen-from-arizona.3482/post-49741>

My chiropractor gave me this website and suggest I check it out. I am a 48 YO female suffering from chronic dizziness and almost daily headaches since May 2011. My only past major health concern was a detached retina in 2007.

I have been through all the tests the Mayo Clinic can provide since May 2011 - 2 MRI's, an MRA/MRV, a cervical MRI, CT scan of my sinuses, extensive vestibular testing, EKG, etc. All are 'normal'. I have seen 3 neurologists (MS, General and Headache), an ENT, an optometrist, an OBGYN, a non-Mayo chiropractor (who referred me here - she is treating me with neuro-cranial integration) and my Primary Care Physician more times than I care to count.

I have been diagnosed with Chronic Migraine and have been put on a low dose of Verapamil, which does not seem to be helping much at all. I have adjusted my diet to eliminate possible migraine triggers, to no avail. We tried to take me off my birth control pills but I crashed and burned, so they put me back on them, because the OB and the Headache guy both think this may be somewhat hormone related. My anxiety level is through the roof.

My lower back and neck constantly hurt, I am having digestive issues now, and experience strange vibrating sensations throughout my body. I try to exercise at least 3 days a week but do not have a lot of energy and although I have the strength to lift, my muscles shake. When I get on the treadmill for a simple 20 minute walk, I am completely dizzy when I stop and it lasts for quite some time.

I want desperately to be healthy and active again. There is too much in this life to look forward too and there are too many things I want to do that I can't because I feel like absolute \*\*\*\* 6 out of 7 days each week.

I understand that Dr. Kruse may be able to take a look at my records and make some recommendations. How do I go about that?

Karen

Glendale, AZ

### nonchalant — 2012-09-06T23:00:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/karen-from-arizona.3482/post-49843>

Hi Karen, sounds like you came to the right place! The first thing I would suggest is that you stop trying to exercise. Sounds like your body is not healthy enough for it, and you are probably just using up stem cells coping with the added stress it brings.

You might want to start with the Leptin Prescription, to start getting your body back to the proper circadian rhythm. And I imagine in a consult with Dr. Kruse, which I highly recommend, he would suggest you get tested for vitamin D, various hormones including cortisol, and highly sensitive CRP (cardiac).

Someone else posted with a strange vibrating sensation, you may want to search for that.

If you sign up for a consult, you need to write up a history (there's a thread about doing that) and a list of medications/supplements, and a list of goals.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/karen-from-arizona.3482/post-49860>

### caroline — 2012-09-07T01:40:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/karen-from-arizona.3482/post-49876>

Hi Karen. Welcome - you have definitely come to the right place. Start reading all Dr. K's blogs. It is hard work and you might have to read them many time and then ask questions. Everyone here is very supportive and as Nonchalant said - if you can sign up for a consult after you have done your history etc. and circadian rhythm is so very important. Good luck and keep posting so we can follow your journey - it helps everyone and you never know when you are going to have a 'light bulb' moment!

### mafixit — 2012-09-11T01:13:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/karen-from-arizona.3482/post-50838>

> **caroline;50420 wrote:** Hi Karen. Welcome - you have definitely come to the right place. Start reading all Dr. K's blogs. It is hard work and you might have to read them many time and then ask questions. Everyone here is very supportive and as Nonchalant said - if you can sign up for a consult after you have done your history etc. and circadian rhythm is so very important. Good luck and keep posting so we can follow your journey - it helps everyone and you never know when you are going to have a 'light bulb' moment!

Waiting patiently for word on an email educational consult so I can figure out what labs to get ordered. I sit here suffering from one of my daily migraines, hoping that we can discover something causing the various symptoms I deal with that my doctors have not looked for or caught. Headaches and bad neck muscle tension (diagnosed as migraine), dizziness and lightheadedness, major loss of energy (I prospect for gold in the desert and last year could work like a dog without issue), minor digestive issues, night sweats and warm flashes, strange pulsating sensations all over my body when I lay down, strange buzzing sensations all over (like my muscles are twitching but they are not), lower back discomfort right where your dimples are, intermittent pain in my upper back between my left shoulder blade and spine, leg/calf cramps (that go away when I take Magnesium, even though my doc told me my Magnesium levels were 'normal', the vision issues like I am wearing the wrong prescription on one eye - (I do have a vertical deviation from a scleral buckle around my right eye- had eye surgery in 2007), light sensitivity (like my eyes are not adjusting to light) and other strange vision things, and of course the increased anxiety that is sometimes unbearable.

In the meantime, I have an appt with my headache neurologist at Mayo tomorrow, because the Verapamil they put me on not only did not work, but made me feel like I got hit by a truck. I know he is going to put me on Pamelor. I hate the thought of taking any drugs, much less an anti-depressant.

Between my food allergies (to the squash family, celery, cucumbers, cantaloupe and watermelon) and trying to deal with eliminating all possible migraine triggers, it seems there is not much more I can eat. I am seriously confused and look forward to some guidance by someone who will look at the big picture and put everything together for me.

Karen

### caroline — 2012-09-11T02:01:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/karen-from-arizona.3482/post-50843>

Good luck Karen - Did you manage to sign up for a consult? You are suffering so much - I hope you find answers really soon. I am sending you a big hug!

### mafixit — 2012-09-11T03:00:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/karen-from-arizona.3482/post-50860>

> **caroline;51402 wrote:** Good luck Karen - Did you manage to sign up for a consult? You are suffering so much - I hope you find answers really soon. I am sending you a big hug!

Thanks, I love hugs

I am waiting for Misty to set me up with an email educational consult so I can get the proper labs, then I will sign up for the face to face. I am anxious to get started.

Karen

### mafixit — 2012-09-18T16:33:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/karen-from-arizona.3482/post-52342>

Received my email consult - thank you  I am taking the recommended lab list to my doc today so she can order, however some of the links to the lab website are dead links, and I want to be sure that we order the correct labs.

Specifically, the Female Extensive Hormone Panel is not listed as one of the available labs. The only ones listed are: Female Basic Hormone Panel; Female Comprehensive Hormone Panel; Female Hormone Add-On Panel.

Also there is not a Complete Thyroid Panel listed. There is a Thyroid Panel and a Thyroid Add On Panel.

Diurnal Cortisol is a dead link.

Thanks,

Karen from Arizona

### mafixit — 2012-09-20T01:07:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/karen-from-arizona.3482/post-52804>

I have ordered most labs, but we are having an issue with the Saliva tests. My doctor called ZRT and they are not aware of the 4 panel ASI Adrenal Stress Index with cortisol, DHEA and salivary melatonin. The links given to me below do not include anything called ASI.

Where oh where can we order this test from? It's the last one on the list that we need. Neither Labcor nor Quest labs are aware of it either.

Karen from Arizona

YOU SELECT SALIVA PANEL ([http://w3.zrtlab.com/test-kits/combo-test-kit.html](http://http://w3.zrtlab.com/test-kits/combo-test-kit.html)) CRITICAL for you! you need a 4 panel ASI (adrenal stress index) with cortisol, DHEA and add a salivary melatonin.

I included the rest of the salivary labs in case you wanted to assay your hormones by saliva instead and for you to compare cost

SALIVA KITS ([http://w3.zrtlab.com/test-kits/saliva-testing-kits.html](http://http://w3.zrtlab.com/test-kits/saliva-testing-kits.html))

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3AxBxpB-fEw&feature=plcp\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;3AxBxpB-fEw\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3AxBxpB-fEw&feature=plcp\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;3AxBxpB-fEw)

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ggb1QYEqLpc&feature=plcp\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;ggb1QYEqLpc\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ggb1QYEqLpc&feature=plcp\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;ggb1QYEqLpc)

I guess I'm having a bit of an issue when Crawford comments on agriculturalization of the oceans. He basically says we're behind, we figured out how to use the land for agricultural purposes 10,000 years ago to feed ourselves but what we really need to do is the same with the oceans. Our fishing practices, according to him, are out-dated.

My hesitation with that: Look where agriculture of the land has gotten us. Do we really want to see the same happening to the oceans? What if attempting to harvest more from them or make it more accessible will result in degradation of the final product as it pretty much has in land agriculture and as we can see in the seafood farming operations?

I'm also getting a bit fascinated with what I am learning about eyes, brains, and DHA. My eye health entirely correlates with my brain health as I am looking back over my life..... but no one ever made the friggin' connection??? We now have an idea of why DHA in the brain is so important for optimal function (special electrical properties)..... why is it so important in our eyes? Same thing? It just makes me wonder.... eyesight is also the most important one of our senses.... when you lose it it is the hardest loss of sense to cope with. Did evolution have some particular ideas for the super-high DHA levels in our eyes? The back of the retina is the richest DHA place in our bodies, even higher than the brain, iirc.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-06T21:03:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/crawford-on-seafood-humans-evolution-and-health.3483/post-49822>

On this point I think Crawford is wrong.....but the reason he is saying it should be eye opening. Eating bad seafood is better for a big brained mammal. That is the implication of his thoughts. On this......I believe he is correct.

### Michael — 2012-09-10T21:13:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/crawford-on-seafood-humans-evolution-and-health.3483/post-50762>

Good stuff.

I like the way Australian TV uses subtitles for him at one point. He has a kind of English accent with some Scottish overtones in there -as I suppose a man called Michael Angus Crawford would.

I'm now imagining people Down Under saying, "Did you catch what that Pom said? They need to subtitle him, or its anyone's guess otherwise."

### nonchalant — 2012-09-06T18:25:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-browsing-impossible.3486/post-49769>

So the very medium that is enabling Jack to show me the way to health, is undermining my health. I can keep my laptop off my lap, and limit its use to daylight hours, and use f.lux, and blueblockers. But it is far from optimal. At least the monitors is an LCD, not LED. I'm going to just have to stop my computer use between dusk and dawn.

Jack, please tell us that you will eventually publish a book, in paper form?

# Thread: oyster season!

### ealachan — 2012-09-06T18:51:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oyster-season.3488/post-49780>

Oysters are said to be best in months that end with "R", at least here in New England, so technically it's oyster season! I like to slurp 'em raw, but my husband is NOT a fan of the raw shellfish, so I need to cook his. I've only ever cooked oysters in oyster stew. Anybody have any good COOKED oyster recipes?

slurrrrrrrp...

### nonchalant — 2012-09-06T19:04:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oyster-season.3488/post-49784>

Oh, that's right! I was waiting earlier for oyster season to start before trying them raw, but finally I said 'what the heck' and tried them in July. Reminds me, need to get some today, to keep up my Once a Week Oyster Slurp.

### ealachan — 2012-09-06T19:07:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oyster-season.3488/post-49790>

### kathylu — 2012-09-07T04:08:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oyster-season.3488/post-49911>

Sorry Dr. K, but GI distress including diarrhea and vomiting can occur very quickly when eating contaminated shellfish.

0.5 – 3 hours Mild gastroenteritis (nausea, vomiting,

diarrhoea and abdominal cramps); chills;

headache; fever

Okadaic acid

TOXIN

Diarrhoeic

Shellfish Poisoning

(DSP)

Molluscs such as mussels, clams,

oysters and scallops

### colleencoble — 2012-09-07T04:54:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oyster-season.3488/post-49916>

HOW do you guys force them down? I think they are the nastiest, slimiest things on the planet. I'd like to like them but there's no way in the world I'd eat them raw. My mother used to put them in dressing. Shudder. And even then they were slimy. How do you fix them to actually get them down?

### kathylu — 2012-09-07T05:08:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oyster-season.3488/post-49920>

Colleen, the tastiest way is fried, but I haven't tried a "paleo approved" batter yet, so I don't have a recipe to give you. Next would be oyster stew, also difficult if you are not doing dairy because it contains butter and heavy cream. Cioppino (italian seafood stew) can be made with clams, fish and oysters and is quite delicious. Good recipes at Epicurious. Canned smoked oysters are ok in salads.

### kathylu — 2012-09-07T05:10:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oyster-season.3488/post-49921>

I also like them raw on the half shell, with lemon and tabasco, but I guess that takes a little bravery.

I have only had them raw.

### BJK77 — 2012-09-07T17:09:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oyster-season.3488/post-50002>

> **colleencoble;50461 wrote:** HOW do you guys force them down? I think they are the nastiest, slimiest things on the planet. I'd like to like them but there's no way in the world I'd eat them raw. My mother used to put them in dressing. Shudder. And even then they were slimy. How do you fix them to actually get them down?

I can't stand them either, but I feel a strong pull to eat them. I buy them already shucked from ilovebluesea.com so I don't even have to mess with the shells at all. I freeze them in small quantities and generally just drop 2-3 down the hatch each night with dinner. No chewing, very little tasting. Some are a bit large to swallow, but I manage! Much better than the alternatives IMO

### kathylu — 2012-09-07T18:19:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oyster-season.3488/post-50035>

Hmmm...I haven't been able to bring myself to try a MHS either, partly because I don't have a blender that could handle it, partly because...well, yuck! Here's another idea. Go to your asian food store and buy fresh or frozen flying fish roe and eat a couple big spoons of it every day. Believe me, it's yummy! That's the tiny orange fish eggs that are served with sushi and sashimi. Fish eggs just have to contain all the good stuff that MHS do...it just has to.

### ealachan — 2012-09-07T18:46:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oyster-season.3488/post-50046>

### nonchalant — 2012-09-07T18:54:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oyster-season.3488/post-50050>

Crunchy! Salty.

I bought some fresh from the asian store, packaged on a styrofoam tray like meat. But the label said "seasoned", whatever that means.

I rinsed them, but who knows what evil stuff lurked. They had a long expiration date, so preservatives of some sort.

### BJK77 — 2012-09-07T20:23:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oyster-season.3488/post-50061>

>

Here's another idea. Go to your asian food store and buy fresh or frozen flying fish roe and eat a couple big spoons of it every day. Believe me, it's yummy! I love salmon roe! I haven't been able to find it locally at a reasonable price. I'm so jealous of everyone who has these great Asian markets where they can stock up! I order mine online, but it's delish

I agree with nonchalant - it's salty and just slightly crunchy. Back in my egg-eating days, I loved to top eggs with roe. YUM! Now I just eat it by the spoonful

### kathylu — 2012-09-08T01:04:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oyster-season.3488/post-50128>

Yup. Crunchy, not as salty but like the ocean. Good topping for fish, crudites. Mix with sour cream (if you eat dairy) for a dip. Top a half avocado with crabmeat and roe, sprinkle with vinegar. Wrap roe in a nori sheet with shrimp and something spicy (sriracha?) for a sushi handroll. Eat roe plain.

Note: sometimes the tiny eggs will lodge in your teeth and you will find one and pop it later. Bonus!

### nonchalant — 2012-09-28T23:12:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oyster-season.3488/post-55086>

Ealachan, you are right. Oysters on the half shell are tasting better now. I don't feel like I have to make an herioc effort to consume them now. Instead of a half dozen, I've moved on to a full dozen now. I just put a bit of horseradish on each one. I've even learned to slurp the extra juice in the shell now. Heck, I've had oysters twice this week! Love those months with an "R".

Optimal, here I come!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-29T02:24:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oyster-season.3488/post-55125>

I went to place as the main guest and we gave a little talk.......and then they came for the order. We order some wine.......and I was last to order around the table. I ordered a large shrimp cocktail, and order of scallops in bacon, and a dozen grilled clams for the appetizer. He ask me if this was for the table and I told him this was for me. Then I ordered dinner. I ordered a Kruse surf and turf. He asked me what that was. I told him it was a 14 oz filet, covered in crabmeat with a 12 oz piece of seabass cooked rare. He looked at me.........in disbelief. Then he has me how I wanted the Filet mignon.......I told him raw would be best. I passed on all veggies. He asked me what I wanted for sauces and I told him lemons and my saliva would do just fine. HE then watched me demolish it before his eyes. Not a speck left. He asked for card and said he needs this diet.

### ealachan — 2012-10-01T17:04:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oyster-season.3488/post-55568>

I found non-smoked canned oysters yesterday, and bought a can, figuring I could sneak them into the clam chowder I was making. I popped the can open, drained and rinsed them, tasted one...and they were HORRIBLE. Ugh. Hork. Badness. I can understand how anybody who has only had canned oysters can hate them! Blech. In other news, I made a really nice clam & cod chowder, using pureed cauliflower to thicken rather than a roux. I did put some cream in it, but next time I won't. Recipe is on my blog if anyone wants to try it.

### ealachan — 2012-10-01T17:40:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oyster-season.3488/post-55580>

> **Cavemam;56723 wrote:** Thanks for the inspiration! We were just talking about it being clam chowder time!!! I found some Okra at the farmers market yesterday - I'm going to attempt a roux free gumbo. Paleo Comfort Foods makes a roux with coconut flour and almond flour, but it just doesn't do the same thing chemically, so I dunno. Any suggestions ms Chef?

Hmm...I've never made gumbo, so I'm not much use to you there, I guess. I also hate, hate, hate okra...LOL Okra is right up there in a close second behind lima beans on the list of Veggies I Do Not Want To Put In My Mouth.

I'm always a little bit skeptical of cookbooks and websites that say "oh you can still make a roux, just use almond / coconut flour instead!", because it's the starch in the wheat that acts as the thickener, and nut flours contain far less starch. You'd have to use WAY more of them to get the equivalent thickening of wheat flour.

Some recipes call for arrowroot, but I'd stay away from that as it has even more starch in it than wheat. Unless you're into that whole "safe starch" thing, anyway...

### Lyndra — 2012-11-01T19:57:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oyster-season.3488/post-61345>

Any tips for shucking and/or varieties of oysters that are easier to open? Our local seafood market can get 30+ varieties and last time I got Chesapeake 'cause that's what he recommended. They were a bear to open up, though. Had to resort to using pliers to squeeze them which left shell fragments inside.

### Stavity — 2012-11-01T21:58:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oyster-season.3488/post-61392>

> **Lyndra;63079 wrote:** Any tips for shucking and/or varieties of oysters that are easier to open? Our local seafood market can get 30+ varieties and last time I got Chesapeake 'cause that's what he recommended. They were a bear to open up, though. Had to resort to using pliers to squeeze them which left shell fragments inside.

I find the smaller thick one's are the easiest to open and really like the Blue Point. Your are right the Chesapeake's were very hard to open and I won't buy them again. Things I have learned. Store them with the biggest part of the shell on the bottom. When you go to shuck them, tap the bottom and make sure it is not hallow (means they died).

I use this glove a knife cut and slash resistant glove on the hand that I hold the oyster and use an oyster knife. Here is a good video on shucking oysters. [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YdLUE3fKmjM\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;YdLUE3fKmjM\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YdLUE3fKmjM\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;YdLUE3fKmjM)

# Thread: Body by Science

**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness

### Raiken3712 — 2012-09-07T08:01:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/body-by-science.3498/post-49936>

So I just got done reading The Art and Science of Low Carbohydrate Living/The Art and Science of Low Carbohydrate Performance and Body by Science. I'm revved up and ready to go...but somehow I missed the part where they tell you where to start as far as weight goes.

I read something about 80% of your max ...I don't know my max. I got into the fitness room and lets just say I didn't get anywhere close to going to positive failure. Which for those who haven't read the book means pulling or pushing the weight until you can't anymore. You keep trying for a good fifteen seconds at the end of a move like chest press and that's when you are done with that set.

If it helps I'm 6'1" 28 years old 29 on the 27th of October...I guess I could just say 29....its close enough..anyway. I've been obese for awhile and am still losing weight...I think I'm technically overweight now...not sure but I'm down to 241 from a high of 275 in recent history and my high quite awhile ago was 297. I'd really like to get some strength but need to know where to start. Also either the machines at this Recreation center suck...I think they do and/or I need to make adjustments because I couldn't seem to get in the leg press without my lower back hurting. I tried adjusting the seat and such but couldn't get it to a good point.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-07T15:00:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/body-by-science.3498/post-49959>

cool......let us know how it goes.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-07T17:00:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/body-by-science.3498/post-50000>

Raiken, about two years ago I was doing "Slow Burn" fitness, where I lifted weights in slow motion until muscle failure. Did it once a week, for 30-45 minutes. Every week I had to use bigger weights to get to the point of failure in 3-6 repetitions. It was awesome, how my muscles responded to this. It was literally 'no sweat'. I was amazed at how strong I became in just a few weeks, eating VLC Paleoish. It was explained that it is when you really try to lift the weight, but the muscle can't do it, that was when the real improvement starts.

I haven't exercised in well over a year, though. Lately I've been relying on CT to build my muscles. One of these days I'm going to try combining CT, EPI paleo, and weight lifting, when I'm through addressing hormonal issues. I think it will be fun and interesting.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-07T17:24:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/body-by-science.3498/post-50009>

I've got the book... and I've kicked it around (reading that is) and occasionally I'd like to get back into heavy lifting... but for the longest time didn't have access to a gym - now I DO - I could swing down 1x/week and do a BBS lift session (There's going to be plenty of spotters there too) I could knock it out legs, back, chest, w/ heavy weights. I may think on this. b/c I can pay $5 for a daily pass 1x/week instead of buying a whole 6mth pass.

But I'm also interested in seeing what basic convict conditioning of BW exercises done slowly in good form until failure can do for me as well strength wise...

things to think about.

### ealachan — 2012-09-07T17:34:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/body-by-science.3498/post-50010>

> **Nonchalant;50545 wrote:** It was explained that it is when you really try to lift the weight, but the muscle can't do it, that was when the real improvement starts.

In that case, wouldn't it make sense to just skip right to the part where you lift something REALLY heavy just a few times, then? That way you're entering that phase of failure much more quickly / efficiently?

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-07T18:00:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/body-by-science.3498/post-50022>

It helps to ease into it for the first 3 sessions, get your muscles the muscle memory going full throttle so you can really start to bulk up the weights. With that being said, when starting you can do it 2X a week or once every 3-4 days. If you've not done a particular movement or machine before - this is important.

Sometimes when working on your own, you just can't start right in with a 90 second maximum weight with any accuracy. . .

So just go start. Pick some weight that feels heavy, but that you can lift with good and smooth movement. You may have to do more reps when you first start.

By session 4, you will find your body knows what's happening, as well as your mind - then you can start counting seconds and cranking weight and really getting the metabolic effects.

My point - this isn't something that you just START with a perfect movement so your workout is only X mins. The important thing is to start at something comfortable but meaningfull (if that means more reps, then so be it). . work till you get the wobbly arms/legs. Then you can start to "tighten up". things AFTER your neurons and muscles get the feel for what is going to happen.

If your weight is over 200 lbds body weight is probably a good choice. . .although I find it more boring and it doesn't satisfy my like for tracking and increasing in a measured way.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-07T18:44:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/body-by-science.3498/post-50045>

Quote Originally Posted by Nigel View Post

Find a weight you can just about shift, it shouldn't take long. Then, use a weight 75-80% of that and use good form to raise and lower it taking between 10 and 12 seconds or so per rep. When you just cannot move it any more, try for just a couple of seconds and then stop. Keep breathing, I sort of hyperventilate to make sure that I do not hold my breath and strain. When I get to 7 to 9 reps, I go to the next weight up. I get jelly legs and spaghetti arms after a session. I've been doing this since the beginning of July at one session a week and have always done more reps or had to add more weight.

What does one do when the maximum weight is too small? I ask as I'll be using the maximum for my Leg Press starting next week.

Just about shift as in almost lift but not quite? So that would be a weight above my max. That's why you said 75-80 instead of 80....that makes sense. If I understand you correctly.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-07T22:55:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/body-by-science.3498/post-50106>

> **Raiken3712;50651 wrote:** [http://www.marksdailyapple.com/forum/thread65529.html#post947356](http://http://www.marksdailyapple.com/forum/thread65529.html#post947356)
>
>
>
> I posted also in this thread.
>
>
>
> Quote Originally Posted by Nigel View Post
>
> Find a weight you can just about shift, it shouldn't take long. Then, use a weight 75-80% of that and use good form to raise and lower it taking between 10 and 12 seconds or so per rep. When you just cannot move it any more, try for just a couple of seconds and then stop. Keep breathing, I sort of hyperventilate to make sure that I do not hold my breath and strain. When I get to 7 to 9 reps, I go to the next weight up. I get jelly legs and spaghetti arms after a session. I've been doing this since the beginning of July at one session a week and have always done more reps or had to add more weight.
>
>
>
> What does one do when the maximum weight is too small? I ask as I'll be using the maximum for my Leg Press starting next week.
>
> Just about shift as in almost lift but not quite? So that would be a weight above my max. That's why you said 75-80 instead of 80....that makes sense. If I understand you correctly.

You can switch to single leg on the leg press at ~50% of current weight. (helps to keep your working leg relatively centered under your hips).

Sweet spot is 90 seconds to failure. You measure progress by energy expended, not by weight or reps.

Time under load, not reps is what matters in this style of training. Once you get over 90 you up the weight. Your tempo may be 10 seconds, or 7, or 13, the key for tempo is that you want smooth action without stick points.

You could use this method with isometric loading (non moving) as well as long as you are going long enough to exhaust all muscle fibers, including the fight/flight fast twitch fibers. This is how bio-density training works, though you feel odd just pushing against a stationary object. It's certainly more fun to move.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-09-07T23:00:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/body-by-science.3498/post-50109>

[http://s1239.photobucket.com/albums/ff518/Raiken3712/](http://http://s1239.photobucket.com/albums/ff518/Raiken3712/)

These photos are recent. If that helps determine my starting level at all or any advice you might have for me.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-07T23:35:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/body-by-science.3498/post-50116>

> **Raiken3712;50655 wrote:** [http://s1239.photobucket.com/albums/ff518/Raiken3712/](http://http://s1239.photobucket.com/albums/ff518/Raiken3712/)
>
>
>
> These photos are recent. If that helps determine my starting level at all or any advice you might have for me.

Hard to judge strength by pics, as size does not dictate work capacity. Some body builders are much much weaker than they look, and some small guys are deceptively strong.

Try a weight you can manage, lift at a non HIT tempo as many times as you can and plug it in here.

[http://www.timinvermont.com/fitness/orm.htm](http://http://www.timinvermont.com/fitness/orm.htm)

I'd recommend starting with 65% of that value for the first HIT session. You will fail at some point, it is not imperative to be within a certain time.

Ensure you are set up properly (joint of machine action is centered on your joint of action).

Try to resist the fight/flight temptation to stop (it can be overwhelming). You might start shaking, remember to breathe. Know that at that point you are too weak to hurt yourself and go as hard as you can. Be careful standing up after the leg press, as you have replaced them with two bags of jello.

From there, it's interpolation/extrapolation, or trial and error. If you do say, 250lbs on the leg press and you fail at 3 minutes there is too little load.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-09-18T07:25:33+0300

I'm obviously not very good with doing new things....

> Electricity for the centrifuge was provided by a gasoline-powered generating set.
>
>
> Interesting.
>
>
>
> I can't get the paper "Apparent absence of stroke etc." (Also Lindberg) to display in this browser. But Google seems to be finding the following fragment in it:
>
>
>
> Electricity, telephones and motor vehicles are absent.

### Michael — 2012-09-07T21:08:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-it-the-food.3502/post-50073>

BG11 was quite something.

You of all people - someone who's said that standard paleo is not enough \*and\* been able to substantiate that - now turn round and say light is more important.

I thought I knew light mattered. I'd read "Lights Out" and all that. But I guess "Lights Out" is 5 to 10 years out of date.

Now we get that about the extra vision centre and the 48%. And just the name of the diabetes drug in itself. And most of the doctors prescribing it probably don't know how it works and why! Mindblowing! I think the Cockneys would say when they named that drug they were "having a larf." They call it that and no-one notices. And if the doctors were only half-awake they'd maybe prescribe that in the short-term but draw the obvious conclusion and just tell their patients to get some sleep.

BG12? Can't wait.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-test-to-get-question-for-the-masters-here.3503/post-50029>

There are 2 main companies that provide salivary cortisol tests -> ZRT and Diagnostechs.

The ASI is offered by Diagnostechs; see link below. I believe the confusion is similar to the way the terms Kleenex or Xerox are used .... multiple companies make similar products, but one name is used most often (even though another company has actual rights to the name used).

# Thread: Brazil needs to read BG-11
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brazil-needs-to-read-bg-11.3508/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-09-07T19:53:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brazil-needs-to-read-bg-11.3508/post-50060>

[http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-09-body-conscious-brazil-overweight.html](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-09-body-conscious-brazil-overweight.html)

Kind of sad story from Brazil. I don't think it's just 'beans and fat' that's the problem. I'd be willing to be that moving 30 million people from poverty to middle class involved electricity, lighting, TVs, computers, and IPads.

"As a result of an unbalanced diet and a rise in more sedentary lifestyles, Brazil, which moved 30 million people out of poverty and into the middle class over the past decade, is paying a heavy price for its frenetic growth.

"Brazilians have a poor diet. They could eat green vegetables but they prefer beans and fat," said Solange de Goncalves, a 38-year-old from Rio who weighs 123 kilos (271 pounds) and is among Brazil's 30 million obese citizens."

### Michael — 2012-09-10T19:26:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brazil-needs-to-read-bg-11.3508/post-50742>

> **AKMan;50606 wrote:
>
> "Brazilians ... could eat green vegetables but they prefer beans and fat," said Solange de Goncalves ... who weighs ... 271 pounds ...
>
>
> Right.
>
>
>
>
> > ... and is among Brazil's 30 million obese citizens."
>
>
>
> Really? I'd never have guessed he was.
>
>
>
> I'm not sure how he thinks anyone could live on green vegetables alone, but there we are.
>
>
>
>
>
> That's an interesting comment, AKMan. Maybe a carbohydrate-heavy diet _and_ electric light is just too much.
>
>
>
> There's an interview of Gary Taubes by Jimmy moore, and Jimmy says to him that people say that there's these people in the Trobiand Islands called the Kitavans and they eat x% of their total calorie intake in the form of carbohydrate but are not fat. Gary responds, "Do they have sugar?"
>
>
>
> Maybe Gary should have asked, but didn't know to ask, "Do they have electricity?"
>
>
>
> Start googling "Kitava electricity" and a few things pop up.
>
>
>
> She \[a woman who was sent to live with the Kitavans by a TV company\] said she didn't miss creature comforts, including electricity**, as her life simplified.

Mexicans may have more refined carbs now than in the past, and that may be playing a part. If it's possible it would be interesting to track a few parameters, including waistlines, in Mexico past and present and see how they fit in with electrification.

# Thread: Why are MHS and raw fish good for us?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-are-mhs-and-raw-fish-good-for-us.3513/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 49
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### indigogirl — 2012-09-07T22:19:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-are-mhs-and-raw-fish-good-for-us.3513/post-50088>

I really have an aversion to eating raw fish or meat. I like my meat well done!

Why is raw seafood so good for us? Maybe if I understand this I can make myself eat it.

Just thinking of how a MHS must smell makes my stomach turn : ) .

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-07T22:24:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-are-mhs-and-raw-fish-good-for-us.3513/post-50096>

Well BG 12-16 should clear that all up. It also may explain why you feel like crap all the time. Your aversion is your speed bump to optimal.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-07T22:34:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-are-mhs-and-raw-fish-good-for-us.3513/post-50100>

Indigo, you may want to try the MHS before you condemn it. Raw fish smells much less than cooked fish does. In fact, I'm getting to where I prefer it. And my youngest child (who doesn't like the smell/taste of fish, he says) will eat raw fish in sushi and ceviche any day of the week. Once you cook it you begin to degrade the DHA (and I'm guessing the plethora of bacteria that are good for our gut, but this is just a guess). I'm not saying that MHS tastes yummy, but it tastes much more like sea water than it does like fish. (I think with the meat, the issue is keeping as many of the enzymes alive as possible). Good luck.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-07T23:34:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-are-mhs-and-raw-fish-good-for-us.3513/post-50115>

> **indigogirl;50634 wrote:** I really have an aversion to eating raw fish or meat. I like my meat well done!
>
> Why is raw seafood so good for us? Maybe if I understand this I can make myself eat it.
>
> Just thinking of how a MHS must smell makes my stomach turn : ) .

Indigo, I was in your shoes earlier this year. I'd have seafood maybe once a month. All that has changed, and the secret is that I didn't overcome any aversion. I just started eating the seafood that I didn't mind eating, just more often. Sardines, tuna, occasional salmon. And I found I didn't mind eating it more often, after all. My attitude had changed. Then I started developing an interest in it, and branching out with different shellfish and other seafood. I found out that the "Chilled Seafood Salad" I had been enjoying so much at a local seafood restaurant was actually ceviche. I had been eating 'raw' seafood and didn't know it.

I found that I was looking forward to seafood. I gladly went on the 30 day seafood challenge because I was liking it so much.

I didn't overcome any aversion. I think 2 things happened. My brain started getting a whiff of DHA, and started changing my mind about fish. And my gut bacteria mix changed, and now it's clammoring for more seafood. So I'm just going along for the ride. It's a joyride, actually.

The MHS I had may have accelerated this process. Not sure about that.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-08T00:11:40+0300

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-08T01:27:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-are-mhs-and-raw-fish-good-for-us.3513/post-50132>

> **indigogirl;50659 wrote:** I've got a decent blender-a cuisinart.
>
>
>
> Have you eaten a MHS ealachan?

You don't need a blender. I've done it without one (just a semi-decent food processor). But I recommend a blender for a more smooth smoothie. The eyes are the easy bit. I've eaten them like raw oysters (they taste of nothing if they're fresh... just don't chew ). I'd start with small, frozen fish (I often use fresh, wild caught sardines). That way the bones etc are very small and blend thoroughly. Though as Nonchalant says, you can start anywhere. The amazing thing is that as you make changes, you change. My advice would be to start with the freshest raw fish you can find. It should smell slightly of the sea. The flesh should be moist looking and shiny, the eyes bright, not dull. It will not smell 'fishy.' Then either just eat it raw (I often just roll it in some nori with avocado) or make it into ceviche. Then you can progress to whole fish, heads and all. I promise that once you start you'll get both the kick of DHA and the boost of having done something you thought impossible. Once you do one impossible thing, you start to realise that maybe nothing actually is!!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-08T05:35:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-are-mhs-and-raw-fish-good-for-us.3513/post-50192>

> **indigogirl;50725 wrote:** Thanks for the info Lauren. I live right near the ocean so fresh fish should be easy to find. You're not worried about parasites in the fish?

Well, I have yet to see live, wiggling worms in mine like Inger has (but she's eaten it anyway). Am I worried? Sort of yes, but sort of no. I definitely only make the raw smoothies with fish that's been frozen first, so I'm hoping that is helpful. Though I eat raw roe daily and it is often not frozen first. I think I've just jumped in with both boots and I can't spend too much energy worrying about the 'what if's.' Who knows, maybe the Epcot Rx contains raw worms?  So far, so good anyway. By the way, I don't even hold my nose. (And, in case you missed the suggested add-in's, lemon juice really helps! I also put a bit of salt and sometimes some chlorella.) It's a bit like the ice bath... not nearly as bad as you think. (I was doing ocean CT the other day and I started wondering what I'd do if I saw a fish swim by. Would I have the nerve to pluck it from the water and bite into it?)

### vlynnb — 2012-09-10T01:52:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-are-mhs-and-raw-fish-good-for-us.3513/post-50594>

Well, after taking the seafood challenge I have eaten fish (that I didn't particularly like) for 2 meals a day for six weeks. Now when eating beef for breakfast, which I used to prefer, I find myself wishing that it was fish. i have also found I'd rather go to a seafood counter and grab a quarter pound of their shrimp than spend time looking for something healthy to eat at a restaurant when I am trapped away from home for lunch. However the fresh fish at the sea food counters here is almost always pre-frozen and not fresh! I have been buying frozen, label wild caught, cod. scallops, shrimp, and salmon. But now I notice that it is a product of China and that makes me cringe. Is it really wild? I just don't trust that claim.

Do you think it is still a better choice than grass fed beef which I could raise in my own back yard, well back field? At least I would know that it was as optimal as grass fed beef can be if I raised it myself.

### Inger — 2012-10-18T21:34:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-are-mhs-and-raw-fish-good-for-us.3513/post-59000>

Anna, I have not much money. And so I figured it would be a shame to throw the mackerel heads away. The mackerels are quite expensive here and I get them only some times in the year. From other heads I make broth but you cannot do that from fatty fish. So I figured I'll make a smoothie then and drink it.. like food. Nothing wasted. I did it once.. and got addicted.. makes me feel so good. No I do it with other fish heads too.

I have come to realize, to be poor can be the biggest blessing ever. That is the times when I found great stuff to improve my well beeing! Like wild edibles.. like wild berries.. mushrooms.. all for free/or much cheaper to buy then exotic fruits etc.. If I had had lots of money I would never realized them I guess but still today be eating lots and lots of expensive exotic fruits year round.

Just the opposite what many thinks.. you need to be rich to eat / live healthy. I thought so before too, until I got backed up in a corner and needed to think all new because I was worried for my health but yeah.. had no money at all.

What something tasted like? It had to go. How I felt become the important thing. Hard reality.

But oh so sweet in the end.

You know I am very weak. Very weak health. I must be very strict if I eat just something not so optimal I suffer. Like this week I ate the cold smoked raw salmone with fresh dill and mayonnaise at work. It was sooo good. But made me feel terrible. Tired, heavy, terrible. The light, happy, beautiful feeling was no more there!!! And only because of some mayo..??! Everything else was super food these days, MHS, raw heart... But the mayo destroyed it. I tested it for days and I know it was that. Today I had no.. and wow I felt so good the whole day, I did not get tired at all. But took me some days to realize how bad the small amount mayo did me.

One might think, only raw cold-smoked salmon, fresh dill, mayo. A perfect paleo meal!

Not for me.

You see now how weak I am. When I eat my fishheadsmoothies I feel so good so very good. Or my raw lambs hearts or liver. Or any fish without mayo or other no good stuff in it. I mostly eat my fish just gently cooked with curcumin and salt or then raw as tartar. So satisfying to me. Easy, nothing fancy but so good!

I am not buying much vegetables eather or salad anymore. Very seldom. I eat wild edibles or seaweeds. From wild edibles I need just a tiny amount here and there and I feel great. They are all for free!

Just organic onions and ginger.. that's about it..lol. Wild foods are almost magic to me for sure.

### LinD — 2012-11-09T19:34:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-are-mhs-and-raw-fish-good-for-us.3513/post-63165>

> **Jack Kruse;50642 wrote:** Well BG 12-16 should clear that all up. It also may explain why you feel like crap all the time. Your aversion is your speed bump to optimal.

Someone (Leanne Ely) must be reading here, too!:

[https://savingdinner.com/articles/holy-mackerel/](http://https://savingdinner.com/articles/holy-mackerel/)

We know that we should be adding fish to our diets for optimal health, but which fish should you be reaching for?

It’s hard to go wrong as long as it’s wild fish caught in a sustainable, responsible manner, but one of the healthiest fishes you can buy is mackerel, a fatty deep water fish with a very firm flesh and a strong flavor.

Mackerel is easy to cook, it tastes great and it’s very good for you.

Let’s look at some of the health benefits of this delicious fish:

Omega-3. Mackerel is famous for being a very oily fish. It’s very high in those omega-3 fatty acids that we all should be eating more of.

Heart health. Mackerel can actually improve the condition of the heart. It lowers blood pressure and can prevent stroke.

Low in calories. A 3-ounce serving of this firm fish contains roughly 230 calories (providing it’s been cooked without added butter or sauces), which makes a fillet lower in calories than other meats.

High in protein. There are 21 grams of protein in that 3-ounce serving of mackerel and like other fishes, it contains no carbs, making it a wonderful item to add to your dinner plate.

Minerals. Mackerel contains calcium, potassium, phosphorous, magnesium and selenium. It’s a good source of folate, choline, Niacin, Vitamins A, D, K, B12 and C.

Mackerel lowers cancer-causing agents in the body, improves our immune systems and is great for brain health. Mackerel can help ease symptoms of arthritis, arthrosis and even migraine pain. Holy mackerel!

Now, one thing to be aware of when purchasing mackerel is that it is a deep water fish and depending on where it came from it may contain mercury. Pregnant women should not eat mackerel or any food that may contain mercury because it may harm the developing baby’s nervous system.

To cook mackerel, simply pan fry it at medium heat until it flakes with a fork!

Have you ever tried mackerel?

[http://www.facebook.com/savingdinner](http://http://www.facebook.com/savingdinner)

### AKMan — 2012-09-07T23:00:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whats-the-latest-i-should-drink-coffee.3514/post-50108>

I've never tested my Adrenal Stress Index, [http://www.pureprescriptions.com/adrenal-stress-index-asi-lab-services/p1073](http://http://www.pureprescriptions.com/adrenal-stress-index-asi-lab-services/p1073), maybe should. But my thoughts on coffee are that it helps prevent the absorption of iron [http://ajcn.nutrition.org/content/37/3/416.abstract](http://http://ajcn.nutrition.org/content/37/3/416.abstract), so I drink coffee heartily when I'm eating seafood, which is every day. I quit drinking it around 2pm as if I have any later, it will keep me up at night. Your mileage may vary. Also read: [http://www.livestrong.com/article/284339-foods-that-inhibit-iron-absorption/](http://http://www.livestrong.com/article/284339-foods-that-inhibit-iron-absorption/)

> **kathylu;50845 wrote:** If your ferritin level is high, donate blood. Quick fix.

It was just at top of range. Nobody wants my blood...I spent 95-99 in UK (mad cow years) and took several rounds of anti-malarials on trips to Zambia, plus spent considerable time in Iraq. Screen-out questions for blood donation, unfortunately. My iron was always spot-on until last labs, I think I got it handled now. Eggs also block absorption of Fe.

In fact, I hit about all their screen-outs:

Wait 12 months after travel in an area where malaria is found. Wait 3 years after living in a country or countries where malaria is found.

Wait 12 months after travel to Iraq. This requirement is related to concerns about Leishmanaisis.

Persons who have spent long periods of time in countries where "mad cow disease" is found are not eligible to donate. This requirement is related to concerns about variant Creutzfeld Jacob Disease (vCJD).

Persons who were born or lived in certain countries in Western Africa, or who have had close contact with persons who were born in or who lived in certain West African countries are not eligible to donate. This requirement is related to concerns about HIV Group O. Learn more about HIV Group O, and the specific African countries where it is found

### JanSz — 2012-09-09T18:07:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whats-the-latest-i-should-drink-coffee.3514/post-50503>

> **AKMan;50841 wrote:** My iron levels were high, I think from the recent addition of oysters in my diet.

What is your

Hgb---hemoglobin

Hct----hematocrit

RBC---red blood count

EPO---erythropoietin

If any of above is close to the top range, be on alert

If any of above is over the range, slightly, be on alert more

At certain point you have to remove pint of blood, hell or high water.

Go to Emergency room.

Recheck 2 weeks or month latter, figure out your situation (how fast Hgb raises) and mach that with frequency of checking for Hgb.

Usually the top three, specially Hgb & Hct go together, so it is enough to check Hgb.

Hgb is easier to check (at blood donation places), it works real similar to how one may check for glucose level with pin prick to finger then testing drop of blood.

\-----------------------

Other than oysters,

liver is high in iron

Supplemental testosterone may increase Hgb.

Genetically, if you are of european lineage, specially Celtic, Irish, chances are higher for this type of problems.

\----------------------

Get script for therapeutic phlebotomies (if Hgb is high).

Script for therapeutic phlebotomy

AKMan Doe DOB-15/11/1983

elevated Hgb levels

check once a month,\*

perform therapeutic phlebotomy

if Hgb>=15.8

repeat 12 months

\------------------------

The 15.8 I put there is for your comfort.

HGB g/dL\*

MALES: 13.2-17.1\*

FEMALES: 11.7-15.5\*

You want to check Hgb first, then it is your choice if you will give blood or not.

Unles Hgb is trending up, 15.8 is no reason to drop blood.

Some say that Hgb=16 is ideal value (for men, not sure about women)

\---------------------------------

There is this thing about

EPO---erythropoietin

Lance Armstrong is accused of benefiting by manipulating it.

It increases Hgb and the other indicators (and performance).

This is another way of saying that above laboratory ranges are not really set in stone.

Laboratory ranges are really statistical averages of nearby population. That population is often of questionable health.

I wish dr Kruse was willing to tackle this subject.

But with Lance Armstrong's history, this may be touchy subject.

So bottom line, higher values (to a point) are beneficial.

..............

### Stavity — 2012-09-09T19:08:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whats-the-latest-i-should-drink-coffee.3514/post-50514>

@JanSZ ==I think hs cRP needs to be factored in also. Mine was 0.7 which is just okay from my understanding. In my case all 4 red blood count cell, hemoglobin, hemoglobin Alc and hematocrit were high. I still, without further, testing can't just get my blood taken out. I can guarantee all this may have changed since being on the Epi-paleo diet. None of these changes would have taken place with just the paleo diet and I would have stayed a mess. I'm just saying, I am finally feeling like I am getting somewhere, not even close to being comfortable, and comfortable for me is not an option.

I am eating the seafood, kevita drinks, and bubbies pickles. I can't do any sulfur veggies at this time. Not sure what else I can do. I want it fixed fast. ### kathylu — 2012-09-08T02:00:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-diet-to-fix-gut-flora.3515/post-50139>

Supplemental DHEA (if you are low), l-glutamine, address any dysbiosis such as candida or parasites, lots of bone broth. Let your bubbies pickles sit out at room temp for 3 days to get cloudy and fizzy (lots more bacteria that way!), and drink the juice...water it down if too salty. Lots of garlic in everything.

### kathylu — 2012-09-08T04:31:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-diet-to-fix-gut-flora.3515/post-50177>

> **Cavemam;50694 wrote:** What up with the garlic? This is the First I've heard of it! I love the stuff! Can it be cooked?

It's great for clearing candida colonies. It destabilizes candida biofilm matrix and hyphae formation (in english: garlic keeps candida from forming sticky gunk to hide in and grow those long legs that pierce your gut wall). [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC538912/](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC538912/)

"Garlic extract can prevent biofilm development by Staphylococcus aureus, Staphylococcus epidermidis, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Acinetobacter baumannii and Klebsiella pneumoniae, and caused a 2–5 log reduction of the bioburden within Enterococcus faecalis biofilms. Additionally, garlic extract disrupted partially developed biofilms produced by S. aureus, S. epidermidis and A. baumannii."

It also tastes great.

Raw is definitely better, but many benefits are retained when lightly cooked. However, don't microwave...it totally inactivates allicin in garlic. I lacto ferment raw garlic cloves, then add them to salads and use for cooking. They keep for months. Pretty strong garlic taste though, but I like it.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-09T04:26:33+0300

I lacto ferment raw garlic cloves, then add them to salads and use for cooking. They keep for months. Pretty strong garlic taste though, but I like it.

Thanks! Haven't owned a microwave in 15 years... and will never again. I've been instinctively eating raw garlic daily. I'm so pleased to know it has good reasons and good effects. Thanks again.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-09T04:32:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-diet-to-fix-gut-flora.3515/post-50403>

When I was growing up and would get a cold my mom would serve us raw garlic sandwiches, due to its proposed antibiotic-like qualities. These were made with sliced garlic, mayo lettuce and tomato and the ever-famous wheatberry bread. They actually tasted great! The downside for mom and upside for us kids was that we'd get the next day off from school because the smell of garlic would be coming out of your pores!!!

### kathylu — 2012-09-09T06:13:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-diet-to-fix-gut-flora.3515/post-50441>

> **hope2conceive;50908 wrote:** kathylu, do you have a recipe for the fermented garlic?

It's easy. It's just like you make any lacto fermented "pickle". Peel a bunch of garlic cloves, enough to fill a pint glass jar with a tight fitting lid. Or you can cheat and buy the pre-peeled cloves! If you really want to keep friends and neighbors away, put in some sprigs of fresh oregano and a few cayenne peppers. Make a brine, I use 1 tbsp of celtic sea salt per quart of liquid, add 2-4 tbsp of whey (drain whey through cheesecloth from yogurt) to get starter lactobacilli in the mix. If you don't use whey, double the salt. Let ferment at room temperature for 3-7 days, until bubbly and slightly cloudy and sour. Loosen cap once per day, then re-tighten. Refrigerate and use for whatever garlic is good in. If you don't mind the strong flavor, eat some raw, it's good for you.

### Eve — 2012-09-15T16:32:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-diet-to-fix-gut-flora.3515/post-51805>

What is the reason for avoiding ferments if you have Hashi's? I missed the boat on this one, I think.

### PaleoDentist — 2012-09-08T06:13:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-question.3519/post-50196>

I asked my doc to test my DHEA levels, she complied.

I have been supplementing with DHEA fo rthe past 3 months.

I increased my dose form from 15 mg to 50 mg incrementally over these months. I am a 42 y/o male

Dr K stated that ideal is between 500-640 ng/dL

my test results were 761 ng/dL the range is 61-1636 ng/dL

should I continue with 50mg dosing or increase it? as my levels ar a little under 1/2 of the top range.

why would 500-640 be ideal when the top range is 1636?

am I missing something???

Normal: - Ideal:

Women 65-380 μ/dL - 250-380 μ/dL

### JanSz — 2012-09-08T21:08:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-question.3519/post-50296>

> **PaleoDentist;50745 wrote:** I asked my doc to test my DHEA levels, she complied.
