I appreciate your response Lauren. I always enjoy your posts!

I think you've really hit on something for me - I suppose I do need to learn to make myself more of a priority. I feel like I do this in some ways and maybe I slack in other ways. It's tough to get past the guilt - of not being there for every one of my children's performances, for not being the one to take them to all of their activities, for letting down 12 teenage girls who have come to depend on me (some of which see me as the only stable figure in their life). These are the things I feel obligated not to fail on and I think a lot of that is because I couldn't do many of these things 2 years ago. I was too ill to go out and I missed a lot and now that I finally can, I want to embrace it and cherish it all.

I love that you were able to make arrangements that worked for you while allowing your children to still lead their normal lives. I've been thinking about how I can work that out all day and spoke to a few parents. We are going to work out some carpooling, but I just don't have it in me to not take the kids at all, so I'll be taking them about once every 2-3 weeks . . . not so bad. I need to give some more thought to the small group I lead for junior high girls, but even that is only one night a week and the more I think about it, the more I feel like I'm going to be getting so much out of it that just maybe it's worth dealing with some fake light one night a week. Maybe in this case the benefits will outweigh any damages.

Thanks again for your encouragement!

### LinD — 2012-09-13T02:58:24+0300

Blessings.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-13T07:41:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-such-a-thing-as-a-healthy-balance.3616/post-51389>

> **BJK77;51896 wrote:**
>
> I think you've really hit on something for me - I suppose I do need to learn to make myself more of a priority. I feel like I do this in some ways and maybe I slack in other ways. It's tough to get past the guilt - of not being there for every one of my children's performances, for not being the one to take them to all of their activities, for letting down 12 teenage girls who have come to depend on me (some of which see me as the only stable figure in their life). These are the things I feel obligated not to fail on and I think a lot of that is because I couldn't do many of these things 2 years ago. I was too ill to go out and I missed a lot and now that I finally can, I want to embrace it and cherish it all.

Honestly, I believe that what my 'taking care of myself' looks like will be different than yours, even if we both follow the general precepts of this forum. When I refuse to go out at night and stay home to sit by candlelight and do 'nothing' it looks totally selfish (and, to many, totally nuts). But, in fact, this is as much for my children as it is for me. I want to be there for them in the future. I also want to be there for them now. So it's a fine balance. There are no 'rules'. I do what feels right and I do what I feel is important. That can change from day to day.

What I have learned is not to react from a place of fear. The outcome can look the same (eg taking daily ice baths) but the rationale behind it can be radically different ("I am afraid I will get sick again if I don't do this" vs "I believe in this and I do this for my health, both now and in the future.") I think there is a balance here with everything (which is not the same as moderation, by the way). The balance is being able to embrace now with keeping an eye toward future goals; the balance is about being proactive and committed while also letting go of fear. Part of this is realising that we don't know what tomorrow brings.

As Hafiz wrote long ago:

\[h=1\]Fear is the cheapest room in the house.

I would like to see you living

In better conditions.\[/h\]I am no one's role model. I had a cancer diagnosis. Everyone knows this is a wake up call. But to actually get this call is very different than just knowing about it. Strangely, you'd think that a life-threatening illness could push you into a life of fear (and, for many, it can). For me, it freed me. To take care of myself, to live passionately, to not tolerate bulls\*\*t, to pursue what I believe is right and to drop everything that is unneeded. I love my life and I am finally learning to act like it!

Take care,

Lauren

### vkiernan — 2012-09-13T15:03:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-such-a-thing-as-a-healthy-balance.3616/post-51408>

> **Jack Kruse;51952 wrote:** Pressure? Nonchalant........time is not promised to you. Every day you waste is a day you will never recover in the road to optimal. Time is our most valuable asset. If your thoughts of beliefs subjugate you......who really pays? You do.........swim in that pool for a period of time. Embrace your discomfort. It is the best that you may ever get.

This is what I need to put all over my house. This is probably one of the best things you have ever written that speaks to me. Thinking about embracing our discomfort cuz that is as good as it will probably get is a real wake up call. I don't want to live in my discomfort for the rest of my life! And to think it could get worse is not really where I want to go. I watched my mother and brother die literally for years and then saw them both pass when their day came. I took it to heart let's say and never wanted to be like them and die like that. That has been my mantra and unfortunately, I have not completely lived up to that. I "say" I want to be healthy but my actions speak louder and overrule what I should be doing. I think that by eating cake, my life will somehow be enriched. When you say I can't eat cake anymore, feels like a big part of me is gone. This is where my thinking is killing me. I have to change it. Thank you Jack for always being honest with us. Right now my gratitude is unmeasurable to you! I would be so much worse than I am right now had I not found you. I have a goal and it is up to me to fulfill it. You HAVE led me down the right path; now I need to take the lead and do what is right for me and I might not struggle so bad mentally if I do. Perhaps as soon as I get the fat to the brain, this road will be a little straighter for me. Workin' on it!

### Dali Dula — 2012-09-13T15:53:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-such-a-thing-as-a-healthy-balance.3616/post-51415>

> **BJK77;51816 wrote:** I find myself seriously stressing out lately over not being absolutely perfect with CT and minding the light cycle. Thankfully diet is not an issue for me at all. Ever since I started on a real foods diet over two years ago when I began GAPS I can honestly say I havenâ€™t cheated once. Following the light cycle is proving to be a bit more difficult for me though and Iâ€™m afraid Iâ€™m so used to striving for perfection that every little failure is stressing me out to the point of detrimental health.
>
>
>
> When I believe in something I go â€œhog wildâ€ with it. In my mind, there is no point in putting time and effort into anything unless youâ€™re going to give it all youâ€™ve got. I still wholeheartedly believe in that, but Iâ€™m beginning to wonder if itâ€™s possible to achieve some sort of healthy balance. I agree with Jack that moderation is not optimal and I donâ€™t want to shoot for mediocrity, but I donâ€™t know how to live life in this day and age and stick 100% by the light cycle and get in my CT. Iâ€™ve been doing a 30-minute cold bath every morning before work, but had to skip a day this week and a day last week because I had to go out of town and had to leave the house much earlier than normal. >
>

We actually have been doing really well the last 2 weeks with implementing candles at sunset, no fake light/electronics, bedtime soon after, etc, etc. BUT with school just starting and my kids having activities they go to in the evenings and winter juts around the corner I donâ€™t know how weâ€™ll be able to continue that. Do I tell the kids they canâ€™t participate in any activities? They do things that help them grow and enjoy life in other ways so I donâ€™t think thatâ€™s an option. My son does karate and just started cub scouts. My daughter does a weekly book club, dance and participates in a youth small group that I actually lead. These are all worthwhile and enriching activities for them. For the most part theyâ€™re home by 7:30-8:00, so itâ€™s not even super late, but of course theyâ€™re going to be exposed to fluorescent lights the whole time theyâ€™re away from home.
>
>
>
> I know my hormones are off now, so that could explain my needing to vent and my total stressing out here  I guess Iâ€™m just curious how others plan to manage it. If it were just me, I think I could easily give it all up, but I canâ€™t do that to the kids. I want them to be healthy, but I want them to enjoy life too. Make sense? Not to mention my daughter is at a really critical point socially. She just started middle school and I think itâ€™s a tremendous help to make new friends who share similar interests. There has to be a balance somewhere, right?

I know there are other people doing CT more aggressively, but your CT regimen is admirable and I am sure it is of great benefit. The stress you are feeling ,however, is not beneficial. As I have progressed on this optimal path I have only recently begun to recognize stress. As I recognize it I try to find ways to deal with it. One way is to recognize a stressor and eliminate it. Stress is often the opposition of percieved needs ie. the need to do CT or the need to be somewhere else. Make your decision as to what is more important and do that. Lately even when I think the thing I need to do is more important and for some reason I don't want to do it (usually because it is something stressful) I don't do it and see what happens. It all usually works out and I have left a little more stress behind. As you eliminate even small stressors it creates more calm for you to handle other stressors.

I am an empty-nester and I know that implementing these changes with young children and a family must be incredibly difficult. The positive side is whatever your children will get from this will benefit them for life. The difficulty now is an investment in their future.

So as an exercise identify something that causes stress in your life that you can do without and eliminate it. It feels good to look back weeks later and say "Why did I let that bother me? Done"

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-13T17:25:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-such-a-thing-as-a-healthy-balance.3616/post-51438>

In my experience life is a balance of what you have vs what you want/need. unfortunately in today's society we've confused want/need. There is a gaping emptiness in our daily efforts to achieve what? a better life/car/iphone etc? The reality is we've forgotten what it means to feed the need of the internal spirit/intuition. I'm not talking spirituality or religious belief here. I'm talking about the challenge to grow within your own personal boundaries. Its about stepping outside your personal comfort zone and embracing something bigger than your own ego or fear. Its doing something entirely selfish in pursuit of your own gain. yes, others benefit from your growth but only you get enrichment from it. Only you own the direction. stepping out on that edge, taking the risk of being different, pursuing something others don't understand - its a scary thing. YOU HAVE TO OWN IT. Only then do you grow.

Change is what makes us better, as individuals, as parents, as children, as members of our community. Yet we've forgotten how important change is to the survival as a species. Who we are today is not the same as we were 5, 10, 15, or 20 yrs ago - nor will we be the same in the next 6mths. When we stop embracing change and the fear that sometimes goes with it - we stagnate.

I can't tell you how to balance the demands of your life. hell there are days I can't balance the demands of my life. I'm not perfect nor will I ever be. And there are days where I fail. that failure makes me a better individual. embrace the failure, learn from it, then step back out of the comfort zone. I can't be everything to every one. I can only be me at a given moment. and yes there are things I can be better at - but they're not a priority to me at this point.

My priority is fixing my health. Am I 100% on everything Jack has laid out - no. But I give 110% in the things I can right now. I'm 100% on board w/diet and managing artificial light, so are the kids - they've actually gotten to the point - they now demand to eat in the dark at night. They don't want the light (ds now throws tantrums when the light is on in the kitchen and will refuse to eat dinner unless its turned off) DH - he's not quite there yet. he's not comfortable eating in low light. getting to optimal will never be the same for any of us. we each have our own paths to chart. CT has fallen off b/c ICE has been hard to find at a reasonable price.

you have to find what works for you in your situation. you also have to own what works. Jack can't do that for you, and neither can anyone else. when you finally take ownership of everything you want in your life - you'll be surprise how peaceful your soul is - even in the midst of chaos. its a beautiful thing. the trees will become clearer, the skies will be brilliant, and the smells will permeate your existence. The rat race of making ends meet won't be as stressful, nor will figuring out how to make everything work. it will all fall into place in the right order at the exact moment its supposed to, and no sooner. That doesn't mean its going to be easy nothing worthwhile ever is.

Ponder where you are at today, and where you want to go. Identify what's going to get you there, including the challenges/risks/fears. You don't have to work on all of them at the same time. pick one and go w/it. Own it until it becomes a part of you. then move to the next thing. right now you may not be able to everything you want to do, but you can't build the house without a proper foundation. Build your foundation. then continue to build until you reach your pinnacle

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-09-13T19:03:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-such-a-thing-as-a-healthy-balance.3616/post-51454>

> **BJK77;51896 wrote:** I appreciate your response Lauren. I always enjoy your posts!
>
>
>
> I think you've really hit on something for me - I suppose I do need to learn to make myself more of a priority. I feel like I do this in some ways and maybe I slack in other ways. It's tough to get past the guilt - of not being there for every one of my children's performances, for not being the one to take them to all of their activities, for letting down 12 teenage girls who have come to depend on me (some of which see me as the only stable figure in their life). These are the things I feel obligated not to fail on and I think a lot of that is because I couldn't do many of these things 2 years ago. I was too ill to go out and I missed a lot and now that I finally can, I want to embrace it and cherish it all.
>
>
>
> I love that you were able to make arrangements that worked for you while allowing your children to still lead their normal lives. I've been thinking about how I can work that out all day and spoke to a few parents. We are going to work out some carpooling, but I just don't have it in me to not take the kids at all, so I'll be taking them about once every 2-3 weeks . . . not so bad. I need to give some more thought to the small group I lead for junior high girls, but even that is only one night a week and the more I think about it, the more I feel like I'm going to be getting so much out of it that just maybe it's worth dealing with some fake light one night a week. Maybe in this case the benefits will outweigh any damages.
>
>
>
> Thanks again for your encouragement!

...and remember you can wear orange lenses at the meeting too. You can just tell them you suffer from migraines so you need the glasses to help with that. Like Shijin does, she gets prescription (well for her, no Rx...clear lens) but the orange lens from her eye dr. so that you can still get the stylish frame and not look like a freak with UVEX or anything. Then...with the teenage girls, one of them might have a migraine problem that she never spoke of..and b/c you had your blue blockers, you end up helping her out.

There are times I have no choice for fake lights at night (thankfully not that often) and I wear the blue blockers to minimize damage.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-09-13T19:08:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-such-a-thing-as-a-healthy-balance.3616/post-51456>

> **Dali Dula;52003 wrote:** I know there are other people doing CT more aggressively, but your CT regimen is admirable and I am sure it is of great benefit. The stress you are feeling ,however, is not beneficial. As I have progressed on this optimal path I have only recently begun to recognize stress. As I recognize it I try to find ways to deal with it. One way is to recognize a stressor and eliminate it. Stress is often the opposition of percieved needs ie. the need to do CT or the need to be somewhere else. Make your decision as to what is more important and do that. Lately even when I think the thing I need to do is more important and for some reason I don't want to do it (usually because it is something stressful) I don't do it and see what happens. It all usually works out and I have left a little more stress behind. As you eliminate even small stressors it creates more calm for you to handle other stressors.
>
> I am an empty-nester and I know that implementing these changes with young children and a family must be incredibly difficult. The positive side is whatever your children will get from this will benefit them for life. The difficulty now is an investment in their future.
>
>
>
> So as an exercise identify something that causes stress in your life that you can do without and eliminate it. It feels good to look back weeks later and say "Why did I let that bother me? Done"

My CT is not the best b/c I chose to be more seasonal (b/c I don't have Hashi's etc.) Instead I take cold showers only when I take my showers. I swim in the lake (AND THANK GOD IT'S NOT LAKE ONTARIO ANYMORE...EW) I will continue to swim in this lake throughout fall, winter etc. I've decided that for me, I won't do tub dunks (I don't have a tub anymore in my new apartment). I think using outdoors as often as possible will be better. For example, in winter I will take advantage of the snow, and I will rub that on me and take a bucket of cold water and I will dump this over my head. I will go back in. (This will be morning right before breakfast). It's things like this that I prefer for CT. I also noted that on the Ask Jack when someone posted about the humunculous and CT. So while it is beneficial for some people to have total tub submersion as a therapy...I think working on the hands, feet and face (the face dunks are crucial as the dive reflex and the trigeminal nerves...and they are like a concentrated bundle of the most cold sensitive). I've actually done this before in the Spring, if I had to drink (well of course I didn't have to...and now I don't drink at all)....where I would go into the person's washroom and give myself a face dunk. It helped.

CT!!

### BJK77 — 2012-09-13T20:07:14+0300

Love this Gretchen. And I love that you just now reminded me of a time earlier this year when I saw for the very first time how blue the sky was. I was taking a walk during a break at work and actually had to call DH and ask him if he ever noticed just how amazingly clear and crystal blue the sky could be. I realized then that I had never truly LIVED for the moment before. People talk about it all the time, but until you experience that one moment that completely colors your vision you just can't have any idea what it means to truly be present.

I knew this was happening to me because I was finally finding health and taking charge of my own destiny. I felt in control, powerful, yet serene and calm. I had an inner peace that I'd honestly never felt before in my life. I vowed that day to do whatever it took to hold onto that feeling. Thanks to Jack I wake up each morning and make an effort to take in the beauty of the sunrise and the amazing world that envelopes me. Unfortunately I think I was getting complacent about truly appreciating it all - although I'm going to blame that somewhat on hormones

Today is a new day and I'm recommitting to experiencing LIFE to its fullest every.single.day. In fact, I think I'll take a walk right now and soak in the beauty of my surroundings.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-13T20:16:42+0300

# Thread: Metformin dosing question
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin-dosing-question.3621/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### SeaJane — 2012-09-12T23:31:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin-dosing-question.3621/post-51258>

I had a education consult with Jack and he suggested that Metformin might be useful in helping my gut and hormones get back in shape. My ND is on-board with it although she was wondering what the dosing would be for this purpose: 500mg once, twice or three times a day. I don't have insulin or blood sugar issues. It seems that many monitor blood glucose levels in determining dosage. Would this be something I should be doing as well? I'd appreciate anyone who could share what they are doing. I realize that my situation may be different (context you know) although it would help me get a sense of what/how people are using metformin.

Thanks.

PSJane

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-12T23:43:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin-dosing-question.3621/post-51260>

I believe you start w/500mg monitor BG and adjust from there.

### CarolMorris — 2012-09-13T01:26:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin-dosing-question.3621/post-51275>

In my consult Jack suggested I ask my doctor about trying 500mg 4x per day. I had a little problem with taking it on an empty stomach so I take 1000mg with each of my 2 meals.

### SeaJane — 2012-09-13T01:37:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin-dosing-question.3621/post-51281>

> **CarolM;51860 wrote:** In my consult Jack suggested I ask my doctor about trying 500mg 4x per day. I had a little problem with taking it on an empty stomach so I take 1000mg with each of my 2 meals.

Carol--Are you monitoring your blood glucose levels? Thanks.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-13T01:42:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin-dosing-question.3621/post-51284>

What kind of measurements blood sugar measurements are we hoping to see? Obviously FBG lowered - to below 85, or lower? What about pre-meal, post-meal measurements? Are we looking for metformin to help improve these markers? Would that indicate it's helping our insulin (pretty obvious here), as well as our mitochondria? If we can achieve the glucose targets without the metformin, would that indicate we don't need it for further optimizing?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-13T01:47:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin-dosing-question.3621/post-51288>

these are questions for your doctor.........metformin is not just used for diabetes. Your particular context needs to be discussed with your doctor.

### CarolMorris — 2012-09-13T19:44:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin-dosing-question.3621/post-51465>

> **PSJane;51866 wrote:** Carol--Are you monitoring your blood glucose levels? Thanks.

not regularly...I think I need a new glucose meter or new strips, it seems to register high, but when I've had it done it's much lower. In the past I've mostly used it to monitor how I react to foods or to check my 1 hr. post meal.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-13T20:43:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin-dosing-question.3621/post-51483>

I'm taking 500, 3X a day, a bit before meals. When it works out perfectly.

### Destiny — 2012-09-14T01:53:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin-dosing-question.3621/post-51536>

I just started yesterday! Dr.k suggested I speak to my doctor about taking it once or twice a day. I started with 500mg once a day at noon with meals.

### debbiemikus — 2012-09-19T02:27:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin-dosing-question.3621/post-52572>

Could someone please tell me what blog post is dealing with Metformin? I have DM2, hypothyroid, crohns, and cannot lose weight. I have been on Meformin before and stopped because I thought it may have been partly responsible for my crohns. Probably wasn't but I am willing to restart it even tho it makes me nauseous if it will help heal my gut and get my hormones back in shape. Thanks everyone!!

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-19T02:47:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin-dosing-question.3621/post-52574>

I take 500, 3X a day with meals. I got that from the internet. It seems to effect my appetite, and I think I'm eating less - sadly, that isn't translating into weight loss.

### Destiny — 2012-09-26T14:29:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin-dosing-question.3621/post-54391>

I have been taking Metformin 500x2 a day since 9/12. My FBG went up to 100's from my usual 90's when I added DHEA and added more progesterone 6 weeks ago. Now, it is not reacting to Metformin at all. It stays in 100's.

I will increase the dose to 500x3 a day and see if it brings any change.

### ATL_Paleo — 2012-10-11T04:30:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin-dosing-question.3621/post-57514>

Excellent article on Metformin in latest issue of Life Extension magazine.

[http://www.lef.org/magazine/mag2012/nov2012\_Metformin-Makes-Headline-News\_01.htm](http://http://www.lef.org/magazine/mag2012/nov2012_Metformin-Makes-Headline-News_01.htm)

# Thread: No ranges here,  what are yours?  Finding PG/E ratio
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-ranges-here-what-are-yours-finding-pg-e-ratio.3622/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-09-13T01:14:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-ranges-here-what-are-yours-finding-pg-e-ratio.3622/post-51270>

Ok, I give. I'm stuck.

Got my hormones back today - but there are not reference ranges, I thought they were supposed to all be in top quartile- and then how to figure that PG/E ratio again?

And, DHEA has a range, is that one you want to be in the top quartile?

DHEA 2.98 ng/ml (.630-4.7 range)

Estogens 104 pg/ml

Estradiol 47 pg/ml

Estrone 57 pg/ml

Progesterone .31 ng/ml

FSH 12.7 mIU/ml

Testosterone 315 ng/dl (7-46 range)

Yes, I know testosterone is high. 2nd test that came back high, did the 2nd in case there was a lab error on first. Adrenal Tumor? It's not PCOS.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-13T01:29:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-ranges-here-what-are-yours-finding-pg-e-ratio.3622/post-51277>

OK, not sure if I'm reading these right, but it seems you have a CRP not an hsCRP and that you have a TSH but no T3 or T4. So very hard to gauge thyroid function or inflammation. It appears HDL is high. And fasting glucose is high, too. Are you worried about diabetes/insulin issues?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lab-update-help.3623/post-51402>

So very hard to gauge thyroid function or inflammation. It appears HDL is high. And fasting glucose is high, too. Are you worried about diabetes/insulin issues?

Thanks for responding again, Lauren.

Yes, I am concerned about my blood glucose. Last quarter it had gone down into the low 90's, but as you can see, is back up. My diet is semi-epi-paleo, as compared to plain ole paleo. Labs were done about 10 days after returning from vacation, were my diet was tight, but I drank a lot of red wine over the week. Don't know if this could have caused the spike.

Actually, HDL is low (36), but slowly rising over the last few months. Obviously need to get that up. The TSH is ricidulously low (.05), but don't have hyper symptoms...although my sleep has gotten worse during the last couple of months. The CRP is the highly sensitive type, according to my Doc. I asked about this specifically.

Should have some new labs this week on my thyroid panel, which I requested upon seeing that TSH number...last labs had my T3 at the low end of normal. So we have TSH way on the hyper side, yet T3 is low...sumtin goin on here.

# Thread: ASI results in, yikes!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-results-in-yikes.3628/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-09-13T04:10:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-results-in-yikes.3628/post-51351>

06:00 - 08:00 AM 7 **Depressed 13-24 nM

11:00 - 1:00 PM 7 Normal 5-10 nM

04:00 - 05:00 PM 3 Normal 3-8 nM

10:00 - Midnight 2 Normal 1-4 nM

Cortisol Load: 19 23 - 42 nM

The cortisol load reflects the area under the cortisol curve. This is an

indicator of overall cortisol exposure, where high values favor a catabolic

state, and low values are sign of adrenal deterioration

**Depressed morning cortisol**, < 13 nM, is suggestive of marginal

HPA (Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal) performance. Normal rhythms

exhibit highest cortisol value for the day at 7 - 8 AM.

The Cortisol release inducers fall into 4 broad categories shown in the

adjacent flowchart. Long term adrenal axis maintenance and restoration,

require optimization of all the cortisol inducers.

Dehydroepiandrosterone Free \[DHEA + DHEA-S\]

Pooled Value >25 Elevated** Adults (M/F): 3-10 ng/ml

Insulin

Fasting 130 pg/ml

Total Salivary SIgA 15 U/ml

Got this in the mail today, will post separately as well. My cortisol graph wasn't exactly flatlined, but goddamn, close enough. No wonder I am so friggin' beat. I feel like I could go to sleep for the night at 3 pm. Sigh.... I read in one of the many blogs about light first, then something else, then hormones. Light could be better I guess. I'm already taking adrenal support caps, guess I need more/different ones.

Suggestions for recovery? And is this adrenal fatigue, or exhaustion? What about the insulin, SIgA and P17OH?

I am taking 100mg DHEA, and transdermal Progesterone/Testosterone (10mg/1mg per ml) days 12-26.

When I get the GI Effects back, I will book with JK.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-13T09:16:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-some-labs-please-comment.3630/post-51394>

It looks like you are low on all your hormones (your new doc is on board with BHRT right?) and have reduced thyroid function, too. Your hsCRP is OK, but could be lower. Your cholesterol going up is not a problem in and of itself and it's your LDL that's high, so I'm thinking that's a good thing. But your seem to be converting into cortisol (with the high RT3) instead of hormones. Have you had an adrenal stress index done (4 panel cortisol through the day/night and then a nighttime melatonin)? That could be helpful. You are testing negative for Hashi's at the moment, but do have some antibodies present. Dr K has suggested that having 3 negative results in a row over an 18 month period is best to rule it out completely, so maybe keep testing. And talk with your doc about BHRT, including P and E and thyroid. I'm not great at lab stuff, so maybe others will chime in.

### Jude — 2012-09-13T11:36:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-some-labs-please-comment.3630/post-51400>

### Jude — 2012-09-14T03:07:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-some-labs-please-comment.3630/post-51560>

> **Jack Kruse;52000 wrote:** SHBG is a big tell........

Now I'm researching SHBG-guess it's the classic case of yrs of grains & dairy=leaky gut;impacted by adrenal fatigue and abuse of the circadian rhythm. Accelerated by gall bladder removal and vit K deficiency, which caused development of osteopenia and osteoporosis. At least I think I understand how I arrive here.

Jack Says:

Re engineering a human is tough detailed work. That is why it takes time. People dont like spending time or the money to do it until they are sick n tired of being sick n tired. Me…….I learned all this fixing my fat ass. I had patience with me and my attitude, dogma, and personality. Over time I changed. And then I discovered things about me I did not know I had. I found my sixth sense. The power to thin slice. And now I use it to improve my former self.

Gretchen Says:

"Light is the key that starts/stops the engine. (circadian cycles)

Food & hormones are the fuel

CT is the accelerator

The Brain is the navigator

Optimal requires all systems working in concert, if one is off the entire system doesn’t work properly or efficiently.So for those of us who’ve been struggling & seeing minimal if any success w/out paying attention to light should begin to see changes when we pay attn to it. However we must remember we have to turn back our epigenetics switches that have been on for Xyears. We also have to contend with the reversal of disease and illness as a result of our own personal mismatches

This isn’t going to happen over night. I’ve been working on my health in one way shape or form since 1999 only now am I getting some where Each success peels back another layer. Each layer brings me that much closer to cracking the code w/in me.

While we’d all love to have the BodyRock.tv bodies in 30 days we’re broken for most of us that isn’t going to happen over night. Once we get our stuff straightened out & flying right the possibilities are endless. While its not fun hearing it’s going to take time it’s the truth. That’s because we’re each working on our own personal masterpiece. And those things just don’t happen over night. They take time effort an love and many of us have to balance working on the masterpiece with the rest of our lives b/c they are integral

Remember what your masterpiece looks like today is not the final product. Think about the following question and how it relates to your journey: How long did Michael Angelo work on David to get it to its final phase???

AND FINALLY, as Jack Says:

……those are conversations for an educational consult. Details. The devil is there and it takes putting your labs in that context because everyone’s answer is not yours. That is the greatest mistake I see in medicine and in paleo advice. Not everyone gets the same mileage with the same activities or behaviors.

I can hardly wait for my educational consult !

### enyaw — 2012-09-13T17:16:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-soon-my-first-child.3634/post-51433>

My wife and I are due to have our first born in January. Our plan is to breastfeed, then introduce an epi-paleo diet all while establishing a set feeding/playing/sleeping schedule. Having been in the Marine Corps infantry, I know that the first casualty of war is the plan. Does anyone have advice on when to wean from breastfeeding, what baby's first foods should be, getting baby on a schedule, cold, or any other advice we should consider on our adventure?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-13T17:20:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-soon-my-first-child.3634/post-51436>

congrats and I like your plan!

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-13T17:39:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-soon-my-first-child.3634/post-51440>

> **MooseMan;52021 wrote:** My wife and I are due to have our first born in January. Our plan is to breastfeed, then introduce an epi-paleo diet all while establishing a set feeding/playing/sleeping schedule. Having been in the Marine Corps infantry, I know that the first casualty of war is the plan. Does anyone have advice on when to wean from breastfeeding, what baby's first foods should be, getting baby on a schedule, cold, or any other advice we should consider on our adventure?

WHO recommends a child should nurse until at least age 2 - any nursing beyond that is based upon the comfort of the mother and child (I'm still nursing my ds who's 2 &1/2 - I've been working on weaning for over 6mths - I'm ready for him to be done but he's not - he knows what he needs so he still nurses)

Avoid schedules - scheduling babies interferes w/breastfeeding success. Consider safe co-sleeping arrangements (makes it easier on mom and baby and builds the nursing relationship much quicker - LLL, Dr Sears and Mothering.com talk about safe methods for co-sleeping)

first foods, egg yolk, meats, veggies(only what's in season at the time) - wait on fruits until later (after 1yr)

Start going to a La Leche League meetings, educate your self about birth and the birthing process. Consider having a MW deliver your child, and hire a Doula to support both you and your wife during labor.

Books I recommend:

anything by Ina May Gaskin - here's her website [http://www.inamay.com/](http://http://www.inamay.com/) she's a leading Midwife - she runs the Farm in TN. I've known women who've delivered with her.

Watch the documentary the business of being born.

Any books by Dr. Sears -

Consider taking a birthing class like Bradley (Forewarning there are some OBs that won't take you as a client if you use bradley), Hypnobirthing, hypnobabies - these classes involve the father in the birthing process and give the mother ownership of her childs birth.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-13T19:03:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-soon-my-first-child.3634/post-51455>

...what she said! As a mom of three and a natural childbirth teacher (Bradley et al), you can have a schedule...it will just be dictated by baby! Their innate wisdom is more reliable than ours at this point. Breast feed AS LONG AS POSSIBLE! Do not let your doc pressure u to introduce solids too early. When baby is interested, it will eat...and that is the right time. Again, it may be new to this world but it has millions of years of evolutionary wisdom within its cells.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-13T19:30:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-soon-my-first-child.3634/post-51459>

1\. Family Bed!

2\. Family Bed!

3\. Eat around baby, baby will start reaching for for YOUR foods when ready. Give tiny amounts of whatever you are eating (assuming epi-paleo). You can even pre-chew (read about this before balking!) on things if you feel the need. Blendingand over processed food is such a new invention for babies. Totally unnecessary. Baby will start wanting more of your food, less of mommies. We seem to be built from 2-4 yrs of breastfeeding - it's unclear what ideal/optimal is, so go with your primal sense for your baby

4\. No experience with this, but would love to hear on how baby falls into a good schedule with proper circadian input.

Most importantly relax about it all! All babies eventually learn to eat and potty. They are born with programming for things like sleep and love (did I mention family bed!).

Don't get caught up in sending baby away from the tribe to try and get on some schedule you determine all by themselves. . .so unatural.

Annnnnnnd, family bed. I totally think kids should have a room/bed - but should be a full member of the family (tribe) until they are ready for their privacy.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-13T19:31:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-soon-my-first-child.3634/post-51460>

> **cjco;52043 wrote:** ...what she said! As a mom of three and a natural childbirth teacher (Bradley et al), you can have a schedule...it will just be dictated by baby! Their innate wisdom is more reliable than ours at this point. Breast feed AS LONG AS POSSIBLE! Do not let your doc pressure u to introduce solids too early. When baby is interested, it will eat...and that is the right time. Again, it may be new to this world but it has millions of years of evolutionary wisdom within its cells.

I agree, if you are providing good input (circadian natural environment and epi-paleo foods for the tribe) then I bet baby has a better sense for what's best than you two ever will.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-13T19:38:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-soon-my-first-child.3634/post-51462>

Congratulations, MooseMan, that's wonderful news!

### CarolMorris — 2012-09-13T21:06:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-soon-my-first-child.3634/post-51489>

Congratulations such an exciting time of life!

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-09-14T00:27:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-soon-my-first-child.3634/post-51516>

> **MooseMan;52021 wrote: My wife and I are due to have our first born in January. Our plan is to breastfeed, then introduce an epi-paleo diet all while establishing a set feeding/playing/sleeping schedule. Having been in the Marine Corps infantry, I know that the first casualty of war is the plan. Does anyone have advice on when to wean from breastfeeding, what baby's first foods should be, getting baby on a schedule, cold, or any other advice we should consider on our adventure?**

**

It is a very happy time to be welcoming a child into the world. Congratulations. This would be my advice to a new father:

1\. Try to give the new mother as much support as possible. Birth and breastfeeding is very stressful on a women's body.

2\. Makes sure she has the best food possible and lots of rest. Try to reduce sources of stress before the baby comes. You partner's quality of milk will directly come from her quality of food.

3\. After the baby comes, consider a _babymoon_ at home, which is one to three weeks of reduced activity and few or no visitors. (This also reduces the chance of infection.)

4\. Encourage your partner to breastfed longer. Many people breastfed for a few weeks to six months. A year or more would be better for your child.

5\. This is the Weston A Price Foundation's baby feeding schedule:

[http://www.westonaprice.org/childrens-health/nourishing-a-growing-baby](http://http://www.westonaprice.org/childrens-health/nourishing-a-growing-baby)

It is a very personal decision, but the place to give birth is where your partner feels safest. I had two home births with a midwife. I would highly recommend using a midwife and having a home birth, if that is something that your partner would want. (Home births have similar outcomes to hospital births but lower rates of infection.) Co-sleeping is another personal choice. I did it but sometimes I wished I hadn't because it negatively effected my sleep. I breastfed both my children on demand. Sonja self-weaned at two years. I weaned Erika at three years. Again, every family is in a unique situation and has to make there own decisions about these important choices.

Here are some of my favorite essay from the Weston A Price Foundation**. They have some great ideas about first foods that I wish I had known about when I had my children:

[http://eatkamloops.org/thinking-about-motherhood/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/thinking-about-motherhood/)

Good luck. Having children is a wonderful experience. It's not always easy but it is always rewarding.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-09-15T22:21:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-soon-my-first-child.3634/post-51864>

Congrats on the happy news!

You know it's funny, since becoming healthier and healthier as a result of this website etc. I have really been feeling the effects of 'biological clock' and wanting kids. This is in stark contrast to my severe aversion to wanting any kids at all from even a year ago.

I started getting positive thoughts in about June. Now I've decided that it would be the right thing for me. Ok, so I need to find a suitable partner, LOL, I think this will change my perspective in who I select though.

It's as if I want to say 'thank you' to Jack and everyone for giving me what I consider a freedom. I feel more grounded and centred so as a result I feel more confident that there would be success with having kids.

### TribalSpice — 2012-09-16T06:41:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coming-soon-my-first-child.3634/post-51905>

Massive congrats!

If you only read one thing about birth, let it be this: [http://www.joyousbirth.info/articles/ecstatic.html](http://http://www.joyousbirth.info/articles/ecstatic.html) It is a brilliant article on birth and hormones etc. Be aware that how your wife births impacts breastfeeding and the health of your baby. It is a time when bubs hormonal system will be to some extent "set", a base level of receptors will develop in that first 24 hours on the outside. Being on this site means you must understand the gravity of that for you baby's future health. As Caroline mention, home might be the best place for you bub to enter the world.

With breastfeeding, support, support, support and allow mum and bub as much time doing skin to skin contact as possible. Allow bub to attach to the breast on their own terms, especially the first time. Forcing babies on the breast can be one of the most detrimental things to do to a breastfeeding relationship.

With introducing solids, it really isn't that important in the first 12 months, bub will get their nutrition from breastmilk and any food play, is more a learning experience than a nutritional need.

Enjoy it, parenting is a great experience, birth is amazing, babies are magical and kids are so much fun.

I love my Pg... I feel I need MORE! this is based off my cycle, migraines, sleep etc...

I'm taking a BHRT Progesterone gel (5% Formula 1 gel 50mg/ml)

the amounts vary depending on where I'm at in my cycle:

TOTM: .4ml = 20mg

10days after TOTM: .8ml = 40mg (Also taking .1ml of TriEst)

remainder of cycle: 1.0ml = 5omg

intuitively I know I need more Pg.

### tweety — 2012-09-14T01:04:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-lets-hear-about-yours.3636/post-51520>

I have been using 3% cream, 1/2ml (15mg) from day 15 to TOTM. I gained two pounds every month. I took a break, but knew I needed it. So I decided to up my dose- went to 1 ML(30mg) twice a day from day 15 to TOTM- no weight gain! 1lb weight loss! So my doc just called in a 10% cream, and I will be doing .6ml (60mg) from day 15 to TOTM, break during TOTM, then .25ml (25mg) from day 7 to day 15.

I feel much calmer, but still fly off the handle more than I feel I should. I am attributing that to skipping last month. And, after nine months of dosing, when I did my follow up labs using the 3% cream at the 1/2ml dose- my levels had dropped further. Food for thought.

### Destiny — 2012-09-19T01:25:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-lets-hear-about-yours.3636/post-52558>

> **KiwiLauren;53175 wrote:** Dosing question for all you with P experience... my doc has finally given me some compounded bioidentical P cream. It is 20mg/g, a low dose I know. She has told me to put 1 g on my skin x1 per day on days 12-26 of my menstrual cycle. This doesn't seem to be cycling correctly as I understand it as the dose is either 'on' or 'off'. I can obviously tweak this (the script came with no instructions, just her verbal suggestion) and would love some input as to how to make this better. Thanks, Lauren

Dzugan has the cycling in the following manner - micronized progesterone 50 mg/ml:

1-14 days 60 mg a day (divided into morning and evening doses 20-40)

15-25 days 70 mg (20-50)

last 5-6 days 40 mg (10-30)

He actually says to take the stronger dose in the morning and weaker one in the evening but I am doing the opposite to help my sleep.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-09-19T17:41:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-lets-hear-about-yours.3636/post-52704>

I wonder how Vitex and betaine helps with progesterone production. I know that Chocolate has discussed using this as a method to increase her pg. levels. Hmmm....sounds very interesting.

See, in beginning of July I was coming out of a prolonged constipation and absence of period (and probably ovulation) of 3 months. I felt 'mad'. Anyways out of such a desperation I went to a naturalpath and got prescribed a tincture of 3 ingredients which included Vitex. I took it for about 3 weeks. (This is still in the presence of eating pretty well and having done CT since March and following this blog etc.) Then on July 21 I finally menstrated. I also started the seafood-rich diet in mid-July. So I don't know what was giving me the 'boost'. I felt immediately better after that date. At the end of July I felt so positive that I ditched ALL supplements (b/c I felt like I was being consumed negatively by them etc.) So for August I did not menstrate, but did get spotting the week before I 'should' have menstrated. I did not feel 'worried' though. I still felt pretty damn good. I'm pretty sure I ovulated at the end of August, but did not menstrate this month. Hmmm... The thing is, I DO feel good though so that is interesting. Now with epi-paleo and super good circadian cycles, my mind is much clearer, and I have acquired and assimilated even more information in the last two months in this better mind state. So I think it would be a good idea to re-visit betaine and Vitex. I have been taking Maca for the last three weeks. I decided I should try that and be more disciplined with it...since in the past I never took it for long enough. lol I feel much more familiar with my body chemistry and what my unique needs are. This is helpful as I can have a better base from which to address things. Especially if in the presence of epi-paleo and circadian cycles (and CT in the form of nature...hee hee). So I know that despite super healthy, that mechanisms from past epi-genetics is still needing to be addressed. Overall though, I feel blessed and privileged to have even come across this website at all. Honestly I have to say life-changing and even 'saving'. I had the mentality that "I will NOT let myself get worse...I HAVE THE POWER TO HELP MYSELF...I'LL STOP AT NOTHING TO GET THERE!" While this can be viewed as extreme and it takes you on some dark paths occasionally, when you come out the other end, being stronger and triumphant...then you cannot argue against the necessity of having such a strong opinion.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/strange.3643/post-51530>

I have been taking Cytomel over 1 month so my doctor ran thyroid labs. It looks that only my rT3 was tested and nothing else. I was surprised to say the least. Can I assume that I am clearing rT3 based on the results below?

**July result:

rT3 - 29 (11-32 ng/dl)

September result:

rT3 - 20 (11-32 ng/dl)

**

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-14T01:31:31+0300

### Destiny — 2012-09-14T13:20:23+0300

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-14T17:32:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/strange.3643/post-51643>

I don't think it would go down but as complex as our bodies are I suppose all things are possible. Plus it really only stays in the body I think, around8 hours total and peaks at 2-3 hours so depending on when you test the results may not show you the actual picture.

Oh Destiny, that's really unfortunate about the doc not scripting you. Can you find another doc? Read Paul Robinson's book Recovering With T3. It talks all lot about symptoms and testing plus more.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-your-thoughts-if-the-warrior-diet.3657/post-51644>

Most of the articles I read I find agreement with on most of the issues. This one...seems to conflict quite a bit with things I've read here.

[http://fitness.mercola.com/sites/fitness/archive/2012/09/14/intermittent-fasting-benefits.aspx?e\_cid=20120914\_DNL\_artNew\_1](http://http://fitness.mercola.com/sites/fitness/archive/2012/09/14/intermittent-fasting-benefits.aspx?e_cid=20120914_DNL_artNew_1)

At first I thought they were typical Mercola articles...but didn't think Mercola wrote something called The Warrior Diet...than I noticed the author is Ori Hofmekler I guess hes a guest author for some of Dr. Mercola's articles?

So I'm curious about the discrepancies and why the mismatch. He seems to think Dinner is the most important meal and breakfast is the one you should skip. He mentions Circadian Rhythms as the reason sorta interesting.

Maybe I need to reread Leptin posts. As I don't really remember why it was important to eat three meals. I have been skipping dinner quite a bit and figured it might be ok as long as I eat breakfast. The three meals a day was important so that you aren't digesting all day long...and the breakfast was important for some reason....definitely need to reread as I'm forgetting all the important things I have a feeling.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-14T22:26:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-your-thoughts-if-the-warrior-diet.3657/post-51685>

# Thread: Light at night
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-at-night.3659/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 29
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-09-14T19:00:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-at-night.3659/post-51652>

I am trying to figure out the issues of artificial light at night for my household. I am looking into Jack's sites about how light disrupts circadian cycles, and everything I pull up seems to emphasize that blue light after dark is the worst. Of course we already know this, it suppresses melatonin production, and disrupts the hormone cascade into the night, and the next day, etc. However, I can't find anything compelling that says I need to turn out the incandescant lights as well. Do we need to turn out the incandescants as well? Can someone point me to some compelling study showing it? I personally, want to get this done for my family (candles after dark). But my (technology loving, stay-up-late) husband needs more data before he's willing to get on board for the time before the kids go to bed.

### ealachan — 2012-09-14T20:10:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-at-night.3659/post-51665>

I believe that the quick-and-dirty is that normally your brain doesn't start producing melatonin until you've been in complete darkness for 4 hours. When you sit there with the lights on in the dark, you're basically giving your brain a whole lot less time to produce the melatonin that you desperately need.

Oxytocin appears to be a short-cut on the 4 hour thing. If you have an orgasm and your brain releases oxytocin, you then go straight to melatonin production without having to wait for 4 hours of darkness to pass.

Again, just my rudimentary understanding.

edited to add: In regard to your husband wanting articles - Dr. K has posted several links to good ones on his FB feed lately, including one to a monster paper (over 100pgs) done by the European Union back in the spring of this year. It's dense reading, but interesting stuff.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-14T22:24:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-at-night.3659/post-51683>

Your understanding is fine.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-14T22:51:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-at-night.3659/post-51687>

Okay, so I need to look for sites that say we don't produce melatonin unless we are in complete darkness.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-15T00:09:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-at-night.3659/post-51698>

He doesn't want to read Jack's interpretations, he wants to see the cites.

I sent him this one ([http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/48682547/ns/health-mens\_health/#.UC5BG47T1UQ](http://http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/48682547/ns/health-mens_health/#.UC5BG47T1UQ)), but he took home that you need to sleep in darkness (okay, this is a good first step!), and that people who sleep more have less cancer, less depression. I also sent him the article about blind women getting less breast cancer (here: [http://m.medicaldaily.com/articles/7369/20111011/cancer-sleep-cancer-epidemiologist-steve-jobs-antioxidant-melatonin-cortisol-hydrocortisone.htm](http://http://m.medicaldaily.com/articles/7369/20111011/cancer-sleep-cancer-epidemiologist-steve-jobs-antioxidant-melatonin-cortisol-hydrocortisone.htm)). He said, the study failed to remove the fact that the blind women got more sleep than the women who weren't blind. Sleep has been shown time and again to help longevity, cancer, etc. So it "could" be simply that the if the non-blind women got the same amount of sleep as the blind ones, they would also get less cancer. And, quite frankly, he has a point.

I'll look at CT-7 cites and all, next.

### ealachan — 2012-09-17T18:12:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-at-night.3659/post-52129>

> **bigknitwit;52743 wrote:** How did you handle the kids? Mine are young, and I can't "just remove myself to a dark room" in the evenings when they are awake. If kids were not part of the equation, I wouldnt have an issue.

Why can't you? Take them in a low-lit room and play a game with them or read them a book or something. It doesn't have to be dark. You can read a book or play a game by candle light. I'm not trying to antagonize here, btw...just trying to help.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-17T18:17:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-at-night.3659/post-52132>

> **bigknitwit;52743 wrote:** How did you handle the kids? Mine are young, and I can't "just remove myself to a dark room" in the evenings when they are awake. If kids were not part of the equation, I wouldnt have an issue.

Kids - well, back this spring/summer I didn't worry about the kids. couldn't had to worry about me and me only. that didn't mean I didn't want them to be sitting in that light. but as Jack has said you've got to fix you first, then you can worry about others.

my situation was different than yours b/c dh actually had an appt in Jack's spine clinic - and basically dh walked out of his office as a changed man. - threw out all the grains and crap out when he got home, and asked me for orange glasses. Its only been recently since we've really started pushing the lights at night. initially it was just at story time, then it became at bath time and now its dinner time. when I get home from work I no longer turn on any lights in the house. as soon as it gets hard to see in the house on go the candles. we try to be outside during dusk if we can - b/c it makes it easier - cooking on the grill, etc limits the need to use lights inside. we've also taken advantage of the nice weather to use the fire pit at night and do story time there instead of in the house.

both kids have their own orange glasses, and we try to use them when the lights are on at night - but both parents are using this.

parenting is about compromise - why not approach DH about doing it his way for a month (already doing this) then trying a month your way - your question to DH is what can one month hurt? why not try it and see what happens Call it an experiment in your children's behavior, and ask him to see if he notices a difference in the kids. also ask him to see if he's noticed a difference in his own patterns. you might be surprised - the kids will get into it, and may not want to go back to DH's way... mine now refuse to eat at night unless the lights are off and candles are lit. and yes occasionally DH grumbles - b/c lets be honest - 2yo aren't the neatest eaters on the planet. (its a lot harder to clean up a toddler mess in the dark - but we're working on it - good thing the dog likes what's left of the floor!)

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-17T18:55:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-at-night.3659/post-52148>

Thank you for this... I'm not worried right now about my kids either - just fixing me (as selfish as that sounds - 2 of mine have autoimmune conditions). Dh is against them living in the dark after sunset. I could make a dark room(s) for myself, but the kids still have free reign in the house. They are 11, 9, 6, 3. The older 2 spend a lot of time working on their lego league challenges after school and after dinner. They have a dedicated room for this, so I could potentially let lights be on in there and just not go in, and not allow the younger 2 in there after dark, because I can't go in after them (lit environment), and they're the ones most likely to cause chaos requiring my intervention! The younger 2 want to be where I am, so in theory I could watch them and keep them entertained in dark lit rooms. They will adjust to this. Dinner could be before dark (usually already is before 5pm, so not too bad to do). I won't have free reign of the house, especially after dh gets home from work (lights on)... So all preparations for the next school day, dinner clean-up, etc, has to happen before he returns. That's a challenge, but doable. I still have after-school activity driving to do (piano lessons, gymnastics drop-off/pick-up, soccer, etc). I am responsible for this. In these circumstances, I could use the blue-blockers while out and about. Maybe I should get an attractive pair... Often we have to wait from piano lessons to finish (1 hour), and I take my youngest shopping to pass the time. Or, i can see about moving their lesson time earlier after school (if it's available). We'll still be in the dark in Nov/Dec/Jan returning (dusk is at 4pm near solstice), but it will be closer. No way around the gymnastics pick up time (6pm) If I fill in the imperfections with the blue-blockers, am I still making moves that will effect my health positively, or is it closer to an all or none deal?

> **Shijin13;52749 wrote:** Kids - well, back this spring/summer I didn't worry about the kids. couldn't had to worry about me and me only. that didn't mean I didn't want them to be sitting in that light. but as Jack has said you've got to fix you first, then you can worry about others.
>
>
>
> my situation was different than yours b/c dh actually had an appt in Jack's spine clinic - and basically dh walked out of his office as a changed man. - threw out all the grains and crap out when he got home, and asked me for orange glasses. Its only been recently since we've really started pushing the lights at night. initially it was just at story time, then it became at bath time and now its dinner time. when I get home from work I no longer turn on any lights in the house. as soon as it gets hard to see in the house on go the candles. we try to be outside during dusk if we can - b/c it makes it easier - cooking on the grill, etc limits the need to use lights inside. we've also taken advantage of the nice weather to use the fire pit at night and do story time there instead of in the house.
>
>
>
> both kids have their own orange glasses, and we try to use them when the lights are on at night - but both parents are using this.
>
>
>
> parenting is about compromise - why not approach DH about doing it his way for a month (already doing this) then trying a month your way - your question to DH is what can one month hurt? why not try it and see what happens Call it an experiment in your children's behavior, and ask him to see if he notices a difference in the kids. also ask him to see if he's noticed a difference in his own patterns. you might be surprised - the kids will get into it, and may not want to go back to DH's way... mine now refuse to eat at night unless the lights are off and candles are lit. and yes occasionally DH grumbles - b/c lets be honest - 2yo aren't the neatest eaters on the planet. (its a lot harder to clean up a toddler mess in the dark - but we're working on it - good thing the dog likes what's left of the floor!)

### Lyndra — 2012-09-17T19:17:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-at-night.3659/post-52152>

I won't use candles either. I know too many people whose houses have caught fire because of candles, including one who died. I'm making do with orange light bulbs everywhere and blu-blockers. Is that optimal? I think risk/reward has to factor into a determination of optimal. I've forgotten to turn off a burner when I removed a pot, sometimes for hours. I can easily imagine letting a candle burn somewhere unattended, maybe even overnight. I have pets which greatly increases the risk of something untoward happening with an open flame. My assessment of my personal situation is that candles are not worth the risk. I think it's great that you are making it work, as that seems to be more true to the principle of no lights after dark. But I can absolutely see why someone cannot get behind that practice.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-17T20:03:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-at-night.3659/post-52164>

> **Lyndra;52769 wrote:** I won't use candles either. I know too many people whose houses have caught fire because of candles, including one who died. I'm making do with orange light bulbs everywhere and blu-blockers. Is that optimal? I think risk/reward has to factor into a determination of optimal. I've forgotten to turn off a burner when I removed a pot, sometimes for hours. I can easily imagine letting a candle burn somewhere unattended, maybe even overnight. I have pets which greatly increases the risk of something untoward happening with an open flame. My assessment of my personal situation is that candles are not worth the risk. I think it's great that you are making it work, as that seems to be more true to the principle of no lights after dark. But I can absolutely see why someone cannot get behind that practice.

I'm with you, I've been too close to someone who died in a fire - and I have a healthy amount of care and knowledge that "crazy bad things DO happen". Candles are ok now and then, but I don't want my less than careful child thinking we do things with candles - as the next step in his independence will be to do it himself and set himself on fire. I've had a lot of people in my family die from freak accidents - I'm taking no chances! Lighting strikes the same people over and over!

edit: He is also not driving till age 25, nor is he going to be riding with anybody under the age of 25. We have said this to him over and over again since birth so it's not a surprise. I'm fully prepared to move to a city where nobody has cars if that is what it takes.

### Jonathin — 2012-09-17T20:12:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-at-night.3659/post-52170>

I have set up a variety of homemade amber LED lights for reading and general room lighting in our small house. The amber LEDs are a very narrow band of light and contain no blue or green light. I have done it in a way so that the level of light is purposely low but sufficient for normal activity. Of course, the power cost is minimal and they last for about 50,000 hours. If you reduce the voltage or add a diode to a regular incandescent bulb they take on an orange-ish color. A turnkey solution is to install standard dimmer switches. I think there is a do-able solution for every situation. Depending on the space and the air circulation, even the cleanest burning candle will lower oxygen levels. I guess that might help with sleep.

# Thread: The 4th most important thing you need to know about diet and fitness
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 39
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Barry — 2012-09-14T19:15:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-51653>

Before I start, I should emphasize that these posts are "in my opinion"... and I realize my opinion comes from the other side of the bridge. I just want to make that clear before people start complaining about me not seeing it from their perspective etc etc... this is not the debate. This is just my opinion, thats all.

No.4: Create your own orchestra with the best conductor.

To recap

1\. There ainâ€™t noâ€¦ nothing is more important than the other, everything counts

2\. Listen to your own body and go on feel

3\. Optimal does not have to be extremism

This leads me on to no.4. What sparked me to write this was a recent ted talk I watched about the paradox of choice. You see most people think choice leads us to freedom and happiness. However, the opposite is often true. Too much choice can make us paralyzed and this results in us becoming dissatisfied.

It is this paradox of choice that I see happening everyday when it comes to health and fitness. You can go paleo, ketogenic, epi-paleo, VLC, LCHF, etc etc. You can do crossfit, movnat, tabatha, metcon, eccentric loading, high rep, low rep, run/walk, cycle/swim. These are all choices and they all have their merits. However, the amount of choice and the sheer number options is what can lead to paralysis. You simply cannot decide on what is right for you due to you not being able to figure out what is the best choice! The other problem that arises then is when you do actually make a choice and it doesnâ€™t work, or more importantly, you think it doesnâ€™t work as good as it should. This then leads to dissatisfaction and regret.

Let me put this into perspective for the people following this forum. You now have the choice of light cycles to follow, blue light glasses to wear, fish smoothies to make, veg to ferment, temperatures at which to CT, hours to intermittent fast, oils to cook with and tests to take. These are all choices. You might CT at 55F, eat salmon, use ghee and fast for 14hrs. But you donâ€™t lose weight and your health doesnâ€™t improve. You then look and read further and realise that shrimp is better, you should cook with avocado oil and not ghee, CT at 45F is better and so is fasting for 16hrs. If this keeps happening, and you are provided with even more choice as you go along, you are on the road to paralysis and dissatisfaction. This is obviously completely counterproductive and is the antithesis of what you are trying to achieve. Contentment, happiness, positivity and self satisfaction are what you want with the aim of reducing the very thing that is likely causing your problems in the first place i.e. stress !

An analogy that can be used is that all these choices can make up your own orchestra. You canâ€™t have all the best musicians in the world in the same orchestra. So lets say you choose a certain number of them. Being content and satisfied that you have the best you can get is first base. However, regardless of how good these musicians are, they donâ€™t play well together unless you have the best conductor. The conductor in this case is your thought process. As long as you have the right thought process, the choices will work well for you. More importantly, even the most average musicians can play well together if the conductor is top class. Thatâ€™s the key. Everything relates back to the central command centre, Epigenetics.

So create your own orchestra. Donâ€™t worry too much about how big or how talented your orchestra is, just make sure youâ€™ve got the best conductor.

â€œBeyond the walls of intelligence, life is definedâ€￾

### Inger — 2012-09-15T07:29:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-51768>

Good points Barry. To me Jack has limited my choises greatly..lol

Now I go shopping.. directly to the fishcounter.. I know what to pick. It is very easy.

I know.. minimal fake light 24 / 7.

As cold as possible, what I feel comfortable with.

The adaption occurs.. slowly but it is fun. I start to crave MHS, oysters (sadly I get them not too often).. cold..

I thought my cold adaption is sooo slow, it barely happends. I do baby steps, swim in the river 3 to 10 minutes every day. River is around 50-52 degree F. I do facedunks most mornings for 3 minutes. Thats it. Have it cool in my appartement.

Now, these babysteps must have done something because yesterday my brothers were here and we all got in the river together. One could barely do 20 seconds and the other that already have done some CT could do only 2 minutes. They said it hurts! I feel no pain. I could not understand. Then I realized I must have adapted to some degree! I can swim for 7 minutes before I start to feel stiff. Ok I thought it was nothing because others here do 1 hour in icebath.. but in comparison to my strong and fit brothers I still did very well. I guess we need to not compare with others, just do as good as we can and enjoy. We need to enjoy the trip otherwise it makes no sense. Our thoughts. Exactly as you says Barry. The toughts are so very important. I often wonder about why someone struggles so bad and have these negative thoughts. Jacks concept is not hard to do. It is very easy. It is much about to letting go. Letting go of choises. Everything becomes so much more simple.

My brothers have not read Jack sites at all.. but they do so well!!! 2 of them use only candlelight at nights so far, and go early to sleep.. and eat lots of seafood and MHS when I make them one.. I am so proud of them. They do not know ANY of the science behind, but they intuitively know it is the right thing to do. They still have the mind of a child in some way even if they are 22 and 26 yo. Like.. "if we do not become like children, we can not enter heaven.." I think I know what this is about. It is huge. It is so beautiful. It makes one so happy and free.

Life is so much about letting go... very paradoxical. Who tries to save ones life will loose it... You need to experience this to know what it is about. It is magic for sure.

### Barry — 2012-09-15T17:23:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-51813>

Apart from choice , the message I am trying to get across relates to the other point that the author of the book makes .. Life is about creating your own fish bowl.

What I see happening here is that people are taking the advice and recommendations to the extreme. Most then realise that modern life, family, work and just general hassle means that implementing properly everything is actually more trouble than it's worth.

This is the issue I have with chasing "optimal" . You are more likely to reach paralysis and dissatisfaction if you continue the chase. So create your own fishbowl and chase contentment not perfection.

Again , just my 2 cents...

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-15T17:43:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-51816>

I dont see it as extreme.......i see it as looking at life through our biochemistry. When you do that, what you thought was extreme because of socialization and culture then become bizarre. How you feel trumps what you are taught to believe is true. Once you get in tune with that it becomes pretty easy. Just my two cents.

### ealachan — 2012-09-15T18:12:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-51821>

> This is the issue I have with chasing "optimal" . You are more likely to reach paralysis and dissatisfaction if you continue the chase. So create your own fishbowl and chase contentment not perfection.
>

> Again , just my 2 cents...

I have to agree with Inger's post. I don't see "chasing optimal" as having given me MORE choices, but rather, as having simplified my life. I am one who truly used to get paralyzed by choice. Now, I have a few things I know to shoot for: fish, cold, sunlight, darkness. Everything else is just kinda...background noise, now. I know I'm not perfect, and frankly, I'm not worried about ever BEING perfect. I spent a whole lot of years feeling really effing \*\*\*\*ty, and starting to feel GOOD now is more than enough to keep me motivated to keep "chasing optimal".

Optimal, to me, is like nirvana - it's something you strive to acheive and you'll probably never actually GET it, but that's ok because it made you a better (or healthier) person through the striving. It's a life's work. It's not a quick fix.

### Barry — 2012-09-15T18:16:23+0300

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-15T21:51:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-51858>
