### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-15T23:00:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-51866>

I think I understand what Barry is alluding too......I agree with him that all of our present biochemistry is a puzzle......so everyone has their own fishbowl.......but where he and I dont agree.......there is an epistemologic basis of how the laws of biochemistry apply to our species.......and that is universal for our species. I am trying to show you how it works.......and not how we think it works. I guess that is where Barry has his issue..........so I get that.

### Linz — 2012-09-17T12:43:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-52082>

Barry, Ten years ago nothing was extreme in my fishbowl. I lived a normal, happy life with a great deal more respect for the natural environment than most. Brought up with home grown meat and veg and plenty of holidays camping on the coast and catching fish to cook for breakfast, healthy athletic children who were not afraid to go out in the freezing cold in winter or race to be first into the sea on holiday, they even enjoyed eating raw shellfish on occasion and no home computer. I enjoyed an athletic, outdoor lifestyle. My doctor even asked me what I did to stay so healthy!

Chronic illness puts a whole new perspective on life. A few symptoms and a hospital visit sent me sliding down a horrible slippery slope 'but don't worry this can be kept under control if you keep taking the medicine'. Just what I wanted to hear so I didn't listen to the tiny voice in my own head. Seven years ago when it became obvious that the drugs were not even masking the problem effectively and were themselves causing metabolic havoc I took a leap of faith and changed my diet radically. Thanks to Jack I now know the real reason why it worked (probably why it happened in the first place), why some things went wrong on the way, and I am making much more progress. When you face a lifetime (probably short) of chronic disease cold baths and dim lights don't really seem so extreme - and the raw fish was already an occasional part of normal life.

Could I run 100k....... I'm going to need a lot of good science first. Curing ourselves by taking our natural lifestyle to more extreme than is usual is hugely preferential to any kind of medical intervention. Would I have taken that more extreme action before seeing the effect of chronic disease and artificial steroid chaos on myself? Ice bath or a few little white tablets?

Some of the players in my orchestra still need some serious retraining, and all our children urgently need proper scientific education on their natural envronment so they never need to go extremes to rebalance.

### Dali Dula — 2012-09-17T14:12:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-52085>

Adaptation reduces extreme to normal.

### Barry — 2012-09-20T23:40:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-53014>

> **Jack Kruse;52480 wrote:** I think I understand what Barry is alluding too......I agree with him that all of our present biochemistry is a puzzle......so everyone has their own fishbowl.......but where he and I dont agree.......there is an epistemologic basis of how the laws of biochemistry apply to our species.......and that is universal for our species. I am trying to show you how it works.......and not how we think it works. I guess that is where Barry has his issue..........so I get that.

not quite. My issue is that what applies to our species now has several variations. This is due to adaptation, gene expression and mindset. Ironically, I think you are possibly overlooking the very thing you are focusing on.

In my opinion, the biggest thing the paleosphere and krusosphere has overlooked is how people have managed to live long healthy lives that don't conform to the "epistemolgic basis of the laws of biochemistry" ... why aren't we looking at the these people ?

Okay, so the Inuits prove that high fat/zero carb diets don't cause heart disease. Fine. But what about the french village, where the people ate croissants everyday, and never died from heart diseases, not given the same attention ?? What about the italian families that eat fish once a week, yet have never had any cognitive decline or disease and lived into there 90's ?? What about my Irish grandmother that hits 100 next week and is nowhere near senile, yet has lived on diary and starches ??

I am on the same side as the fence with you when it comes to the biochemistry. Its simply the application and the variation where my thoughts differ. You seem to believe that in order to be healthy, disease free etc that you need to follow the exact ancestral epi species specific way of eating and living. In terms of ethnicity, evolution and epigenetics, how can this not have changed ?? And aren't the people that I just described living proof of this ??

My 1-4 blogs are just spin-offs of the sort of mindset that this creates. Chasing perfection leads to extremism. There is no perfect. Choice leads to dissatisfaction. Ideology raises expectation. High expectation distances us from happiness. Happiness is the goal.

The questioning continues, thats all this is...

### Barry — 2012-09-21T18:36:46+0300

### Inger — 2012-09-22T08:16:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-53368>

> **Barry;53846 wrote:** The aim is to be constructive , that's the bottom line . Writing doesn't convey my tone.
>
>
>
> My goals are the same , real science that helps people. I am just looking at it from a completely different angle so my perception is different . I'm not claiming to be right and I'm not saying Jacks perception is wrong. I'm simply thinking out loud like you , but my thinking is just different to most on here.

I think I got it Barry!

Your perception is different from most of the forum because you are very healthy. That is why you think different..?

Most on here are very broken that's it. I think I see you now Barry.

Now, to regain health you need to change things dramatically. It won't work going on like you did before because that is what made you sick after all. But people are often afraid of change. That is when it becomes painful and a horror. When forced. It does not need to be. If we loose our fear for the unknown it is exciting to make changes, very much so. Then we start to feel like flying.. But it starts all - with our thoughts. Like you said.

And yeah.. I think it is very important to feel allowed to do baby steps. There is only so much we can take, not more.. but it gradually increases. I do very small babysteps all the time. And once in a while a big one..lol. But I have also quite a good health in comparison to many here (but.. when I see what I can reach I am so far from that - this fact makes it only so much more exciting to me). The sicker the more extreme/strict one needs to be... or the faster the healing. Kind of backwards huh. But I guess, when one is suffering grately one might also be more willing to do extreme things to feel well again soon. I hope so at least..

It requires a lot of willpower to turn the wagon.. when it is heavy. Where does the willpower comes from?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-22T15:21:56+0300

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-09-22T20:47:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-53441>

> **Barry;52251 wrote:
>
> It is this paradox of choice that I see happening everyday when it comes to health and fitness. You can go paleo, ketogenic, epi-paleo, VLC, LCHF, etc etc. You can do crossfit, movnat, tabatha, metcon, eccentric loading, high rep, low rep, run/walk, cycle/swim. These are all choices and they all have their merits. However, the amount of choice and the sheer number options is what can lead to paralysis. You simply cannot decide on what is right for you due to you not being able to figure out what is the best choice! The other problem that arises then is when you do actually make a choice and it doesn’t work, or more importantly, you think it doesn’t work as good as it should. This then leads to dissatisfaction and regret.
>
>
>
> Let me put this into perspective for the people following this forum. You now have the choice of light cycles to follow, blue light glasses to wear, fish smoothies to make, veg to ferment, temperatures at which to CT, hours to intermittent fast, oils to cook with and tests to take. These are all choices. You might CT at 55F, eat salmon, use ghee and fast for 14hrs. But you don’t lose weight and your health doesn’t improve. You then look and read further and realise that shrimp is better, you should cook with avocado oil and not ghee, CT at 45F is better and so is fasting for 16hrs. If this keeps happening, and you are provided with even more choice as you go along, you are on the road to paralysis and dissatisfaction. This is obviously completely counterproductive and is the antithesis of what you are trying to achieve. Contentment, happiness, positivity and self satisfaction are what you want with the aim of reducing the very thing that is likely causing your problems in the first place i.e. stress !
>
>
>
> “Beyond the walls of intelligence, life is defined”**

**

Hi Barry,

I have enjoyed the conversation you have started. In a lot of ways you are talking about a common problem that we all face. Where do we put our limited time and resources? **Chris Kresser** talks about focusing on your weakest link** not focusing on what we might know the most about or have formally studied:

[http://chriskresser.com/reflections-on-the-9-steps-the-biggest-obstacle-to-perfect-health](http://http://chriskresser.com/reflections-on-the-9-steps-the-biggest-obstacle-to-perfect-health)

From your website you know a lot about biochemistry, nutrition, and exercise. You also seem to have an interest in the power of the mind to heal. If we take Chris Kresser's post at face value, it would be of little use to you to focus on these areas for self-improvement. Your conductor has the orchestra playing beautifully in these areas. What are your weakest links?

Before I came to this forum, I didn't think very much about artificial light, though I knew about its dangers. I just had never done anything about it. (I haven't had a TV in 30 years and I am careful about EMF exposure.) After experimenting, I find artificial light at night does negatively effect my depth of sleep. Cold thermogenesis has had a less positive effect on me in the short term. I can not common on long term exposure but it does fit into what I would like my new life to look like. (I want my next house to be off-the-grid.) I do sleep in a naturally cold room and try not to fear getting cold when I do. My diet has been good for a long time; more seafood is a easy change but I don't go crazy because seafood is not a locally available food in my area.

I think my weakest link is I work way too much and I don't have enough fun. I also don't "work" my passions. How do I want to spend my most precious resource - my life energy? I only have one fragile life. Do I want to be remembered as a Jimmy Patterson or a Wendell Berry?

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jim\_Pattison](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jim_Pattison)

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wendell\_Berry](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wendell_Berry)

Some of my best work, or most meaningful work, is unpaid work. But I have realized where I am going the cash economic means little. I have decided to sell my business which might take some time. My business is my creation. You could call it a prison of my own making! The only problem is that I will just build another prison somewhere else. Or will I?

_The longest journey you’ll ever make is from your head to your heart._

**Sioux saying**

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-22T23:27:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-53485>

I am more in line with B on this one. . .my spin is this:

1\. When you take the 30K view of your life as a whole, perfect optimal for whatever you think that is - might not be the perfect path for the specifics of your life - the one you want, not the one you have.

2\. Practically speaking, does it make any sense to create stress and inflammation over minute details when there are big low-hanging fruits to pic?

Everybody chill, thnx!

### Barry — 2012-09-23T02:08:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-53519>

> **Jack Kruse;54141 wrote:** I dont disagree........but when you read ask Jack questions.......that is not how most human view their problems or their life. This site is about them getting answers they cant get at the doc.........there in lies the paradox.

The "Ask Jack" questions are proof of my 1-4 blogs. People don't go on feel, they chase magic bullets, they focus on the tree's instead of the woods and they don't create their own fish bowl.

For the record, I'm not looking for any fans here. I'm just telling it how it is. That's it.

I think my view is skewed though in terms of what people are dealing with here. I'm looking at health from a health body and mind. Most on this site are looking at health from a sick body and mind. This means my biochemistry works differently so my points are not equivocal in that sense. Therefore, I question the continuation of this series in terms of its relevance... I don;t think people see where I am coming from, apart from a few and those that have DM'd me.

So I might just put an end to this, not because its wrong, but more because it is likely to cause more confusion to those on this site than constructiveness

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-23T03:09:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-53534>

> **Barry;54161 wrote:** The "Ask Jack" questions are proof of my 1-4 blogs. People don't go on feel, they chase magic bullets, they focus on the tree's instead of the woods and they don't create their own fish bowl.
>
>
>
> For the record, I'm not looking for any fans here. I'm just telling it how it is. That's it.
>
>
>
> I think my view is skewed though in terms of what people are dealing with here. I'm looking at health from a health body and mind. Most on this site are looking at health from a sick body and mind. This means my biochemistry works differently so my points are not equivocal in that sense. Therefore, I question the continuation of this series in terms of its relevance... I don;t think people see where I am coming from, apart from a few and those that have DM'd me.
>
>
>
> So I might just put an end to this, not because its wrong, but more because it is likely to cause more confusion to those on this site than constructiveness

What I like about your posts, Barry, isn't about whether I agree or not (often I don't know what I think!). I like that you are articulating a clear position and that you question. I don't want to blindly follow anything or anyone and I like that you come from a 'healthy' position with a combined understanding of the science. I may be trying to make up health ground that you are not, but that doesn't invalidate what you say. I like that you are provocative, too.

For me, the thematic link between those who 'chase magic bullets' and those who 'create their own fishbowls' is fear. The 'chasing' of an answer (or even believing there is an answer) comes mostly from a place of fear and anxiety. I know that place well. It is perhaps the most toxic thing that we inherit from the CW mindset. And it may be your position of health that means you don't have such intimate experience with this sort of fear. When you create your own fish bowl (as I understand it from what you've explained) it revolves around self-empowerment and self-design, to create something that makes sense and works for you in a positive way. Inherent in that when you are 'broken' means taking a huge leap of faith in yourself. If that isn't something that comes naturally to a person, you have to dig really deep because it will never be encouraged by those who are your medical support out there in the world. It's hard to convey in words what this is like when you are not healthy. You turn your back on everything that is set up to help you and essentially tell them they are both wrong and potentially harmful (and incompetent to help you in the way you now understand you need to be helped). Dr K often says that others in the paleo community struggle to understand where he comes from because they've never been obese. I think the same is true for people who have never had a health crisis. It's hard to make a bridge to connect the positions and tension emerges. For me that bridge is fear and how much it guides what we see, what we can't see, and what we're willing to see.

I can embrace your position while also wanting to learn and understand more because my life is not a sole focus on my physical health. I was diagnosed with cancer but during the entire thing, I was able to remain authentically grateful and happy, even while scared and sad, to. My fish bowl is constructed in spiritual terms and my physical/medical health resides within that. Learning through Dr K has meant that I have let go of a lot, changed a lot and questioned a lot. But it is a process that is integrated within my larger value system and who I am in a very genuine way.

Last thought: another reason I appreciate your different point of view is because I, too, hope to be able to sit from a position of total health and re-evaluate my life. I don't expect to always be in catch-up/recovery mode. And I like the view you offer, even if it isn't mine.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-23T03:14:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-53537>

perspective colors your life.........KiwiLauren paints with that brush.

### Barry — 2012-09-23T19:22:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-53624>

> **Jack Kruse;54166 wrote:** I agree with Barry..........I dont think your skewed........I think your the pot at the end of the rainbow that they are all striving to find. It should not end...........Never let people behavior's interupt your stream of consciousness........especially when your white hot. If they cant handle it........that is on them. I like where your taking this.

I'm not afraid of others opinion or afraid of taking criticism. My consciousness won't change and I'lll stick to my guns. The problem is more related to the scientific basis of my arguments. Just like the physiology of a cold adapted person versus a warm adapted person is different, the same applies for a sick/obese person versus a healthy/fit person. Therefore, the function and understanding of hormones, cell signalling, partitioning, inflammation, gene expression etc... works differently for me. Thus, what I recommend and what I know works for me may not apply to the biochemistry of people on this forum. So my information is skewed.

### prAna303 — 2012-09-23T19:44:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-53628>

Have never been sick for a day in my life and i am also a ultrarunner. Not racing anymore, just for the pure love of motion. To have a good dynamic in this forum your voice is needed because with time many others will soon be seeing things more from your point of view. Keep your thought coming...

### Dali Dula — 2012-10-02T20:15:15+0300

I would also like to say that as a person who has quested spiritually with no impressive satisfaction dropping into circadian life is one of the most profound experiences I've ever had. As I surf the diminishing light of autumn I feel a sense of peace I have never encountered. It's all here if you let it guide youl. I'm not sure what it is, but I am following.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-15T02:14:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/slow-cookers.3666/post-51733>

> **Lyndra;52311 wrote:** I'm in the market for a new one. I love mine (an old Corning ware version) but it only holds about 3 quarts. I'd like something double that size. Every time I start looking there are too many choices (per Barry's recent post) and I get frustrated and stop looking. The only hard-and-fast requirement is not teflon (or other non-stick) coating. I'm willing to pay more to get quality and durability. Anybody have one they love and can recommend?

I bought a big 7 q one at Kohls, on sale for $19. Hate it. It's low is too high, it boils. My ancient (from college!) one which is small is great. Let me know if you find something, I keep eyeing the pricey ones at Williams and Sonoma, but that seems crazy to spend on one of those. At least if I spent that much, I would definately return it if it didn't simmer low enough!

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-15T02:51:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/slow-cookers.3666/post-51741>

> **Jack Kruse;52330 wrote:** The girls in my office bought me a stainless one for Xmas last yr.......I already need a new one.

I got the same Crockpot Stainless Steel and it committed suicide a couple nights ago. Boiled over or something. And the handle of the lid melted off.  Lasted all but a few months. Going back to Bed Bath and Beyond.

### tweety — 2012-09-15T03:34:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/slow-cookers.3666/post-51749>

It's not 7 qts, but I got a vita clay slow cooker (really for rice) last year and I LOVE it! My only complaint is I should have gotten the bigger one. It was about $70, and so worth it. I don't worry about any toxins, and it makes wonderful broths and stews.

For bigger items I bought a large basic crockpot with the black enamel coating. I think I found it at target for around $50? It's good for larger batches of broth or big roasts. But the flavor doesn't compare to the clay cooker.

### Lyndra — 2012-09-15T05:03:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/slow-cookers.3666/post-51758>

Yeah, a time or two I've been close to buying an expensive one online (Amazon or W&S) and then I read the reviews and get discouraged. Nobody seems to like them, the insert breaks, etc. I should have also said I want one that is safe to leave cooking for hours while we're not home. I'm using a pot on the stove these days but I really don't want to leave the house with a burner turned on.

### vkiernan — 2012-09-15T15:39:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/slow-cookers.3666/post-51796>

I have a Hamilton Beach and I absolutely love it. It has a ceramic insert and cleans very nice. I love that I can program it for how many hours I want and then it automatically goes to a warm cycle until you shut it off or 24 hours have passed. It also has a thermometer to cook by temp. So far, no downsides.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-15T16:01:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/slow-cookers.3666/post-51800>

We replaced our "crockpot" brand this past spring. Found a 7-9qt oval one on amazon w/free shipping for around $30

That thing gets a workout at least once/week

### JanSz — 2012-09-15T15:16:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fatty-acid-profile-how-does-it-look.3667/post-51789>

Scan each page of your Fatty Acid Profile test report, post it here.

Name the laboratory that did the testing.

Scan and post all other labs (on this thread) if you wish).

Post information about your self.

...........

You are presenting 6 fatty acids on your post.

Krieger lab (that dr Kane uses) tests for 43 fatty acids

Genova Diagnostics tests for 24

\-----------------------------------------------------------------

The good thing about your laboratory is that it presents upper limits.

EPA 3.1% (.1%-2.5%)

too much

if you reduce EPA, AA will go up.

You need DHA & AA.

It is better to be in mid range on all fatty acids than have excess of EPA.

If you eat fish oil or krill oil they usually contain way too much of both, but specially too much of EPA.

...

Your lab is using considerably different ranges when compared to Kriegers lab that I compare to.

...

Some transfats are natural (and we pay $$ to get them).

Which trans fats have you tested?

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trans\_fat](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trans_fat)

> The process of hydrogenation adds hydrogen atoms to unsaturated fats, eliminating double bonds and making them into partially or completely saturated fats. However, partial hydrogenation, if it is chemical rather than enzymatic, converts a part of cis-isomers into trans-unsaturated fats instead of hydrogenating them completely.
>
>
>
> Trans fats also occur naturally in a limited number of cases: Vaccenyl and **conjugated linoleyl (CLA)** containing trans fats occur naturally in trace amounts in meat and dairy products from ruminants, although the latter also constitutes a cis fat.
>
>

>
>
>
>
>

# Thread: Seriously stubborn kid
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sem — 2012-09-15T07:16:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-51767>

I started adding seafood into my kids' diets about a month or so ago. I try to have one meal a day with some form of seafood. It doesnt happen every day but about 5 days a week. My 8 year old doesn't love it but eats it to be done with it. My 5 year old has a complete and total fit about it EVERY SINGLE TIME. I'm talking tears, screaming, gagging, the works. It can't be healthy to have this kind of scene every night at dinner. Anyone have a similar issue?

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-15T15:54:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-51798>

Yep 2yo did this initially. Then we did the whole if you didn't eat breakfast then it becomes lunch if you didn't eat it for lunch it becomes dinner & so on. No snacks either. Eventually they'll get it & they'll start eating

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-15T16:31:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-51804>

You think it is healthy to give in to what he wants at 5? you control his environment, you are the parent. IF he does not like dont let him eat. Gretchen is spot on here.

### Sem — 2012-09-15T17:34:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-51814>

That's exactly what I'm doing. I just worry that I am creating negative emotions around food. But I guess I just have to wait him out. Last night he went to bed with no dinner and total hysteria. I'm just surprised it has lasted this long!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-15T17:41:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-51815>

who cares about WHAT might happen when you already know what happens when you eat bad food. That is how bad thinking leads to suboptimal living. Eradicate that thinking and you will have optimal kids.

### Sem — 2012-09-15T20:14:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-51831>

> **Jack Kruse;52428 wrote:** who cares about WHAT might happen when you already know what happens when you eat bad food. That is how bad thinking leads to suboptimal living. Eradicate that thinking and you will have optimal kids.

Good way to think about it! That helps. Thanks.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-15T21:01:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-51842>

> **Sem;52427 wrote:** That's exactly what I'm doing. I just worry that I am creating negative emotions around food. But I guess I just have to wait him out. Last night he went to bed with no dinner and total hysteria. I'm just surprised it has lasted this long!

What really turned the corner for me was realizing eating disorders are brain chemistry disorders. You don't "give" someone an eating disorder.

We did that whole tantrum and no dinner, and get served it for the next meal. Worked pretty quickly too. Still get fusses every now and then. . but he is 6, they come with a built in "I dont' like it" for everything right?

The reality is they are not going to starve themselves at this age, if their bodies need food - they will eat what you give them!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-16T04:30:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-51893>

> **Jack Kruse;52417 wrote:** You think it is healthy to give in to what he wants at 5? you control his environment, you are the parent. IF he does not like dont let him eat. Gretchen is spot on here.

Sem, I agree with what has been said. But I think there is another piece to the puzzle that can help. That is the relational piece. When we as parents have to insist upon something and be in charge, that's just a fact of parenting life. There are a lot of times we have to set limits with our children or enforce rules that make them upset but we know it's important to do. Our children must be able to count on us to do what is right, regardless of how much they don't like it. However, the relational piece means that we can do this with compassion. The ability to see things from the child's perspective and to empathize helps them regulate their emotions and bodies (and HPA axis) and can deepen their connection to use, even in the face of something they don't like. So while you insist that this IS dinner, you can also say 'I can see how much you don't like this. I can see how angry you are that I won't fix you something else. It's really hard to have to eat something you don't want to, isn't it?" We don't have to be stern or punitive when we are setting limits.

As parents, we often think we have to solve our children's problems. But sometimes (most times), what they need is to be seen and understood. If we move to fix everything instead of staying with the experience, they don't learn how to tolerate difficulty and negotiate it. And to learn that they are always - always - connected to us in a heartfelt way, no matter what sort of issue we are dealing with. You can retain your parental authority AND be kind and compassionate. This sort of combo can heal a lot of ills.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-16T19:08:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-51939>

> **KiwiLauren;52507 wrote:** Sem, I agree with what has been said. But I think there is another piece to the puzzle that can help. That is the relational piece. When we as parents have to insist upon something and be in charge, that's just a fact of parenting life. There are a lot of times we have to set limits with our children or enforce rules that make them upset but we know it's important to do. Our children must be able to count on us to do what is right, regardless of how much they don't like it. However, the relational piece means that we can do this with compassion. The ability to see things from the child's perspective and to empathize helps them regulate their emotions and bodies (and HPA axis) and can deepen their connection to use, even in the face of something they don't like. So while you insist that this IS dinner, you can also say 'I can see how much you don't like this. I can see how angry you are that I won't fix you something else. It's really hard to have to eat something you don't want to, isn't it?" We don't have to be stern or punitive when we are setting limits.
>
>
>
> As parents, we often think we have to solve our children's problems. But sometimes (most times), what they need is to be seen and understood. If we move to fix everything instead of staying with the experience, they don't learn how to tolerate difficulty and negotiate it. And to learn that they are always - always - connected to us in a heartfelt way, no matter what sort of issue we are dealing with. You can retain your parental authority AND be kind and compassionate. This sort of combo can heal a lot of ills.

Just Awesome Lauren... true parenting from a primal spirit.

### indigogirl — 2012-09-16T20:14:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-51953>

Lauren, that was wonderful advice, not just for parenting, but as a way to treat all human beings.

We all just want to be acknowledged.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-16T21:56:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-51965>

> **KiwiLauren;52507 wrote:** Sem, I agree with what has been said. But I think there is another piece to the puzzle that can help. That is the relational piece. When we as parents have to insist upon something and be in charge, that's just a fact of parenting life. There are a lot of times we have to set limits with our children or enforce rules that make them upset but we know it's important to do. Our children must be able to count on us to do what is right, regardless of how much they don't like it. However, the relational piece means that we can do this with compassion. The ability to see things from the child's perspective and to empathize helps them regulate their emotions and bodies (and HPA axis) and can deepen their connection to use, even in the face of something they don't like. So while you insist that this IS dinner, you can also say 'I can see how much you don't like this. I can see how angry you are that I won't fix you something else. It's really hard to have to eat something you don't want to, isn't it?" We don't have to be stern or punitive when we are setting limits.
>
>
>
> As parents, we often think we have to solve our children's problems. But sometimes (most times), what they need is to be seen and understood. If we move to fix everything instead of staying with the experience, they don't learn how to tolerate difficulty and negotiate it. And to learn that they are always - always - connected to us in a heartfelt way, no matter what sort of issue we are dealing with. You can retain your parental authority AND be kind and compassionate. This sort of combo can heal a lot of ills.

Thanks so much for this Lauren, Such a nice reminder to me that guiding and correcting behavior does not have to be me bossing and bitching at my child! Sometimes in efforts to be stern, I find myself using a stern tone, and jumping in with my fast talking speech (instead of honoring his slower pace). This was perfectly said.

### Sem — 2012-09-17T08:25:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-52074>

Couldn't agree more Lauren. I have a problem walking that line sometimes. I'm right there with them - the model of patience and compassion. And they keep pushing and pushing and bam! I lose it. All out the window. Very hard.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-17T21:38:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-52192>

> **Sem;52691 wrote:** Couldn't agree more Lauren. I have a problem walking that line sometimes. I'm right there with them - the model of patience and compassion. And they keep pushing and pushing and bam! I lose it. All out the window. Very hard.

Sem, I think we ALL have trouble walking that line. It's really important to be kind to yourself and also to 'stay with' your own experience. As you are talking them through theirs, you can talk through your own (internally), reminding yourself that it's bloody hard to be a parent, not to mention one pushing an epi-paleo template!  I also think the psycho-neurological concept of 'rupture and repair' is critical here. Not only does an apology mend things, but it shows our kids that we can get reconnected even after big breaks in connection. Relationships are never smooth and easy, no matter how much we love someone. It's important for kids to learn how to negotiate the rough patches; and it's important for us to know this can be done! Good luck. I really think we're all in this same boat with you, so hang in there.

### Linz — 2012-09-20T02:30:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-52825>

I love your thoughts Lauren. When my children were small I always told them to taste everything we ate but never made them finish it so they didn't feel threatened by something they really disliked. Often they surprised themselves (and me) and usually ate anything they were given but I always let them have have something else healthy but not a favourite if they had really tried our meal first.

A little of the thing they thought they were not going to like usually became a whole meal without protest quite soon and I was rapidly overtaken as far as shellfish were concerned - catching up now but MHS did make me consider a screaming tantrum! Seeing it back on my plate at breakfast wouldn't have made me want to try again.

### Sem — 2012-09-21T06:55:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-53120>

Thanks ladies. Appreciate all the reassurance. I'm glad some people have made it through to the other side. We had another terrible experience at dinner tonight with him over eating cod. I think it's become a huge power play or something. I hope to see some positive changes soon.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-21T17:37:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-53188>

One thing that became a family habit for us was "Courtesy Bites". You must take courtesy bites of any food you are served - one day you may be having dinner with the president or a dictator, and refusing could get you killed! We play it up like a game, it's been fun.

It goes right into "you have to eat it 7 times and then your tongue will like it".

So, when we know he likes the food -we make him eat it, or serve it for the next meal, this isn't a restaurant. . if you are hungry you eat your food. Whoever pays gets a say! and on and on.

But when it's a new food or one he doesn't like - we go the courtesy bite route! I put my hand to my forehead and swoon and say. . .you MUST, I can't have my baby being killed by a crazy dictator over fish eyes (or whatever).

More than working for him, making it fun keeps ME on track and not gravitating to that bossy dictator tone that sets up a power play.

I would like the blog to have a global feed for comments, just like Peter Attia's [http://eatingacademy.com/](http://http://eatingacademy.com/) with [http://eatingacademy.com/comments/feed](http://http://eatingacademy.com/comments/feed), its icon in between the _tweeter and the blog posts feed_ ones.

# Thread: Two steps forward, one step back
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/two-steps-forward-one-step-back.3676/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 50
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-09-15T14:30:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/two-steps-forward-one-step-back.3676/post-51784>

I have been wanting to start a journal for quite some time. Today is the day.

The back story: I have 4 kids (11, 9, 6, 3). The last 3 were born at home, all breastfed until after 2 years old. I have hashimoto's. It started when I was in my early 20s. Synthroid worked "fine" for me, and life kept going. Each pregnancy resulted in a 60 pound weight gain, followed by easy loss over the course of a year - except the last one when the weight loss never happened. I fought off some of the pounds, and was left with an extra 15, but I was weight stable, and could eat "whatever I wanted."

When my 4th was 16 months old, we got a really bad herpes infection. My mouth was covered in painful sores. I could not eat for 10 days, the pain was so bad! I dropped my extra 15 pounds. Then shortly after, I decided to try an elimination diet because my baby seemed to be reacting to all kinds of foods and we couldn't sort it out. Since she was nursing, I did the diet too. Turkey, rice, pears, palm oil, then gradually we added a few things in - butter. Everytime we tried to add in something else, there would be trouble and we'd back off. This went on for 6 weeks. I stayed at my new found low weight. I ate a lot of rice and pears, and brown rice syrup, and rice cakes...

She started receiving immunotherapy shots, and we were gradually able to get off the diet. I started adding in foods like crazy. It was December, and there was lots to add in!! My weight ballooned. 1-3 pounds each day. I gain 15 pounds in 3 weeks, and finally in a panic go to low carb eating. Weight gain stops. But I cannot touch a carb without gaining.

Feb 2011 - I start P90X. I am determined to lose this extra weight. I gain strength and endurance quickly, but no weight loss. I have continual trouble with never feeling full/satiated, ever. I am always thinking about eating, even low carb foods.

Sept 2011 - Continuing the LDA shots with 4th child and myself. The protocol includes 3 days of very limited food choices to minimize cross-reactivity with ingredients in the shots (food/air allergens). I get fed up, and decided I would feel better just fasting. I water fast for 5 days, and I'm delighted to lose the weight! I continue to intermittent fast throughout the fall. I am tired of low carb eating (and starting to gain slowly on the diet), and fasting is the only thing that seems to â€œworkâ€￾ to maintain my weight. I often do days of not eating until 4:30pm onwards. I eat LOTS after 4:30, and I don't restrict. This is my way to â€œhave my cake and eat it too.â€￾ Sometimes I don't eat for 2 days.

Jnauary 2012 - I can't keep up the intermittent fasting to maintain my weight. I gain 8 pounds over 6 days at Christmas, and then I gain another 11 pounds in 3 more weeks eating â€œregularâ€￾. I decide to start looking for other answers.

I do another elimination diet (turkey only) for 2 days, and then slowly add foods back in to figure out which foods are causing such dramatic weight gain/water retention results. I am horrified at the results. Foods that cause quick, dramatic weight gain (1-2 pounds/day):

eggs

all nuts, seeds

dairy

all grains and starches

mushrooms

apples

avocado

green beans

pork, especially cured.

ground chicken, ground pork

suspect foods: summer squash, sauerkruat?

March 2012 - I go to the Women to Women clinic in Maine. They do extensive GI testing, and salivary HPA axis testing. I am insulin resistant. I am at my highest postpartum weight and climbing.

March 2012 - I begin immediately on a strict diet including 3meals/day only (to combat the endless eating habit), and only eat the foods I know are safe (basically meats and vegetables, cocoa is okay too). I start taking insinase, probiotics, and digestive enzymes. I slowly start dropping weight. Aroundt his time I discovered the leptin RX and tweaked it into my plan.

I try to shift from synthroid to Armour thyroid. It is difficult. I end up very hypothyroid on 2 grains, with adrenal symptoms as well (high heart rate, low blood pressure, orthostatic hypo-tension).

June 2012 I begin taking DHEA per saliva labs. I take 4mg/day sublingually. My thyroid dose increases to 4.5 grains of Armour, 25mcg synthroid. I don't seem to absorb much of the T4 in the Armour.

I continue to struggle with high morning heart rates (and low blood pressure), which I attribute to low T4 levels. Labs show reasonable T3, low normal T4. In hindsight, I don't think the low T4 is the cause, but not sure what may be.

July 2012 My weight has really dropped off - 20 pounds since March. I am 3 pounds from my â€œwedding weight,â€￾ previously optimal, never gain or lose weight, but I still cannot deviate on the eating plan without immediate weight gain. I ate a small piece of kielbasa at a BBQ, went up 3 pounds overnight.

August 2012 - I am forced to drop back to synthroid only due to debilitating symptoms. I drop DHEA, Armour, and pregnenelone. High heartrates continue all day long, and low blood pressure. I was no longer functional to care for my family. This takes my T3 from top 1/4 of the range, down to below the bottom end. I feel a different kind of awful! Depression sets in, whereas all summer, even though I felt poorly physically, mentally I was optimistic.

Next entry: the weight I lost while doing the leptin RX, plus adjusting my thyroid meds starts coming back on, 10 pounds over 4 weeks, and I'm losing more foods.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-18T20:35:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/two-steps-forward-one-step-back.3676/post-52466>

Yesterday I finally took the plunge and found fresh raw oysters at a local fish market. The guy shcucked them for me right then and there, put them on ice with lemon, and told me to eat within a hour! No time for procrastination - I went home, and ate my first raw oysters ever. I was expecting to not enjoy them, but I was really very pleasantly surprised! I ate them all, and kept thinking about eating more of them all afternoon.

I seem to be gaining some traction. My energy levels are improving, especially yesterday (was it the oysters, and the iodoral I threw in?). I'm guessing my thyroid hormones are improving. I'll retest in another week or 2 and see where I'm at. Still taking 150mcg tirosint (hoping for better absorption from regular synthroid), and 15mg cytomel. I can feel it when it's time for my next cytomel dose. I start to feel my heart pounding just a little stronger. I feel like there's a little bit or air-hunger. I take the T3 and it all goes away.

I ate an apple yesterday, and expected the worst today. Instead I dropped almost 2 pounds overnight. This in addition to dropping 2 pounds overnight Sunday. Something seems to be working...

I stopped the cycloset 2 days ago, to see if it would help the new neuropathy in my fingers. I am trying to stay calm about it (going to the neurologist on Thursday) until I can get more testing done. Not sure what to test to further rule out (or diagnose) LADA. Trying to stay calm...

Been seeing the sunrise every morning. The past several mornings it comes up a bright orange ball of fireo nthe horizon, just a minute or 2 after I get outside. Spectacular! Blue-blockers are on at dusk, and I'm putting my littlest to bed at 7:30ish (she asks), and I stay in the dark.

I've been avoiding my favorite roasted veggies - brussel sprouts, Kale, and cauliflower. All seem to make me gain weight (water retention). This is new in the past month or so. I'd been eating them all summer long without a problem. I'll try cooking them at lower heat and see how it goes. I dropped down to fish only on Saturday, and was going to slowly add foods back in to see which were causing the water retention (given the 10 pounds increase in the past 4-5 week). I dropped 3.5 pounds overnight. I was pretty surprised considering that's how much I used to dropped after 24 hours of water fasting. I was already low carb going in... Then the next day I failed at my plan of adding the foods in one by one. Ate fish for breakfast, but added in too many things at lunch - some beef in broth (cooked with many vegetables), and then proceeded on to eat 1.5 of the apples we had just picked. Regular epi-paleo dinner. I expected the worst for Sunday morning, but only found I had regained 1 pound.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-19T02:01:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/two-steps-forward-one-step-back.3676/post-52567>

Okay something is starting to have some serious traction - 2 days in a row of feeling pretty darn good and energetic. I'd forgotten what it feels like to have energy and actually want to get stuff done! And then, this afternoon, did I finally feel satiety after a meal? Twinges of it anyways. Is it the seafood with an extra push on shellfish lately? The raw oysters yesterday (they're still on my mind)? Is my thyroid hormone dose finally starting to work somewhat reasonably? Have I found the right supplements to support all of this (took a iodoral the past 2 days)? Are the shorter days (less light exposure) helping? I pushed my T3 dose up too. I've been taking 15mg for the past 10ish days and late this afternoon felt the familiar feeling of needing to take my next dose. So I pushed up to 20mg and so far so good... Signing off the computer - it's dark out now!

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-20T15:39:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/two-steps-forward-one-step-back.3676/post-52888>

Never mind the 20mg the other day, yesterday I could barely do 15, and had elevated heart rate for most of the afternoon/evening. I wonder if my T4 meds are actually having an impact and maybe even some converting is going on?

After yesterday's re-read of BG 12, I felt like I had just been landed a terminal illness diagnosis. It was all going well until I read that Hashi's people don't get to use iodine very well because of the destroyed TpO enzyme. I looked at my last labs, and my antibody levels were 82. It's been much higher in the past... I'm not sure if my understanding is correct (and I will have to go look up cites to feel better (worse) about it), because none of the other hashi's people seem to be in a mild panic the way I am. I felt like I should start getting my affairs in order lol!

Off to the neurologist this morning to find out more about the reduced feeling in my fingers, not to mention the elevated heartrates. But after reading B12, I think I get it. Reduced iodine levels make for reduced synaptic function since it's needs in large quantities in the neuron synapses for optimal signalling. The next stop is the epithelial cancers.

### Lyndra — 2012-09-21T00:06:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/two-steps-forward-one-step-back.3676/post-53024>

It sounds like you are doing quite a bit better than a while ago, so that's something to be optimistic about. How say your TPO antibodies have also dropped - how much? are you following a complete AI protocol diet? Good luck with the neurologist, I hope you get the neuropathy figured out.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-21T02:16:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/two-steps-forward-one-step-back.3676/post-53053>

The neurologist was uneventful. I passed the tests (I already knew that). He said he wanted to MRI my brain just to make there's no MS starting. I'm not sure how I feel about doing that procedure. The neuropathy has improved (but it's not gone), and I'm wondering if more time with improved circulating thyroid hormones will make it better still. Still wondering how the cycloset fits in (if at all). The neuropathy didn't start until 10 days into taking the cycloset. I've stopped for the time being...

I don't have labs from several years back, but I think my TpO antibodies used to be the 260ish range. Not sure what brought them down specifically, although I wonder if the gluten free diet (3 years) played a big role. I'll ask to get my antibodies tested again in a few more months. Those levels were from April (paleo + AI protocol, but not epi unti mid-summer). Thanks for checking in!

### kathylu — 2012-09-21T02:59:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/two-steps-forward-one-step-back.3676/post-53064>

Just found this listed as a side effect for cycloset:

Musculoskeletal

Musculoskeletal side effects have included muscle cramps, facial pallor, abnormal involuntary movements, ataxia, edema of the feet and ankles, and erythromelalgia.

Rare musculoskeletal side effects including tingling of fingers, cold feet, numbness, muscle cramps in feet and legs, and exacerbation of Raynaud's Syndrome have been reported during bromocriptine therapy.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-21T03:33:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/two-steps-forward-one-step-back.3676/post-53080>

Yay that you've had some good days!!!! You are doing so much, you deserve them. I'm sure it's one of those non-linear things, but I bet you get more and more as time goes on.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-25T21:03:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/two-steps-forward-one-step-back.3676/post-54205>

I am feeling much, much better these days than over the summer.

However, I think the sweet spot of my new thyroid regimen went sailing by last week, and now I'm crossing into the land of over-driven. I can't seem to handle any T3 anymore. My heartrate is high into the afternoons now (80 just now), but blood pressure is holding reasonable. I just feel over-pushed. I had been doing the equivalent of 150mcg T4/day, but I've dropped down to 112, and had blood drawn this morning. My endo won't run an RT3. She says the Mayo Clinic found the lab to be non-reproduceable... I haven't checked into it yet to refute it, but she did agree to run an HS-CRP instead. I am also supping 10000iu of D/day now, and my level a month ago was 54 (I went UP over the summer, 10 points, without supplementing!! Maybe I'm not so horrendously broken after all??!). I'm wondering if this is somehow pushing me more hyper. Gotta say I'm feeling pretty darn great though the past week or more.

Tomorrow morning I will have the MRI of my brain... I couldn't decide whether to go through with it or not, but at this point I've run out of cancellation time.

Still have lots of food issues. Went apple picking with the kids on Saturday, and ate an apple. Then on Sunday I ate a small amount of cantaloupe I was cutting up for them for their lunches. Yesterday I was up 2.5 pounds in the morning, complete with my usual sore left wrist that goes along. I dialed back in my diet and this morning I dropped 3 pounds... I'm taking this as a good sign. In the past it would take several days to clear that kind of inflammation. Now it is leaving more quickly.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-26T20:32:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/two-steps-forward-one-step-back.3676/post-54492>

Did the MRI this morning. I didn't think I was claustrophobic... until I saw where they planned to put me!! The procedure went fine. I settled in after about 5 mintues and felt calm enough to lie with my eyes opened. My procedure involved an IV contrast as well, for extra creepiness. Overall, it's the kind of procedure that leaves you feeling somewhat "handled," for lack of better words, despite wonderful staff, etc. I wasn't sure whether or not to go through with the scan, but it's done now.

Did my thyroid labs yesterday, and I am trying to be patient for the results. I had pushed my T3 all the way to 15mg, but have gradually found I needed to back to down. Today I've done 7.5 so far. Not sure if I will add in another 2.5 later in the day or not. Yesterday I took no T3 (I usually take 5 first thing in the morning, and I didn't want it to be in my bloodwork, and then I didn't feel like I needed it later). And put on of the pounds I had just finished peeing out the day before. And my diet was rock solid. Sooo, T3 levels play a huge role in my level of water retention. I'll have to read up on this some more. And today I feel a little more hypo than my new usual.

Dh has been out of town the past few nights, and usually means crappier sleep for me. All the sounds of the night are suddenly only my responsibility, and I'm sole protector of my sleeping children! I'm ready, I'm oxidizing all night long lol!!

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-28T20:46:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/two-steps-forward-one-step-back.3676/post-55052>

Wow - oysters roasted (400, 20 minutes!) with green onions and garlic on top - wow!! I haven't been enjoying this necessary organism very much, and have been avoiding them. Now I need to figure out how to source more!!

I seem to be still trending upwards, feeling better and better. Brain fog is lifting, energy level is improving, overall I am feeling more stable. Still awaiting thyroid bloodwrok results from Tuesday. Could it be that T4 therapy with a touch of T3, is actually being effective?

I have been taking magnesium today (citrate), trying to find bowel tolerance (and beyond). I've been using the powdered calm form dissolved in hot water, and I've been drinking and drinking, and still not getting results... Yesterday I took magnesium malate, tons of it, and not results. How much do I need to take...?

My weight and water fluctuations are still terrible.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-30T00:38:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/two-steps-forward-one-step-back.3676/post-55299>

Bye bye microfilms... at least here's hoping. Not taking any more Mg for the time being. I've also thrown down some enzymes in the hopes of breaking up the films a little further.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-01T19:24:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/two-steps-forward-one-step-back.3676/post-55603>

Mg doesn't seem to be giving me the consistent results that I think are needed for this biofilm business. I've been reading up about them, and it seems there's a lot to breaking them up, not to mention the systemic effects that are felt as they are compromised and bacteria is released. This site seems to have a lot of thought provoking information. [http://coolinginflammation.blogspot.com/2009/09/cure-for-inflammatory-diseases.html](http://http://coolinginflammation.blogspot.com/2009/09/cure-for-inflammatory-diseases.html) Also other places I've looked at mention that this process can take many months - the right enzymes taken between meals can perforate these biofilms and start to compromise them... I still need to hear the Q&A from the webinar (will check next if it's up yet) to hopefully grab more info.

Meanwhile, on the homefront, I'm feeling very proud of myself. Last night we had a pipe start leaking raw sewage (which was thankfully shower water at that time) into the basement. What a mess! Dh and I were up for many hours dealing with the aftermath of all that water. Me up past 9pm is an extra-ordinary day lol, and there we were with towels and buckets and bleach. I didn't have my blueblockers on, and I was very thankful for all that artificial light lol! We contained the problem, but dh couldn't figure out if it was a partial plug in one of our pipes, or into the septic tank. He went out to the septic tank and didn't notice anything. We still had minimal water use. This morning the plumber was supposed to come... Well, I decided to get out open the septic tank, and take another look, and what should I see but a gigantic wad of toilet paper plugging the incoming pipe. I grab a stick, clear the plug, and the water starts flowing again. Hooray!! I cancel the plumber. All my children are going out there this afternoon after school to see the final destination of all things put down the toilet. Maybe that will help curb excessive toilet paper use... Dh can stick to making microchips, I'll handle the home-front

Now back to all things epi-paleo...

### Endless — 2012-10-11T03:53:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/two-steps-forward-one-step-back.3676/post-57508>

You know, I listened to the Q&A and Jack said something about Calm having a bunch of not so great stuff in it...but I don't know what and all it says on the label is stevia, magnesium carbonate and citric acid (to make magnesium citrate). Not sure what is so bad about those!

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-16T01:51:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/two-steps-forward-one-step-back.3676/post-58453>

Posting otday because it's been some time...

I'm 13 pounds up since I hit my "goal" weight back in August. I am trying not to worry about it, but it's frustrating. I am hoping that as my thyroid labs improve, the weight will slow, stop, and then reverse..? Here's hoping... Meanwhile it's showing me how much my weight responds to T3 blood levels. I never used to have a weight issue, even when very hypoT. In the past when my meds needed adjusting, my (post-partum, effortless) weight loss would just cease until the issue was corrected (with more T4), and then I'd continue downward. Now it's different, not to mention I'm not absorbing my T4 much. That never used to happen either.

I started taking HCL with my meals. I figure that if I'm hypoT, my HCL levels are probably lower than they should be. What I notice of significant difference is that I feel more HUNGRY, especially an hour or 2 after a meal. My stomach feels different... The hunger feels almost like the endless hunger I used to feel in the first few months of pregnancy (without the nausea). No more undigested food passing through either.

I am still struggling with never feeling satisfied after eating a meal. I always wish I could keep on eating. Maybe I am getting a little traction - when i eat my BAB, I don't feel like I want to eat more of it. In the summer, I did, even at crazy high amounts of protein. I guess that's something. But it's not turning off the desire for "other" foods, that's for sure. That is an endless battle.

Honestly, I am struggling to keep my resolve and stay with the program. I am eating more apples that I should be, and I know there's no wiggle room left for me given the maturity of my Hashi's. I feel like once I get my thyroid hormones at a reasonable level, it will be worth a shot again. Right now feels like endless uphill climbing and I'm starting to wear down...

> Honestly, I am struggling to keep my resolve and stay with the program. I am eating more apples that I should be, and I know there's no wiggle room left for me given the maturity of my Hashi's. I feel like once I get my thyroid hormones at a reasonable level, it will be worth a shot again. Right now feels like endless uphill climbing and I'm starting to wear down...

Mornin' BKW. I am in your shoes and never had a weight issue either even when extremely E dominant. Weight gain isn't fun and now my pants are getting too tight too wear. I think it is a thyroid issue that needs to be resolved but I really don't know what is going on. My dh said I it might be that I am actually getting something out of my food now. It sounds like for you the weight gain is the last 2 months. Sort of seems like "fattening" up for the long, cold, dark winter.

This is all tough to take and do and seems quite unfair at times. But, I have to ask you, if you go "off" the protocol, where do you go from here? How bad will things be and what kind of diet trouble will you be in? Apples may not be the best but they aren't apple pie either. I made a decision a few weeks ago after talking with my doc that I was going to eat a few veggies as I had cut most of them out. Not optimal in Jack's eyes but this is my life and I need to live it within my boundaries and it is my "report" card. This has certainly helped me stay the course. I still eat a BAB with a little squash. Lunch is seafood with a little sweet potato or something with a mufa fat if any. So still trying to follow the rules. Pretty soon I will cut back on those and get to a much lower carb point but for the moment it is what I needed to do. Perhaps you should cut yourself some slack until you get past this feeling and then step your game up. A lot of people would disagree with me but I know how hard I struggle and it seems you are on the same page as I was recently. Kinda funny, right now the fat in my meat is really tasting good to me. I used to despise fat. Like you said, you might just need to get your thyroid hormones in balance (I know, easier said than done) and the ship will start righting itself. We are also women and go thru hell every month with our thoughts.

Did the hunger issue get better or worse with the HCL? Are you CT'ing? That drove my hunger up a lot. It dropped but never like some people's out here. Hell, I have been doing BAB's for 1.5 years and I still am hungry at noon for lunch. We are just a little more broken and need more time and fixing. Have you done the Metametics GI Effects test to see what is really going on in your gut? Lot's to be told by that test.

All of this is really hard for me as I used to be a really good cook and enjoyed serving a really nice meal. Now I heat up the same thing a couple times a day some days, fill my gut and call it good. Most days, I am also too tired to cook by the time I get home anyway.

I think you have just hit a bump in the road and it needs a little time to settle. I hope things turn soon for you. No fun to be feeling this way!

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-16T16:23:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/two-steps-forward-one-step-back.3676/post-58518>

Please hang in there BKW! I know how frustrating it is to gain weight (I gained 15 lbs on the reset in a few months), but look at the big picture: are you feeling better? Do you have more energy? Do you sleep better? Is your mood better? It might not be linear, but if you see overall improvment, it means you are getting there. As soon as your labs will get better, the weight will come off. It's telling you sometimes is wrong and that you have to work on it.

I also know what you mean by feeling always hungry. That should stabilize everytime soon. If you are truly hungry, just eat!! See if you lower your carbs if it's better. And what if you drop HCL? I was hypo and never took any. Might not the the best, but you can try it. In CT7, Dr K talks about ghrelin and how it stimulates appetite...might be worth a read. When we understand, we do better

Have a nice day, and come back here if you need support!! K

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-16T16:24:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/two-steps-forward-one-step-back.3676/post-58519>

> **vkiernan;60020 wrote:** Mornin' BKW. I am in your shoes and never had a weight issue either even when extremely E dominant. Weight gain isn't fun and now my pants are getting too tight too wear. I think it is a thyroid issue that needs to be resolved but I really don't know what is going on. My dh said I it might be that I am actually getting something out of my food now. It sounds like for you the weight gain is the last 2 months. Sort of seems like "fattening" up for the long, cold, dark winter.

In my case, I was taking the most T3 back in August (36mg) via Armour. I had gradually increased to that level over the course of the summer. Then, I abruptly stopped all T3 meds, and moved to T4 only, because my heartrate was so high, and my blood pressure was so low, and I couldn't function, except in the cold tub! Dropping to T4 only, and then a month later adding in 10mg T3 (now I'm taking 15), I think, was what got the weight gain going. Then about 2 or 3 weeks ago, I started reading about stripping biofilms using Mg, and I thought I'd give it a try (originally I thought it would only take a few days. Now I know it's a much more involved process). Somehow, I decided, that it would be a good time to eat some nuts (I struggle with wanting to eat them every single day). So I did, and then I did the next day, and the day after. And then I would stop the nuts for a few days (swearing them off), only to restart again. My weight has been creeping up faster, and it's almost always in 1 pound increments overnight (I gained a pound between yesterday and today, and same with the day before that). My eyes are swollen, my fat jeans are tight. And I feel like a bit of a failure. I've been pristine with my AI diet until I let my guard down and started on the nuts. Well, I'm running out of clothes to wear, so today is the day to start fresh. Fish for breakfast, fish for lunch if I want it, and then maybe a steak for dinner. No carbs, no snacks, back on the protocol like I used to be. I did a fish only day a month or so ago and dropped 3.5 pounds overnight. That's what I used to drop after a 24 hour fast... Much easier than water fasting!! I'm trying to say to myself, "I _don't eat that," as opposed to, "I won't_ eat that." I often sautee green onions, ginger, and coleslaw (raw, pre-chopped, from the bag!) with my fish (with scallops is great good). I will move back to that routine, if I can stand a few days of fish/meat only.

This is all tough to take and do and seems quite unfair at times. But, I have to ask you, if you go "off" the protocol, where do you go from here? How bad will things be and what kind of diet trouble will you be in? Apples may not be the best but they aren't apple pie either. I made a decision a few weeks ago after talking with my doc that I was going to eat a few veggies as I had cut most of them out. Not optimal in Jack's eyes but this is my life and I need to live it within my boundaries and it is my "report" card. This has certainly helped me stay the course. I still eat a BAB with a little squash. Lunch is seafood with a little sweet potato or something with a mufa fat if any. So still trying to follow the rules. Pretty soon I will cut back on those and get to a much lower carb point but for the moment it is what I needed to do. Perhaps you should cut yourself some slack until you get past this feeling and then step your game up. A lot of people would disagree with me but I know how hard I struggle and it seems you are on the same page as I was recently. Kinda funny, right now the fat in my meat is really tasting good to me. I used to despise fat. Like you said, you might just need to get your thyroid hormones in balance (I know, easier said than done) and the ship will start righting itself. We are also women and go thru hell every month with our thoughts.

I had been cutting myself a bunch of slack (apples, nuts, other fruits too. Still paleo, but not nearly as LC, and not AI friendly from those blasted NUTS!), and now I'm wearing it... So, I know the process is self limiting, unless I like buying a new size of clothes every week. The thing is, everyone wants me to take "a day off" for Thanksgiving. "Surely, you'll take a day off your diet for Thanksgiving, won't you?" I wish it were that easy.. I think, in a pinch I will allow myself more fruits/veggies, but I really need to stay on the AI protocol, difficult and limiting as it is. For me, one day off suddenly leads to a landslide of over-indulgence, in anticipation of the upcoming "clamp-down!" It's really awful. I never used to have to think about food choices, or portions. I just ate, and wondered what the big deal was... I will keep the carbs (veggies, apples if really desperate) lowish, but not winter JK style, until I get my thyroid supplementation in a better place. I have no operating gland left, so when I don't get enough from the pills, there's literally nothing more. And I find when I'm really hypoT, I can' tolerate the really low carb diet as well.

> Did the hunger issue get better or worse with the HCL? Are you CT'ing? That drove my hunger up a lot. It dropped but never like some people's out here. Hell, I have been doing BAB's for 1.5 years and I still am hungry at noon for lunch. We are just a little more broken and need more time and fixing. Have you done the Metametics GI Effects test to see what is really going on in your gut? Lot's to be told by that test.
>
> !
>
>
> My CTing has also dropped away, with going really hypoT over the past few months. Things are stabilizing a little now, i should get started again. I keep the house cold, and try to stay under-dressed at least for the mornings. I did the Genova Diagnostics GI test back in the spring before starting any diet. NO yeast, no parasites, low bifido bacteria, slightly butyrate levels, a few other things. Nothing really glaring came from it (at least according to the practitioner who ordered it for me).
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> All of this is really hard for me as I used to be a really good cook and enjoyed serving a really nice meal. Now I heat up the same thing a couple times a day some days, fill my gut and call it good. Most days, I am also too tired to cook by the time I get home anyway.

I feel like I've entered Oz or something. Food used to be fun and good, and whatever I wanted and I felt fine and great. Now, I have to cook all kinds of wonderful foods for my family, and not touch them myself. I think that is the worst of it. Feeling left out, and getting stuck with all the work!! Tomorrow is Dh's birthday, and I still don't know what my plan is for that. I need to prepare him something "special." But it annoys him that I won't eat the food. Heh - in the summer, at my lowest weight, he was worried I was becoming anorexic and wanted me to track my calories. I did, and showed him that I eat roughly 2200 calories/day. Now that the weight is going on and I'm complaining about weight gain, he's saying it's probably because of all those calories I'm eating!! I'm eating exactly the same way (except for the nuts the past few weeks)!! Good Grief!! He is a die-hard calories-in, calories-out person.

### vkiernan — 2012-10-16T20:46:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/two-steps-forward-one-step-back.3676/post-58574>

All the swelling leads me to thyroid issues, especially the eyes. Mine have been swollen since I have been on t3 only. I want to do the t3 only protocol though to clear my rt3 so I am living with it for now. Are you able to drop some of the t4 and add a little more t3? You made some very abrupt changes to your system and it might just be in shock a bit. You don't have your thyroid anymore? Wouldn't you have been better to cut back on the Armour instead of stopping all the t3? Just thinking out loud. I don't have a ton of experience in this area.

It sounds like the nuts are an issue. Maybe swap the fruit/veggies for the nuts and see how you fair but still keep them limited. Good luck with the fish days and so glad you are not giving up.

As for Thanksgiving, people don't and won't understand that you cannot eat those things if you wish to remain healthy. They clearly think it is about weight loss and nothing to do with being healthy. I'm still preaching to my dh. As much as he has heard from me, he still doesn't get it. I made him fried fish (flour coated) for dinner last night and he kept offering me a bite. Kept telling him I can't. Hold to your guns and do what's right for you. Remind them that eating once will cause a flareup for up to 6 months.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-16T21:40:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/two-steps-forward-one-step-back.3676/post-58578>

I think the swelling is also in part due to bad thyroid levels. I do have a gland, but I need to take full replacement doses (and then some), and basically get nothing out of what's left of it. I get such little absorption of T4 thatit's already pretty low. Doc won't test my RT3 (says test is non-reproduceable). Honestly, I wonder about the T3 protocol. If you find the underlying reason for your high RT3, then in theory, shouldn't the problem start to correct itself as you address that reason? Otherwise, I see the T3 only protocol as "forcing" the issue, without addressing the cause.

The problem for me, on Armour, was that at 2 grains, I was very hypoT, at 3 grains, I was all over the place, and as I increased the dose, things got worse, not better. I wasn't absorbing the T4 almost at all. At my worst, I was on 50mcg synthroid, plus 4.5 grains of Armour, and my T4 levels had just crossed into the lower end of the normal range. My doctor and I, at the time, decided that after 4 months of fiddling to get it right and only making things worse, it was time to "reset." Drop all T3 and start over again. Meanwhile, I also switched doctors. I had been driving 2.5 hours each way, and it was becoming unsustainable, especially given my need for frequent lab work. So now I am seeing someone in town. At least she is open to me increasing the T3 a bit (from 10 to 15). I am hoping to encourage her to allow me to move to 20 at some point, and maybe even 25. We'll see.

If I tell my dh eating these items once can cause a prolonged response, he will not believe it, and ask for more data. I can't keep all the data in my mind, and I come up short. I try not to say much at all, because I don't have enough background to really know what I'm talking about, and because it's straining for him whenever it comes up.
