I can tell ya' what my grandparents ate.......conch, sardines, eggplant, bitter greens, olives, anchovies, clams, peppers, lots of eggs,liver and onions, salads, semolina bread, pasta with ? (vegetable), cheese (for grating, ricotta) coffee, tea, wine, anisette .....Im hungry!
>
> oh, steak ...porterhouse, seldom chicken, turkey. ground sirloin. chick peas, peas, escarole was big, broccoli rabe, potatoes with something....like eggs or stew, not alone. pork...bacon, ham, garlic, basil, oregano, tomatoes, zucchini, veal, capers. only olive oil.
>
> one of my grandmother's ate a raw egg every morning at 6 am, she never, ever went to a doctor or dentist! she was 86 when she died. had all her teeth, worked until about 80 yrs old. loved her dogs and cats.
>
>
>
> I think its more than diet, its a different attitude toward living and what you eat.
>
> word that comes to mind is JOY.

My grandparents/great grandparents were German immigrant settlers of the Black Swamp in NW Ohio. They had no access to seafood other than freshwater fish. I know from the stories that they ate lots of brains, organs, and lard along with raised animals and wild game. They were said to eat the brains of everything they killed. We all laughed at this when we were kids thinking, 'Wow, must suck to be that hungry'. Who's laughing now?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-23T20:38:51+0300

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-17T20:56:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnenolone-lets-hear-about-yours.3703/post-52183>

> **bigknitwit;52636 wrote:** Does anyone know how long pregnenelone (or DHEA) takes to stabilize in serum (for testing purposes)? I know typically endocrinologists wants 5-6 weeks for TSH and thyroid hormones to stabilize (wondering about oral T3 as well here though). I'm wondering how long to hold a dose before testing to see where it's at.

Don't know about DHEA or preg but I tested after 8 weeks and there was definitely a noticeable increase.Although I don't know if they have a half life or not which is why you would need to wait, I think.

TSH is a practically useless test when you are on thyroid meds IMHO. If you want to know how you are doing on one you should be monitoring your FT3 & FT4 and know where your antibodies are. There is a half life associated with T4 so you need at least six weeks for the T4 to be in your system. T3 only lasts approx 8 hours in your body so you don't need extra time to see where you are at. However, if you are switching form a T4 to a T3 med you have to wait 6-8 weeks to get that T4 completely out of your system before you can test how you are doing on the T3.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-17T02:10:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2-4am-wakings.3705/post-52027>

Sorry if I am asking a question here that has been answered over and over again  I still wake up almost every night exactly 4 hrs after falling asleep. I'm wide awake within minutes and I'm pretty sure this is a misplaced cortisol spike. I stay awake for hours, usually at least until 6am, then sleep in late. Still a zombie during the day despite sleeping in.

So my questions:

1) Reasons for waking during the typical 2-4am period...?

2) How do I turn off the cortisol spike if that's what's waking me up?

PS is the only thing that comes to my mind but I would have to take it during the night when I wake. I've also tried it during the past with limited success. I've also tried oxytocin, melatonin, GABA, Kava root, Ativan, valerian/sullcap tea in all sorts of combinations... no success.

### chocolate — 2012-09-17T03:24:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2-4am-wakings.3705/post-52039>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22330017](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22330017)

Protein ingestion before sleep improves postexercise overnight recovery.

Res PT, Groen B, Pennings B, Beelen M, Wallis GA, Gijsen AP, Senden JM, VAN Loon LJ.

Source

Department of Human Movement Sciences, NUTRIM School for Nutrition, Toxicology and Metabolism, Maastricht University Medical Centre+, Maastricht, The Netherlands.

Abstract

INTRODUCTION:

> The role of nutrition in modulating postexercise overnight recovery remains to be elucidated. We assessed the effect of protein ingestion immediately before sleep on digestion and absorption kinetics and protein metabolism during overnight recovery from a single bout of resistance-type exercise.
>
> METHODS:
>
>
>
> Sixteen healthy young males performed a single bout of resistance-type exercise in the evening (2000 h) after a full day of dietary standardization. All subjects were provided with appropriate recovery nutrition (20 g of protein, 60 g of CHO) immediately after exercise (2100 h). Thereafter, 30 min before sleep (2330 h), subjects ingested a beverage with (PRO) or without (PLA) 40 g of specifically produced intrinsically \[1-C\]phenylalanine-labeled casein protein. Continuous intravenous infusions with \[ring-H5\]phenylalanine and \[ring-H2\]tyrosine were applied with blood and muscle samples collected to assess protein digestion and absorption kinetics, whole-body protein balance and mixed muscle protein synthesis rates throughout the night (7.5 h).
>
> RESULTS:
>
>
>
> During sleep, casein protein was effectively digested and absorbed resulting in a rapid rise in circulating amino acid levels, which were sustained throughout the remainder of the night. Protein ingestion before sleep increased whole-body protein synthesis rates (311 Â± 8 vs 246 Â± 9 μmolÂ·kg per 7.5 h) and improved net protein balance (61 Â± 5 vs -11 Â± 6 μmolÂ·kg per 7.5 h) in the PRO vs the PLA experiment (P < 0.01). Mixed muscle protein synthesis rates were ∼22% higher in the PRO vs the PLA experiment, which reached borderline significance (0.059%Â·h Â± 0.005%Â·h vs 0.048%Â·h Â± 0.004%Â·h, P = 0.05).
>
> CONCLUSIONS:
>
>
>
> This is the first study to show that protein ingested immediately before sleep is effectively digested and absorbed, thereby stimulating muscle protein synthesis and improving whole-body protein balance during postexercise overnight recovery.

I'm not backing off on night time protein. It helps a little when I wake up. I just eat a couple of bites and pass right back out. I know it doesn't align with the study, but if my tummy is talking or my brain wants me up, I figure its for a reason. I don't do so much fat at night. I seem to need more protein. Its usually cold chicken. I keep a kind of decent one in the fridge to hit hard if I don't have time for fish. Im not uncomfortable with hunger, I just can't sleep and want to. I hope I get enough gut flora and veggies to make my own butyric acid someday. Then I can sleep through again. I don't even have to pee usually, just eat a couple of bites.

Already doing that  I take a supplement "shot" with BAB and dinner. I'm going to increase it anyways though I think. Going to make some dandelion/milk thistle concentrated tincture to add throughout the day and see where that gets me. Upping all the liver stuff to at least 3x/day.

I slept a bit fitfully last night but I slept and didn't spend hours awake in the middle. So I'm going to continue refining my current plan of attack with this. Yesterday was:

\- lots of NT support during the day,

\- increased L-theanine in the AM,

\- ginseng and gingko in the AM to increase ATP during the day (which will help with adenosine increase in the PM)

\- milk thistle/dandelion root tea + 400mg mg chloride with dinner

\- valerian/skullcap tea an hour before bed

\- 1hr CT before bed (tap water temp)

\- GABA+oxytocin+melatonin while going to bed

\- new rule of staying out of bed during the day.... bed is for sleeping only

\- new bed sheets... I think those made all the difference

\- I ate dinner right before 8pm. I know this isn't optimal (got a late start into the day yesterday and didn't eat most of the day so it was necessary....) but I think it's better for me to have a "snack" or later dinner if that helps me sleep than not sleeping at all.

Took forever to fall asleep. I've switched back to regular green tea from decaf in the AM (trying to wake myself up a bit for the day) so if the trouble with falling asleep stays I will have to go back to decaf. My heart was pounding once again.... going to try to fix that with increasing my electrolyte support today.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-09-17T18:40:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2-4am-wakings.3705/post-52142>

### atkarlovsky — 2012-09-18T12:01:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2-4am-wakings.3705/post-52312>

I joined just to drop some information by for you all.

[http://www.polyphasicsociety.com/polyphasic-sleep/overviews/segmented-sleep/](http://http://www.polyphasicsociety.com/polyphasic-sleep/overviews/segmented-sleep/)

From what I understand segmented sleep is actually a natural sleeping pattern. I tried to change my sleeping patterns to segmented sleep without knowing about lighting conditions that cause melatonin disruption, and I struggled. As soon as I stopped leaving overhead lights on and using the no-blue bulb lamps at night I started naturally sleeping segmented.

Those of you who wake up after 3.5-5h sleep normally, you are actually lucky, because you are doing something right!

There are also documented polyphasic sleeping indigenous peoples, so polyphasic sleep is a natural ability for humans. TMYK

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-09-18T19:06:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2-4am-wakings.3705/post-52386>

I find the balance of blacking out my room (b/c of a little bit of street lights, but NOT bad like how it was in Burlington) to getting morning sun in the room. I close my curtains and put my blanket like a fort so it's 'black' where I'm sleeping. I'm lucky though that my room is south facing (window faces south) and the other side is east. I was staying in a west facing bedroom for 2 weeks at my new place to 'test it out'. I was noticing I was starting to sleep later, like past 7am...almost 8am despite 10pm bed time. Yikes. Now that I've moved 'back' to my east-south room I'm waking up before 7am again. I wonder though, I never really manage to get up at 6:30, but rather 6:40 or :50. This might be due to changing light patterns of the Fall. Anyways, I wonder if blacking out a room is better if it means no 'morning sun' shining in the room to 'wake' you up.

Hmmm...

Blackout curtains on top of the cover. My room is pitch dark at night but the tiny bit of morning light still wakes me up.... which tells me how important even the least bit of light exposure is. The day before I swam in the lake and did notice a decrease in performance, but didn't think much of it. Yesterday I felt my throat scratchy and a little achy. ### Souldanzer — 2012-09-19T17:53:15+0300

I want 7-8 hours of uninterrupted sleep...that when I can get it, makes me feel pretty good...

I have not been awake at night for 3 nights in a row now and the last one was the best sleep of all of them so I'm hoping this is a new pattern awakening. So maybe try some of the things I listed earlier. Really honestly, I think this is the liver-related issue which gets helped by eating carbs before bed..... I don't remember the name for it. Personally I have decided I'm going to do what I need to do to get sleep for now, if that involves eating a snack/dinner right at 8pm so be it. I was feeling really, really bad the last few weeks on no sleep and all that's supposed to happen by keeping your insulin down before bed won't happen anyways if you're chronically awake during the night. I'm going to keep trying the move the last food earlier and less insulin-spiking. But slowly. I need to refresh my memory about what exactly causes this to happen but I remember it being discussed before.

If you already are awake, I know this is a no-no, but I used to fix that (before Kruseism) by eating a snack when I wake. At this point, anything for sleep in my case, even snacking in the night. Until I know how to fix it.

# Thread: newbie from Va with Hashimoto's
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/newbie-from-va-with-hashimotos.3706/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### renwoo — 2012-09-17T05:55:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/newbie-from-va-with-hashimotos.3706/post-52061>

Hello all,

I am very intrigued by everything I have been reading on this site but I also feel like I need an advanced degree to understand some of this! LOL I was hoping for some guidance from some more experienced folks. I am 40 yo woman and have Hashimotos and have been taking synthroid for about 6-7 years. I also have almost zero vit d levels (17 at last blood work) and now take 50,000 2x a week. I am very overweight, exhausted, thinning hair, crazy heavy periods that come early, moody and irritated. I am very interested in making a big shift in my life regarding my health, and I would love to get off synthroid. Of course I need to lose a huge amount of weight, I am 5ft 4 and 215lbs. I tend to be all or nothing and then revert back to my crap eating...because I get overwhelmed and honestly I read too much and then I don't know 'who to trust' so to speak. I need to get the inertia to make the shift...Ironically I own and have read "Make S#it Happen" Great read... So I ask where do I begin? Anyone with Hashimoto's have any success getting off meds? Help! Thank you for reading

### caroline — 2012-09-17T11:41:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/newbie-from-va-with-hashimotos.3706/post-52080>

Hi renwoo and welcome - no question - there is a lot of reading! You kind of have to keep reading Dr.K's blogs over and over until it soaks in. It will take time and there are lots of smart and kind people here to answer your questions. I think the biggest thing is to know that you have to take charge of your own health. It is a lot of hard work but you can do it. Have you thought of having a consultation with Dr. Kruse? there are a few different ways to do it and you can read about people's experiences. There are alot of people here with Hashis. Dr. K. is very supportive of everyone and is involved on this forum every day - we frankly often wonder if he ever sleeps! Have you checked the easy start guide? Have a look at it and just start!! It won't cost you anything except time and it could save your life. good luck and keep posting to let us know how you are doing - maybe start a journal - about your road to optimum. This forum has been a life changer for a lot of us - but make no mistake - it is hard work and won't happen overnite. Dr. K. always tells us - we didn't get broken overnite so don't expect to get fixed overnite. Have a look at the journals and you can follow people's journey and see all their ups and downs and struggle with balancing hormones or whatever their problem is and fitting all this into their daily life. I can promise you - if you put in the work - you won't look back!

### mehreen — 2012-09-17T12:00:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/newbie-from-va-with-hashimotos.3706/post-52081>

Very nice ,

thanks a lot.

### Dali Dula — 2012-09-17T14:55:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/newbie-from-va-with-hashimotos.3706/post-52092>

here are my recommendations

1\. Read the leptin RX and implement. You should start feeling better soon.

2\. Get labs if you can. If your T3 is low it needs to be addressed. Synthroid only addresses T4. Post your labs as Hack My Labs. You will get help here, but it is not medical advice. It is interpretation to take to your doctor. Like most here your hormones are probably trashed. I have been here almost a year and I'm just getting started on the hormone situation.

3\. Start reading the blog. It is dense. Read the comments.

4\. After learning more schedule a consult with Dr. K. Record it if you can. The amount of info in these consults is amazing and you will want to review it.

You have found a great site that can change your life.

### renwoo — 2012-09-17T16:28:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/newbie-from-va-with-hashimotos.3706/post-52099>

Hi all,

Thank you for the replies and encouragement. My last labs were in July and my Endocrinologist doesn't test my T3, he talks 90 mph and every time I bring anything off his course up he tells me 'monkey's are on the internet, can't trust anything'...in other words...shut up and do as I tell you. I am embarassed to admit that until recently I didn't know Hashi peeps are supposed to avoid soy/gluten/etc...  I was reading late last night in here and learned about SSKI and was amazed...I have sebaceous cysts all over my scalp that various drs have attempted to take out with minimal success and no one has ever told why I get them...they would say 'oh it just happens to some' I am not a fan of that vague answers. I am the kind of person that really needs, wants to, HAS to understand what is going on. I ordered some SSKI last night. Should I post my July labs? Where would I do that?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-17T17:57:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/newbie-from-va-with-hashimotos.3706/post-52124>

I already responded to the labs.......you need to get to LEF.org and begin to order the ones the endo wont order so you understand what needs to be done and you are not told what will be done.

### MartiD — 2012-09-17T18:07:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/newbie-from-va-with-hashimotos.3706/post-52127>

As a new Hashi's patient I totally understand your frustration with your Endo. My suggestion is to find a new Dr. I am on my 3rd! Also, if you go on a strict Auto Immune diet which excludes all gluten, soy, sugar, eggs, dairy and nuts, I would imagine you will start to feel the effects that that very quickly. I would so implement the Leptin RX program with the AI diet. It isn't easy and it won't fix things over night but if you are eating alot of gluten you will see the effects of cutting that out soon! First week may be rough with the detox but trust me it is worth it! Good luck!

hi all these are my labs from July, as I just discovered this site this week. I am a 40yo woman with Hashi's, 5ft 4 very overweight..215lbs, exhausted and terrible cycles... I think I need a new Endo after reading some of this site...I realize that he is not even checking my T3...thank you for your input! ### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-17T17:50:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brand-new-here-please-hack-my-labs.3711/post-52120>

There is not enough to go on here. But your TG need to get better by eating better. Your GLc is solid but only a one time measure. You need a HS cRP and Vita D to see if your TSH is useful or not......my bet it is not. And I think a rev T3 would show how well the synthroid really is. you need a SHBG, free T3, rev T3 and a lot of other things to really assess how good bad or ugly things are. You really need to read BG 5, 6, 9, 11, and 12 a lot............then you may begin to see why this is all important.

Learning what it is and observing Circadian Rhythm

Lying down in the bathtub, 30+min/day neck deep, cold water, 10+ pounds of ice.

First two weeks are terrible.

If not possible to bear-----do fatty acid analysis, likely too much Omega6 and not enough Omega3

Do not, do not eat fish oils or krill oil. (More complicated than that).

No exercising, or very very minor.

Eating 1+ pounds/day mix of oysters, scallops, muscles, shrimps

olive oil, coconut oil, clarified butter (ghee)

and

drink green tea

aim for 30% proteins, 70% fat (by calories)

\-----------------------------------

Circadian Rhythm maintenance:

Sleep in legal dark room, within 10PM - 8AM period

Eat 50% of your daily food within first 30min after wkeup

Exposure you whole body to the best bright sunlight you can arrange, there are some discussions about how good is high intensity artificial light (when you are not able to arrange sunlight).

lunch may be, may be not

dinner, within 4-6 hours of bed time

no snacking

no artificial lights after sundown

use flux.com program on your computer screen, amber filter, better do not use any electronic devices.

### renwoo — 2012-09-17T20:06:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brand-new-here-please-hack-my-labs.3711/post-52165>

This was my yearly checkup, my vit d is at the bottom its 17.5. He didn't like that and upped my synthroid I go in for an ultrasound and new labs in Nov. I honestly don't think he has ever measured T3, much less the others listed. I might have to find a new doctor.... How can I eat within 30 min if your supposed to wait 60 after synthroid?? Oh wow, and how do you get around not liking seafood?? This is all so foreign...Why no exercising? ### [email redacted] — 2012-09-17T20:11:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brand-new-here-please-hack-my-labs.3711/post-52168>

> **renwoo;52782 wrote:** This was my yearly checkup, my vit d is at the bottom its 17.5. He didn't like that and upped my synthroid I go in for an ultrasound and new labs in Nov. I honestly don't think he has ever measured T3, much less the others listed. I might have to find a new doctor.... How can I eat within 30 min if your supposed to wait 60 after synthroid?? Oh wow, and how do you get around not liking seafood?? This is all so foreign...Why no exercising? Thank you both for responding, wow this is all very new info to learn...I will go and read your blogs and get out my medical dictionary and paper to take notes  thank you!

Renwoo, please give yourself some time to read Jack's blogs. So much in there will help you.

MOST IMPORTANTLY, there is no "might" about it. Find a new doc. 1st, look up other endo's and make at least 3 appointments and go interview them. I know this is so hard to do - but you must, this guy is not helping you. You are better off doing it yourself with info on here than what he is doing.

When you have a lot of things to fix, you have to triage and make sure you take care of snagging the low hanging fruit - biggest thing is circadian cycles. . .then not eating damaging foods, then eating more good foods,and on down the line.

Sometimes getting lost in the details and giving up is a way of avoidance. So what if you are not perfect on one thing way down the list when you start? You will find over time, these things - are easier done than said.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-17T20:34:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brand-new-here-please-hack-my-labs.3711/post-52178>

> **Cavemam;52785 wrote:** MOST IMPORTANTLY, there is no "might" about it. Find a new doc. 1st, look up other endo's and make at least 3 appointments and go interview them. I know this is so hard to do - but you must, this guy is not helping you. You are better off doing it yourself with info on here than what he is doing.

I agree on you finding a new doc. I my personal experience, I couldn't find an endo to help. I called up several when I was looking for thyroid help and just asked the receptionist if he scripts natural thyroid or T3 (natural compounded T3 even better). They always said no so I went on to find a doc with thyroid experience and it turned out to be a hormone specialist. Use the phone to help as an interview method and save time and money. Once you have the docs you want to visit in person at least you will have the preliminaries out of the way.

Also agree on the reading here. I know it seems overwhelming but try to digest what you can. And go back and reread certain ones several times. I still do that and get new info from the blogs each time. I have Hashis too and it is a pain to deal with and all the ramifications that come from it. But know you can be better than you are. Lots to do but well worth it! Start somewhere and you will already be further along than most! Good luck and enjoy the journey along the way...it's quite a ride!

Not enough details in article. At the end it did state people eating fish had benefits rather than supplementing isolated substances which would not have what eating fish would have.

### hellojtm — 2012-09-18T20:27:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/omega-3-studies-showing-no-positive-results.3721/post-52460>

Yeah Joann they completely contradicted themselves with that final statement since the previous paragraphs say it didn't show any difference.

I eat fish, shellfish, from wild sources whenever I can, within reasonable effort limits. On other days I supplement 1-2g EPA/dha. Some days I do neither. I have a back problem that is very sensitive to dietary induced inflammation, if I have sugar for example 2 scoops of ice cream, it flares up the next day for 1/2 a day. Inclusion of omega3 never helped with that back pain prior to going paleo. However, once I went paleo and thus reduced my omega6 my pain went down an order of magnitude. At that time it was daily for several years.

Sooooo, my point is that I wasn't surprised by this study because I assume those people have excess omega6 that are the underlying cause vs. an n3 deficiency.

But I wonder if that's not why dr. K. wasn't surprised.

# Thread: Perplexing Thyroid panel

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-09-18T01:05:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/perplexing-thyroid-panel.3722/post-52233>

My previous labs done mid-August, showed a drop in my TSH from .5 to .05. This was the only part of the thyroid panel pulled. So, I ask for a complete panel and get these results today:

TSH/.06 reference .4-4.5

T3f/2.6 reference 2.3-4.2

T4f/1.9 reference .8-1.8

rT3/16

The rT3 was conveyed verbally over the phone, and don't quite know what to make of it.

I was advised to cut my Levothryoxin from 300mcg down to 250mcg. It appears that I am being treated as hyper based on my TSH and T4f, while my T3 looks to be very marginal. JanSz told me last time around to consider dessicated porcine. Sounds reasonable to me based on the polar results of T3 and T4 that I am getting.

Opinions??

### vkiernan — 2012-09-18T01:36:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/perplexing-thyroid-panel.3722/post-52239>

I'd do anything but t4 all by itself. Your ft3 is not good and needs to be at the upper range. Got to get the range and u/m for the rt3 and figure out the ratio. With your ft3 where it's at, I'm pretty sure your ratio is not good. Then you need to teach your doc all about this and if he/she isn't willing to listen and help, then it's time to find a new doc. There are plenty of them out there.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-18T01:45:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/perplexing-thyroid-panel.3722/post-52243>

Your T4 is at the top of the range.....that is good. Your Free T3 is in the toilet. Your rT3 is coming down.....good. Your doc is only looking at the TSH........it appears to me. If your free T3 is low you need to see the HS CRP, Vitamin D levels, SHBG, TBG, and pregnenolone levels to know truly what is best. If your inflammation is up due to HS CRP, haptoglobin, ferritin or rT3 you should not use TSH as your screening tool for thyroid function to make many clinical decisions because it is very slippery slope. I think you must make an appt and sit down with the doc and be prepared to state your case.

Levothyroxine, also L-thyroxine or T4,

You are eating 300mcg-T4

that is equivalent of 4grains of Natural thyroid

assuming 1(T3)=4(T4)

4grains=(152mcgT4)+(36mcgT3)=304mcgT4

Most people could use 3grains of Natural Thyroid

doses larger than that suppress (excessively) TSH.

That looks like your case.

You may want to switch to Natural Thyroid

I buy mine from Amazon.com (Thyroid-S 1000pills)

If you do, drop all T4

replace it with 2 grains (2 pills) of Thyroid-S

After about 2week to a month you may want try adding another pill.

Aim at TSH(0.5 - 1.5)

As dr Kruse told you, your TSH may not be representative of your status due to possible variety situations.

Consider making more detailed checkup.

///

# Thread: Young men obesity sheds more light on BG 12:
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/young-men-obesity-sheds-more-light-on-bg-12.3724/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-18T02:42:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/young-men-obesity-sheds-more-light-on-bg-12.3724/post-52253>

[http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/cen.12018/abstract](http://http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/cen.12018/abstract)

This shows that serum testosterone alone is destroyed when brain signaling is altered by brain inflammation. Obesity is an inflammatory brain condition and low T is real concern. It is not a SHBG or E2 alone story. It is an inflammation LR story.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-21T09:02:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/young-men-obesity-sheds-more-light-on-bg-12.3724/post-53135>

Fascinating. Back when I weighed 265 lbs and had clinically low Free T, I was told it was due to high SHBG produced by all the excess fat. Went paleo soon thereafter and over the next 2.5 years lost over 125 lbs....but just shrank, not changing my feminine curvy shape (moobs and hips). Mind you, all the big names in Paleo-land said that eating paleo vs low-fat/high carb would cause you to change body shape and improve body comp as you lost weight. Not true for me, even though I did everything right by Paleo standards. I went from being a bearded obese moob-monster to a mushy skinny-fat little girl with a hairy rack.

Since starting Epi-Paleo and respecting the light cycles by aggressively wearing blue blockers after sunset and making sure all electronic displays are no higher color temp than midday sun (5500K) during the daytime, I have now gone a total of 139 lbs down and my body is started to get a more vaguely masculine shape to it. Moobs have shrunk more in the past month than they have in the past 3 years and my waist-to-hip ratio is looking like it's starting to equalize.

Can't wait to get back into CT hardcore and see how that furthers things. I might finally actually get a man-like body for the first time in my life, at 31 years old.

Thank you so much more than I can ever possibly express, Dr. Kruse. I'm tearing up as I type this. Words fail.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-21T17:52:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/young-men-obesity-sheds-more-light-on-bg-12.3724/post-53197>

> **CuiviÃ«;53770 wrote:**
>
> Can't wait to get back into CT hardcore and see how that furthers things.

I'd say don't wait. I started the ice baths again a month ago and I'm glad I did. Just let the bath water sit overnight and it gets down to room temp. Then a reasonable amount of ice gets it just right. You will find that you can quickly adjust to CT again.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-21T19:27:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/young-men-obesity-sheds-more-light-on-bg-12.3724/post-53220>

> **Nonchalant;53832 wrote:** I'd say don't wait. I started the ice baths again a month ago and I'm glad I did. Just let the bath water sit overnight and it gets down to room temp. Then a reasonable amount of ice gets it just right. You will find that you can quickly adjust to CT again.

Oh, I'm already doing ice baths--just not able to them 'hardcore' yet. I'm just only able to keep skin temps between 50-55 for 20 min before the water warms up too much. I'm already maxed out on ice capacity (taking two days to freeze 30 1-quart containers for ~60 lbs of ice, plus three ice packs from the Cool Fat Burner), and it takes that much to cool down 78 degree SoCal tap water in a stuffy 90+ degree apartment.

Thanks for the suggestions. I tried leaving a tub full overnight, but it only cooled the 78 degree water down to 76, and I have a roommate who needs to shower in the morning.

Given that I'm already at peak ice capacity, I'll just have to make do with 20 minute sessions and spot-CT until the tap cools down and the extra ice can be used to lengthen the sessions up to the recommended 45 minutes and/or lower the temp down more (I find I have to get skin temp down to 48 before I get the cherry-red/lobster effect, so my body might need a lower temp range in general; when I did CT this past winter I had to get the water down to 46 for the lobster-red/skin-on-fire/internal furnace ignition to happen, anything warmer and I just get pale white and severely fatigued).

Incidentally, didn't see much moob reduction back then; it seems to be the Epi-Paleo and serious minding of light cycles (as much as I can on an evening shift job) and control of exposure to artifcial blue light that induced the most substantial change. Now that those elements are in place I wonder if the CT in the new context will have an interaction effect.

Rest assured, Project Moob-Be-Gone is well underway, just waiting until conditions permit it to be cranked up to the next level.

### hope2conceive — 2012-10-14T04:39:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/young-men-obesity-sheds-more-light-on-bg-12.3724/post-58033>

Since starting Epi-Paleo and respecting the light cycles by aggressively wearing blue blockers after sunset and making sure all electronic displays are no higher color temp than midday sun (5500K) during the daytime

Cuivie,

how do you change your color temp on say smartphone?

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-17T05:39:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/young-men-obesity-sheds-more-light-on-bg-12.3724/post-58667>

> **hope2conceive;59371 wrote:** Cuivie,
>
>
>
> how do you change your color temp on say smartphone?

I jailbroke my iPhone and iPad and installed f.lux on them. And after sunset I always wear blue blocker glasses regardless.

If you have a smartphone that f.lux isn't ported to, or if our have the latest version of the iPhone or iOS (for which there isn't a jailbreak), then take a look at Gunnar glasses. They don't block all blue, but do block excess blue. I'm thinking about getting some in my prescription. That would take car of any device and all other artificial light during the daytime.

# Thread: Recent blog comment
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recent-blog-comment.3728/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-09-18T11:56:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recent-blog-comment.3728/post-52311>

This post by Jack in the comments to BG12 interested me. (I can't post there, because it's a different log-in, and I've screwed up my password somehow.)

> ... Many of the gym onwers and Phdâ€™s in paleo have this belief that exercise is critical. What other species lift weights in a gym or does WOD? I think Erwan LeCorre is so far ahead of the rest of the paleo krewe on this topic it is not funnyâ€¦â€¦.he moves naturally like a human should.

That clicked immediately. As soon as I heard what Erwan was doing it interested me. I heard of him through reading some stuff by "Barefoot Ted" having got interested in minimalist footwear and so on. In those days I don't think the paleo people were even aware of him.

I can remember first reading about Erwan's concept of the "zoo human" and my immediate thought was that that was a bit rude. But the more you think about it the more you see it makes a lot of sense. We're just in this state where what should be natural and what we're ideally fitted for by descent is alien to us, and what is unnatural and even harmful to us seems normal. I think we just don't see the abnormality (in biological terms) of what's our usual environment. That's not just movement, which Erwan specializes in. It's not even that plus food. It's so many things. There's sleep. There's blue light. There's the cold: as I guess Jack would point out, we put a jumper on if it gets a little cold, but few hunter-gatherers would bother to add more clothing unless it got _really_ bitter. We sit in chairs for hours. We go by car instead of walking. We allow chemical companies to panic us about "cleanliness" and treat our homes and persons with harmful products. We wear sunblock. And after twenty or thirty years of this if we were put back in our natural environment, that evolution fitted us for, we could no longer cope with it. We are members of a human zoo.

And its kind of symptomatic that when we recognize that our physical capabilities aren't what they should be our notion is that we go indoors somewhere and perform repetitive and highly stylized motions in a kind of industrialized conception of how to "get fit".

Yeah, I think Erwan is light years ahead.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-18T13:57:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recent-blog-comment.3728/post-52316>

There is no doubt he is.

### Dali Dula — 2012-09-18T13:59:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recent-blog-comment.3728/post-52317>

Not light years ahead, 10,000 or so years behind. Dial it all right-food, sleep, light, temp,activity and everything clicks. This is the path to optimal. And it feels good. Thanks, Jack.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-18T17:55:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recent-blog-comment.3728/post-52359>

Having an aversion to oysters, or shellfish, or seafood in general is ironic.

### JoeBranca — 2012-09-21T07:54:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recent-blog-comment.3728/post-53127>

I took a Movnat course a couple of months back and can't recommend it enough for anyone who is not confident in natural adaptive movements. It's hard not to gush about it, the pragmatic skills we need to re-learn, but also the mindset in which it is delivered. I feel personally implicated because I've known many of these things throughout life, they're intuitive, but turned my back willingly for more of a life of convenience and the comforts of technology. As if the immaterial self is all that mattered. The "wild" is mediated through packaged and contained experiences designated as escapes from the main course of life. Compartmentalizing or ignoring the wild, if not paving it over with a parking lot, how has that worked out for us?

We are already highly adapted to these wild natural environments we find ourselves in, to move exist and breathe in. It is therefore way more bizarre to NOT do something like MovNat. The fact that it even has to be a movement or organization needing to grow in popularity, or worse written off as another trendy cutting edge idea for paleo hipsters, is pathetic in the big scheme. I mean think about it, how to walk, how to breathe, how to move, has to be demonstrated and taught in the realm of commerce, which doesn't speak so much of the exploits of the teachers as the profound rarity of the jewel of how to physically exist as the most adaptive creatures on the planet that we already are. So onward with turning around this WALL-E esque disaster.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

### MsYo — 2012-09-18T19:33:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-52411>

I had an ASI test done in late April but I didn't get the results until a couple of weeks ago. I am taking a new test in a couple of weeks to have a comparison. My Cortisol spiked in the afternoon likely due to stress. I did not have melantonin results and my DHEA did not appear optimal (increased dosage) I am working diligently to reset my circadian clock as well as reduce stress. I am researching TM techniques and I have purchased Phosphatidylserine to help with the afternoon cortisol spike.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-18T19:40:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-52420>

After getting my results last year and finding out they were all in the low part of the ranges I made some changes. Only walked for exercise (no more boot camp workouts), this was when I stopped eating gluten, made sure i was in bed by 10pm and slept at least 8 hours, meditate, try to remove as much stress as possible, and also took some supplements from my doc for adrenals. Then I started taking my T3 early in the morning a couple of hours before waking and BAM! with all that my adrenals (except my high morning reading) are practically perfect.

I think it is wise to know what is going on. Sometimes you may not feel a dysfunction and it's best to catch it before it goes worse. To me the ASI test is a biggie!

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-18T19:45:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-52423>

Then I started taking my T3 early in the morning a couple of hours before waking and BAM! with all that my adrenals (except my high morning reading) are practically perfect.
>
>
>
> I think it is wise to know what is going on. Sometimes you may not feel a dysfunction and it's best to catch it before it goes worse. To me the ASI test is a biggie!

Hmmmm. These are all changes I've already made. Was your HS-CRP high? Mine is not, I've already re-engineered my life to be very low stress, very happy inducing and no longer boot camping it etc. I just don't know that there is anything ELSE I can do that this test would prompt.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-18T19:48:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-52426>

> **Cavemam;53046 wrote:** Hmmmm. These are all changes I've already made. Was your HS-CRP high? Mine is not, I've already re-engineered my life to be very low stress, very happy inducing and no longer boot camping it etc. I just don't know that there is anything ELSE I can do that this test would prompt.

hehehe, the worst thing I do for my health now is read this damn website!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-18T19:56:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-52433>

> **Cavemam;53046 wrote:** Hmmmm. These are all changes I've already made. Was your HS-CRP high? Mine is not, I've already re-engineered my life to be very low stress, very happy inducing and no longer boot camping it etc. I just don't know that there is anything ELSE I can do that this test would prompt.

My hs-CRP is in the 2 range so it's high. But I'm going into menopause and have Hashis so that is kinda expected even though I am doing everything to lower it. Another thing to consider is stress is not always an outside force. What if something is going on inside your body that is causing internal stress and you didn't know about it but it is affecting you? Just a thought... I was on thyroid meds and that had stopped the fatigue. I didn't know I had an adrenal issue and if you asked me I would have told you I didn't. I got up at 5:30am every morning, felt great, rarely drank coffee, etc. The test is what warned me I had an adrenal problem. For me this test was invaluable but may not be for you. Everyone is different.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-18T19:58:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-52434>

> **Patty Cakes;53056 wrote:** My hs-CRP is in the 2 range so it's high. But I'm going into menopause and have Hashis so that is kinda expected even though I am doing everything to lower it. Another thing to consider is stress is not always an outside force. What if something is going on inside your body that is causing internal stress and you didn't know about it but it is affecting you? Just a thought... I was on thyroid meds and that had stopped the fatigue. I didn't know I had an adrenal issue and if you asked me I would have told you I didn't. I got up at 5:30am every morning, felt great, rarely drank coffee, etc. The test is what warned me I had an adrenal problem. For me this test was invaluable but may not be for you. Everyone is different.

So let's say I do it, and find out it's an issue - what would I do (take?) that I'm not already? Is this used WITH the thyroid panel to decide if you want to treat with t3 etc?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-18T20:11:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-52440>

It is connected to thyroid where usually one gland goes whacky then the other. IIRC, the thyroid usually goes first. I don't know if you would _take_ anything or just become more aware of things around you and make some subtle changes. Being more mindful in everyday life can be very helpful especially in adrenal distress.

So what is keeping you from getting the information? Are you reluctant to get any testing? Why is this different from say a thyroid test that shows everything is normal except your FT3 is slightly low? Do you want to take something for that? Maybe not. But it may make you aware of other changes such as maybe I should eat shellfish 3x/day to get that iodine in my system to help raise the FT3 production. Awareness creates thoughts that will help you make the most appropriate choices....again, mindfulness.

I hope I'm not sounding argumentative but that is surely not my intent. My intent is just to make you think deeper about this test. This is just one piece of the pie. I view this test as another diagnostic on the road to optimal. Obviously I'm a believer!

### vkiernan — 2012-09-18T20:27:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-52459>

This really is a big piece of the puzzle. It tells you if you are on track or not. If one of them is out of rhythm, then you need to dig a little deeper to find out why. But, you said you feel well. Maybe wait for your gut test to come back and see how it fairs. Ins. usually doesn't pay for this one as far as I know.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-19T00:13:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-52537>

The ASI test you can get for $129 (IIRC) through STTM (stopthethyroidmadness) and mymedlab. It's done through ZRT. I just did it and you also get DHEA-s. I don't know if you can do an add-on of melatonin though to the special STTM kit.

### Lyndra — 2012-09-24T01:03:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-53706>

I think you ask a great question - would your lifestyle change based on the results of the test? Are you already doing everything advisable to live optimally? If you wouldn't do anything different, then it seems to me it's not an essential test. It could still be useful as a baseline for comparison down the road. Or, it could be useful for peace of mind if you are a worrier. But honestly, if you feel you wouldn't make any changes regardless of the outcome, then why bother?

It was useful in my case because it confirmed the adrenal fatigue and made me realize I had to stop all exercise. That was really, really hard to let go of and I wouldn't have done it without the test results and a lot of reading & getting doctors' opinions.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-24T01:33:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-53708>

ASI changes the most when you have an unstable thyroid situation of a really screwed up gut. I love following it with a salivary melatonin for people with BG 11 issues.......I also like trending IGF-1 to get insights into what people are really doing over what they think they are doing.

### caroline — 2012-09-25T06:01:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-54043>

I thought I was relatively okay until I had a consult with Doctor K. the other day. I couldn't have been more wrong! He said he hadn't seen a hormone panel that bad in a long time. The one thing he said - actually more than once - mostly because I kept saying I don't feel that bad at all - was .... You \[me\] have been living in bad for so long you \[me\] don't know the difference. and now as I keep re- listening to the consult - I realize how very right he is and I can't understand why I didn't see this for myself. I kinda think when you grow up in an abusive environment - your reality and perception is different and you just push thru. My 2 cents worth anyway. Love to know your thoughts.

### CarolMorris — 2012-09-25T20:38:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-54188>

> **Cavemam;52988 wrote:** Ok,
>
>
>
> in getting my hit list together on "what's next", big at the top is the ASI/salivary cortisol stuff.
>
>
>
> My question for you folks that have done it - is are there any big changes in your life that you've made since getting sub-optimal results that made it worth it?
>
>
>
> I'm all for having the info, but what actions would one take if it were good vs bad?
>
>
>
> For me, I'm already living as epi-paleo, circadian focused as I can. Is there some other magic that one would do to fix a bad asi?

I haven't done the ASI yet(that's next) I did the Diurnal 4X cortisol..morn, noon, 5pm and bedtime. I was sure it would come back high in the evening and bedtime because my sleep was so bad...it came back exactly the opposite, high in the am and noon, I was shocked.

Jack's educational prescription was a fun one! First in am..sun..second...sex/orgasm...third BAB.sooo...sometimes a bad test has a great solution!

# Thread: Nutrition and Cramping
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-and-cramping.3759/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-09-19T07:43:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-and-cramping.3759/post-52645>

This is an interesting post that's just gone up at Mark's Daily Apple.

It's an Australian who's complaining:

> I'll get cramps every now and then, or pain in my calves (sometimes at night - I also seem to be waking up a few times throughout the night, the worst time was about 12 times in a 10 hour period, but is currently averaging about 5 times).

He seems to be doing everything that someone like Mike Eades would advise on a low-carb diet: water, seasalt, magnesium, potassium. Yet he's still waking in pain.

Interesting that he gives a food-log. And, yes, there's no seafood in it at all - although, AFAIK, really good quality diverse seafood is easily available in Oz:

[http://www.marksdailyapple.com/forum/thread66220.html](http://http://www.marksdailyapple.com/forum/thread66220.html)

### Michael — 2012-09-19T19:05:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-and-cramping.3759/post-52729>

No comments from anyone on this one? I'd have liked to help the poor guy (except I lost at least 2 MDA logins - and besides I still don't fully understand). Can anyone here post a response for him? I don't know what people at MDA are going to say: either nothing or "safe starches" I guess. There must be a better answer.

I have a vague memory that DHA is what's important here. But I don't really understand why.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-19T19:43:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-and-cramping.3759/post-52731>

Michael I'll try to take a look... I'm still absorbing BG12...

### Michael — 2012-09-19T19:49:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-and-cramping.3759/post-52733>

> **Shijin13;53359 wrote:** Michael I'll try to take a look... I'm still absorbing BG12...

Yeah, appreciated.

Jack makes my head reel.

I've had cramps in my calves, and they woke me once or twice, and they did hurt, but up to ten times a night ... I feel both paleo and low-carb are letting this bloke down. But the Australian Aborigines who lived in that area pre-contact could probably have put him right immediately.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-19T22:03:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-and-cramping.3759/post-52752>

> **Michael;53270 wrote:** This is an interesting post that's just gone up at Mark's Daily Apple.
>
>
>
> It's an Australian who's complaining:
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> He seems to be doing everything that someone like Mike Eades would advise on a low-carb diet: water, seasalt, magnesium, potassium. Yet he's still waking in pain.
>
>
>
> Interesting that he gives a food-log. And, yes, there's no seafood in it at all - although, AFAIK, really good quality diverse seafood is easily available in Oz:
>
>
>
> [http://www.marksdailyapple.com/forum/thread66220.html](http://http://www.marksdailyapple.com/forum/thread66220.html)

Cramping came up here a few months back, seems like some started cramping when adding in CT. I also notice if I drop my magnesium for too many days in a row it tends to hit.

1\. Change FORMS of magnesium, perhaps what he is using isn't working. . . try a different brand/type. That can really make a difference. Many people with trouble absorbing the magnesium orally go to mag-oil and report great things from it.

2\. That's all I got!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-19T23:24:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-and-cramping.3759/post-52776>

Mike Of course there is no seafood in it.......MDA is 100% paleo........they don't talk about seafood much.

ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/14527626 This was written before Mark had a blog or a book.

### Glamazon — 2012-09-20T04:52:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-and-cramping.3759/post-52852>

I take mag maleate before I go to bed. And I also spray magnesium oil around my calves and across the top of my feet. If I neglect to do so BAAAAAD, crippling cramps return during the night. Then I have to take some mag maleate and spray and it takes about 20 minutes before the cramps calm down.

### Michael — 2012-09-20T08:26:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-and-cramping.3759/post-52868>

> **Glamazon;53483 wrote:** I take mag maleate before I go to bed. And I also spray magnesium oil around my calves and across the top of my feet. If I neglect to do so BAAAAAD, crippling cramps return during the night. Then I have to take some mag maleate and spray and it takes about 20 minutes before the cramps calm down.

Thanks. I have got some "magnesium oil" spray, so I'll try that direct on the calves and feet if it returns for me.

And this seems to be what others in the thread are saying they find, too - Mg.

I do take magnesium malate pills regularly. Maybe that's why I don't currently have problems, though I don't recall if I'd run out or something when I did get it. I'm pretty sure it _was when I'd decided to reduce carbs a bit more than usual.

If the cramping is mainly down to low magnesium (when carbs are pretty low) then it's still a pretty interesting phenomenon. It seems to be a very_ common problem for people doing "low carb paleo" or low carb in general. When someone like Dr. Eades says "take some magnesium" it's good advice, but it kind of begs the question: "Where was the magnesium coming from in hunter-gatherer diets?"

From seafood?

If it's simply that depletion of magnesium from the soil has become so bad, because of intensive farming methods, acid rain, and so on, then by golly a low-carb diet throws the problem of soil mineral-depletion into sharp and painful relief.

# Thread: Circadian stuff....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-stuff.3763/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-09-19T17:13:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-stuff.3763/post-52695>

Posted this in my journal, but wanted to throw it out here for opinions/impressions/discussion

"Something else I've been delving into is the major move we made December 31st 2007. We moved from lat 34 degrees to 43 degrees (SC to NH). In the middle of winter. I was pregnant. From the charts I'm looking at, it looks like about an hour of light difference at that time of year. Wondering aloud about what impact that may have had..."

also, a story...

"My husband told me a really interesting story this weekend. Something that he didn't attach any significance to until now, after listening to My Call With Jack (like My Dinner with Andre, but less boring LOL). I capitalize it because I consider it a pivotal event in my life. So, my husband was a skinny guy after boot camp. Seriously. Skinny. Then he entered the submarine service. Gained 40 lbs. No changes in diet or exercise (non existent b4 and after). THE LIGHT! He also shared the story of a nurse that he recently hired. She had been working nights at her previous job and complained that she got fat while working that job. Now, she's back to regular shifts. Lost the weight."

### TheKid — 2012-10-01T22:26:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-stuff.3763/post-55654>

Interesting that you mention latitude. I just "Asked Jack" about that with reference to eating seasonal carbs. Does the latitude matter? Do fruits from the same latitudes have similar carb content? Do we even really care about the season, or is it more about just monitoring total carbs? I which case it wouldn't matter eating out of season fruits, so long as you didn't exceed the carb limit for that season.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-02T00:17:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-stuff.3763/post-55691>

I think it's about eating them out of season not necessarily no carbs. Context of your health environment. I can't eat a lot of carbs yet even in summer but that's ok my carbs right now even w/an occasional apple or dark chocolate is below 25g

The reason you don't want to eat blueberries or bananas out of season is b/c in winter you are in a reduced oxidative state. Eating those shoot your oxidation out if the park

However not everyone does well in cold, just as others don't do we'll in heat context. What do your labs say? What are you vitD3 levels now and again in deep winter? What does your T3 & rT3 look like? What does your lipid panel look like? It all tells a story and tells you what you can/can't eat

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-02T00:19:13+0300

### TheKid — 2012-10-02T16:06:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-stuff.3763/post-55805>

@Shijin13, as I mentioned in Ask Jack, I understand the problem. I'm trying to zero in on WHY so that I can manage the problem better for me and my family.

I have raging Hashis, so I need to be VLC pretty much all the time until things are better under control. But I'm thinking about my family members who are fairly healthy, albeit mismatched.

Jack mentioned that the brain is literally counting energy intake. I get that. But does the brain really distinguish between an out-of-season carb and an in-season carb? Or between and indigenous, local carb versus and imported on?

This may sound nit-picking to some, but I live in the Middle East where nearly absolutely all of the food available isn't indigenous to this region. Most everything is imported.

So whose season am I to follow? Cause there ain't too much that grows here.

It would make it easier if what we were really driving at was just numbers. That way, if my daughter wants some blueberries in winter, she can have a few, but just not too many that she would exceed the targeted carb intake for the given season.

### Dali Dula — 2012-10-02T16:52:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-stuff.3763/post-55809>

My interpretation is that the light cycles tell your brain at what latitude you are living. Your brain programs your body/gut/cells to deal with the food availability of that season. This program and it's info are stored in our genome from evolution. So if you are in an equatorial region with little seasonal light variance your brain is programming your body for a more or less consistent supply of carbs. You would not require a change in cell membrane fluidity. If you are in a temperate zone the light tells your brain when it's going to get cold and that your carb sources will be ending. You now need increased membrane fluidity which you will get because your 06/03 ratio will be changing. It's really a beautifully orchestrated dance if we let it happen. If you eat stuff when it grows where you live and abide by circadian cycles your brain knows how to run your body. Of course if we are broken by disease our brain and body can

t put it all together.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-02T17:09:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-stuff.3763/post-55816>

I would love to hear more about this, because you simplify and consolidate things nicely for my brain!

In any given season, if my FT3 is great (and RT3 is under control), and my HDL is high, and my LDL is under control (I'm a little hazy here, because I know that pregnenelone is made from LDL, so don't we want to see a decent amount of it? I guess we do, in the context of reasonable FT3 numbers. This would show we have plenty of raw hormone building materials, but we don't want a bottleneck either), then our hormone profiles are probably humming along. I'm not sure about using D3 as a measure of this, in the context of supplementing it orally. Doesn't oral supplementation overide the hormone creation cascade, and "fill the last hormone bucket?" Like a little cheat...

So, what I expect to see in winter is a need for more iodine/seafood to keep FT3 from falling. If I don't do this, then my cholesterol (LDL) would go up, because of a lack of T3 to convert it into pregnenelone. I would see crappier hormone profiles falling out of this scenario, with reduced numbers from pregenelone, all the way down to vitamin D (again, I'm guessing D isn't the best measure given we're supplementing it orally). If I choose to eat bananas all winter long in NH, then what happens? How does the light get it's say in the hormone cascade, that produces the havoc we see in non-seasonal eating? It directs the hypothalamus, which choreographs the entire hormone cascade. This is the point where my understanding gets hazy though. I would love more clarification right here!!

Do I have this all correct?

Then likewise, in the summer, as carbs come into season, the seafood/iodine can take a backseat, and carbs can drive the FT3 levels to produce a similar outcome. How do increased carbohydrates = increased FT3?

> **Shijin13;56848 wrote:** I think it's about eating them out of season not necessarily no carbs. Context of your health environment. I can't eat a lot of carbs yet even in summer but that's ok my carbs right now even w/an occasional apple or dark chocolate is below 25g
>
>
>
> The reason you don't want to eat blueberries or bananas out of season is b/c in winter you are in a reduced oxidative state. Eating those shoot your oxidation out if the park
>
>
>
> However not everyone does well in cold, just as others don't do we'll in heat context. What do your labs say? What are you vitD3 levels now and again in deep winter? What does your T3 & rT3 look like? What does your lipid panel look like? It all tells a story and tells you what you can/can't eat

Bingo! That's a nice breakdown Dali. If what you're saying is correct, then latitude is what we're after in this issue. And I would assume you could then choose from the variety of fruits that naturally grow at your latitude.

In my case, we're at about the latitude of Miami. Now I don't feel so bad about the mangoes my daughter likes so much when they come flooding in during the season from our corresponding latitude in Pakistan.

Thanks.

Dali breaks it down very well... however, one component your missing is where are you from? Are you from that area originally? or are you from Maine?

I do best in winter and in cold (When at my current Latitude or higher)- while others don't - why going back to my 23&me - my mitochondrial DNA originated during the HEIGHT of the LAST ICE AGE, in Northern Europe/Asia.

I do 2nd best in Guam - which is ~3deg Latitude - but while I was there I was eating low carb a La Zone diet) I was the skinniest and strongest I'd been since graduating college 2yrs prior. sun rises/sets at 6am/6pm yr round. out of season carbs were hard to find due to the importation costs.

I do 3rd best in the middle east - dryer weather, less allergens, less access to fruits/veggies out of season (during Dec - Feb) - I ate mostly meat/veggies, and locally fermented camel's milk (yogurt, lassi, keifer)

In Okinawa which is at ~ 20 deg lat - I didn't do as well - why? I went vegetarian, w/some fish - & ate lots of carbs... (however they were mostly rice/pasta etc - as out of season fruits/veggies were hard to come by b/c of the importation costs)

I think you have to look at the context of who you are, as well as the context of where you're from, and the context of where you are.

so from my n=1; its about knowing where you stand with health (via blood work etc), as well as following optimal circadian cycles, and seasonal cycles. to determine what you can or cannot eat.

>
>
> LDL -> Pregnenolone (when acted upon by Vit A and T3 (T3 is converted in the gut from FT3 ->T3 - so if your gut is off, there's a limited production of T3)) From LDL -> Pregnenolone -> progesterone & all other steroid hormones. If you're on the survival pathway LDL-> Pregnenolone -> cortisol (pregnenolone steal)
>
>
>
> I think the general idea is that your premise bigknitwit;57007 wrote:**
>
> So, what I expect to see in winter is a need for more iodine/seafood to keep FT3 from falling. If I don't do this, then my cholesterol (LDL) would go up, because of a lack of T3 to convert it into pregnenelone. I would see crappier hormone profiles falling out of this scenario, with reduced numbers from pregenelone, all the way down to vitamin D (again, I'm guessing D isn't the best measure given we're supplementing it orally).

however it doesn't always work like this... my n=1 shows that last winter (not eating epi-paleo but eating seafood at least 3x/week) that my LDL went up, my HDL went up, Trigs stayed the same, saw D tank (from 116 to 64) my TSH went up, my T3 remained the same on the low end, but my rT3 was reduced - causing my T3:rT3 ratio to improve (All w/o medication). this summer, with the addition of carb (seasonal berries) saw my HDL decrease, Trigs increase (not bad but it was an increase), and saw LDL decrease, vitamin D increase to 127, saw TSH stabilize mid range, T3 remain the same, but an increase in rT3 (I'd been on 1/2 grain of Armour for close to 3mths, as well as BHRT progesterone, and tri-est, as well as DHEA and Pregnenolone)

What was the difference for me - CARBS - seasonal ones no less. my body functions best in ketosis, carbs are a limited thing in my world right now, at some point they might be part of what I do in the spring/summer - but I won't know until we test.

Remember, that not everyone is like me. everyone's n=1, ymmv. It took me a long time to understand why Jack says test, test, test... you can't see the patterns that drive your health, if you don't test.

### TheKid — 2012-10-02T20:28:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-stuff.3763/post-55888>

@Shijin. Good points. However, the question is whether the Circadian response is a genetic or Epi-genetic phenomenon. or maybe both.

What I understand from Jack is that light flips Epi-genetic switches, so that we would be constantly adapting to the new latitudes we find ourselves in.

But I have really wondered about this too. I mean "if a cat has her kittens in an oven, you don't call them biscuits."

So I wonder if an equatorial African living in Alaska will have the same circadian responses to light that an Inuit would have.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-19T22:29:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/get-your-lab-reading-certification-here-throw-in-your-02-cents-and-win.3767/post-52753>

OK Brainiacs! Here is your chance to win some home made vanilla!

HACK MY LABS! Tell me what you know! Who ever is closest to what Dr. K says on our consult on 9/29 wins a little package of handmade vanilla from moi. G, if you win - you get something else, but equally awesome.

I will mail it to you! I won't stalk you.

Background:

Female, 44, 210 lbds, 1 round of HCG in June of this year.

Gastric Bypass 12 yrs ago.

HR: Averages 90/60

Pulse: Resting about 60, low activity bout 70

Blood Glucose, low risk for beetees. Fasting 77ish, rarely goes above 120.

Epi paleo with seafood 1X a day on average. . .been doing that for 6 months, mostly Ketogenic with a few fail days (maybe 1X every 2 months?). Consume lots of oysters and a reasonable amount of local and homegrown produce. Do consume HWC and some goats/sheep cheese now and then. Recently started to add in more fermented stuff.

Very circadian focused, blue blockers and minimal lights at night. Get up with the sun and go outside for bright lights. Active with lots of natural lighting. Very low stress - I've spent years engineering my environment and life before I even conceptualized the term optimal as we know it.

At the time of drawing these labs I was on:

B12 Shots

Lots of Iron supps

Mag at night

30-50K a day of Vit-D (a lot of good that did!)

I had done months of CT, but stopped over summer to clear out for the round of HCG and for drawing a hormone panel.

I have recently had a vaginal ultrasound with additional ovary looking to check, not a fibroid or cyst in sight. . .obgy said the ovaries were beautiful.

Symptoms: Hair Loss

Poops: Ideal.

Energy: Consistant and good. Sleep good. Mood reasonable, been pretty cranky and anti-social in the last month.

I have taken a DEXA scan and sent in for the Metramix GI, will add those as soon as they come in.

TAKE YOUR BEST SHOT AND WIN!! WHAT'S SHOULD I BE DOING DIFFERENTLY!

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-19T22:54:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/get-your-lab-reading-certification-here-throw-in-your-02-cents-and-win.3767/post-52762>

You've got elevated TPO AB - you're going to need to re-check this at least 2-3x/s over the next year to rule out autoimmune

your T3 isn't great b/c its all going to rT3. your FT3:rT3 ratio is 11

you' need to have a hormone panel run btwn cd 19-21 to see what your Pg:E2 ratio looks like

You're total T is out of control, girl hate to break this to you but you're a testosterone producer - but your Free T is in range so there's a win!.

you LDL is low. you need some LDL to make hormones... you're not making the T3 to convert what LDL you've got to hormones.

but w/o an ASI can't say for sure if you've got pregnenolone steal going on...

You've also got issues w/your Vit D3. - so that means you have issues w/your gut, but we know that b/c of the gastric bypass.

so you some how have to fix your gut the best way you can... what does your K2 supplementation look like? also are you taking any reversitiol sublingulially?

most likely everything is related to your altered gut... as to how to fix it IHNC - but I bet JAck does.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-19T23:03:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/get-your-lab-reading-certification-here-throw-in-your-02-cents-and-win.3767/post-52766>

> **Cavemam;53391 wrote:** Hormone stuff is there. . .pg/e ration is 5.7 according to the calc we've been batting about here!!!!!

yeah... but look at these:

[http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/02/Hormones\_estradiol%2C\_progesterone%2C\_LH\_and\_FSH\_during\_menstrual\_cycle.png](http://http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/02/Hormones_estradiol%2C_progesterone%2C_LH_and_FSH_during_menstrual_cycle.png)

specifically the Pg one (2nd one). at the beginning of your cycle its low, then it increases... you want to get the measurement at its PEAK and compare it to where you E2 is at the same time, so if your test dates were on CD4 - then you only have 1/2 the picture - where your Pg is at the start of your cycle... you need the CD19-21 to get the real picture.

I had Pg drawn on cd 19 (Aug 11), and cd 4 (jan 12) both times my PG was .5ng/mL - as in Pg didn't elevate like its supposed to in the charts above. w/BHRT it bumped up to 19 ng/dl which is better but not great when compared to my E2.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-19T23:35:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/get-your-lab-reading-certification-here-throw-in-your-02-cents-and-win.3767/post-52778>

With the elevated TPO, you to probably eat autoimmune although not enough to call it Hashis but enough for eating to change until proven you don't have it (18 mo - testing every 3-6 mo). The FT3 is way too low. Maybe you need more iodine to help in the conversion process. Ditto what Gretchen said about the T4 getting converted into rT3 instead of FT3. How's you temp? I would guess it is a little lower than it normally would be. Your FT4 is also low as it should be at the very least mid to high range. So basically you arent making enough FT4 and what you are making is going to rT3 causing the FT3 to fall. I only know how to read (as best my non-doc brain will allow me) PTH in patterns...no thin slicer here! It helps your calcium levels in your blood. Too much calcium in your blood makes you a crazy person. It happened to my mom and she was literally CRAZY! PTH helps regulate that.

Your iron is out of whack too. Your ferritin (storage iron) is low. Your serum iron is a little low too and should be higher (around 110, IIRC). The % saturation s/b around 35% so you are not storing the iron. Absorption issues maybe?

Your cholesterol looks a lot like mine. I always wondered how it could not be higher with all the fat I eat. It was fat-malabsorption and low stomach acid. Ox bile and HCL is taking care of that! Your HDL should be higher and the lower number is probably back to the gut (leaky).

The Pg & E2 testing needs to be done on cd 19-21 of a 28 day cycle. Then you can figure out the proper ratio. My guess is it is still going to be out of whack and you will need bio progesterone. DHEA is a little low but close to the top quartile at 3. But if the range is age adjusted, you may want to supplement with 7-keto DHEA. It would be nice to have a pregnenolone test in here as well.

Both B12 and Vit D are low. Absorption issues again I assume with all the supps you are taking. Also the K2 helps to allow the D3 to get processed easier. I wonder what your other vitamins look like (choline, other Bs, antioxidants). Maybe a Spectracell Micronutrient test is also in order.

That's all I know!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-20T23:16:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/get-your-lab-reading-certification-here-throw-in-your-02-cents-and-win.3767/post-53001>

> **Patty Cakes;53556 wrote:** Destiny try for the thermography, like Seahorse mentioned if they want you to do another one. My old gyn was just starting to do those a few years ago. That is the only thing I would consider nowadays. Also is your estrogen high? Sorry can't remember the details....

Destiny, I think it's really normal (and appropriate) to be afraid. You can't just ignore fear. It can be intelligent. When making the decision, my advice (as someone whose had breast cancer and a lot of mammograms during the process to figure it out) is to make sure your decision isn't based only on fear. It's easy for people to say 'don't do a mammogram' if they've never had any red flags. On the other hand, thermography is a good option. You need to educate yourself on this, however, and figure out what you feel is best. Thermography is not a detection screening tool like mammography is - mammograms can detect tumours and cancerous growth (when done right). Thermography detects breast temperature changes, thought by most to be a pre-cursor to cancer. However, even a thermographer is going to refer you for a mammogram if they are concerned. You can also ask for an ultrasound (another option I also had when mammogram wasn't clear) and MRI is also an option (though, radiation as well). What I would want (and do want for myself) is a doctor who can discuss this with you and help you wade through the risk-benefit analysis. Good luck!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-20T23:18:21+0300

>
>
>
> Unfortunately, I got a call in the afternoon to come back next Tuesday since there are some changes in my left breast. I don't want to go but I am afraid not to go.

PS if you decide for another mammogram, you can ask for a thyroid shield and a protective drape to cover as much of your body as possible.
