# Thread: Destruction of Thyroid Peroxidase enzyme
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/destruction-of-thyroid-peroxidase-enzyme.3768/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-09-19T22:30:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/destruction-of-thyroid-peroxidase-enzyme.3768/post-52754>

I re-re-re-read B12 this afternoon, this time taking notes...

Here is the part that jumped out at me from a Hashimoto's perspective:

_"Venturi reported in 1985 that iodine was the first inorganic antioxidant to be described in any living system on this planet. He showed that iodine is is collected in cells by proteins in its iodide form and is bound to amino acids to form iodoproteins. It was later discovered that in all vertebrate cells iodide acts as electron donors in the presence of hydrogen peroxide and thyroid peroxidase enzyme. This enzyme is the one that is destroyed in Hashimotoâ€™s thyroiditis by auto antibodies. This helps explain why Hashimotoâ€™s is often seen as the first step in many neolithic diseaseâ€™s that oxidize human tissues to alter cellular signaling. It also helps explain why Hashimotoâ€™s disease is now so common today."_

So, if we don't have any thyroid peroxidase enzyme (destroyed in autoimmune attack), then it seems to follow that the iodide cannot act as an electron donor (lipid antioxidant) since the enzyme to produce this is not present (destroyed). Does this mean the cell is significantly hindered in acquiring the life-saving antioxidant that is iodine? I take replacement T3/T4 meds to offset the lack of hormone output from my gland, but it seems that without this enzyme, my body can't make iodine do what it needs to do to protect my cells.

How do Hashimoto's patients deal with this? If the disease progression is only partial, then I suppose there might still be some enzyme (reduced) around. But once advanced, does our body just self-destruct, and the only thing we can do is slow down the progression of oxidation with a heavily "reduced" diet, plus CT, plus low light, etc? How do we achieve the "reduced" state without being able to use the iodine as an electron donor? How on earth did I manage to produce 4 full term pregnancies with this condition?

Is there a way to reverse this horrendous disease?

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-20T00:15:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/destruction-of-thyroid-peroxidase-enzyme.3768/post-52787>

Am I the only Hashi's person sitting here, re-reading this, and trying to stay calm (minimize my entry into the cortisol pathway!)?? Now I think I understand why Jack says Hashi's is like a cancer diagnosis... I want my Tpo enzymes back! I must be missing something, but the way I read this is that Hashi's will gradually (as antibodies climb) deplete you of your ability to use iodine at the cellular level as an antioxidant. You will be oxidizing and oxidizing and oxidizing some more, and there's not a whole lot you can do about it, except live in a cold, dark, ketogenic place and count your days as your body degenerates. I must be missing something. This is looking horrendously bleak.

### Dali Dula — 2012-09-20T00:16:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/destruction-of-thyroid-peroxidase-enzyme.3768/post-52789>

Jack refers to Hashi's as a dynamic disease. I interpret this to mean it will respond negatively or positively to given circumstances. I don't know the answer. Great question.

I have recently come to an awareness of how devastating this disease can be and the myriad factors that influence it. It's rising prevalence is alarming.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-20T00:52:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/destruction-of-thyroid-peroxidase-enzyme.3768/post-52798>

eat correctly, pay attn to your labs, light, and temperature and you can live long with it........if you fail too......you will be seeing lots of MD's. It is a disease that growing into a massive problem under everyone's noses because they do not seem to understand the dietary links.

# Thread: BG6 Questions re: Coffee or Tea and what do you put on your Seafood
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bg6-questions-re-coffee-or-tea-and-what-do-you-put-on-your-seafood.3773/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ljbprrfmof — 2012-09-20T01:46:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bg6-questions-re-coffee-or-tea-and-what-do-you-put-on-your-seafood.3773/post-52817>

I am considering starting the Epi-Paleo Rx to eliminate my inflamation and to possibly reverse some arthritus.

I just read BG-6 today and noticed Dr. Kruse wants coffee to be eliminated to help reduce inflamation. I am okay with that, I will miss it, but do not depend on it. I was wondering if tea and ice tea are allowable substitutes?

Secondly, as I transition into more seafood, I got to wondering what to put on it for added flavor. I always have mixed up either cocktail sauce or tartar sauce, but one is made with nightshades and the other has fat in it. So what do you put on it?

Thanks

L J

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-20T01:50:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bg6-questions-re-coffee-or-tea-and-what-do-you-put-on-your-seafood.3773/post-52818>

Coffee is about context. If you think you need to drop it (that's often wise) green tea is the best substitute. If caffeine is an issue, pre-steep for a minute or two, drain the water and then refill.

Stock up on spices - cilantro, coriander, curry powder, oregano, thyme, mint - and garlic and onions and try different flavours with your seafood. Fat is OK as long as it's not saturated - so you can use olive oil, mac oil or even bacon (mostly MUFA).

Good luck.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-20T01:53:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bg6-questions-re-coffee-or-tea-and-what-do-you-put-on-your-seafood.3773/post-52820>

coffee is not inflammatory.....it just can cause a problem with your parasympathetic and sympathetic nervous system balance.

### ljbprrfmof — 2012-09-20T02:08:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bg6-questions-re-coffee-or-tea-and-what-do-you-put-on-your-seafood.3773/post-52823>

Thanks. And back to wikipedia I go.

### ljbprrfmof — 2012-09-20T04:34:18+0300

### ljbprrfmof — 2012-09-20T15:14:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bg6-questions-re-coffee-or-tea-and-what-do-you-put-on-your-seafood.3773/post-52883>

Okay I have discovered what the parasympathetic and sympathetic nervous systems are for. I took inventory of me since my stroke and diagnosis of the cervical stenosis. I understand that part of my bladder urgency at times was the med I was on (New doctor and new med, less urgency) and part is probably corrupt nerve communication along the parasympathetic path. So giving up coffee may help balance my nerve communications. I know it won't heal it, but it may help. I will give it a couple months to see how I am doing.

I am probably a little off on my conclusion, but it is a theory I can run with now.

# Thread: Calling all Hashimoto's people

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-20T16:53:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-52895>

How did BG12 change the view you have of your health and your plan going forward? I keep looking around for other people feeling the way I do (like all that knowledge just crushed my head), and I'm not seeing it! After the 3rd read of it (while note taking), suddenly, it all came crashing in together and I "got" it, "get" it, whatever. And it's not good!

All this reading about the life of the cell, inflammatory versus reproductive pathways, ketosis, epi-paleo, CT, all of it. I think it's getting into my thick skull. We (Hashi's people) live in the inflammatory pathway all the time, and there doesn't seem to be a way to get out of it (I'll be looking though!). We can only minimize our stressors putting us there (epi-paleo, CT, light, low stress living). Last night going to bed the thought crossed my mind I'd better get my affairs in order (did I mention I'm 36 and have 4 young kids?). This morning my resolve is stronger (adding in more CT, low FT3 or not!), getting into the dark with sundown more than just using the blue-blockers. Diet is already pretty clean.

Anyone here of cases of people reversing their Hashimoto's? How about reversing other auto-immune conditions? I think Terry Wahl did it, right?

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-20T17:14:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-52898>

I think your final statement is key - CAN one get out of the Ipathway?

Along the lines of thoughts can change your DNA? Guess what, goes both ways. I bet we don't know all the ways nature has set up for us to heal ourselves, and a constant mental focus on what is wrong may just be doing us as much harm as say - buying sardines in oil instead of water (or whatever minute detail people are stressing over).

I think if you are letting all of this knowledge weigh you down, instead of offering you hope and freedom - it might be time to try and live a little outside the "Worry about health" pathway and enjoy the life you DO have.

I know it's much easier for me to say, and I don't suffer from any chronic stuff like you do (yet?) but life is ultimately very short, and enjoying the life you have is the key to happyness.

### MartiD — 2012-09-20T17:38:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-52903>

I'm recently diagnosed with Hashi's, still hunting for the right Doctor as I am not convienced the T4 only script was the right option for me. I know there is alot of controversy over the Iodine and Hashi's. Besides eating a Epi-Paleo diet best I can afford, I had also been taking Chlorella tablets. Within a few weeks I really started to feel really bad, very anxious, rapid heart rate, extrememly "hyper" feeling so I discontinued it and feel 1000% better! There is alot of "internet" talk about how increasing iodine or taking iodine supplements is not good for all Hashi's patients and I am definately one of them. I haven't had much seafood in the last 10 days but now that the Chlorella has cleared my system I am going to start adding it back and see how I feel. This is just my experience and probably is not that of everyone with Hashi's. I think you just need to figure out what works well for you.

I had mentioned on another thread that I would love to hear from thyroid people who are finding success with all of the factors...prescription of either T3, T4 alone, compounded, Armour plus CT and epi-paleo, AI Diet, etc. We have lots of people still having issues but I am hoping someone out there is finding success after more than a year using some or all of these protocols.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-20T18:06:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-52911>

As someone well acquainted with Hashis (5 years now) I have found both natural dessicated thyroid (NP Thyroid, Thyroid-S, and Naturethroid) as well as T3 only to be effective treatments for raising my FT3 and causing me to feel better. After finding Dr. K and the information in the blogs, forum, etc I have changed my treatment to not only the meds but including the AI seasonal epi-paleo, circadian living, balancing hormones (gotta keep getting that estrogen lowered), CT (now up to 5x/wk), etc and will add more as things are revealed.

I have read of people "getting rid" of this condition but what exactly does that mean? The antibodies are now within range? I have a friend whose TPO antibodies were over 200 four years ago and now they are just within range. Is she cured? I don't think so because one false move with her diet, lifestyle or something else that will cause inflammation. thus possibly causing her antibodies to increase. I know that I have to do all that is available to me to fix what I can and live in this moment, not where I might be a few years from now. I have super duper long telomeres so I don't want to live a long painful life. I am so much better than I was 5 years ago that I am positive I will continue to be better off as I continue on my path toward optimal.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-21T08:18:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-53130>

> **Cavemam;53533 wrote:** I think your final statement is key - CAN one get out of the Ipathway?
>
>
>
> Along the lines of thoughts can change your DNA? Guess what, goes both ways. I bet we don't know all the ways nature has set up for us to heal ourselves, and a constant mental focus on what is wrong may just be doing us as much harm as say - buying sardines in oil instead of water (or whatever minute detail people are stressing over).
>
>
>
> I think if you are letting all of this knowledge weigh you down, instead of offering you hope and freedom - it might be time to try and live a little outside the "Worry about health" pathway and enjoy the life you DO have.
>
>
>
> I know it's much easier for me to say, and I don't suffer from any chronic stuff like you do (yet?) but life is ultimately very short, and enjoying the life you have is the key to happyness.

+1

I may or may not have Hashi's, am definitely hypothyroid and have had a cancer diagnosis. And I agree with what you've said. Using knowledge to make yourself miserable or make you feel like you are doomed is not a recipe for success. But ignorance is not bliss either. I think the point of the latest blog - as well as the specific science of it - is to point out that many diseases are growing at rapid rates and we are ignorant of their causes and etiologies. There isn't a health condition that you shouldn't take seriously. They are all progressive if you continue down the pathway that caused them. It's just that we view some of them as more grave than others. Diabetes, cigarette smoking, cancer, autoimmunity, stress... the list goes on. It can all kill you. And you can live with/overcome all of it.

Maybe our particular genetic & epigenetic make-ups and switches mean we will always be prone to certain things flaring up if we're not careful. But it doesn't have to make you miserable.

I know what being afraid is like. It sucks. But I also know what being alive is like. It's great.

So I use this information to help me live and live well. It is not my only tool. And it can't make me happy. But it can be very helpful. But it has to be empowering to be helpful. I think getting to that point is different for everyone.

### vkiernan — 2012-09-21T14:33:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-53145>

From Digital Surgeon on the old Monster thread:

The Hashimoto's thing......it makes up 95% of all hypothyroid human cases today and it treated by medicine with CW. Most who have it have leaky guts and low levels of intracellular glutathione. Glutathione is a very important antioxidant that plays an important role in maintaining the correct oxidation-reduction potential inside cells. It scavenges oxidising free radicals, detoxifies heavy metals, pesticides, tylenol and leukotrienes. It stores and transports amino acids, regulates the cell cycle, protein synthesis and gene expression, and protects thyroid cells from self-generated hydrogen peroxide. Glutathione is quite important for patients with Hashimoto's. Glutathione protects the body in an extraordinary number of ways, but the fact that it protects the thyroid against hydrogen peroxide is particularly significant for the thyroid. In untreated Hashimoto's, the body's levels of T4 (thyroxine) begin to fall, and TSH (thyroid stimulating hormone) levels generally start to climb. In order to stimulate the thyroid to make more T4, the TSH activates the thyroid cells to make hydrogen peroxide. If the TSH levels remain high, the thyroid cells continue to produce more and more hydrogen peroxide, and this can lead to increased inflammation, scarring, and ultimately the destruction of thyroid cells. If the hydrogen peroxide manages to gain entry to the cell, the damage becomes far worse.

Hydrogen peroxide is thought to play a significant role in the development and progression of Hashimoto's disease, despite the fact that the thyroid gland actually requires hydrogen peroxide for thyroid hormone formation. Hydrogen peroxide is normally produced during the oxidation of the iodine ions. It is an essential part of the thyroid function. But the critical factor is the protection of the thyroid cells (glutathione level), and the place in which the iodine ions are oxidized. If oxidation occurs inside the cells, the hydrogen peroxide is produced inside the cells, and the cells sustain damage.

A study found that if hydrogen peroxide is allowed to enter thyroid cells, it attacks and cleaves thyroglobulin (protein within the thyroid), producing fragments that are able to diffuse into other cells â€“ and these fragments were recognized by autoantibodies taken from people with Hashimoto's disease. This suggests that hydrogen peroxide entry into thyroid cells may actually be the cause of Hashimoto's disease!!!! SO if you have Hashimoto's your glutathione levels maybe a proxy of how bad your disease really is. It can be a measure of really how leaky your gut is too.

The predominant infiltrating cells in autoimmune diseases such as Hashimoto's thyroiditis are phagocytic macrophages, neutrophils and various T-cells; the macrophages and neutrophils damage the tissues by releasing inflammatory cytokines and proteins, including hydrogen peroxide making things worse because this further depletes glutathione inside the cells. This is complicated by co-morbid B12 and folate deficiencies in the diet because of the leaky gut. Glutathione requires both to be on hand to work well!

The body's stores of glutathione can become depleted through any stressor. For example, alcoholism, HIV infection, cirrhosis, diabetes, surgical trauma, fasting, environmental toxins, overuse of acetaminophen, chronic stress, inadequate diet, elevated adrenaline release, extensive strenuous exercise, or infection. It is also associated with cigarette smoking, as smoking increases the rate at which the body uses glutathione.

Glutathione is not easily absorbed through oral supplementation, but is synthesized in the body from amino acids, and body levels can be increased through oral administration of glutathione precursors such as N-acetylcysteine (NAC), S-adenosyl-L-methionine (SAMe) and L-gluatmine. Getting it into cells is even tougher and requires IV infusions using lipid soluble mediums. For serious autoimmune diseases it is often a great treatment. Oral administration of NAC of doses up to 8,000 mg/day was not found to cause clinically significant adverse reactions, although doses of 600mg/day were enough to significantly improve symptoms of chronic lung disease like COPD. I have used over 3000 mgs a day when my HS-CRP was around 3.0. When I have falls in Vitamin D levels I also reach for NAC or sometimes WHEY protein. Whey also breaks down into glutathione and reduces inflammation. This is why Whey use can augment weight loss in people with high CRP's

Intake of NAC does increase zinc loss through urine, so zinc supplementation would be advisable for anyone supplementing NAC over an extended period. This can deplete your sex steroid hormones if you do not eat a lot of foods with zinc. Me personally when I take a ton I always make my oysters rockerfeller recipe.

Glutathione can also be up-regulated by vitamins C and E, and alpha lipoic acid, PQQ, spinach, offal, yeast extract, and broccoli. N-acetylcysteine is considered to be the most effective, fastest route to raising levels of glutathione. It also increases levels of glutathione only when there is an actual need, and it seems to concentrate only in tissues where glutathione is required. This need corresponds to high MMP9 levels in cells.

The fact that NAC is able to restore glutathione levels and thereby protect the thyroid is particularly interesting given its ability to crack open bacteria such as chlamydia pneumonia, which hide inside the body's own cells during one part of their life cycle, using the host cell's machinery to replicate. Chlamydia pneumoniae has been implicated in autoimmune diseases such as multiple sclerosis, and N-acetylcysteine is considered an important part of treatment. I do not consider these diseases as part of AI's. I consider finding titers of these in patients to be patho-mneumonic that glutathione levels are very very low in these people.

I was digging on the old monster thread last night and since I potentially have Hashi's I found this to be of interest and I thought I would share.

### Dali Dula — 2012-09-21T15:49:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-53156>

Thanks for digging V. I mentioned recently that during consult DR K impressed upon me that Hashi's is a dynamic disease. I believe this means we can have some control over it's progression. I also believe that like diabetes there really is no "cure". You can probably get to a point where your labs do not indicate it, but I would assume without vigilance it would return.

### kathylu — 2012-09-21T16:00:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-53164>

I don't have Hashi's but I thought I would share a bit about my own experience with AI problems. I had some type of reactive, inflammatory arthritis, never clearly diagnosed. I was in such pain that I was living on narcotics, often stayed in bed and walked with a cane. I also had a positive ANA (anti-nuclear antibodies) and I find making antibodies to my own cell's nuclei incredibly disturbing! To make a long story short, dietary changes seemed to do the trick for me. No grains, low carb, no omega 6 oils, working hard on gut flora and leaky gut issues. Lots of fish and fish oil too. My ANA is negative now, no more pain, inflammation markers are decreasing. So, I think it is possible to reverse an AI disease. It seems like the most important thing with Hashi's may be to go all out in protecting your remaining thyroid cells. So a thorough AI protocol and diet would be critical. I fully expect to hear that all of you will be doing significantly better in 6 months, now that you have taken charge of your health care and education!

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-21T20:23:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-53243>

Definitely thanks for digging up that post about NAC and so on V. I will be re-reading it!

I am trying to be optimistic, and will continue to do what needs to be done. I've been diagnosed hypothyroid for 13 years. Only 6 years ago did I finally step up and read about my condition, and ask for antibody testing, etc (endo didn't even want to, since, "the treatment is the same"). That's when I discovered that the doses of synthroid I was taking were full replacement doses, and then some. I never fully realized before then that my thyroid gland was more than just "sluggish." I woke up at the point where it was pretty much destroyed, and has been since my 3rd child was born. I lived for 7 years with hashi's not even really paying any attention to it. Synthroid seemed to work fine, I had my babies, breastfed them, lost my post-partum weight easily, could eat what I wanted. Life seemed normal, except I took a pill every morning and did occasional bloodwork.

Things changed with #4. The pregnancy was more difficult (I was far more tired despite massive doses of synthroid - 277mcg). My ASI showed a flat line 13-11-11-11, and cortisol levels should increase (double, if I remember correctly) during pregnancy. Baby was 9 pounds 6 ounces (pushed her out all on my own!!), which tells me in hindsight I probably had gestational diabetes (I was not tested). I didn't lose the post-partum weight (should have been a real clue that my body was operating differently) effortlessly. Then I had a massive herpes (oral) infection when #4 was 16 months old. Dropped all kinds of weight very quickly because I couldn't eat (and I was still nursing). Then came the elimination diet to figure out her food sensitivities that we just couldn't get a grip on. And when all that finished (6 weeks of the diet), things were never the same. Runaway weight gain due to all kinds of food sensitivities (basically everything epi-paleo + AI friendly works, everything else doesn't). 6 weeks prior, I could eat the foods (had already restricted gluten and dairy), and then after the elimination diet, I couldn't unless I wanted to get really fat, really fast. Inconceivable, really. Bloating, carbohydrate intolerance, and no doctor could shed any light onto what was going on (not to mention the advice from other family members, don't get me started here lol!).

Now I have fingers and toes showing signs of nerve damage (I'm still optimistic it has more to do with a side effect of the cycloset I was trying), and higher than optimal blood sugar elevations when I eat, even on this diet. I have low blood pressure, and often have high heart rates in the mornings for reasons I don't yet fully understand, but seem to have to do with control of the sympathetic/parasympathetic nervous system.

But most importantly, I have 2 children who also have Hashi's, each diagnosed at age 3 (the littlest I even kept gluten free her whole life trying to avoid this exact diagnosis since her brother is also celiac I guess gluten wasn't the trigger...). What's coming for them as they age? Will my daughter even be healthy enough to have her own children? How close are the other 2 from the same diagnosis? I wish I had the info and understanding I have now, at the onset of my illness. It seems to unfair to me that all I was given (by many, many doctors) was some replacement T4 and follow-up labwork.

I often feel like I am barely keeping my head above water, and there is still more coming my way.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-23T00:41:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-53502>

> **Jack Kruse;54139 wrote: Souldanzer that angle is covered too in my Vitamin D blog. You can choose to live with a low level of D.......I choose otherwise.
>
>
> I just translated this blog for a German friend so I know it pretty well.
>
>
>
> This is the major part I find in it on AI:
>
> 7\. In autoimmune diseases we need to advocate for much higher levels of Vitamin D. Why? In order for circulating vitamin D to perform its functions, it must first activate the vitamin D receptor (VDR). The problem is that many people with autoimmune disease have a genetic polymorphism that affects the expression and activation of the VDR and thus reduces the biologic activity of vitamin D. Studies have shown that a significant number of patients with autoimmune diseases have several VDR polymorphisms. There are over 25 variants of VDR polymorphisms now known and the list grows monthly. If you have a VDR problem you require much higher circulating levels of vitamin D to bind to these defective receptors. As we have mentioned in multiple previous blogs, a leaky gut predisposes to the development of autoimmunity. Moreover, optimal Vitamin D levels are also linked to â€œtighter junctionsâ€￾ between the enterocytes of our intestinal lining making our guts â€œless leakyâ€￾. If the gut is less leaky our immune system is stronger, because it does not have to be activated constantly to protect the rest of the body. We have also seen above, that vitamin D levels play a huge role in our immune surveillance in our GI tracts. It appears to be critical to push your levels to much higher plasma levels in these cases. I strongly recommend talking this over with your doctor. The fears of toxicity are very overblown in my estimation and the risk of too low a level for disease propagation is far too common and risky for your health.**

**

It suggests that there is a VDR dysfunction but it does not go into detail what exactly that dysfunction entails.

Marshall and some others suggest that the VDR function is slowed down b/c of bacterial infection. The more supplemental vitamin D someone with AI takes the more the necessary healing response of the body is getting suppressed by "steroid" supplementation and the more VDR function is slowed down. I don't know much about this kind of in-depth physiology/biochemistry but what I've read so far is making sense. I'd like to know why it is invalid if it is.

[http://autoimmunityresearch.org/transcripts/AR-Albert-VitD.pdf](http://http://autoimmunityresearch.org/transcripts/AR-Albert-VitD.pdf)

Jack Kruse;29025 wrote:** The finding, published in the May 23, 2012 online issue of Science Translational Medicine, more fully explains how the immune system functions in different situations and presents a new avenue for treating infections, perhaps as an alternative to current antibiotic therapies (see also Skin Cancer).

The immunological benefits of vitamin D are controversial. In cultured cell studies, the fat-soluble vitamin provides strong immunological benefits, but in repeated studies with humans and animal models, results have been inconsistent: People with low levels of dietary vitamin D do not suffer more infections. For reasons unknown, their immune response generally remains strong, undermining the touted immunological strength of vitamin D.

Working with a mouse model and cultured human cells, Gallo and colleagues discovered why: When levels of dietary vitamin D are low (it's naturally present in very few foods), production of parathyroid hormone (PTH), which normally helps modulate calcium levels in blood, is ramped up. More PTH or a related peptide called PHTrP spurs increased expression of AMPs, such as cathelicidin, which kill a broad spectrum of harmful bacteria, fungi and viruses.

"No one suspected a role for PTH or the PTH-related peptide in immunity," said Richard L. Gallo, MD, PhD, professor of medicine and chief of UCSD's Division of Dermatology and the Dermatology section of the Veterans Affairs San Diego Healthcare System. "This may help resolve some of the controversy surrounding vitamin D. It fills in the blanks."

For example, the findings relate to the on-going debate over sun exposure. Sunlight triggers the production of vitamin D. Low levels of vitamin D have been claimed in some studies to increase the risk of cardiovascular disease and cancer, but other studies have failed to confirm this. On the other hand, high levels of solar exposure that could increase vitamin D have been shown to increase the risk of skin cancer.

"Since sunlight is a carcinogen, it's a bad idea to get too much of it," said Gallo. "PTH goes up when levels of vitamin D from diet and sun exposure are low. PTH may be what permits us to have low D in the diet and not kill ourselves with too much UV radiation."

Gallo said PTH's newly revealed immunological role provides a new connection between the body's endocrine system (a system of glands secreting different regulatory hormones into the bloodstream) and its ability to fight invasive, health-harming pathogens.

It appears there still is an upregulation of the immune response even with low vitamin D just a different one?

I'm not saying we should live with low vitamin d I understand how it makes sense for someone who does NOT have any AI illness. Right now I'm not understanding why I would want for my D to be high as long as I have an AI issue.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-23T01:05:22+0300

>

> Something does not set will with me about this. What are your tangible factors that make you identify with having hashi's?

As far as I understand it (so take this with a big grain of salt, as I'm just learning), Hashi's is defined by having positive thyroid antibody status. The reason there is 'gray' area is because the antibody levels can fluctuate. Which is the reason for the recommendation that you get 3 no-antibody readings in a row over an 18 month period to rule it out. If you do that, I think the conclusion is no Hashi's. But if you have thyroid issues and any amount of antibodies present, then you should ideally look to a 3-test result before making a conclusion. Antibodies can fluctuate based on diet, season, stress, etc. It is the positive antibody testing that is the distinguishing factor between other thyroid issues and autoimmune thyroid issues (ie Hashis). Does that make sense? (And others please tell me if I've got this wrong.)

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-23T19:10:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-53620>

As far as I know, It's this quote from B12 that made me jump out of my skin and post this thread:

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?4087-Destruction-of-Thyroid-Peroxidase-enzyme](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?4087-Destruction-of-Thyroid-Peroxidase-enzyme)

"Venturi reported in 1985 that iodine was the first inorganic antioxidant to be described in any living system on this planet. He showed that iodine is is collected in cells by proteins in its iodide form and is bound to amino acids to form iodoproteins. It was later discovered that in all vertebrate cells iodide acts as electron donors in the presence of hydrogen peroxide and thyroid peroxidase enzyme. This enzyme is the one that is destroyed in Hashimoto’s thyroiditis by auto antibodies. This helps explain why Hashimoto’s is often seen as the first step in many neolithic disease’s that oxidize human tissues to alter cellular signaling. It also helps explain why Hashimoto’s disease is now so common today."

I had been reading along, taking dilligent notes (helps me understand and internalize), all excited about how iodine was going to change everything and restore my health, etc. Then I hit that one paragraph, which basically said, "iodine will do all kind of wonderful things, but not for those with Hashi's (me), since one of the other cofactors (TpO enzyme) is destroyed by your very own auto-immune antibodies. Hit me like a ton of bricks! I wonder if we can keep our antibodies low enough, if we can receive at least some of iodine's benefits?

> **Souldanzer;54258 wrote:** What's the sinister twist? Unless that's covered in BG12 that is.....

### TheKid — 2012-09-23T21:43:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-53676>

Thought I would share a few thoughts.

I've consulted with Jack for a while now and my picture is crystal clear now: I am about a hair's breadth away from a cancer diagnosis. Thank you, undiagnosed lifetime Hashis.

What Jack has impressed upon me time and again is that Hashis requires an all hand on deck approach. It will absolutely kill you, and much sooner than is acceptable to most of us.

I have another autoimmune disease by the way: anti phospholipid syndrome (Hashis was probably the gateway). Although theoretically I could make the antibodies dip below detectable levels, I assure you that every day I am alive on this planet I will inject myself with Heparin in order to avoid a catastrophic clotting event. It's just that critical.

Hashis is the same way, just on a different time scale. it requires diligence at a level that most everyone else will never understand. And while it is nice to dream about beating this thing into submission with just certain diet and lifestyle changes, that just isnt good enough.

It will require strict diet, awesome sleep, CT, supplementation and for many hormone replacement.

Look, there are some people who are just fat, that's it. They are basically the bear that gorged itself all summer and fattened up for the winter. That can be fixed rather easily. Hashis folks aren't like that, and the sooner we accept this, the sooner we will be optimal.

A Paleolithic disease can be fixed with a Paleolithic solution. Deep, insidious, multifactorial Neolithic disease requires Neolithic solutions, that include getting back to our Paleo roots, as well as other treatment modalities.

Jack has helped me understand why I am where I am. Hopefully we've done that in just the nick of time. If there are those of you out there who are yet to get that clarity, I highly suggest you get it ASAP, because Hashis is a ticking time bomb. And believe me, you don't want to be where I am right now.

>
>
>
> I have another autoimmune disease by the way: anti phospholipid syndrome (Hashis was probably the gateway). It's just that critical.
>
>
>
> Hashis is the same way, just on a different time scale. it requires diligence at a level that most everyone else will never understand. And while it is nice to dream about beating this thing into submission with just certain diet and lifestyle changes, that just isnt good enough.
>
>
>
> It will require strict diet, awesome sleep, CT, supplementation and for many hormone replacement.
>
>
>
> Look, there are some people who are just fat, that's it. They are basically the bear that gorged itself all summer and fattened up for the winter. That can be fixed rather easily. Hashis folks aren't like that, and the sooner we accept this, the sooner we will be optimal.
>
>
>
> A Paleolithic disease can be fixed with a Paleolithic solution. Deep, insidious, multifactorial Neolithic disease requires Neolithic solutions, that include getting back to our Paleo roots, as well as other treatment modalities.
>
>
>
> Jack has helped me understand why I am where I am. Hopefully we've done that in just the nick of time. If there are those of you out there who are yet to get that clarity, I highly suggest you get it ASAP, because Hashis is a ticking time bomb. And believe me, you don't want to be where I am right now.

Have just about read all your posts from day dot and now, to read this beautiful post, it reveals how much you've progressed in understanding and managing what is going on with you. I'm so glad. I hope Jack asks you to write up your journey, would love to read about how you've implemented all the strategies necessary to successfully manage such a difficult disentanglement story. I really wish you well.

### Inger — 2012-09-29T12:46:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-55180>

> **TheKid;56144 wrote:** Hey folks. I was wondering if any of you all have treatment protocols that vary depending on the conditions you face. For instance, I now know long haul travel destroys me for months, literally. So will need a supplementation protocol that will bridge me through such travel.

>
>
>
> Thanks.

The Kid, no idea if I have Hashi's but I am far from optimal and on my way there so I thought I might share as well...

I am in a few weeks traveling very far by flight and I have hold my eyes open to help minimize the damage. What I found is, take a lot of Iodine before the trip. So I will do that. I do it with Epi Paleo foods (everything from sea 2-3 times / day) and a few days before the flight I will load up on Iodine-rich seaweeds for real..! I mean in huge amonts..lol. My own N1. I am going to eat lots seafood and seaweeds at the flight too.

About the daily stressors. I know I need to go very strict when I loose my well-beeing-feeling. Like yesterday. It was awful.. I had to work and I had no time to prepair for foods because it come as a suprise, so I ate goats cheese (not raw) with tomatos and coffee with cream and macadamianuts and Inka berries at work. Sounds quite Paleo.. huh? But guess what! It made me feel not well at all. I lied in bed at night and could not feel the nice feeling at all, I was kind of.. locked. I hated it. So what I did.. I put a icepack under my head, just lied there looong in the dar and cold bedroom until I fall to sleep.. and today morning I loaded up with a huge breakfast of raw salmon tartare with seaweeds in it and I looked into the sun... And now I will eat seafood only for days. Today I already feel that nice feeling again. It goes very fast, just in a day or two max. and I have it back.

To me the regular Paleo diet is a disaster. I never feel well on that. I need much raw, not all but much. I need NO dairy, very very little nuts. and if they must be very good source not rancid or so. Hard to find in regular shops for sure.

I also can eat no fruit, only berries. I tested. I ate apples from the garden.. and I got candinda between two toes and bloating. I stopped the experiment and candinda went away in one (!) day. I tried again, this time with plums from a garden, lokal ripe organic fruits.. but no. The same happened, candinda on my toe again. Now I am done with fruits and will stick with wild berries only. From them I can eat as much as my heart desire, I never get anything bad.

Maybe this tells I am very broken still, but when I am strict very strict I feel just so very good! To not spiral downwards.. make your diet as tight as it gets.. and light and temp factors too.. that will turn it into a spiral upwards very fast...

Oh.. and one more thing.. the boring factor..lol. I need to limit action. Very much. I need to have much time where I do nothing, or where I must do nothing. Might look very boring to others but to me it is my salvation.. my best thing for healing. Something that misses huge in moder life for sure. I have learned to not care what others thinks about my "boring" life because the diamond I have found there is so much worth it does not matter what the whole word thinks..lol

### kathylu — 2012-09-29T16:56:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-55207>

> **Souldanzer;54144 wrote:** I just translated this blog for a German friend so I know it pretty well.
>
>
>
> This is the major part I find in it on AI:
>
>
>
>
>
> It suggests that there is a VDR dysfunction but it does not go into detail what exactly that dysfunction entails.
>
>
>
> Marshall and some others suggest that the VDR function is slowed down b/c of bacterial infection. The more supplemental vitamin D someone with AI takes the more the necessary healing response of the body is getting suppressed by "steroid" supplementation and the more VDR function is slowed down. I don't know much about this kind of in-depth physiology/biochemistry but what I've read so far is making sense. I'd like to know why it is invalid if it is.
>
>
>
>
>
> I'm not saying we should live with low vitamin d I understand how it makes sense for someone who does NOT have any AI illness. Right now I'm not understanding why I would want for my D to be high as long as I have an AI issue.

Soul, I just finished reading the Albert and Marshall review. A few thoughts occur to me. First, you should keep the author's bias in mind. Marshall postulates that autoimmune disease is caused by persistent, subclinical bacterial infection. Maybe or maybe not. He thinks that the bacteria are "controlling" your immune system by producing ligands that block the VDR. Again, maybe...or maybe not. Also, though vitamin D is a secosteroid and can show anti-inflammatory effects, they would be rather weak. I think the consensus seems to be that vitamin D enhances rather than suppresses immune response.

If you think this is an issue for you, perhaps the thing to do is to try to determine if you have a persistent infection. Klebsiella can be detected, even chlamydia and bordatella (though they are more difficult). The metametrix GI effects test and maybe some Genova tests would give you that information. Marshall seems to feel that the definitive test is for vitamin D metabolites. If your 1, 25 D is high while your 25 D is low or deficient, he feels that indicates that your VDR's are blocked by bacterial endotoxins and the inactive 25 D itself.

The Marshall Protocol is a 3-5 year antibiotic treatment regimen. Marshall feels that eradicating the persistent infection is the key to curing many AI diseases. However, I wonder what 3 years of pulsed minocycline treatment would do to your gut flora? My knee-jerk response is that it wouldn't be so good.

### chocolate — 2012-09-29T20:09:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-55245>

Minocycline is a treatment for schizophrenia in Japan. My understanding is that it is a calcium chelator. The calcium excitosis in the brain is what causes the episodes, if I understand correctly. So now we are back to thyroid and magnesium. Which is what makes me keep running back to getting everything in. Its a little rough though, the vitamin d needs taken with the magnesium. But you can't soak up just by taking it. All I remember is that you can't take in the iodine until you have the magnesium and selenium, which you can't take in until you replace the carnitine. And we're back to needing the average FBG of less than 87.

Marshall seems to feel that the definitive test is for vitamin D metabolites. If your 1, 25 D is high while your 25 D is low or deficient, he feels that indicates that your VDR's are blocked by bacterial endotoxins and the inactive 25 D itself.

However, I wonder what 3 years of pulsed minocycline treatment would do to your gut flora? My knee-jerk response is that it wouldn't be so good.

Thanks for your input Kathylu. I keep trying to state clearly that I am \*reading\* about the MP which is much different from advocating it. Just b/c I am exploring other's theories doesn't mean I'm in support of everything they say.

I'm finding some inconsistencies. In the MP and in the KP (Kruse Protocol ). I'm reading more, I'm learning more. At this point, following my intuition, I am feeling hesitation to artificially elevate a hormone in my body that obviously wants to be low. I'm feeling hesitation about a lot of things lately. My intuition is telling me no more meds, no more supplements. I watch you and Shijin be sick on Cycloset. Maybe cycloset will "fix" your issue, but guess what, if you don't figure out how to correct the condition that caused your body to go offroad in the first place..... it's not going to help you in the end. Your body is giving you a message with your labs and health issues. The message isn't "I need cycloset". The message is "I need proper light cycles, I need proper food at the proper times, etc.". Maybe some of us are too broken to do it all naturally. I probably fall into that category. And yet, my intuition is telling me differently. My intuition is telling me to be cold and dark. Definitely not sick on cycloset. How do you explain the sickness? And how do you know it's not hurting your body in some sort of way? You're obviously reacting to a foreign substance that you ate. Maybe your body is telling you that the substance is toxic??

I keep coming back to what animals do naturally when they are sick or not well for whatever reason. They seek quiet and they seek dark, and with that they seek cold and they seek sleep. And they fast. They will drink water and broth (my dog at least) even if they throw it back up the second after.

What my body needs is healing naturally. Provide the correct environment and bodies naturally will heal. I now know what the correct environment is. Now I just need to continue to put it into action and be patient. I'm exploring if AI falls into this concept.

The "maybe" you are pointing out goes both ways. There is a consensus about VDR dysfunction in AI.

I think the consensus seems to be that vitamin D enhances rather than suppresses immune response. I guess I don't know where you found this b/c I've been reading the opposite... that there is a lot of opposing opinions on what supplemental vitamin D really does.

Part of what the people involved in research in this are saying is that the human microbiome is huge..... no way you can test for infection, some of the microbes we don't even know. In fact, they are looking for submissions from researchers who have found new strains. And this just involves the areas where the body meets the environment, they aren't even close to investigating what is going on inside of us. I think they have now established that CFS is an infection.

I'm not saying it's one way or another. I'm saying it appears this issue has not been resolved at this point in time and in order to make a decision for myself I need to learn more about it. And the more I learn about the human microbiome the more I can see the blank area.......

I'm considering the 25-D & 1,25-D testing but am not decided b/c it is not a definite measure as well. I've decided not to do the GI testing. I feel that for now I want to provide my body with the most optimal conditions for healing on its own. My $$ are going towards optimal food and sanity for now. While I am learning.

\*My decisions and plans are subject to change. I'm adapting

### cjb — 2012-09-29T21:52:37+0300

Does anyone here know/understand the difference between the thyroid antibodies? My TPO antibodies are "in range" at 7, but my antithyroglobulin antibodies are high at 131. Everything I read about Hashi's talks about TPO antibodies and I can't find anything that talks about high antithyroglobulin antibodies - it's all very confusing.

My doctor says I obviously have something autoimmune going on but doesn't think it's Hashi's because of the TPO being in range, and I've never had a reading where TPO is out of range but have had several where the TgAb are above range.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-30T01:32:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-55306>

> [cjb said:](/goto/post?id=0)
>
> Does anyone here know/understand the difference between the thyroid antibodies? My TPO antibodies are "in range" at 7, but my antithyroglobulin antibodies are high at 131. Everything I read about Hashi's talks about TPO antibodies and I can't find anything that talks about high antithyroglobulin antibodies - it's all very confusing.
>
>
>
> My doctor says I obviously have something autoimmune going on but doesn't think it's Hashi's because of the TPO being in range, and I've never had a reading where TPO is out of range but have had several where the TgAb are above range.
>
>
>
> My understanding was that if either of the antibodies were raised it was a sign of Hashimotos.
>
>
>
>
> > The mechanism of disease progression in Hashimoto's thyroiditis (HT) is still unclear. Thyroglobulin antibody (TgAb) is a diagnostic hallmark of HT.
> > [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2940150/](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2940150/)
> >
> >
> >
> > Given the relatively non-specific symptoms of initial hypothyroidism, Hashimoto's thyroiditis is often misdiagnosed as depression, cyclothymia, PMS, chronic fatigue syndrome, fibromyalgia and, less frequently, as ED or an anxiety disorder. On gross examination, there is often presentation of a hard goitre that is not painful to the touch;\[3\] other symptoms seen with hypothyroidism, such as periorbital myxedema, depends on the current state of progression of the response, especially given the usually gradual development of clinically relevant hypothyroidism. Testing for thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), Free T3, Free T4, and the anti-thyroglobulin antibodies (anti-Tg), anti-thyroid peroxidase antibodies (anti-TPO) and anti-microsomal antibodies can help obtain an accurate diagnosis.\[4\] Earlier assessment of the patient may present with elevated levels of thyroglobulin owing to the transient thyrotoxicosis as inflammation within the thyroid causes damage to the integrity of thyroid follicle storage of thyroglobulin; TSH is concomitantly decreased.
>
> [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hashimoto%27s\_thyroiditis](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hashimoto%27s_thyroiditis)
>
>
>
> Also check out [www.stopthethyroidmadness.com](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com). There should be lots of info there.

**

Hi BKN,

Are you taking any supplements to support yourself like NAC? Read that article of Dr K's who mentions the use of glutathione, know of anyone trying it? Have a friend with what I think is a full blown case of Hashi's and the Dr's basically have told her it's all in her head!-and not to come back to the surgery until she can control her (hypochrondrical ) stress.......They have never done full thyroid workup, even though she had a partial thyroidectomy at 21 & has never had any treatment.... She's now 76 and a real mess. Osteoporosis, Stent x 1, pacemaker, diabolically out of control blood pressure-on 3 medications to reduce same......and had a cholecystectomy 3 yrs ago. Her present condition reminds me of a post Dr K wrote describing it iirc, as sinister and attacking all organs making diagnosis for main stream CW difficult. So, here I am wanting to ensure I've got all recommendations in place. Have taken her home cause she's just not well enough to be by herself. Please all and sundry give me any tips to help this lady through this crisis- have a week to go before getting to major city for cardio specialist treatment at hospital.** Am going with her so hopefully be a good advocate.

Epi-paleo, CT, light, low stress living. She's just too exhausted today to try dip in the pool but wants to have a go. Just a quick thought on the woozy head. Often with untreated thyroid the adrenals pick up the slack and they go next. My blood pressure completely went down with eating epi-paleo and no sugar. For me the HBP was occurring from the sugar. Also vertigo (whoozy head) may be from imbalanced hormones but in someone that age I'm not sure. She would definitely need a full thyroid workup for precise identification of Hashis and proper eating to help in the mean time. Also a full adrenal workup would help as well. I'm sure there is more.... She is lucky to have you to help her.

Here is an essential question to me. What on earth causes Hashi's to start?**

If we know that, we can answer the next question: is it possible to fix it so that process no longer occurs, and so Hashi's no longer occurs, so you respond to missteps with diet the same as a normal, non-Hashi's person?

I don't mean "manage" it with the super strict, super obsessive diet/CT/light protocols we're talking here; I mean live like Dr. Kruse does with cheese & butter oil & the occasional late night TV once in a blue moon without having to worry that you just set off another 9-12 months of thyroid destruction as a result.

Dr. Kruse seems to be pointing to my low fT3, roller coastering TSH & LDL, major family history of various AID, long history of hypothyroid symptoms, random loss & regaining of upper arm & upper thigh hair, and some other nebulous indicators from labs & personal hx I don't yet catch. ### TheKid — 2012-10-03T23:01:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-56117>

@MamaGrok - I'll tell you what a number of health practitioners have told me: if you figure out a way to CURE an actual autoimmune disease, you will win the Nobel Prize.

I have 2: Hashis and Hughes. And what I know is that once antibodies are produced, they are there for life. So at present there is no cure, because that would entail unringing the antibody bell.

The best you can hope is to never have those antibodies called into action. But since triggering them is such a multifactorial response, you can never know what little thing is going to send you into an autoimmune flare.

Yes, you may never touch gluten or lectins and you may take Armour and other pills; but you are only hedging your bets in doing so.

I not happy about that, and so I am indeed hoping that medicine will figure out how to unring bells in my lifetime.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-03T23:42:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-56119>

> **MamaGrok;57357 wrote:** What differences have you noticed before/after epi-paleo/CT/circadian/hormone changes? Have you noticed any one of those things doing things that the others didn't?

I have made so many changes this year that honestly it has been hard to pinpoint what has worked specifically for the thyroid. I also had low adrenal function but turned that all around this year. I switched from NDT to T3 only back in March trying to clear the rT3. Now it's cleared and I am deciding whether to go back on NDT.

I eat one fish meal per day and one beef meal. I CT 5x/wk in 53-55 water, except for the two month break I took this summer. I wear orange glasses after sunset and go in a dark room but I do read and get a little computer time in then as well so not the best. I get up eat my BAB and look at the sun a bit. I am on BHRT for progesterone and working on lowering my very high estrogen (collector). So I am doing a lot all at the same time.

I have a serious leaky gut which I have been told by more than one doc that that specifically was the cause of my Hashis. It is suspected that I was born with a very leaky gut since my mom was a 39 yr old, probably low progesterone woman. My bro and one sis have Hashis and I think the other sis does too.

I do believe that the "cause" may be a bit different for everyone and in this cause the cause may not help reverse the damage that has already been done. I want to live my life and feel energized like I used to. I feel so much better but realize I have a long way to go.

Iodine OK for Hashis? It's controversial but IIRC Dr. K states it's OK. But I think everyone has to do their own N=1 to see how that works for them. Take selenium if you are going to take iodine to balance it. Also I believe Mg needs to be high. I'm currently in my own trial of seeing if it works.

It's the journey for me and the destination changes as the journey progresses. After five years I have realized that more than ever. Pick a route to go on and it constantly needs to be revamped along the way just as road construction and detours may create alternative paths along the way.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-04T00:06:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-56123>

> **MamaGrok;57356 wrote: Here is an essential question to me. What on earth causes Hashi's to start?**

We had an interesting discussion about this on the informal chat Cavemam organised. Kathylu and others explained the theory that AI diseases are potentially caused by infection. Then (in oversimplified terms) the body doesn't fight the infection properly, the infection then exists in all the cells, and the body begins to attack itself. If this is true, then it stands to reason that curing it may not be possible in the traditional sense, but managing it would be. I suppose management means to figure out two things: how to get yourself in optimal health (which, of course, includes stress levels and overall happiness) and how to figure out the triggers for when your antibodies increase.

I'm sort of in your situation, MG, my antibodies are well below range, but there is a sense that other symtoms and the general picture points to potential Hashi's. I quit eating eggs many months ago. However, on a consult, Dr K suggested I could try eating them and then re-test antibodies and see what effect that has. Because I'm struggling to get a doc to support me well, I've opted for not eating them. But I'm not sure this is 'right.' I'm also not sure that being 'super strict' is right, ,though it may be safer.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-04T00:14:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-56125>

and from Grizz (on iodine thread) re: Hashi's and iodine:

The warnings about iodine and Hashi is a myth, a part of iodophobia. The facts are that iodine cannot be used by itself. If you take iodine you MUST also take selenium and other[co-supplements](http://http://tinyurl.com/Iodine-Supplements). Selenium is required to prevent thyroid damage. See "Selenium" in Iodine References.

[http://goo.gl/G4dLP](http://http://goo.gl/G4dLP)

The other facts are that both hyper & hypo thyroid are caused by iodine deficiency. See[hyperthyroid](http://http://goo.gl/G4dLP)

Both hypo & Hyper result from Toxic Halogen chemicals displacing iodine in your thyroid & other organs. [90% of Americans suffer](http://http://www.oasisadvancedwellness.com/learning/iodine-deficiency-under-recognized-epidemic.html) from severe iodine deficiency leading to thyroid problems, prostate, fibrocystic breasts, PCOS, Cysts, Uterus problems. This is because[halogen chemicals displace](http://http://www.freegrab.net/Iodine%20update.htm#section18) what little iodine we do have.

For more, including testimonials, refer to Iodine References

[http://tinyurl.com/iodine-references](http://http://tinyurl.com/iodine-references)

Hope this helps,

Grizz

### vkiernan — 2012-10-04T04:17:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-56159>

> What will having fat malabsorption tell you? (I have this.) In what ways do you expect the stool test to confirm or eliminate Hashi's as a possibility? I've already had mine, so I'm wondering what to look for.

MG, If I got this right, Jack says Hashi's is NOT a gluten issue but more of a dairy and corn issue; although everything you read is says it goes hand-in-hand with gluten intolerance. This test will tell me if my body is reacting immune wise against the BCM-7 in dairy and against corn. Not sure if this rules out hashi's but will certainly tell me if I can eat those things safely or not at this time. The fat malabsorption goes with the gluten issue. This test will tell me how bad I have it - meaning how destroyed is my intestine. Metametric did not tell me how bad, just that I had it. This test will further define how bad things are. I don't think it will tell me if I actually have celiac or just gluten intolerance but will confirm. You can specify what you want tested. Mine also includes eggs, nuts, some meats, nightshades, etc. If you read on Enterolab's website, they give you a lot of info and if you e-mail them with questions, they respond quickly.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-04T18:55:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-56245>

> **MamaGrok;57357 wrote:**
>
>
>
>
>
> What symptoms led to you to have them evaluated? What tests led to their diagnoses?
>
>
>
> I feel you on the "what next?!" path.

Sorry I didn't see this earlier.

Getting my kids tested was an interesting story. With my son, he had always, always been a very difficult baby, toddler, preschooler. At age 3 we took him to the psychologist for help. Stubborn, angry, miserable, removed, delayed (speech), very bright in other ways. I always told people I could control the behavior (put him in the sunroom when he'd start the relentless ear-piercing screaming), but I couldn't control _the mood._ He was unstable. Any little thing would set him off, and once he'd get started, there was no way around it but through it. He was really something else, from birth, really. His older brother nursed during his pregnancy, and I tandem nursed the boys for a year. I ate poorly, but felt everything was fine. I was still losing my pregnancy weight (despite Hashi's), making plenty of milk for exclusive breastfeeding, etc. I reasoned with myself that my body must "need" the foods I was choosing. Me, with an Electrical Engineering degree... I'm disgusted with myself even as I type this.

Well, the psychologist made the comment to me to "rule out any medical issues." Since I had a thyroid problem, I decided to have the boys tested. I should have asked for antibody testing right away, but didn't. Their TSH's were elevated (5ish), and doc wanted to re-test a few months later. So we did, and my younger son's TSH was still elevated, so then I requested anti-body testing, and his was positive. My older son's were negative. He is checked annually.

With my youngest, I kept her gluten-free from birth (my son was later diagnosed celiac, and I decided that probably the celiac had come first, and then the thyroid condition). I had her labs drawn at 2.5, and her numbers were good, not great, but negative antibodies. Then, last January, she started to act a little different. A little more miserable, a little more tired, almost like she was dealing with a low grade infection. It was subtle - dh didn't notice a change. Having been there before, I knew. I took her in, had them check her ears, throat, lungs, etc. When all that was clean, I requested full labs (including antibodies). They gave me the run around about how typically children with hypo-thyroidism grow slower (she's at the top of her growth curve). I told them she needed to be tested... When the labs came back her antibodies were 40ish (the lab says below 20 is "normal"). So low, but positive. Now everybody gets annual thyroid labs...

Either way, antibodies then exist in perpetuity.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-04T23:39:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-56332>

I personally have not had experience with LDN but know of several people who are on it for Hashis and often they have other AI issues too. Results seem pretty good for those who have trouble managing the symptoms of Hashis. Less pain, less thyroid meds, less symptoms. To me that is just another drug to take and I would rather have my food be my medicine. With that said, for those who really need help and are doing everything they can with their diet, then LDN may be a possibility and I'm sure for some a life saver. But as a scripted med it comes with it's own set of issues. There is an LDN group on FB and I know some of the thyroid boards speak often of LDN.

### SeaJane — 2012-10-05T16:21:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-56473>

> **bigknitwit;57721 wrote:** My mother has never, ever altered her diet. She eats an upgraded SAD (doesn't eat out much). I'm going to have to push on her to push on her doctor to look at the records and find out what the heck that treatment was that she received. She literally high high antibodies, and no thyroid tissue destruction - for 30 years...
>
>
> From what I understand, not all people with Graves have the TPO antibodies that lead to hypothyroidism over time due to the damage of the thyroid. The antibodies that cause the hyper state are associated with the TSH receptors (TSI--thyroid stimulating immunoglobulin). Per Wikipedia...
>
>
>
> Three types of autoantibodies to the TSH-R are currently recognized:
>
>
>
> \*Thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins (mainly immunoglobulin G) act as long-acting thyroid stimulants, activating the cells in a longer and slower way than the normal thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), leading to an elevated production of thyroid hormone.
>
>
>
> \*Thyroid growth immunoglobulins bind directly to the TSH-Rs, and have been implicated in the growth of thyroid follicles.
>
>
>
> \*Thyrotropin binding-inhibiting immunoglobulins inhibit the normal union of TSH with its receptor. Some will actually act as if TSH itself is binding to its receptor, thus inducing thyroid function. Other types may not stimulate the thyroid gland, but will prevent thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulin and TSH from binding to and stimulating the receptor.

For myself, I have Graves with the TPO antibodies. First, I was hyper and on meds for about 6 months before returning to a euthyroid state without meds. I stayed there for a couple of years until I started to experience hypo symptoms last fall. A re-check of my antibodies showed that they were all high (hyper and hypo), but net effect was being hypo. I wish I had checked my antibodies when I was euthyroid so I knew whether my antibodies were low or whether I was in a place where the antibodies effects were balancing each other.

Just a note on allergies and food....some responses like histamine won't show up on tests. I get reactions that seem like allergies but are caused by my body over producing histamine, or not processing histamine. I'm sure this is partially what is making it so hard to heal my gut. So runny noses, headaches swelling etc. isn't always caused by Igg Iga or Ige responses. I was told I only had a few food allergies, but I get massive reactions to lots of foods I tested safe for.

This may have nothing to do with your situations...just giving my story.

> From Enterolabs:
>
> What about sensitivity to other antigenic foods like milk, egg, soy, other grains, meats, nuts, and nightshades?
>
> Since the 1960's, research has shown that people who are immunologically sensitive to gluten have a higher than average chance of being sensitive to other dietary proteins, especially to those in other immune-stimulating (antigenic) foods such as milk, eggs, soy, other grains, meats, nuts, and nightshades (potatoes, tomatoes, peppers, and eggplant). Furthermore, with the irresponsibly common corporate agricultural practice of over-hybridizing and genetically modifying our plant foods (mainly wheat, corn, and soy), combined with overuse of stomach acid-inhibiting drugs that reduce digestion of proteins in the gut, food sensitivity is now commonplace and on the epidemic rise. Immunological sensitivity to non-gluten food antigens can be detected by the same procedure of detecting in stool antibodies to these dietary proteins, and our patented stool antibody tests can reveal these to be present before they can be detected in blood. Sensitivity of the immune system to milk, egg, soy, and rice proteins has been reported to cause intestinal syndromes and damage mimicking that caused by gluten and celiac sprue. The other food antigens can cause symptoms, and possibly syndromes as well, contributing to irritable bowel syndrome, and probably aggravating rheumatoid arthritis and other arthritides, for example. Furthermore, recent research has linked antibodies to milk proteins to the development of eczema, diabetes, multiple sclerosis, autism, and other immunologic syndromes.
>
>
>
> To encourage people to get the maximum benefit from their testing and their dietary elimination treatment, EnteroLab is now offering testing for the main food antigens (gluten, milk, egg, and soy) in economical panels that accompany gluten sensitivity testing, or as an economical testing panel for an array of foods beyond the main four food antigens

.

Not a allergy test but an antigen test. I have to do more research to understand it better but I don't believe it is the same.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-06T15:43:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-56585>

Also, I can say I've had blood allergy testing (came back clean), IgG allergy testing (lots of positives here, but also my celiac son had it done and the test said wheat/gluten was okay for him! It was a year before his diagnosis, but still!), and intradermal testing. Intradermal was a lot more sensitive (came back with several positives), but many of the foods that cause me problems came back clean. In addition, I had it done 2 years later, and many of my positives changed (I had gone gluten free in that timespan, so maybe that changed things up?). Didn't matter much for me though, I still needed to eliminate everything to get real answers to what I could eat. My 3year old tested positive for peanut, egg, and rice intradermally. We have been avoiding those foods for quite some time, and the chronic rash she had around her mouth has cleared (she's also gluten free)... Also, my celaic son had intradermal allergy testing done a few years ago, and pretty much all the foods he tested positive to were foods he naturally refused - most fruits, a lot of veggies, corn. Who doesn't like strawberries? Or so I used to think... Like I said in another post, now I stay out of his way with food choices.

This also may have nothing to do with people's situations, but I thought I'd share our allergy testing experiences too.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-56694>

If I remember correctly, Enterolabs looks for antigens (IgG, IgE, and IgA) in the stool. This makes the test far more sensitive than other allergy testing, because those antibodies are created right there in the GI tract. It takes a long time for antibody levels to reach a critical number to migrate out of the GI tract, and be measurable on the skin, or in serum. This is at least what I remember about the test. We used it years ago to see if my oldest was celiac. Ironically, it was his symptomless little brother who was the diagnosed celiac kid in my household.

> **MamaGrok;57867 wrote:** So has Dr. Kruse said that he believes enterolab's antigen testing is reliable? I know most people say allergy tests aren't reliable, but this isn't that. I guess I'm asking which category it's in:
>
>
>

>
>
>
> OR
>
>
>
> \- This is definitive. Your test shows you MUST avoid these things.

### kathylu — 2012-10-07T20:15:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-56762>

Just wanted to clarify a few points...an antigen is a substance (almost always a protein) that elicits the production of an antibody such as IgG, IgA or IgE. So enterolabs is screening for the presence of antibodies, not the presence of antigens. This actually makes sense because if you detect an antigen, it would simply mean that you had eaten some of that substance recently. However, if you detect an antibody to that antigen, it would tell you that your body is mounting an immune response to that antigen, which is certainly of more concern.

Also, a histamine reaction is an immune response as well. Foreign antigens start a chain reaction where (usually) IgE destabilizes mast cells and basophils in nearby connective tissue. These cells release histamine and other inflammatory cytokines.

### vkiernan — 2012-10-07T21:58:00+0300

Jack answers:

> food allergies are low grade inflammation that is fought off by the GALT behind the brush border. An AI is the same process further along that has altered the cellular signaling in the GALT to allow for abnormal AB production that seeks and out and destroy our own tissues that have cell membrane receptors that look similar to the foods that have cause the chronic low grade inflammation over time. The generation of an AI revolves around the ability of chronic inflammation to subvert the Toll Receptor proteins in the immune system to affect both sides of the immune system. (cell mediated and AB production) The type of AI you get depends upon the Ag presented chronically by the food and how the homology of the AB looks to your normal tissues. It is a very complex cellular event that always involves inflammation and micronutrient depletion to slowly destroy cellular signaling. The goal to treatment is to cease the inflammation, and allow the body to heal itself by removing the toxins and antigens that set the process off.

### kathylu — 2012-10-08T18:17:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-56900>

> **bigknitwit;58196 wrote:** But don't we need to be eating the foods to present antigens into our intestines, to give the body the chance to make the antibodies? I wonder how long we'd have to consume the foods to get the results...?

Ok...the way enterolabs does their antibody testing, the answer is "usually no". They are looking for secretory IgA antibodies. IgA is secreted by the cells of the gut lining, GALT (gut associated lymphoid tissue). IgA tends to be secreted in small amounts for a long time (possibly always) after exposure to the antigenic substance. The lab will mix your sample with the appropriate tagged haptens (haptens are the reactive portion of the antigen) and even if a miniscule amount of IgA is present it will be detectable.

This is different from blood testing because there they are looking for IgG or IgM which are produced in detectable amounts only after a significant exposure to the antigen. That's why you have to eat gluten for a "gluten challenge". You have to exceed a threshold amount of the antigen in order to stimulate the body to produce a detectable amount of antibody.

Hope that helps.

This is a very simplified explanation, there are exceptions of course. But in general, to detect antibodies in blood there must have been a recent antigen exposure but stool antibody testing does not require that.
