Jack talked in the last webinar about re-engineering the gut, and it seems to me, the starting place is getting a decent hormone profile, and then stripping out gut biofilms, and then re-seeding using the epcotx. He mentioned in passing about using Mg and/or coconut oil for "2-3 days" to remove existing biofilms. However, when I have done more reading about these biofilms, it seems they need far more treatment than just "a few days" of diarrhea. The bacteria create a polyssacharide (hard shell, I think) exterior and live happily underneath it. Antibiotics don't reach them in any measurable quantity, and they are very difficult to erradicate. I was reading the other night on another site about them (here: [http://coolinginflammation.blogspot.com/2009/09/cure-for-inflammatory-diseases.html](http://http://coolinginflammation.blogspot.com/2009/09/cure-for-inflammatory-diseases.html)). This author has a lot of similar ideas to Jack's, so of course I like a little consistency. But other places I've read about biofilm removal taking months (protease enzymes between meals can apparently do some decent work at cracking their shells).

The thing is, I think caution is needed, because without the right initial conditions after the clean-out (and this involves hormones, like the Pg/E2 ratio, for reasons I can't articulate, and would love if someone else could!, good cortisol profile, probably others), I would think you would cultivate something less than desireable.

So, these tidbits have been dropped, but without any real measurables (like how longshould you continue with diarrhea Mg loading? How do you know that they're cleared out and you're ready for the next step? Would CO be better, if once cleared you know it because the diarrhea stops - or does it stop?). BUt it seems imperative that hormones are step 1...

### kathylu — 2012-10-08T23:32:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-56956>

> **MamaGrok;58239 wrote:**
>
>
>
> Can someone please translate this last sentence for me?
>
>
>
> And why is it possible to get an AID if you have never had food allergies before?

Wow, you don't ask the easy questions, do you?

Ok, here goes...my absurd oversimplification of immunology! You can divide the immune system into two branches (there are more, but let's keep it at two) the innate and the adaptive immune system. The innate system, which includes beauties such as the Toll receptors which (of course) respond to Toll receptor-like proteins, are responsible for activation of inflammatory cytokine cascades such as NF kappa beta. This means that when TLR proteins (many are bacterial endotoxins and viral proteins) activate the receptor, this allows transcription of factors that activate cellular defense mechanisms, often leading to apoptosis of the infected cell (cell death) or phagocytosis (engulfment by a white cell) which results in elimination of the invading antigens..in a perfect world. Of course, that's not what always happens. The innate system is evolutionally highly conserved, which means it is ancient and quite resistant to genetic modification. This is important because it means we are "hardwired" to respond to many invading antigens without ever having been exposed to them before, since our ancestors acquired the ability to respond and passed it to us.

Adaptive ("specific") immunity on the other hand is quite sensitive and variable...wikipedia gives a good definition:

"The system is highly adaptable because of somatic hypermutation (a process of accelerated somatic mutations), and V(D)J recombination (an irreversible genetic recombination of antigen receptor gene segments). This mechanism allows a small number of genes to generate a vast number of different antigen receptors, which are then uniquely expressed on each individual lymphocyte. Because the gene rearrangement leads to an irreversible change in the DNA of each cell, all of the progeny (offspring) of that cell will then inherit genes encoding the same receptor specificity, including the Memory B cells and Memory T cells that are the keys to long-lived specific immunity. Immune network theory is a theory of how the adaptive immune system works, that is based on interactions between the variable regions of the receptors of T cells, B cells and of molecules made by T cells and B cells that have variable regions." So, we easily make antibodies to foreign substances (antigens).

So, what goes astray to lead to AI disease? One proposed mechanism is that pathogens are capable of "molecular mimicry", the ability of expressed proteins to resemble other proteins. Plants have this ability as well, it seems to be one of their defense mechanisms. Cutting through all of the confusing details, the bottom line is that plants, fungi, bacteria and sometimes viruses have the ability to produce proteins that look like proteins that we produce. The advantage to them is that they sometimes escape the notice of our innate immune system, but not necessarily the adaptive immune system. If the antigen presented to the adaptive system is different enough to trigger antibody formation, but similar enough to escape the innate system, it may result in memory B (antibody) cells and memory T (lymphocytes) being produced to that antigen. Since the invading antigen was similar to one of our proteins (human, remember it escaped the notice of the innate system, therefore was assumed to be "self"), we may now be producing antibodies that mistakenly recognize and attack our own tissues.

This mechanism may be involved in many AI diseases (think sarcoidosis, inflammatory arthritis, Hashi's, AS, lupus, MS, etc.). Dietary lectins can cause this response as well, leading to digestive disorders, food allergies and intolerances. As these diseases progress, based on the molecular "appearance" of the antigens, the inflammatory side of the immune system becomes stimulated as well.

So, you can develop an AID without having had food allergies because you may be responding to antigens that are pathogen related and not food related.

As I said, this is a gross oversimplification and there are certainly competing theories and other mechanisms by which we may develop AI diseases.

more to think about (from the Vitamin D blog)....

Can you have a normal plasma D level and still have low vitamin D activity? Yes you can, and it is probably the biggest silent epidemic out there today. I most commonly see this in obese folks with hypothyroidism. 90% of the cases of hypothyroidism in the USA are cause by Hashimoto’s disease. This disease is an[autoimmune disease](http://http://jackkruse.com/where-autoimmunity-cancer-and-disease-collide/) and these patients universally have defective VDR receptors. That means they need very high levels of blood Vitamin D levels and optimization of their thyroid function to get results.

### SCRN2007 — 2012-10-10T08:13:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-57257>

MamaGrok, here is my understanding of the answers to the questions you ask . . .

It is my understanding that the forbidden foods for AI are forbidden because, in anyone who has a comprimised gut (e.g., anyone who has eaten the SAD for very long), especially those who have an autoimmune genotype (a.k.a., HLA genotype), a receptor called the human epidermal growth factor receptor can be triggered by the AI foods. Theoretically if your gut is leaking like a sieve, then any food can get through into your bloodstream; however, the AI foods seem to be able to perform a special trick in getting through your gut wall that other foods cannot. The AI foods seem to be able to go right through intact gut cells via the human epidermal growth factor receptor. Think of the human epidermal growth factor receptor as a door with a lock that a lot of different keys fit into. The foods that must be avoided by those with AI are the foods that can spring the "lock" on the human epidermal growth factor receptor. The problem is, once the lock is sprung, the food molecules go straight into the bloodstream as a large, undigested, molecules that were never meant to see your bloodstream in that form. These molecules are perceived by the immune system as foreign invaders (this is where the food molecules become "antigens") and they get attacked by an arm of the immune system that makes antibodies. It is during this process that autoimmunity can first start. If the body starts producing antibodies to enough food molecules, then, sooner or later, it is going to produce antibodies to one that has a structure very similar to this or that cell somewhere in your body, because there are only so many configurations that exist (this is where the term "molecular mimicry" comes in--the food molecules can look a lot like parts of your cells). The antibodies attack the cells in your body that look similar to the food molecules. This is how your thyroid or your pancreas get attacked in AI. Also, from that point on, every time you eat one of the AI foods, the body declares a new war on the food molecules that come into the bloodstream, and those cells in your body that look a lot like them get attacked all over again. Here is a link to a piece that helped me understand all of this better: [http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2011/11/30/loren-cordain-autoimmune-disease-and-food-triggers/](http://http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2011/11/30/loren-cordain-autoimmune-disease-and-food-triggers/) .

My understanding is that the Enterolabs test tells you to which of the most commonly allergenic foods your body has already mounted a defense (i.e., produced antibodies). However, that doesn't mean that if you have an AI disorder that you should feel free to eat dairy or another AI food just because Enterolabs tells you you have not _yet made antibodies to it. It is my understanding, from what I have read from Jack, and other sources, that in a person with AI, or even a person who has a family history of AI (indicating the possible presence of an AI/HLA genotype), that all of the AI foods can wreak havoc at some point as long as you keep on eating them. As others have stated, it starts with gluten, but once gluten has comprimised the gut and ramped up the immune system, the AI progresses on to other foods. Therefore, it seems that the safest way to stay well the longest is by avoiding the AI foods forever. If you cannot bring yourself to do that, then I guess that is where the Enterolabs test would come in handy to tell you to which foods, of some commonly allergenic foods, you are currently_ making antibodies. Being that the Enterolabs test is testing stool, I believe that you would not have to go through the trouble (and possible organ damage) of reintroducing a food and then getting blood tests, since, as someone else noted, the antibodies persist in stool longer than they do in blood. If you have antibodies to the food via the stool test, then you know your body is reacting to it on an immune level. At that point, you wouldn't really want to mess around with reintroducing it just to see if it is damaging your thyroid or not. If you are producing antibodies to the food, then it will destroy some part of your body sooner or later, in all likelihood, whether it be your thyroid or what have you.

### kathylu — 2012-10-10T16:17:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-57307>

I really applaud the effort everyone is putting into understanding the finer details of absorption through the gut lining, but I am thinking "Why not just get tested?". Then you have an answer, at least to the limit of tests that are available today. The gut lining is beautiful in its selective specificity. Many molecules are supposed to get through, sometimes by passive diffusion, sometimes by active transport. How molecules activate the immune system is a different subject altogether and a very complex one. Sometimes molecules need to be linked together and occupy more than one receptor site to elicit a response, for instance. The condition of "leaky gut" (the pathological term, because a normal gut is designed to be leaky, i.e. selectively permeable) indicates pathology caused by inflammation. This allows molecules to slip through "tight junctions" **between epithelial cells that are normally not open to transport of undigested molecules. This condition is quite different than passive or active transport through** the cells lining the gut. Amount is important too...it's like the difference between a little dampness vs. a flooded basement after a storm.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-10T19:01:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-57353>

> **kathylu;58614 wrote:** I really applaud the effort everyone is putting into understanding the finer details of absorption through the gut lining, but I am thinking "Why not just get tested?". Then you have an answer, at least to the limit of tests that are available today.

I've been tested - many different tests. IgG allergy testing done 6 years ago showed dairy and eggs to be high, wheat was low/moderate. Blueberries were high, and pineapple.

Then 3 years ago I had serum allergy testing - everything was clean except carrots that came back just a touch above.

Then 2 years ago I had intradermal allergy testing - all nuts, wheat/gluten, tomato, tea, sugar, chocolate, banana, and lots of molds

Then this past spring intradermal was repeated - tea, pecans, peanuts, dairy, sugar

When I did my own elimination diet in the spring, I found the only foods I could really tolerate without severe water retention were non-starchy veggies and all fresh meats/fish, no matter what any of the tests showed. Very frustrating. So I'm pretty skeptical about spending more money on testing.

### SCRN2007 — 2012-10-10T20:07:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-57375>

I forgot to mention that when the human epidermal growth factor receptor trap door is opened, gut bacteria and gut bacteria fragments are thought to also make it into the blood stream. Especially if you are eating gluten, because it is sticky and brings things along with it. Gut bacteria are great in the gut, but in the bloodstream they anger the immune system and lead to inflammation. Also, according to Cordain, the molecular mimicry can happen due to similarities in the peptide chains of the bacteria (or viruses) that sneak in with the AI foods and molecules in our own tissues. Cordain says in his white paper on nightshades (see below), "This similarity in molecular structure betweeen infectious agents and our body's own tissues sometimes confuses certain components of the immune system causing 'self tolerance' to break down, thereby resulting in the destruction of tissues and organs by the immune system."

Cordain has white papers on his site that can help you understand all of this and include citations to peer reviewed journal articles. I have just given an oversimplified summary here hoping that it will help you to come to understand, as I have, why AI foods are bad news for folks with AI, or even a family history of AI (I guess this is where HLA genetic testing comes in handy too). There was no way I was going to give up all of those AI foods without understanding why. Especially since my family had already given up a lot to be on GAPS and I had gone as far as ketogenic GAPS. Now that I understand the AI foods, I have given them up.

You have to pay for Cordain's white papers. I did. It was worth it. They are (1) "Consumption of Nightshade Plants, Human Health and Autoimmune Disease," (2) "Cereal Grains: Humanity's Double-Edged Sword," (3) "Egg Whites and Autoimmune Disease." One of the cites in the paper on nightshades is to an article written by Fasano, so, yes, at least in terms of rheumatoid arthritis, you could get a peer reviewed paper suggesting that leaky gut paves the way for AI. Lectin and saponin research is out there, and Cordain says that it is the lectins and saponins that damage the gut to the point where the human epidermal growth factor receptor is easily accessible to AI foods. Also, if your tight junctions (spaces between the cells) are not tight (i.e., classic "leaky gut") then your gut is leaking like a sieve anyway and my guess is that most everything gets through into the bloodstream and can wreak havoc--molecular mimicry and inflammation galore. Also, he talks about how tomato lectin is such a potent immune trigger that it is actually used as an adjuvant in vaccines (an adjuvant is something that elicits an immune response). That was a wake up call for me about tomatoes.

If you download Cordain's white papers they actually have pictoral aids in them, which may help you explain what you are talking about to your doctor . . . but that is only if they are actually willing to read them. Unless your doctor is highly evolved, he/she will likely dismiss it as "research only" and equate it with speculation . . . especially since Cordain has a PhD and not an MD. (However, as noted in the interview I cite above, Cordain and others have shown a lot of this to be true in petri dishes--that might be the best research we ever get, since it would not be ethical to purposely destroy someone's gut lining in order to study what happens). Also, when billions can be made on drugs like Celebrex and Enbrel, is it really in the best interest of the pharmaceutical industry (who sponsors most studies) to fund studies showing that people could avoid AI if they avoided certain neolithic foods? Nope.

The "it's just research . . . you can't say that is _why_ it worked" response is what I got from my child's pediatrician when I explained to her that my child's seizures and severe speech problems (which had persisted for well over a month) had both stopped within 3 days of my removing green beans and dairy (i.e., the only AI foods she was still eating) from her stricter-than-GAPS diet. The pediatrician already thought I was nuts for putting my child on a strict diet like GAPS, nearly a year prior, when she had started autistic-type stimming, other autistic symptoms, and seizures. However, since GAPS stopped the autistic symptoms and seizures for nearly a year, until the recent return of the seizures and onset of the speech symptoms, I was not told that I should not have her on GAPS. In terms of the pediatrician's statements regarding my taking away the only remaining AI foods and it fixing my daughter's recent onset symptoms . . . when I asked her if she would feed her kid foods that caused seizures and speech similar to that of a stroke victim, in the presence of research that had identified a likely mechanism (i.e., inflammation from the gut affecting the brain in a child who had a family history of AI), she said, "no." Enough said.

My rheumatologist, who I saw for post cardiotomy inflammatory disorder, told me that I if want to avoid developing RA and psoriasis like my parents (in addition to the autoimmune issues I already have) that I must stay ketogenic and never touch grains or starches. She even told me to stay away from sugary fruits. I nearly fainted. She is as conventional as they come, and even _she_ knows that grains, starches, and sugars are hurting people. Her office treats patients conventionally. Wow! Who else knows this and only tells patients, like me, who seem willing to change or have already changed their diet?

I say download all of the articles you can and bring them to your doctor. If they read them, you may have helped make a better doctor. Worst case scenario, another doctor thinks you are crazy. Oh no! ;-)

### SCRN2007 — 2012-10-10T20:18:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-57380>

> **MamaGrok;58619 wrote:** Again, I understand this as well, but what you've said here seems contradictory to what was said above. The implication was that those certain foods can get through the gut, LEAKY OR NOT, so if that's true, what difference does a leaky gut make? I don't follow that logic, and my questions were based on it, not on what you've said above.

I think the take-away point is that if you have AI or a family history of AI, then, according to my understanding of Cordain's work, your human epidermal growth factor receptors are more likely to be flapping in the breeze and susceptible to AI foods (because they are not protected by healthy flora and their mucus). If you grew up on a desert island eating shellfish, then an AI food every now and then might not get through your gut wall via active transport through the human epidermal growth factor receptor and wreak havoc. However, in those of us who are already broken, AI foods can break us further.

### MamaGrok — 2012-10-10T20:24:54+0300

> **SCRN2007;58682 wrote:** AI foods are bad news for folks with AI, or even a family history of AI (I guess this is where HLA genetic testing comes in handy too).

I understand that foods on Cordain's AI list are "sticky," bringing pathogens & (?) antigens in with them through the "door" when they turn the "key."

But why is this a problem for people with a family history of AI, or who have an AI, moreso than the general population?

That's what I'm missing.

### MamaGrok — 2012-10-10T20:33:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-57387>

BTW, I've also had that experience where a doctor knows something ,but won't mention it till you bring it up. One doc prescribed antibiotics for my child, and I mentioned yogurt for probiotics, and he said, "Yes, you should definitely do that." Another, an eye doc, steadfastly refused to admit that ANYthing other than genes could affect eyesight until I mentioned studies on Eskimos & monkeys & near work, at which point he said, "Yes, but what are you going to tell people? Stop reading?"

I am at this place where

\- I THINK I have Hashi's, but have no antibody proof.

\- Science THINKS we have to avoid not just most, but ALL grains, nuts, legumes, dairy, tomatoes, potatoes, peppers, eggplants, & eggs, but isn't 100% sure.

\- Some people think I have to avoid all starchy veggies & all fruits other than seasonal berries, as well.

and I'm just having a hard time with it. I've done stints of 6+ months with nothing but meat, seafood, & fat, and seen no improvement in my symptoms (from fatigue to bloating). I have a hard time committing to even one more week of such dreary restriction. If I could know that 100% will help & anything less will lead me to misery (more than I already have), I'M THERE. But all I have are strong guesses. What happens if I eat 99% spot-on, but go to a restaurant now and then where a conscientious, well-paid chef doesn't have any coconut oil or tallow to cook my food in? Will I destroy everything? Will I be in vastly more misery? Will I cut 20 years of healthy living off my life, or just 1?

The scientist in me needs some FREAKING DATA. How can I make a decision without it? How can I limit myself to food misery for the rest of my life without any?

All I want is eggs, trace amounts of red pepper, & butter. Or ghee, I could live with ghee. Seriously. I can live without it if I know it will provide a real, statistically significant benefit. But will it? Again, I'm comparing it to trace amounts on rare occasions, not daily indulgence. (If Dr. Kruse replies here, I'll smack him. He gets to eat eggs, pepper, & butter.  )

### SCRN2007 — 2012-10-10T20:36:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-57389>

> **MamaGrok;58599 wrote:**
>
> Question du jour: if these foods have this ability to permeate intact gut cells, why would leaky gut matter at all?
>
>
>
> And next: Why does Dr. Kruse say to test eggs, when he says absolutely to avoid, say, nuts? He says to see if BCM-7 test says to avoid dairy, but that the right kind of dairy is okay if your test is negative. If these foods are automatically bad for AI, why would he say this?

I guess it would matter because even if your gut is _not_ leaking like a sieve via "loose" tight junctions, and you are someone who only has a slightly damaged lining from years of eating lectins and saponins and/or having an AI genotype, then the AI foods can get through intact cells via the human epidermal growth factor receptor.

Cordain says eggs are a bit different too (as in not as bad as grains), in the interview I linked to above. Perhaps this is where Dr. Kruse is coming from on testing eggs. Cordain also says that eggs were seasonal in our ancestor's time . . . so that even if they had an AI gentoype and started to develop AI the gut could theoretically heal in the off season.

I think that what Dr. Kruse might be saying regarding dairy (he talked about BCM-7 testing to me in my consult too) is that if you are BCM-7 negative, then you might get away with eating heavy cream and ghee (since they are mostly fat and theoretically have only trace protein) from A1 cows, or the occasional dairy from A2 cows. But I cannot think of a time where I have seen him say that it is a good idea for anyone (AI or not) to guzzle A1 milk . . . but that is just from what I have read and recall. The interview with Cordain, that I have cited above, also addresses dairy and may be able to answer some of your questions.

### SCRN2007 — 2012-10-10T20:52:05+0300

> **MamaGrok;58633 wrote:**

> It seems the biggest problem he's talking about is these foods dragging bacteria/viruses into the bloodstream. But how does that play into autoimmunity? Seems like it would trigger immunity against the bacteria/food combo in the bloodstream. And what is the difference in a person with AID having this happen ,and a normal healthy person having this happen, b/c he said this happens in a normal, healthy gut, too? :

My understanding is that strands of peptides can get fragmented off from either bacteria or the foods and that these strands can resemble peptides in the molecules of our tissues and cells. This confuses our immune system and it starts attacking our tissues. He talks more about that in his white paper on nightshades.

In an AI person, who is already living in the oxidized pathway, more antibodies and more inflammation is not good. In the theoretical "healthy person" either the human epidermal growth factor receptor would be better protected by a healthy gut lining, or, the person, being otherwise healthy could handle the small doses of inflammation from time to time . . . especially if he/she were eating in tune with seasons and not eating eggs, tomatoes, and hot peppers every day.

### SCRN2007 — 2012-10-10T21:47:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-57407>

> **MamaGrok;58689 wrote:** So you're saying that a healthy person might get AI in the bloodstream here and there (b/c of sticky transport) with no issues, but when you add in the transport that happens b/c of leaky gut/AI susceptibility, then you've got too much getting in?

My understanding is:

A healthy person with no AI genes could likely get by without problems if eating the AI foods in season from what I understand--because the gut would have time to heal. But if they gorged on them daily, they could get into trouble because the lectins and saponins could degrade the gut lining over time.

For a person with a really leaky gut, the AI foods will leak through like everything else it seems, with the added bonus that they can bring other things through with them and really get the immune system revved up compared to other foods and all of the lectins and saponins would further damage the gut lining and prevent healing.

However, for the person with AI in his/her genetics, I think the major issue is that their immune system is more likely to get confused when distinguishing self from non-self to begin with . . . so they are starting out behind the 8 ball when it comes to the damage that can be caused by AI foods and/or the inflammation that stems from leaky gut.

The person with active AI disease is really behind the 8 ball because they already have antibodies attacking their tissues and every bite of AI food is like pouring gasoline on the fire.

+1

I totally understand where you are coming from. When I was diagnosed a month ago I wasn't necessarily suprised because I knew there was a history of Hypothryoid. I had already been mostly gluten free, I was still losing weigh on Leptin RX, I slept good, no stomach issues at all (no symptons of leaky gut), no aches and pains, minor fatigue at the end of the day but certainly nothing chronic, so all in all I have felt pretty good for last 18 months. That being said, according to the lab work I am sick and need to eliminate some of the limited foods I still eat..eggs, red meat, corn, mushrooms, tomatoes, caffeine, etc. I have done that for the most part, started T4, and feel no different. Granted there are days when I "feel" more hyper than hypo but honestly may just been my imagination since I now know it is a symptom of "hashis". I will get retested by Endo in a few weeks but he has already said he won't do antibody testing again because it is irrelavant. Once they are present they are always present. So, I have no marker for whether my "diet changes" are having any effect on my life. I know I am more limited than I was before on food options which isn't fun and it is very hard to stay motivated to continue when I have no idea if the choices I am making are having any impact on the future.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-10T22:31:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-57417>

Forgive me if I ask an "obvious" question...

If our stomach acid and enzymes are working well (or we take HCL and digestive enzymes with a meal), wouldn't that break everything down enough so that these pesky peptides are no longer in their problem forms? I have seen on the enzymestuff website, they talk about taking certain enzymes _instead of_ the gluten free/casein free diet. Perhaps their perspective is outdated, or lacking. Realistically, I am trying to think of a work-around to help with "special occasions" when I may decide to knowingly forego the "diet," even a little bit (like make the pumpkin pie. with the nut crust, and maybe even use egg in the filling).

Quite frankly, I'm think there's a tradeoff to be made between longevity and quality, and food definitely plays a role in quality... at least I think it does. It may simply be my "bad brain" doing the talking at the moment. Maybe once I've eaten enough oysters, I'll never look back. Until then though, I'm struggling.

### SCRN2007 — 2012-10-10T22:34:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-57418>

> **KiwiLauren;58711 wrote:** So this raises two basic questions for me:
>
> 1) if you have AI are you always living in an oxidized pathway, regardless how optimal you become?
>
> 2) how are we diagnosing AI in those of us who do not have antibodies?

I think if you have AI you are always more prone to living in an oxidized pathway, even when you are doing all the right things, so you need to be more careful than other people. I don't think the 80/20 rule or even the 90/10 rule apply once you have an AI. I think when you have AI you need to do everything right all the time to limit the oxidation. Maximize the protective factors Dr. Kruse talks about and minimize the threats to your health Dr. Kruse talks about--I do not think that we AI folks get any leeway:-(

My understanding is that if you go off AI foods your blood levels of self-antibodies may go down (which is a good thing healthwise, but a tricky thing diagnosis wise). At that point you may be able to still detect antibodies to foods through the Enterolabs stool testing. If positive, that would tell you that you are making antibodies to foods. However, even if you do not have antibodies to a particular AI food, that does not mean that you will not in the future if you continue to eat the AI food--since AI foods are so talented at getting through the gut by flipping the HEGF receptor and good at degrading the gut lining via their lectins and saponins. Antibodies to foods would be a sign, it seems, that you are at least setting yourself up for AI if you continue to eat your particular allergenic foods and the AI foods--because you wouldn't have the antibodies in the first place if the foods were not getting through your gut and into your bloodstream. Antibodies to foods along with a positive test on the Enterolab HLA-DQ test, or some other genetic test for the HLA (AI-prone) genotype, would really suggest that you need to get off AI foods, it seems, because you would know you have an AI genotype AND that you had already started reacting to some foods that had gotten through your gut wall. Eating any food for which you have antibodies would lead to inflammation as long as your gut was allowing it into the bloodstream, which it _is_ doing if your gut is leaky or if the food is an AI food. Dr. Kruse told me in my consult that if I eat a food to which I am reacting that it could result in my thyroid being attacked for 6 months into the future. I guess that is a pretty strong argument for doing the Enterolabs test and finding out which foods you already have antibodies for.

As for definitively saying whether or not you are full-blown Hashimoto's or not, I think you just have to do repeated antibody blood testing over time. You may catch them elevated since forces in your life other than food could worsen your disease from time to time.

I have been mulling over that same question (your question #2) since my last consult. Hopefully, Dr. Kruse will weigh in if my reasoning is faulty or have something to add to all of this. This is all very complex.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-10T23:30:08+0300

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-10T23:33:48+0300

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-10T23:35:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-57435>

> **Shijin13;58736 wrote:** I've suddenly found that eggs don't appeal to me for breakfast right now. Posted an ask jack question about it. Very interesting

I dont eat a lot of eggs in winter because they dont lay as many.......and now you know why. Sunlight is stimulative to egg laying.

When I said I dont eat bannanas in winter because they dont grow (Deans and Harris laughed).......it should make intuitive sense why I don't and wont eat eggs in winter. What did I tell a winter is like for me? I eat like a great white shark and live like a polar bear. Great white sharks feed mostly in colder dark water and nothing but fat and protein in the cold......they dont feed much in the tropical waters. Polar bears eat seals in autumn and early winter, and sleep in the winter and are generally boring while they get real thin and big muscles.......as they sleep in the freezing cold.

Anyone starting to realize now why humans have a major problem in our modern world yet?

They create their environments and do not follow the circadian signals the world gives it. Our brains our wired just like wild animals but because we encephalized so fast (3 million yrs) in the East African rift (BG3-and 4) we became the first eutherian mammals to be able to control our environment after we gained the ability to leave the environment we evolved in. This completely altered our epigenetic signaling compared to other mammals and it is why humans SNP's and traits vary compared to other species. Simple when you get the 30,000 ft view.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-10T23:47:01+0300

### jib — 2012-10-11T03:34:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-57499>

I can't seem to find the "other" AI/Hashi thread at the moment, but I happened upon this quote from Dr K on a different thread and thought that it was applicable to all of the discussion of those who have an AI or Hashi or are wondering about all of this process right now.

"Change is a hard barrier for humans to break. It require fluidity of thought and that requires both a physical and mental motion to be used to get on in the environment. **The new relationship helps you learn new ways of thinking about your situation and your life**. Ultimately, you look at the world in a way that would have been so foreign to you that it wouldn’t have made any sense before you changed. I practice thinking this way and it opens doors to my brain that were previously chained shut."

Most of us here know this and have heard or seen it in many different forms of verbage, but yet we still fight against the words with all of our being. Just thought this was interesting after reading the various threads on AI/Hashi.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-11T04:44:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-57520>

> **SCRN2007;58726 wrote:** I think if you have AI you are always more prone to living in an oxidized pathway, even when you are doing all the right things, so you need to be more careful than other people. I don't think the 80/20 rule or even the 90/10 rule apply once you have an AI. I think when you have AI you need to do everything right all the time to limit the oxidation. Maximize the protective factors Dr. Kruse talks about and minimize the threats to your health Dr. Kruse talks about--I do not think that we AI folks get any leeway:-(
>
>
>
> My understanding is that if you go off AI foods your blood levels of self-antibodies may go down (which is a good thing healthwise, but a tricky thing diagnosis wise). At that point you may be able to still detect antibodies to foods through the Enterolabs stool testing. If positive, that would tell you that you are making antibodies to foods. However, even if you do not have antibodies to a particular AI food, that does not mean that you will not in the future if you continue to eat the AI food--since AI foods are so talented at getting through the gut by flipping the HEGF receptor and good at degrading the gut lining via their lectins and saponins. Antibodies to foods would be a sign, it seems, that you are at least setting yourself up for AI if you continue to eat your particular allergenic foods and the AI foods--because you wouldn't have the antibodies in the first place if the foods were not getting through your gut and into your bloodstream. Antibodies to foods along with a positive test on the Enterolab HLA-DQ test, or some other genetic test for the HLA (AI-prone) genotype, would really suggest that you need to get off AI foods, it seems, because you would know you have an AI genotype AND that you had already started reacting to some foods that had gotten through your gut wall. Eating any food for which you have antibodies would lead to inflammation as long as your gut was allowing it into the bloodstream, which it _is_ doing if your gut is leaky or if the food is an AI food. Dr. Kruse told me in my consult that if I eat a food to which I am reacting that it could result in my thyroid being attacked for 6 months into the future. I guess that is a pretty strong argument for doing the Enterolabs test and finding out which foods you already have antibodies for.
>
>
>
> As for definitively saying whether or not you are full-blown Hashimoto's or not, I think you just have to do repeated antibody blood testing over time. You may catch them elevated since forces in your life other than food could worsen your disease from time to time.
>
>
>
> I have been mulling over that same question (your question #2) since my last consult. Hopefully, Dr. Kruse will weigh in if my reasoning is faulty or have something to add to all of this. This is all very complex.

I hope it's not asking too much of you since you've already explained so many aspects of this (and so well!) but could you elaborate on what 'full-blown Hashimoto's' means. I have never had a thyroid test that concludes I have Hashi's. But I've only been tested after finding Dr Kruse. Which means a very tight diet and all the other epi-paleo regimens. From what I think you're saying, the only for me to figure out if I have it is to stool test and find antibodies. I realise you are also saying that even if I had no antibodies, there is no guarantee that I wouldn't develop them if I ate AI-incompatible foods. But given that, doesn't that mean that everyone is on the AI continuum? And therefore at risk of developing AI if they eat AI-incompatible foods? Or have I missed something basic (likely)?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-11T22:14:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-57650>

> **Jack Kruse;58860 wrote:** Think eggs are always good for you? Read my recent answer here on Paleo hacks and get some knowledge dropped into your experience. [http://paleohacks.com/questions/154753/how-many-eggs-do-you-eat/154892#154892](http://http://paleohacks.com/questions/154753/how-many-eggs-do-you-eat/154892#154892)

Chook farmers here in New Zealand will tell you that commercial hens are programmed to lay all year around. However, the family farmer (many in our country still have their own hens, even in the big cities) will tell you that their more traditional, non-hybrid birds either do not lay over winter or seriously reduce what they produce. This is also when the moult and rest and the family farmers believe in letting them do this so they remain in good health. It seems another way that modern farming has distorted natural rhythms.

### chocolate — 2012-10-22T02:38:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-59558>

[http://www.hindawi.com/journals/ijn/2011/256420/](http://http://www.hindawi.com/journals/ijn/2011/256420/)

> Thyroid hormones (THs) are essential for normal metabolic function of the kidneys. Conversely, the kidney is not only an organ for metabolism and elimination of TH but also a target organ of some of the iodothyronines' actions.
>
>
> Background. The present study is to look for a correlation between oxidative stress and thyroid function in patients with the nephrotic syndrome in the remission phase as well as in a persistent proteinuric state. Introduction. Nephrotic syndrome is a form of chronic kidney disease due to which blood loses protein through the urine. We wanted to know if there was an increased loss of thyroid hormones in urine affecting thyroid function. Methods. 60 patients with nephrotic syndrome and 20 healthy non-proteinuric individuals as control subjects were enrolled in the study. We measured their serum tri-iodothyronine, thyroxine and thyroid-stimulating hormone. Estimation of lipid peroxidation (LPx) catalase, superoxide dismutase (SOD), and Glutathione peroxidase (GPx) were carried out by standard methods. Results. TSH was elevated in the nephrotic patients compared to controls, while TT4 and TT3 were significantly lower in the patients than in controls. Lipid Peroxidation and GPx were significantly higher in the nephrotic syndrome patients than in the controls, while SOD and catalase were significantly lower than in patients than in the control subjects. Conclusion. Nephrotic patients can lose significant amounts of thyroid hormones along with protein in urine, which can affect thyroid status, but this is reversible on remission.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-23T03:58:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-59771>

This Th1 and Th2 issue is one that needs to be placed in proper physiologic context. If you have active positive AB production on your labs than you may have a bigger issue to worry about. It means your thymus is no longer able to turn off your B cells which make your antibodies in our immune system. This must be fixed first in Hashi's. The best way to do this is create a low inflammatory millieu in the surrounding thyroid and thymus. I like the Epi paleo Rx to do it slowly but consistently. Once the thymus becomes altered it shrinks and its physiologic function declines. This is why a positive AB test tells the clinician a lot about the patient. When this is present it can disrupt the entire immune process and lead to further AI disease propagation. Without your thymus's ability to completely regulate your B-Cells actions they continue to make antibodies.

### nuttmegs17 — 2012-11-05T08:43:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-62158>

Hello! its been a long time since I last posted on this forum! I've thought about many of you often, but life was exploding. When I was here last, my job was becoming more and more stressful and toxic (both the environment and the people)...and so started the extremely stressful process of finding another job, interviews, etc. Well I found a great job which I love! BUT its in the middle of busy season which means less sleep, more travel and no spare time.

Health wise, I am sooooo much better than I was last year. I am still working on things, but I no longer have crushing fatigue and weakness. I feel normal-ish  I am cautiously going into the winter hoping to keep these changes, but terrified that the winter will exacberate my hashi's or something else. Last winter, around thanksgiving, i had a huge crash and pretty much had a terrible winter. Doing what I can to keep healthy. Well thats my update and I'm sticking to it

I am so very behind on the Dr K's articles. I did read the epi-paleo one and will do my best to stick with it this winter.

> **KiwiLauren;57585 wrote: I'm wondering what you all think of LDN for Hashi's. I know Chris Kresser is a big fan (and I believe his wife has Hashi's and is on LDN). Anyone have any experience?
> I am a patient of Chris Kressor and was able to get on LDN. My dose is 2.5, the recommended 4.0 was WAAAAY too much for me. His wife has grave's disease and is on a similar dose if i recall. I get it from a compounded pharmacy. When i was feeling so much pain during the winter, my antibodies were off the charts (over 1000)...since getting on LDN they have dropped to 200-300. I'm planning on testing again soon. Hoping that they whatever hurt me last winter won't rear it's ugly head this winter. If i start to have problems, I will look more into lyme disease (Dr. K suspected a bio-toxin at that time).
>
>
>
>
> > **Patty Cakes;57588 wrote:** I personally have not had experience with LDN but know of several people who are on it for Hashis and often they have other AI issues too. Results seem pretty good for those who have trouble managing the symptoms of Hashis. Less pain, less thyroid meds, less symptoms. To me that is just another drug to take and I would rather have my food be my medicine. With that said, for those who really need help and are doing everything they can with their diet, then LDN may be a possibility and I'm sure for some a life saver. But as a scripted med it comes with it's own set of issues. There is an LDN group on FB and I know some of the thyroid boards speak often of LDN.
>
> I think of it more as a supp than anything else. I was in the process of trying so many things: paleo, autoimmune protocol, iodine supplment, HC/thyroid med dose adjustment, bio-identical hormone treatment, that its hard to tell what made the biggest difference. i'd recommend it though. It honestly can't hurt to try it.
>
>
>
> TheKid;57984 wrote:** Has anyone here analyzed if their Hashimoto's is TH1 dominant or TH2 dominant? And if so, what are you avoiding or using as a result? Also, what's he best test for TH1/TH2 dominance? I'd there a gold standard?
>
>
>
> I was reading that green tea can be a problem for TH2 dominant Hashis. And I drink a lot of green tea.

I did the TH1/TH2 protocol (from Chris Kressor) and couldnt figure out which one made me feel worse. I think I may someday just pay for the bloodtest that tells you which one you are. I asked about it either on this forum or to Dr k on his MDA thread and he was onboard with TH1/TH2 being legitimate

MamaGrok;58694 wrote: BTW, I've also had that experience where a doctor knows something ,but won't mention it till you bring it up. One doc prescribed antibiotics for my child, and I mentioned yogurt for probiotics, and he said, "Yes, you should definitely do that." Another, an eye doc, steadfastly refused to admit that ANYthing other than genes could affect eyesight until I mentioned studies on Eskimos & monkeys & near work, at which point he said, "Yes, but what are you going to tell people? Stop reading?"

I am at this place where

\- I THINK I have Hashi's, but have no antibody proof.

\- Science THINKS we have to avoid not just most, but ALL grains, nuts, legumes, dairy, tomatoes, potatoes, peppers, eggplants, & eggs, but isn't 100% sure.

\- Some people think I have to avoid all starchy veggies & all fruits other than seasonal berries, as well.

and I'm just having a hard time with it. I've done stints of 6+ months with nothing but meat, seafood, & fat, and seen no improvement in my symptoms (from fatigue to bloating). I have a hard time committing to even one more week of such dreary restriction. If I could know that 100% will help & anything less will lead me to misery (more than I already have), I'M THERE. But all I have are strong guesses. What happens if I eat 99% spot-on, but go to a restaurant now and then where a conscientious, well-paid chef doesn't have any coconut oil or tallow to cook my food in? Will I destroy everything? Will I be in vastly more misery? Will I cut 20 years of healthy living off my life, or just 1?

The scientist in me needs some FREAKING DATA. How can I make a decision without it? How can I limit myself to food misery for the rest of my life without any?

All I want is eggs, trace amounts of red pepper, & butter. Or ghee, I could live with ghee. Seriously. I can live without it if I know it will provide a real, statistically significant benefit. But will it? Again, I'm comparing it to trace amounts on rare occasions, not daily indulgence. (If Dr. Kruse replies here, I'll smack him. He gets to eat eggs, pepper, & butter.  )

I know this feeling has passed for you but i do feel your pain. i will say that i too went 100% strict without anymore relief than when i was 95% strict. I think over-analyzing this can sometimes be maddening, causing more stress, more harm to your body and AID. I sometimes force myself to break away to ensure I dont get to obsessive about it all. it can drive you mad bc there doesnt always seem to be a rhyme or reason to anything.

I eat primal/epi-paleo for the most part **and think I was letting perfect be the enemy of the good"**...It was causing too much stress and doing more harm than good in my case.

Dr. K wont like this, but I have slowly incorporated more starches: bananas, potatoes (rarely - maybe once a month, no skin though), and rice (once a week when i eat sushi). i feel GREAT eating these things. now, if I ate them every day, i think I would be in trouble. I avoid peppers, but i am sure i will have salsa at some point. I do not eat these things a lot - more like rarely, but they are not "never" foods for me (gluten = never for me though). I basically eat 95-99% autoimmune protocol, but do allow some of the above in once in a while - if anything to see how i tolerate them. I think I'm ok with that. understanding that those are off the table for me in general. i made a pot of chili for my husband - primal style (no beans! just porkbelly, butternut squash, sausage, grassfed beef, the works) and allowed myself to have a bowl and guess what: no bad reaction. I didnt eat anymore (i made it for his dinner for a week when i would be gone), but i think i will continue to let myself have a bowl maybe once or twice during the fall or winter. again, realizing that its not ideal, I wont eat it as much as I normally would, but I will allow myself to have a bowl once a year!

Chris Kressor mentioned that when i was zero carb, I wasn't giving the bacteria in my gut enough to live off of, hence me starting to eat more starches. Not sure if I will continue this as it gets colder though; on the fence. I did notice that my stomach got flatter though.

One thing i have noticed - whatever I have learned here and am doing is working. Knock on wood, but I am the only person on my team who has not gotten sick during busy season. No cold, no fever, nothing. Even while getting less than ideal sleep. jetting off on a plane 1 -2 a week, etc. i do what I can to stay primal on the road but have let some less than ideal things like chocolate or an in and out burger (protein style of course) sneak in.....but generally am just grateful to have artisana coconut butter packs on me  The people on my team are "generally" healthy in the SAD way. They are young-ish, slim etc. I am the only one with an AID and yet....still doing ok! I hope i don't jinx myself by saying that outloud!

This has been quite a brain dump!

i want to mention that i used to have graves disease when i was in college but it has been in remission for over 7 years - no symptons at all. Now just dealing with hashis. and hoping and praying to get it into remission. I am ok if it is a lifelong thing - I know it will be, my hope is to manage it and live a normal life and i feel i am on the right track.

**

i believe many of us get a bit terrified over here bc we know too much** My friends have stomach problems all the time but think its "normal" and go about their lives. I dont think its normal to have stomach problems - I believe it's your bodies way of telling you not to eat something...but I do think there is something to be said for my friends attitude of taking things in stride. I get a stomach ache and I use that to educate myself about what my body is doing/needing/rejecting.

i am in good enough shape - size 2, 4, but do have trouble dropping weight and so that tells me there is still problems. my body doesnt let go of things the way it should. so i still have some work to do...

Hi Nutmeggs, great to hear from you and great to hear you are doing so well!

When I've heard Kresser and others talk about LDN it has been as a short-term supplement. They have described it as upregulating your system via extremely small doses of naltrexone which then enables your own system to take over. That has a lot of appeal to me and seems very different than a maintenance-drug. I would like to learn more.

I don't think Dr Kruse has any issue with carbohydrates in general (though not necessarily ones like rice); from my understanding it is the seasonality that is the key. So fruit in summer when it's abundant and growing, sure. Fruit in winter when all you can grow is swiss chard and broccoli, no. One thing I don't understand is how carbs would help to populate your gut in a good way.... don't so many of the things we're trying to get rid of (candida, etc) feed off carbs? I know that eating raw seafood certainly changed my gut!

I was listening to Abel James interview Nora Gegaudes (who, by the way, gave the perfect story for why cold + keto works brilliantly, though likely without realising it) and she pointed out that there is no such thing as 'zero carb'... you get some from the meat you eat, some from the leafy green veg, etc. She estimated even if you try to do 'no carb' you'll still come out around 15g per day minimum.

Love your point about perfect being the enemy of good if we let it. I'm not striving for perfect - that seems an effort doomed to fail. Imperfect, healthy and happy... that, I can live with.

Lauren

PS _So_ glad you've got a better job and are feeling less stressed... that is so critical to our well being!

> **Jack Kruse said**
>
> This Th1 and Th2 issue is one that needs to be placed in proper physiologic context. If you have active positive AB production on your labs than you may have a bigger issue to worry about. It means your thymus is no longer able to turn off your B cells which make your antibodies in our immune system. This must be fixed first in Hashi's. The best way to do this is create a low inflammatory millieu in the surrounding thyroid and thymus. I like the Epi paleo Rx to do it slowly but consistently. Once the thymus becomes altered it shrinks and its physiologic function declines. This is why a positive AB test tells the clinician a lot about the patient. When this is present it can disrupt the entire immune process and lead to further AI disease propagation. Without your thymus's ability to completely regulate your B-Cells actions they continue to make antibodies.

To continue the point further.......Autoimmunity is a pathway of disease that gives a variety of symptoms.......but one must also remember that you need "decent" functioning T cells (thymus) to turn off the B cells. This process requires methylation to be somewhat decent as well. When methylation is off or sub optimal one of the predisposing factors to auto immunity is then met.........This is why most AI diseases have poor Vitamin D, B12, high serum folate levels, high HS CRP levels. None is the smoking gun........but you should be quite aware where there is smoke........fire is not far behind.

### vkiernan — 2012-09-21T05:30:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-things-t3.3783/post-53114>

Look what I found...

> Reverse T3 Pearls.........
>
>
>
> It is well known that thyroid hormones regulate metabolism and that low thyroid hormone production (hypothyroidism) causes low metabolism, but it has only recently been understood that thyroid production can be fine but there can a problem of activation of the hormones inside the cells that can be a major cause of low metabolism.
>
>
>
> The thyroid gland secretes an inactive thyroid hormone called thyroxine, also known as T4. This is regulated by thyroid stimulation hormone (TSH) produced by the brain (specifically the pituitary). Normally, the inactive T4 is converted inside the cell to the active thyroid hormone called triiodothyronine (also known as T3). Most doctors will check TSH and T4 levels to see if thyroid levels are normal.
>
>
>
> The studies are showing that it is not the production of thyroid that is the problem, but rather it is problem inside the cell that the inactive T4 is not converted to T3 but rather to a mirror image of T3 called reverse T3. The reverse T3 has the opposite effect of T3, blocking the effects of T3 and lowering rather than increasing metabolism.
>
>
>
> It is an evolutionary fall-back that was useful in times of famine or in hibernating animals to lower metabolism. Studies are showing that stress and dieting (especially yo-yo dieting) can set this hormone into action as well as chronic illness such as diabetes, chronic fatigue syndrome and fibromyalgia.
>
>
>
> The production of reverse T3 is found to be a major method by which the body 'tries" to regain any lost weight with dieting. As soon as the body senses a reduction in calories, the production of reverse T3 is stimulated to lower metabolism. With chronic dieting or stress, the body often stays in this "starvation mode" with elevated levels of reverse T3 and decreased levels of T3, which is a major reason for the regaining of lost weight with dieting as well being the mechanism behind stress induced weight gain (it is not due to increased cortisol).
>
>
>
> Testing: There has been a long held belief by endocrinologists and other physicians that adequate thyroid levels can be determined by testing the TSH and T4 levels. Studies are showing that such standard testing will miss 80% of thyroid dysfunction so most endocrinologists and other doctors will tell their patients that their thyroid is fine based on this usual testing. The doctors must run a free T3/reverse T3 ratio. Generally, a healthy person will have a ratio greater than 2 so a person with a ratio less than 2 should also be considered a candidate for thyroid supplementation. Many endocrinologist and physicians are not yet aware of the significance or ability to run this ratio so it may take some searching.
>
>
>
> Treatment Considerations: The standard treatment of hypothyroidism involves the supplementation with T4, including Synthroid and Levoxyl. These are not effective to remedy such a situation because the problem is not the amount of T4 but rather the excess conversion of T4 to reverse T3, blocking effects of the active T3. One must bypass the abnormality by supplementing with physiologic doses of T3, not T4 (preferably timed released T3). It is not appropriate to give thyroid hormone for weight loss, but rather to correct an abnormality diagnosed by appropriate blood tests.
>
>
> nope.......Wilson is dead wrong. Rev T3 is a competive inhibitor to T3 and T4. You must find the cause of the rev T3 and stop and reverse it. If you give someone T3 or T4 on top of it youre "pissing in the wind". This is the most common mistake I see in thyroid hormone 101 Tx. Giving more T3 or T4 in this scenario just raises the revT3.

This is a good start. I need to now decipher the difference between the 2 thoughts from Jack. Everything in context so there has to be something I am missing. Or, maybe that is exactly what the 2nd thought is about. You can't treat everyone the same as we are all dealing with different things even though on the surface they "appear" to be the same.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-21T08:08:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-things-t3.3783/post-53129>

>

>
> I wonder what happens if we eat thyroid glands from hypothyroid animals?????

We have an organic butcher in town who sources only grass fed, fully organic meat. A friend (who is on thyroid meds) and I have been discussing this idea of eating animal thyroid. She knows the butcher and is going to talk with him. Our pig butcher (another one who uses only cage-free healthy pigs) will butcher an entire pig and give it to you. If the organic guy can't do it, I'll speak with the pig guy (I just hope I don't have to buy an entire pig just to get ONE thyroid.) I think I've got a good shot at this. The question is, is it wise?

### Kiera2 — 2012-09-21T09:15:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-things-t3.3783/post-53136>

Lauren, I would consider it as a last resort, if I had no access to any other form of T4/T3. But only because I have no thyroid at all, so until I am cold adapted enough that my brain doesn't care, I need T4/T3 replacement.

There would be risks involved, because you have no way of measuring the dose. Eating a fried sheep thyroid twice weekly is better than nothing, but I don't think it's anywhere near optimal.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-21T16:09:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-things-t3.3783/post-53166>

ok Ladies and Gents

here's my take aways on T3 after BG12 (I posted this on the comments section of the blog) - thought it would fit in well here.

"Epic Post Jack. Its taken me 5 days to truly absorb the importance of it.

This epic has driven me to ask the following questions to you and my PCP (they can be rhetorical for you if they’re too specific).

Is my Low T3 really low if I’m eating an optimal Epi-Paleo RX? before this blog I would have thought having Low T3 is problematic – but I think not. I think my body was doing what its designed to – however, I wasn’t feeding my brain what it needed (which is why I’m in the health state I’m in).

So eating Epi-Paleo and the increase in natural iodine consumption explains why I feel like my thyroid has turned on w/in the past 8 wks of really pushing the Epi-Paleo Rx.

Additionally is that Low T3 suboptimal b/c its impacting hormone production because of circadian mismatches and lack of iodine, with the end result being pregnenolone steal?

so, Theoretically, If this is my only problem, the Epi-Paleo Rx will fix this due to sufficient iodine, correct?

The next statement that’s rolling around my brain is: When you have Low T3, you produce rT3 as a workaround to prevent ROS, I think in certain situations (short term not stressed neolithic life) this could be a good thing b/c rT3 protects us from ROS in times of stress.

Additionally if rT3 is an oxidative protection mechanism it is also a good indicator of pregnenolone steal?

The final takeaway I get from this blog is that If I have Low T3, adoption of the Epi-Paleo Rx will allow my body to recover its Iodine state – which will begin to push changes in the T4-> rT3 conversion, back to T4->T3, which the body can use to convert LDL w/Vit A -> pregnenolone

and from the pregnenolone -> progesterone -> all other steriod hormones.

All I can say is WOW."

### johnnyb — 2012-09-21T17:19:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-things-t3.3783/post-53178>

> **vkiernan;53749 wrote:**
>
> This is a good start. I need to now decipher the difference between the 2 thoughts from Jack. Everything in context so there has to be something I am missing. Or, maybe that is exactly what the 2nd thought is about. You can't treat everyone the same as we are all dealing with different things even though on the surface they "appear" to be the same.

I'll take a stab at this. On one hand we see Jack agree that Wilson's syndrome exists (rT3 too high, TSH and T4 potentially "normal" yet one is still hypothyroid). Then he says Wison is dead wrong. I think issue is that Wilson would treat this by giving lots of T3 to turn off TSH from the brain causing the thyroid to shut off T4 until all the rT3 clears providing a "reset". Then back off the T3 allowing TSH -> T4 -> T3 to return to healthy function. I think Jack disagrees with the cure part. There's no magic reset. You need to find the underlying cause like LR and or inflamation and address that aggressively. I bet you should probably be taking T3 while you address LR and inflamation and clear out the rT3. You do want your Ferarri running as you heal.

This was my hope too after reading BG-12, but then I kind of got a smackdown  Again, I think the whole issue of context needs to come into play. Could epi-paleo be enough to improve T3, increase iodine, and have a positive trickle down effect on all hormones?!?! I think that CAN happen, but I also think it's possible some of us are too broken to have such an easy fix. ### johnnyb — 2012-09-21T17:51:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-things-t3.3783/post-53196>

On my consult jack said my labs/symptoms indicate functional hypothyroidism, mild pregnenolone steal and a little low am cortisol. So I have been bending my mind on this quite intently trying to learn how this works.

I'm betting that low T3 is pretty much suboptimal. If your body is keeping it low I would expect that indicates a problem. Is T4 converting to rT3 due to inflamation or starvation or LR. Is T3 low because it's all getting used to make pregnenolone? which means your not making enough to keep up with demand. Maybe I am not thinking of why a low T3 would indicate a healthy state. One might say a low T3 is good because it's a survival response, but I would suggest that it's not optimal even if survival does come first.

I think you may have cause and effect switched with pregnenolone steal. I think pregnenolone steal indicates pregnenolone is going to the cortisol/ oxidative pathway and not sex hormone/ reproductive pathway. Hormones getting used up on stress to survive uses up the T3 potentially leaving one with low T3 and maybe high LDL. Thats the steal part. The complication is that poor thyroid function, Hashis etc, or LR and inflamation turning your T3 to rT3 makes this worse, but I don't think it causes it. Look how LR and inflamation mess everything up. That's why they have such low numbers on the quilt.

>

**

I think your right context comes into play - most of us have more than just Low T3 going on, so yeah there's going to be a requirement to supplement in some way shape or form. I think Ideally - what I wrote - is how we're supposed to work, and if you're slightly broken it can be fixed w/just diet alone. most of us are well beyond that...but the diet coupled w/the right support will get us there...

I was seriously going to push for T3 this next update w/Dzugan - but after reading BG12, I'm going to focus on the Epi-Paleo RX and fixing my Pg:E2, and my gut. I'm going to embrace my Low T3, and let it ride. what RT3 I've got is protecting me from futher oxidation... so where is that inflammation coming from? That's what I've got to fix first, before I'll see real progress. If I follow BG12 and start pulling threads - that oxidation/inflammation started in the brain. how do I fix the brain Epi-Paleo. where's my other inflammation coming from - my asthma. Where's the rest of my oxidation/inflammation coming from - PCOS and Insulin resistance that's burning up my arteries. I'm going to pick off the easy things first. fix the brain, fix the asthma, fix the PCOS/Insulin resistance. all lead back to hormones, IL6, TNF etc.

So for others who are having Low T3, and producing RT3 - the question isn't just how do I fix it, you have to identify the underlying cause. Fix the underlying cause, and everything else will fall into place (for some of us that will require assistance of BHRT, and Compounded T3, among other things) What does Jack keep saying. All neolithic diseases are diseases of the brain. its all in the brain. If our brain can't work, even if everything is at 100% we're not going to get where we want to go.

go back to my comment on BG11

"**Light is the key that starts/stops the engine. (circadian cycles)

Food & hormones are the fuel

CT is the accelerator

The Brain is the navigator**

Optimal requires all systems working in concert, if one is off the entire system doesn’t work properly or efficiently**

So for those of us who’ve been struggling & seeing minimal if any success w/out paying attention to light should begin to see changes when we pay attn to it. However we must remember we have to turn back our epigenetics switches that have been on for Xyears. We also have to contend with the reversal of disease and illness as a result of our own personal mismatches

This isn’t going to happen over night. I’ve been working on my health in one way shape or form since 1999 only now am I getting some where Each success peels back another layer. Each layer brings me that much closer to cracking the code w/in me.

While we’d all love to have the BodyRock.tv bodies in 30 days we’re broken for most of us that isn’t going to happen over night. Once we get our stuff straightened out & flying right the possibilities are endless. While its not fun hearing it’s going to take time it’s the truth. That’s because we’re each working on our own personal masterpiece. And those things just don’t happen over night. They take time effort an love and many of us have to balance working on the masterpiece with the rest of our lives b/c they are integral

Remember what your masterpiece looks like today is not the final product. Think about the following question and how it relates to your journey: How long did Michael Angelo work on David to get it to its final phase???"

We have to bring everything together. one thing isn't going to fix what's wrong, for most of us. its going to take having everything working together at 100% for most of us to get where we want to go.... that said, some of us can't do everything at once, so we do one thing at a time, it will just take longer. If we fix the brain - it knows how to fix the body, we have to trust it.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-21T19:06:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-things-t3.3783/post-53216>

> **MartiD;53837 wrote:** Find the cause of the Rt3? So how exactly do you do that? So giving T3 or T4 meds is pissing in the wind? So what is the solution? Where did these quotes come from by the way? Are these from Dr. K?

Since you have Hashis you have a different situation. It is not a low FT3 that is caused by something but your body attacking your thyroid. First and foremost you have an autoimmune condition that is affecting your thyroid. As a Hashis person I look at my FT3 and rT3 from a slightly different viewpoint.

rT3 can often be caused by adrenal dysfunction, iron issues, liver issues (IIRC), and maybe some other items. After testing on all of those you will have a better idea of the cause and what direction to go for the solution.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-27T21:12:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-things-t3.3783/post-54808>

### freshveggies — 2012-09-27T22:07:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-things-t3.3783/post-54836>

> **vkiernan;55642 wrote:** They dosed me with what my Armour had in it for t3. I was on 60 mcg in Jan. and didn't notice a thing with it. My eyes were probably still swollen then though. What happended with the 50 mcgs?

How much Armour were you on? 1 grain?

1 grain of Armour has 9 mcgs of T3, then your 40mcg's of T3 would equate to 4.44 grains of Armour

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-27T23:09:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-things-t3.3783/post-54856>

> **vkiernan;55602 wrote:** Patty or anyone,
>
>
>
> I started the compounded T3 Sunday and have woke up every day this week with swollen eyes and am very tired and grumpy. I have not had swollen eyes in quite some time being on the Armour. Not sure what to do at this point. My doc has never used t3 only to clear rt3 out. She said she has had success with diet and reducing stress. Don't know what else I can change.
>
>
>
> Any input is greatly appreciated.

I wonder if it's the filler in it or did you get ginger or some innocuous filler? I know some people don't tolerate T3 easily and they go on very small doses but don't recall anyone getting that reaction.

Armour has 9 mcg of T3 in it plus the T4 as freshveggies stated. The point of using T3 to clear the rT3 (sorry if I have already mentioned this I'm not trying to be a "repeating talker") is to let your body convert all the T4 there to rT3 and T3. Basically let it do what it is going to do all the while supplementing with some T3. After about 8 weeks (approx) the T4 has done it's job and there isn't much left. This is assuming your thyroid is not functioning properly and with Hashis it is not. Then you can up the T3 which has to compensate for the T4 that's no longer begin converted because it's no longer there. That's why people end up on doses around 70-120mcg. I hope some of that helped even though I really didn't answer the question of swollen eyes cuz I really don't know.

### LinD — 2012-11-17T06:40:35+0200

I confess to using Kerrygold in my morning coffee.... Besides occasionally using ghee fur cooking, rarely cheese unless we happen to eat out and I get a salad and it happens to have cheese or I have it with bleu cheese dressing.... That's all the dairy I've had in over a year.

I either start or end my shower with cold water, sometimes both beginning and end. Plus I use a cold pack under my chin/neck area most nights. Not sure why I started that last winter, but I like it. I think I was hoping it would improve my neck line, plus help with cold adaption. What do I know?! ;-)

The doc also started me on testosterone gel.... One or the other (T3) might already be helping with libido. Yay! I was the one actually plotting before the work day was over. Hee-hee. Maybe it's all mental, but maybe the scripts are already helping. Yee-haw!

> I generally have fish/seafood once or twice a day. I try to remember to add Lugol's to a glass of water... But forget s lot. Haven't had any sea vegetables in about a month! What are you having?

Have you read the book The Devil in the Milk? Talks about BCM-7 (betacasomorphin 7) and why A1 dairy is so bad for people and he talks about those with MS a lot in the book and the connection to it. Sean Croxton has done 3 podcasts recapping the book and they are very good and fun to watch. [http://undergroundwellness.com/devil-in-the-milk-part-1/](http://http://undergroundwellness.com/devil-in-the-milk-part-1/). I would make ghee yourself and get rid of the milk solids in it and see if it helps but no cheese while testing. Casein for sure in the cheese. I recently bought some goat's milk, yogurt and cheese as it is made from A2 milk as goats are A2 only. Think Jack might say no completely but I wanted to see what happended. Nothing so far but I don't know about inside of course. The goat's milk was fantastic. Can't tell a difference at all. I did NOT get a stomach ache either. Reg. milk gives me a stomach ache within 15 minutes and it's not pretty. The cheese and yogurt was a little "goaty" (stinky). DH loves the cheese!

I am not eating the sea veggies just yet. That will be my next adventure. I had to figure out protein sources first since pork, chicken and beef are not on the list. I have added Iodoral to my regime and hope to flip that over to getting my iodine from sea veggies. I do eat fish/seafood once per day. I might miss 1 day per week. I have to work into these things but usually adapt over time. Sea veggies are completely foreign to me. No ideas about any of it. I have a bag of kelp that has been in my fridge for a couple of months and I have not done a thing with it. Don't even know where to start.... I really want the t3 support naturally.

Has the cold helped your neckline? I bet the testosterone is already helping you. I remember when I went on it a couple years ago and it helped for sure. Also took away my leaky bladder like when coughing or sneezing. Good stuff as long as it is not too much.

I am also doing minmal goat cheese and goat or sheep yogurt. The sheep is really good. No gamey taste at all. I did a med change about 6 wks ago. I was on synthroid only for 3.5 yrs, but T3 was subrange ad rT3 was above range. I asked endo for some T3 . He changed my 122mcg synthroid to75mcg and 10mcg liothyronine. I had labs 3 wks after this change and THS rocketed to 8+. I was previously in range. He kicked up levothyroxine to 88mcg and 30mcg liothyronine. I haven't had labs since, but my BG was running high which usually means high TSH for me. I kicked up levothyroxine on my own to 112 and liothyronine to 20 mcg 4-5mcg sub lingual doses spread out over the day. After 2 weeks at this dose BG has stabilized and come down and my mid day fatigue has abated. I don't get labs again until Jan.

I have been doing more with sea veg. Start by getting a few kinds dried and make small batches of broth to introduce yourself to the flavors. Throw some in any soup or stew you make. You can treat it like bay leaf. Leave it whole and remove before eating.
